1. **Reception and Weighbridging:** MSW trucks are weighed, inspected, and tipped on a concrete floor. Bulky items are manually removed.

2. **Primary Shredding:** A slow-speed rotary shear shredder reduces particle size to **<150 mm**, breaking bags and compacting volume.

3. **Manual Sorting:** Conveyor-based pickers remove visible recyclables, hazardous items (batteries, medical waste), and large inerts.

4. **Trommel Screening:** Rotating cylindrical screen with **50 mm aperture** separates:
   - **Oversize** (paper, plastic, textile) → RDF line.
   - **Undersize** (organics, fines) → composting.

5. **Magnetic Separation:** Overhead magnetic drum (≥2000 Gauss) extracts ferrous metals for recycling.

6. **Air Classification:** Zigzag air classifier separates light (paper/plastic) from heavy (glass/stone) fractions using terminal-velocity differences.

7. **Drying:** Rotary drum or fluidized bed dryer reduces moisture from **~50% to <20%**, raising calorific value significantly.

8. **Densification (Pelletizing):** Screw-press or ring-die pellet mill converts fluffy RDF into **25–30 mm pellets** of density **600–800 kg/m³**.

**Role of Each Unit:**

| Unit | Primary Role | KTU Marker |
|---|---|---|
| Shredder | Volume reduction, opening bags | Size < 50 mm |
| Trommel | Particle-size classification | Aperture 50 mm |
| Magnetic | Metal recovery | Fe removal > 95% |
| Air classifier | Density separation | Light/heavy split |
| Dryer | Moisture removal | MC < 20% |
| Pelletizer | Densification | Bulk density > 600 kg/m³ |

---

**Q.A (b)** A city of **population 8,00,000** generates MSW at **0.50 kg/capita/day**. The waste has **moisture 60%** and **calorific value 900 kcal/kg (wet basis)**. After preprocessing, moisture is reduced to **15%**. Determine:
- (i) Total daily MSW generation (in tonnes).
- (ii) Calorific value after drying (wet basis).
- (iii) Number of **1.5 m³** bins required if collection is **once daily** and compacted waste density is **450 kg/m³**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Total Daily MSW Generation:**

$$
M_{total} = N \times PCWG = 8 \times 10^{5} \times 0.50
$$

$$
M_{total} = 4 \times 10^{5} \ \text{kg/day} = 400 \ \text{tonnes/day}
$$

**(ii) Calorific Value After Drying:**

Dry-basis CV of original waste:

$$
CV_{dry} = \frac{CV_{wet}}{1 - M_1} = \frac{900}{1 - 0.60} = \frac{900}{0.40} = 2250 \ \text{kcal/kg}
$$

Wet-basis CV after drying to $M_2 = 15\%$:

$$
CV_{2,wet} = CV_{dry} \times (1 - M_2) = 2250 \times 0.85
$$

$$
CV_{2,wet} = 1912.5 \ \text{kcal/kg}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Drying nearly **doubles** the useful calorific value (900 → 1912.5 kcal/kg).

**(iii) Bin Count:**

Daily waste volume:

$$
V_{total} = \frac{M_{total}}{\rho_{waste}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{5}}{450} = 888.89 \ \text{m}^{3}/\text{day}
$$

Number of bins (1.5 m³ each, once daily):

$$
n_{bins} = \frac{V_{total}}{V_{bin}} = \frac{888.89}{1.5} \approx 593 \ \text{bins}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) **400 tonnes/day**
- (ii) **1912.5 kcal/kg (wet basis)**
- (iii) **~593 bins**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]`

**Q.B (a)** Explain in detail the **methods of waste collection** used in urban India. Compare door-to-door collection with community bin system. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Methods of Waste Collection:**

1. **Door-to-Door Collection:** The most recommended method under SWM Rules 2016. Waste collectors (often informal sector formalized) visit each household with a small cart (autorickshaw, e-cart) at scheduled times. Householders hand over segregated waste in two or three color-coded bins.

2. **Curbside Collection:** Households place bins at the curb; municipal vehicles pass on fixed routes.

3. **Community Bin System (CBS):** Large stationary bins (1.1 m³ standard) placed at neighbourhood points; residents carry waste to them. Bins are emptied daily by compactor trucks.

4. **Containerized Bulk Collection:** Roll-on/roll-off (RORO) containers for bulk generators (markets, housing societies, hotels).

5. **Transfer Station Based:** Used in large cities where primary collection vehicles transfer waste to larger compaction trailers for haulage to distant processing plants.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Door-to-Door | Community Bin |
|---|---|---|
| **Segregation quality** | Excellent (if enforced) | Poor (mixed at bin) |
| **Cost** | Higher (labour-intensive) | Lower |
| **Hygiene** | Better (no street bins) | Poor (open bins attract vectors) |
| **Coverage** | Slower | Faster |
| **Vehicle type** | Tricycle, autorickshaw | Dumper-placer, compactor |
| **KTU Verdict** | Preferred | Discouraged |

---

**Q.B (b)** Discuss the significance of **source segregation** in the context of Waste to Energy. Explain the role of the **Material Recovery Facility (MRF)** in the integrated solid waste management chain. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Significance of Source Segregation in WtE:**

1. **Improves Calorific Value:** Removing inert fractions (sand, C&D waste) from combustible fractions can raise CV from **1000 to 2500+ kcal/kg**, making incineration/pyrolysis self-sustaining.

2. **Reduces Contamination of Biogas Digesters:** Plastics, metals, and chemicals inhibit methanogenic bacteria. Segregation protects anaerobic digester performance.

3. **Minimizes Toxic Emissions:** Chlorinated plastics in mixed waste generate **dioxins and furans** in incinerators. Removing PVC at source drastically reduces these carcinogens.

4. **Enhances RDF Quality:** Segregated plastics/paper produce **high-grade RDF** compliant with **IS 17103:2019** standards for cement kiln co-processing.

5. **Economic Value Recovery:** Dry fractions fetch revenue (paper ₹10–14/kg, plastic ₹15–25/kg) that offsets WtE plant operating costs.

**Role of Material Recovery Facility (MRF):**

An MRF is a **centralized sorting facility** that performs:
- **Manual sorting** on conveyors (trained pickers).
- **Mechanical sorting** using trommels, optical sensors, eddy current separators.
- **Baling and storage** of recovered recyclables.

In the **ISWM chain**, MRF sits **upstream of the WtE plant**:

> *Source Segregation → MRF Refinement → WtE Plant → Landfill (residues)*

The MRF ensures that **only non-recyclable, combustible residuals** enter the WtE plant, maximizing both **material recovery** (circular economy) and **energy efficiency**.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Reporting waste in **kg** when the question asks for **tonnes** (1 tonne = 1000 kg). Always show unit conversion.
> 2. **Wet vs Dry Calorific Value:** Mixing up CV(wet) and CV(dry). KTU explicitly asks for the **basis** — write "wet basis" or "dry basis" in the answer.
> 3. **Forgetting SWM Rules 2016 Reference:** Mentioning **SWM Rules 2016** and **MoHUA guidelines** adds **impression points** to any answer.
> 4. **Flowchart Skipping:** Part-B questions often have sub-parts explicitly asking for a flowchart — drawing one is **mandatory**, not optional.
> 5. **Numerical Errors in Bins:** Forgetting the divisor $n$ (number of collections/day) when computing bin counts.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Waste Hierarchy:** Refuse → Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → **Recover (Energy)** → Dispose.
- ✅ **PCWG (India):** **0.45 kg/capita/day** (urban); **0.20 kg/capita/day** (rural).
- ✅ **Collection Frequencies:** Biodegradable — **daily**; Non-biodegradable — **twice weekly**; Hazardous — **weekly**.
- ✅ **Segregation Bins:** **Green** (wet), **Blue** (dry), **Red** (hazardous).
- ✅ **RDF Properties:** MC < 20%, CV > 1500 kcal/kg, particle < 50 mm, density > 600 kg/m³.
- ✅ **Key Formulas:**
   - $M_{total} = N \times PCWG$
   - $CV_{2} = CV_{1} \times \dfrac{(1-M_1)}{(1-M_2)}$
   - $V_{total} = \dfrac{M_{total}}{\rho_{waste}}$
   - $n_{bins} = \dfrac{V_{total}}{V_{bin} \times n_{collections}}$
- ✅ **SWM Rules 2016:** Make source segregation **mandatory** for all urban local bodies.
- ✅ **Five preprocessing unit operations:** Shredding → Screening → Magnetic Separation → Air Classification → Drying → Densification.
- ✅ **WtE Reactors Fed by RDF:** Fluidized bed combustor, moving grate incinerator, pyrolysis reactor, cement kiln (co-processing).
- ✅ **Energy uplift from drying:** Up to **~78%** increase in wet-basis calorific value.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Incineration: Process, Advantages, and Disadvantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT836 - WASTE TO ENERGY TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Incineration: Process, Advantages, and Disadvantages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Incineration** is a controlled thermal Waste-to-Energy (WtE) conversion process in which combustible constituents of solid, liquid, or gaseous waste are oxidized at **elevated temperatures (typically 850 °C – 1100 °C)** in the presence of excess oxygen, producing combustion gases (flue gas), non-combustible residues (bottom ash and fly ash), and recoverable thermal energy.

According to the **KTU 2024 syllabus framework**, incineration is positioned as the most widely deployed thermochemical WtE route globally, accounting for over **70 % of municipal solid waste (MSW) processing capacity in high-income economies**.

The governing combustion reaction for the principal organic fractions is:

$$
\text{Waste (C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z\text{S}_p\text{N}_q\text{)} + \text{O}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{SO}_2 + \text{NO}_x + \text{Ash} + \text{Heat}
$$

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – "The Controlled Bonfire":** Imagine a campfire. An open bonfire of garbage produces thick black smoke (incomplete combustion), foul odours, and leaves unburnt scraps. Now imagine that same fire placed inside a high-tech industrial furnace with carefully metered oxygen, temperatures above 850 °C, a tall chimney with pollution filters, and a heat exchanger that boils water to spin a turbine. *That is incineration* — a bonfire upgraded with engineering discipline. The 3 T's rule (**Temperature, Time, Turbulence**) replaces chance with certainty, ensuring every carbon atom cleanly becomes CO₂ instead of soot, dioxins, or unburnt hydrocarbons.

## 1.3 Why Incineration Matters in WtE

| Parameter | Typical Value (KTU reference) |
|---|---|
| **Mass reduction** | 70 – 80 % |
| **Volume reduction** | **90 – 95 %** |
| **Combustion temperature** | **850 °C – 1100 °C** |
| **Gas residence time (>850 °C)** | ≥ **2 seconds** |
| **Excess air requirement** | 100 – 200 % of stoichiometric |
| **Energy recovery efficiency** | 20 – 30 % (electrical) |

These metrics are board-favourite numbers — they must be memorised verbatim.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Sequence of Incineration (The 4 Logical Steps)

Incineration is not a single step — it is a **sequential cascade** of three physico-chemical zones, often misinterpreted by students as one simultaneous event.

### Step 1 — Drying Zone (Sub-stage: Evaporation)
- Waste enters the furnace typically at **15 – 35 % moisture** (wet basis).
- Sensible and latent heat from the flame drives moisture evaporation.
- Operates roughly between **100 – 250 °C**.
- **Energy sink** — moisture load directly depresses flame temperature.

### Step 2 — Pyrolysis / Devolatilisation Zone (Sub-stage: Gasification of volatiles)
- Organic matter thermally decomposes into combustible gases (CO, H₂, CH₄), tars, and a solid carbonaceous residue called **char**.
- Operating range: **250 – 700 °C**.
- This is *why oxygen must be carefully staged* — premature full oxidation here would starve the drying stage.

### Step 3 — Combustion Zone (Sub-stage: Char burnout & flame oxidation)
- Char and volatiles react with **stoichiometric excess air**.
- Operating range: **700 – 1100 °C**.
- The residence time here is critical; KTU board questions frequently test the **"3 T's Rule"**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 3 T's Rule (Cornerstone of Incineration Design):**
> - **T – Temperature** ≥ 850 °C (prevents dioxin/furan reformation upon cooling).
> - **t – Time** (Residence time) ≥ 2 s at peak temperature.
> - **T – Turbulence** — adequate mixing to ensure O₂ contacts every combustible molecule.

### Step 4 — Energy Recovery & Emissions Control (Post-combustion)
- Flue gas passes through a **heat recovery boiler (HRB)** generating steam at **40 – 60 bar / 400 – 450 °C**.
- Steam drives a turbo-alternator (Rankine cycle) for electricity.
- Air Pollution Control (APC) train: **Cyclone → Scrubber → ESP/Baghouse → SCR/SNCR → Stack**.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step — Engineering Rationale

| Step | Purpose | Failure Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Drying | Remove moisture barrier | Flame extinction, odour, smoke |
| Pyrolysis | Release volatile energy | Incomplete burn, CO spikes |
| Combustion | Convert C → CO₂ | Dioxins, soot, unburnt carbon |
| Energy Recovery | Convert heat → electricity | Wasted 2.5–4 MJ/kg potential |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board-Critical Formulas** — Memorise each one with its unit.

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | $\text{Stoichiometric O}_2 = \dfrac{11.6\,C + 34.8\!\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 4.35\,S}{100}$ | C, H, O, S = mass % of element | kg O₂ / kg waste |
| F2 | $\text{Stoichiometric Air} = \dfrac{\text{O}_2\text{ required}}{0.232}$ (since air = 23.2 % O₂ by mass) | – | kg air / kg waste |
| F3 | $\text{Excess Air (\%)} = \dfrac{\text{Actual Air} - \text{Stoichiometric Air}}{\text{Stoichiometric Air}} \times 100$ | – | % |
| F4 | $\text{A/F Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Mass of Air}}{\text{Mass of Fuel}}$ | – | dimensionless |
| F5 | $\text{Volumetric Reduction} = \dfrac{V_{\text{in}} - V_{\text{out}}}{V_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | In/out volume of waste | % |
| F6 | $\text{Mass Reduction} = \dfrac{M_{\text{in}} - M_{\text{out}}}{M_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | In/out mass of waste | % |
| F7 | $\text{Electrical Efficiency } \eta_e = \dfrac{P_{\text{electrical}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{waste}} \times \text{CV}} \times 100$ | P = power, CV = calorific value | % |
| F8 | $\text{Net Calorific Value (CV)} = \text{GCV} - 2.442 \times \dfrac{9\,H}{100} \times 1000$ (in kJ/kg) | H = % hydrogen | kJ / kg |
| F9 | $\text{Energy recovered} = \dot{m}_{\text{waste}} \times \text{CV} \times \eta_{\text{thermal}}$ | – | kW or MW |

> **Quick numeric for KTU exams:** Typical MSW has CV ≈ **8 – 12 MJ/kg** (wet), **200 – 350 kg air** required per tonne, and **350 – 500 kWh/tonne** electricity exported.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

Incineration powers **district heating in Copenhagen (Amager Bakke)**, **waste-to-electricity plants in Singapore (Tuas Nexus)**, and processes over **300 million tonnes of MSW/year globally**. In the KTU 2024 scheme, this topic bridges **Mechanical (furnace design) × Chemical (combustion stoichiometry) × Environmental (APC systems) × Energy (Rankine cycle)** — making it a multidisciplinary scoring unit.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem — Stoichiometric Air Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A municipal solid waste sample has the following ultimate analysis (dry basis, mass %): C = 30 %, H = 4 %, O = 22 %, N = 1 %, S = 0.5 %, Ash = 15 %, Moisture = 27.5 %. Calculate:
> (i) Stoichiometric oxygen required per kg of waste (as-received).
> (ii) Stoichiometric air required.
> (iii) Actual air supplied if 150 % excess air is used.
> (iv) A/F ratio.

### Step 1 — Convert to dry-basis elemental composition
The dry-basis compositions are already given (C = 30, H = 4, O = 22, S = 0.5). We must remember these correspond to **1 kg of dry waste**. For 1 kg of wet waste, multiply by (1 − moisture) = 1 − 0.275 = **0.725**.

Therefore, per kg of **as-received** waste:

$$
C' = 0.30 \times 0.725 = 0.2175 \;\text{kg}
$$

$$
H' = 0.04 \times 0.725 = 0.0290 \;\text{kg}
$$

$$
O' = 0.22 \times 0.725 = 0.1595 \;\text{kg}
$$

$$
S' = 0.005 \times 0.725 = 0.003625 \;\text{kg}
$$

### Step 2 — Stoichiometric Oxygen (per kg as-received waste)

Apply Formula F1:

$$
\text{O}_2 = \dfrac{11.6\,C' + 34.8\!\left(H' - \dfrac{O'}{8}\right) + 4.35\,S'}{1}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\text{O}_2 = 11.6(0.2175) + 34.8\!\left(0.0290 - \dfrac{0.1595}{8}\right) + 4.35(0.003625)
$$

$$
\text{O}_2 = 2.523 + 34.8\,(0.0290 - 0.01994) + 0.01577
$$

$$
\text{O}_2 = 2.523 + 34.8\,(0.00906) + 0.01577
$$

$$
\text{O}_2 = 2.523 + 0.3153 + 0.01577
$$

$$
\text{O}_2 \approx 2.854 \;\text{kg O}_2 \;/\; \text{kg waste (as-received)}
$$

### Step 3 — Stoichiometric Air

Apply Formula F2:

$$
A_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{2.854}{0.232} = 12.30 \;\text{kg air} \;/\; \text{kg waste}
$$

### Step 4 — Actual Air at 150 % Excess Air

Apply Formula F3. With **150 % excess**, total air = stoichiometric + 1.50 × stoichiometric = **2.5 × stoich**.

$$
A_{\text{actual}} = 12.30 \times 2.5 = 30.75 \;\text{kg air} \;/\; \text{kg waste}
$$

### Step 5 — Air-to-Fuel Ratio (A/F)

Apply Formula F4:

$$
\text{A/F} = \dfrac{30.75}{1.0} = 30.75
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — Energy Recovery

> **Problem:** A 500 TPD (tonnes per day) incinerator processes waste with CV = 9 MJ/kg. Thermal efficiency of the boiler = 75 %, generator efficiency = 35 %, plant availability = 85 %. Calculate the **net electrical export** in kWh/day.

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{waste}} = 500 \;\text{TPD} = \dfrac{500 \times 10^3}{24} = 20{,}833.3 \;\text{kg/h}
$$

$$
\text{Energy in} = 20{,}833.3 \times 9 = 187{,}500 \;\text{MJ/h}
$$

$$
\text{Thermal output} = 187{,}500 \times 0.75 = 140{,}625 \;\text{MJ/h}
$$

$$
\text{Electrical gross} = 140{,}625 \times 0.35 = 49{,}218.75 \;\text{MJ/h}
$$

Convert to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ):

$$
P_{\text{gross}} = \dfrac{49{,}218.75}{3.6} = 13{,}672 \;\text{kW} \approx 13.67 \;\text{MW}
$$

Apply availability (auxiliaries, downtime):

$$
P_{\text{net,export}} = 13.67 \times 0.85 = 11.62 \;\text{MW}
$$

$$
E_{\text{daily}} = 11.62 \times 24 = 278.9 \;\text{MWh/day}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Advantages vs. Disadvantages

> [!NOTE]
> This comparison matrix is a **favourite KTU board-table** format. Reproduce precisely.

| Aspect | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|
| **Volume** | Reduces waste by **90 – 95 %** | – |
| **Mass** | Reduces by **70 – 80 %** | – |
| **Energy** | Produces **electricity & heat** | Energy balance is negative for very wet waste (CV < 5 MJ/kg) |
| **Pathogens** | Destroys all at **> 850 °C** | – |
| **Land use** | Saves landfill space | – |
| **Emissions** | – | Dioxins, furans, Hg, Pb, NOₓ, SO₂, HCl, CO, PM₂.₅ |
| **Cost** | – | **Capital cost ≈ ₹ 40 – 80 cr/MW**; high O&M |
| **Residue** | Bottom ash recyclable (aggregate) | Fly ash is hazardous; needs secured landfill |
| **Public health** | Controlled plant | NIMBY opposition; localised pollution |
| **Carbon** | Avoids landfill CH₄ (28× CO₂) | Net fossil CO₂ emitter if no CCS |

## 3.4 Logical Deduction Chain — Why Incineration Reduces Mass but not Volume of Hazards

$$
\text{MSW input} \xrightarrow{\text{Combustion (Excess Air)}} \text{Flue gas (high V)} + \text{Bottom ash (low V)} + \text{Fly ash (very low V)}
$$

$$
\underbrace{1\;\text{tonne MSW}}_{\text{Volume} \approx 3\;\text{m}^3} \longrightarrow \underbrace{200\;\text{kg bottom ash} + 30\;\text{kg fly ash}}_{\text{Volume} \approx 0.15\;\text{m}^3} + \underbrace{6{,}000\;\text{Nm}^3 \text{ flue gas}}_{\text{scrubbed and released}}
$$

This explains the **paradox**: mass loss is only 70–80 %, but **volume** loss is 90–95 % because flue gas, though voluminous, is dispersed into the atmosphere after scrubbing — while the residual ash is dense and small in volume.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Incineration Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN["Input Stage"]
        A1["MSW Receiving and Weighbridge"]
        A2["Storage Pit 3 to 5 days"]
        A3["Crane and Grab Blending"]
    end

    subgraph FR["Furnace Stage"]
        B1["Grate Furnace"]
        B2["Drying Zone 100 to 250 C"]
        B3["Pyrolysis Zone 250 to 700 C"]
        B4["Combustion Zone 850 to 1100 C"]
        B5["Secondary Chamber Residence greater than 2 s"]
    end

    subgraph EN["Energy Recovery"]
        C1["Heat Recovery Boiler 40 bar 450 C"]
        C2["Steam Turbine Rankine"]
        C3["Generator and Transformer"]
        C4["Bottom Ash Discharger"]
    end

    subgraph AP["Air Pollution Control APC"]
        D1["Cyclone and Quench"]
        D2["ESP or Baghouse"]
        D3["Wet Scrubber SO2 HCl"]
        D4["SCR or SNCR NOx Reduction"]
        D5["Stack greater than 60 m"]
    end

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 --> B5
    B5 --> C1
    C1 --> C2 --> C3
    B1 --> C4
    B5 --> D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> D4 --> D5
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Mass and Energy Flow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1["1000 kg MSW CV 10 MJ/kg"]:::in
    M2["5000 kg Combustion Air"]:::in
    M3["Bottom Ash 200 kg"]:::out
    M4["Fly Ash 30 kg"]:::out
    M5["Flue Gas 6000 Nm3"]:::out
    M6["Energy Out 3 MWh electricity plus 6 MWh heat"]:::en

    M1 --> MIX["Combustion Furnace"]
    M2 --> MIX
    MIX --> M3
    MIX --> M4
    MIX --> M5
    MIX --> M6

    classDef in fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#a30000,color:#000
    classDef en fill:#d9f2d9,stroke:#1f7a1f,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Diagram — The 3 T's Compliance Triangle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1["T1 Temperature greater than 850 C"]:::tri
    T2["T2 Time greater than 2 seconds"]:::tri
    T3["T3 Turbulence Reynolds greater than 10000"]:::tri
    CTR["Complete Combustion and Low Dioxin"]:::goal

    T1 --> CTR
    T2 --> CTR
    T3 --> CTR

    classDef tri fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef goal fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Diagram — Air Pollution Control (APC) Train Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Hot Flue Gas 1000 C"]:::hot
    P2["Quench to 200 C"]:::step
    P3["Bag Filter PM less than 10 mg per Nm3"]:::step
    P4["Scrubber SO2 HCl removal"]:::step
    P5["SCR DeNOx"]:::step
    P6["ID Fan and Stack"]:::step
    OUT["Emission Compliance CPCB Norms"]:::clean

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> OUT

    classDef hot fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef step fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef clean fill:#d9f2d9,stroke:#006600,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define incineration and state the "3 T's" rule.
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**Model Answer (≈ 120 words):**
Incineration is the controlled thermal oxidation of combustible waste at **850 °C – 1100 °C** in the presence of excess air to reduce its mass and volume while recovering energy. The "3 T's" are:
- **Temperature** ≥ 850 °C — ensures destruction of toxic organics and prevents dioxin reformation on cooling.
- **Time (Residence time)** ≥ 2 seconds above 850 °C — guarantees complete burnout of CO and hydrocarbons.
- **Turbulence** — promotes uniform mixing of O₂ with combustible gases, eliminating pockets of incomplete combustion.
Together they ensure complete combustion, minimal emissions, and maximum energy recovery.

### Q2. Differentiate between bottom ash and fly ash from an incinerator.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**Model Answer (≈ 100 words):**

| Property | Bottom Ash | Fly Ash |
|---|---|---|
| Source | Grate discharge | APC residue (ESP/bag filter) |
| Quantity | 75 – 85 % of total ash | 15 – 25 % of total ash |
| Composition | Inorganic, fused | Fine, enriched in heavy metals |
| Hazard | Non-hazardous (used as aggregate) | **Hazardous** (leaches Pb, Hg, Cd) |
| Disposal | Coarse aggregate in roads | Secured hazardous landfill |

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model | 14 Marks]**

> Describe the incineration process for municipal solid waste in detail. Your answer must include:
> (a) Process description with a labelled flow diagram, key temperatures, residence time, and the role of the secondary combustion chamber. **[7 marks]**
> (b) Stoichiometric air calculation from the ultimate analysis of a sample waste, including excess air correction. **[7 marks]**

#### (a) Model Answer — Process Description [7 marks]

Incineration is a multi-stage thermal process. The flowchart has been illustrated in **Section 4.1**. Steps in detail:

1. **Reception & Storage:** MSW is received at a weighbridge, dumped into a **3–5 day storage pit** for partial drying and homogenisation. A crane blends waste to ensure uniform CV (typically **8 – 12 MJ/kg**).

2. **Feeding & Grate Furnace:** Waste is charged onto a **moving grate** (reciprocating or roller) which transports it through three zones — *drying (100–250 °C)*, *pyrolysis (250–700 °C)*, and *combustion (700–1100 °C)*. Primary air is blown from below the grate; secondary air is injected above the bed.

3. **Secondary Combustion Chamber:** Crucial for compliance with the **3 T's rule**. Flue gas is held at **≥ 850 °C for ≥ 2 seconds** to destroy volatiles, CO, dioxin precursors, and pathogens. Excess air of **100 – 150 %** ensures full oxidation.

4. **Heat Recovery Boiler (HRB):** Generates steam at **40 – 60 bar / 400 – 450 °C**. Steam drives a turbine connected to a generator.

5. **Air Pollution Control (APC):** As in **Section 4.4** — quench → bag filter → scrubber → SCR → stack.

#### (b) Model Answer — Stoichiometric Air Calculation [7 marks]

Consider a sample waste with dry-basis composition:

**C = 32 %, H = 5 %, O = 20 %, S = 1 %, N = 1 %, Ash = 20 %, Moisture = 21 %.**

Per kg of wet waste, dry mass = 0.79 kg, so:
- C' = 0.32 × 0.79 = 0.2528 kg
- H' = 0.05 × 0.79 = 0.0395 kg
- O' = 0.20 × 0.79 = 0.1580 kg
- S' = 0.01 × 0.79 = 0.00790 kg

$$
\text{O}_2 = 11.6(0.2528) + 34.8\!\left(0.0395 - \dfrac{0.1580}{8}\right) + 4.35(0.00790)
$$

$$
\text{O}_2 = 2.932 + 34.8(0.0395 - 0.01975) + 0.03437
$$

$$
\text{O}_2 = 2.932 + 34.8(0.01975) + 0.03437
$$

$$
\text{O}_2 = 2.932 + 0.6873 + 0.0344 = 3.654 \;\text{kg O}_2\text{/kg waste}
$$

$$
A_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{3.654}{0.232} = 15.75 \;\text{kg air/kg waste}
$$

For 100 % excess air: $A_{\text{actual}} = 15.75 \times 2 = 31.5\;\text{kg air/kg waste}$.

$$
\text{A/F} = 31.5
$$

### Question B — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model | 14 Marks]**

> Discuss the engineering advantages and disadvantages of incineration as a waste-to-energy technology. Compare incineration with anaerobic digestion and landfilling as alternative MSW management routes.
> (a) Enumerate **six advantages** and **six disadvantages** of incineration with quantitative justifications. **[7 marks]**
> (b) Provide a comparative table covering incineration, anaerobic digestion, and sanitary landfilling on eight technical parameters, and indicate the suitability of each for the Kerala MSW scenario. **[7 marks]**

#### (a) Model Answer — Advantages & Disadvantages [7 marks]

**Six Advantages:**
1. **High volume reduction** — 90 – 95 %, drastically extending landfill life.
2. **Mass reduction** of 70 – 80 %; residue is inert.
3. **Energy recovery** — 350 – 500 kWh/tonne electricity plus usable steam.
4. **Pathogen destruction** at > 850 °C — eliminates hospital waste risk.
5. **Year-round operation** — independent of weather/season, unlike composting.
6. **Avoided methane emissions** from landfill (CH₄ has GWP = 28).

**Six Disadvantages:**
1. **High capital cost** — ₹ 40 – 80 crore per MW installed.
2. **Air emissions** — dioxins, furans, NOₓ, SO₂, Hg, HCl; requires costly APC.
3. **Fly ash is hazardous** — must be disposed in secured landfills (₹ 5,000–8,000/tonne).
4. **Net CO₂ emitter** unless biogenic carbon fraction is separated/credited.
5. **Discourages recycling** — "waste-to-energy first" mindset reduces upstream segregation.
6. **Public opposition (NIMBY)** — siting difficulties, perceived health concerns.

#### (b) Model Answer — Comparative Table & Kerala Scenario [7 marks]

| Parameter | Incineration | Anaerobic Digestion | Sanitary Landfill |
|---|---|---|---|
| Process | Thermochemical (oxidation) | Biochemical (no O₂) | Passive storage |
| Capacity (TPD) | 100 – 3000 | 50 – 500 | Unlimited |
| Volume reduction | **90 – 95 %** | 60 – 80 % (digestate) | 0 % (storage) |
| Energy yield | **350 – 500 kWh/t** | 50 – 150 kWh/t | 0 (CH₄ flared) |
| Capital cost (₹/TPD) | 8 – 15 lakh | 5 – 10 lakh | 0.5 – 1 lakh |
| Waste suitability | CV > 5 MJ/kg | Biowet > 60 % | All categories |
| Emissions | High if no APC | Low | Leachate, CH₄ |
| Kerala fit | Marginal (high moisture ~50 %) | **High (coconut, food waste)** | Limited (land scarcity) |

**Kerala Contextual Verdict:** Kerala MSW has **moisture 45–55 %** and CV **6 – 8 MJ/kg**. Without pre-drying, incineration is energy-negative. Therefore, **anaerobic digestion is preferred** for Kerala's wet waste; incineration is viable only for RDF produced after mechanical pre-processing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Marks Are Lost:**
> 1. **Not stating temperatures:** Always quote **850 °C / 2 s** for the secondary chamber — generic statements fetch zero marks.
> 2. **Confusing mass vs. volume reduction:** Examiners deduct marks if "90 %" is wrongly attributed to mass instead of volume.
> 3. **Forgetting ash classification:** Students write "ash is waste" — fly ash is **hazardous**, bottom ash is **non-hazardous**. Losing this distinction costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the 3 T's rule** in any incineration question is treated as incomplete — at minimum 2 marks lost.
> 5. **Omitting CPCB/environmental norms** — any emissions-related question demands mention of **CPCB emission standards** (PM ≤ 30 mg/Nm³, SO₂ ≤ 200 mg/Nm³, NOₓ ≤ 250 mg/Nm³).
> 6. **Stoichiometric calculation errors:** Apply the **dry-basis formula** then convert to wet basis — students often use wet-basis percentages directly, yielding wrong air quantity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Recall Points (KTU 2024 — OEBBT836):**
> - 🔥 Incineration = **controlled oxidation**, NOT open burning.
> - 🔥 **3 T's**: **T**emperature ≥ 850 °C, **T**ime ≥ 2 s, **T**urbulence.
> - 🔥 **Volume reduction = 90–95 %**, mass reduction = 70–80 %.
> - 🔥 Secondary combustion chamber is the **centrepiece** of any modern incinerator.
> - 🔥 Excess air = **100 – 200 %** of stoichiometric for MSW.
> - 🔥 Stoichiometric air formula (F1, F2) is the single most tested equation.
> - 🔥 Flue gas treatment sequence: **Quench → Particulate → Acid Gas → NOₓ → Stack**.
> - 🔥 **Bottom ash = non-hazardous**, **fly ash = hazardous**.
> - 🔥 Typical electrical export = **350 – 500 kWh/tonne** of MSW.
> - 🔥 Kerala MSW is **wet (45–55 % moisture), CV 6–8 MJ/kg** → incineration needs RDF or pre-drying.
> - 🔥 Capital cost benchmark: **₹ 40 – 80 crore/MW**.
> - 🔥 Energy recovered = $\dot{m}_{\text{waste}} \times \text{CV} \times \eta_{\text{thermal}} \times \eta_{\text{generator}}$.
> - 🔥 Incineration competes with anaerobic digestion (wet waste) and landfilling (residual rejects) — answer any comparison question with this **3-tier hierarchy**.
> - 🔥 Always mention **CPCB emission norms** when discussing APC.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Incineration Technologies and Their Applications
Pyrolysis: Types, Process, and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT836 - WASTE TO ENERGY TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Pyrolysis: Types, Process, and Applications # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Pyrolysis** is the **thermochemical decomposition** of organic (carbonaceous) feedstocks in the **complete absence** or in a **limited supply of oxygen** at temperatures typically ranging from **300 °C to 900 °C**. The word originates from the Greek elements *"pyro"* (fire) and *"lysis"* (separating), which literally means "separating by heat." In the context of the KTU 2024 Waste-to-Energy syllabus, pyrolysis is recognized as a **carbon-negative, resource-recovery oriented** conversion technology that transforms heterogeneous waste streams (biomass, municipal solid waste, plastics, used tires, sewage sludge) into three primary product phases: 1. **Solid residue** – char / biochar / carbon black 2. **Liquid product** – pyrolysis oil (bio-oil), tar, condensable hydrocarbons 3. **Non-condensable gases** – syngas (CO, CO₂, H₂, CH₄, C₂H₄, C₂H₆) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** > Pyrolysis is fundamentally different from *combustion* (excess O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O + heat) and *gasification* (sub-stoichiometric O₂ → syngas dominated). In pyrolysis, the **oxygen equivalence ratio (ER) is maintained below 0.2** to suppress oxidation reactions. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Popcorn" of the Waste World Imagine popping popcorn in a covered pan with **no air circulation** and on **slow heat**. The kernel does not burn; instead, the trapped moisture vaporizes, the starches break down, and you are left with three distinct things inside the pan: - The **charred hard center** (the *char*) - The **aromatic steam** that condenses on the lid as droplets (the *bio-oil*) - The **invisible gases** that escape around the rim (the *syngas*) Pyrolysis of waste is the **industrial, controlled, and engineered version of this exact process**. By manipulating *how fast* we heat (heating rate), *how long* we keep it hot (residence time), and *how hot* it gets (final temperature), we can choose to maximize any one of those three products — char, oil, or gas. > [!NOTE] > **Key Operating Metric:** The standard industrial benchmark for oxygen exclusion is an **Oxygen Equivalence Ratio (ER) ≤ 0.2**, where > $$ER = \frac{\text{Actual Oxygen Supplied}}{\text{Stoichiometric Oxygen Required for Complete Combustion}}$$ ## 1.3 Fundamental Reaction Schema The general stoichiometric representation of biomass/waste pyrolysis can be expressed in its most simplified form as: $$C_x H_y O_z \xrightarrow{\Delta,\; \text{No O}_2} \text{Char (C)} + \text{Bio-oil} \left( H_2O, \; C_x H_y O_z \right) + \text{Gas} \left( CO, \; CO_2, \; H_2, \; CH_4 \right)$$ > [!TIP] > **Memory Aid for KTU Viva:** Pyrolysis is *endothermic* (heat must be supplied) below **600 °C** and tends to become *mildly exothermic* above **700 °C** due to secondary char-forming reactions. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Sequential Reaction Stages Pyrolysis is **not a single-step process**; it occurs in a sequence of overlapping thermal zones. Understanding these stages is critical for KTU 14-mark derivations and reactor design problems. ### Stage 1 — Drying Zone (Ambient → 150 °C) - Free and loosely bound moisture evaporates. - Purely physical; **no chemical bond breaking** occurs. - Energy consumed ≈ **2.26 MJ/kg of water** (latent heat of vaporization). ### Stage 2 — Devolatilization / Primary Pyrolysis (150 °C → 500 °C) - Hemicellulose decomposes first (220–315 °C). - Cellulose decomposes next (315–400 °C). - Lignin decomposes over a wide range (160–900 °C). - Primary volatiles (acetic acid, methanol, levoglucosan) are released. ### Stage 3 — Secondary Reactions (500 °C → 900 °C) - Primary vapours undergo **cracking** into lighter gases. - **Re-condensation** produces heavier tars and secondary char. - **Water-gas reaction** becomes active: C + H₂O ⇌ CO + H₂. ## 2.2 Governing Thermodynamic Parameters The product distribution in pyrolysis is controlled by **four master variables**: | Parameter | Symbol | Range | Primary Effect | |---|---|---|---| | Final Temperature | T | 300 – 900 °C | Determines char vs. gas ratio | | Heating Rate | β | 0.1 – 10⁴ °C/s | Determines oil vs. char ratio | | Vapour Residence Time | τᵥ | 0.1 s – hours | Determines secondary cracking | | Particle Size | dₚ | < 1 mm – > 5 mm | Affects heat/mass transfer | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory Equations for KTU 2024 Examination.** Memorize these derivations and standard values. | # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Oxygen Equivalence Ratio | $ER = \dfrac{O_2^{\text{actual}}}{O_2^{\text{stoich}}}$ | dimensionless | Reactor air control | | 2 | Heating Rate | $\beta = \dfrac{dT}{dt}$ | °C/s | Classifies pyrolysis type | | 3 | Mass Balance (Overall) | $m_{\text{feed}} = m_{\text{char}} + m_{\text{oil}} + m_{\text{gas}}$ | kg | Yield verification | | 4 | Char Yield | $Y_{\text{char}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{char}}}{m_{\text{feed}}} \times 100$ | wt % | Performance metric | | 5 | Energy Recovery | $\eta_E = \dfrac{m_{\text{oil}} \cdot HHV_{\text{oil}} + m_{\text{gas}} \cdot HHV_{\text{gas}}}{m_{\text{feed}} \cdot HHV_{\text{feed}}}$ | % | Cold-gas efficiency | | 6 | Arrhenius (Kinetic) | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | s⁻¹ | Reaction rate modelling | | 7 | Mass Conversion (1st order) | $\dfrac{d\alpha}{dt} = k (1 - \alpha)$ | s⁻¹ | Kinetic modelling | | 8 | Energy Demand (Sensible + Latent) | $Q = m c_p \Delta T + m_w h_{fg}$ | kJ | Reactor design | | 9 | Higher Heating Value (Bio-oil) | $HHV = 0.3491\,C + 1.1783\,H + 0.1005\,S - 0.1034\,O - 0.0151\,N - 0.0211\,A$ | MJ/kg (Dulong) | Fuel quality | | 10 | Gas Density (Ideal) | $\rho = \dfrac{PM}{RT}$ | kg/m³ | Syngas handling | > [!NOTE] > **Constraint Reminder:** In the table above, all vertical bars are rendered as `\vert` to comply with markdown table syntax rules. ## 2.4 The "Why & How" Behind Each Stage | Stage | Why it happens | How it manifests | |---|---|---| | Drying | Moisture creates thermal barrier | Steam plume at 100–150 °C | | Primary Pyrolysis | Weak C–O, C–C bonds rupture | Acidic smoke, condensates form | | Secondary Cracking | Vapours remain in hot zone too long | Tar forms, gas fraction increases | | Char Formation | Recombination of free radicals | Solid carbonaceous residue | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Pyrolysis is the **backbone technology** for: - **Carbon sequestration** through biochar (1000-year stable carbon pool). - **Circular economy** — turning plastic waste into fuel oils. - **Tyre recycling** — recovering carbon black, steel, and oil from end-of-life tyres. - **Defence and remote-area power** — portable pyrolysis units for waste-to-fuel. - **Activated carbon** production for water and air purification. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — First-Order Kinetic Model for Pyrolysis Decomposition The standard **single-step first-order kinetic model** is the most commonly examined derivation in KTU examinations. The derivation proceeds as follows: ### Step 1 — Define the degree of conversion The fractional conversion of the solid feedstock is defined as the mass of volatiles released divided by the total volatile mass available. $$\alpha = \frac{m_0 - m(t)}{m_0 - m_{\infty}}$$ Where: - $m_0$ = initial mass of the sample (kg) - $m(t)$ = mass remaining at time $t$ (kg) - $m_{\infty}$ = residual mass at the end of pyrolysis (kg) ### Step 2 — Assume first-order reaction kinetics The rate of conversion is proportional to the unreacted (volatile) fraction. $$\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = k(1 - \alpha)$$ ### Step 3 — Apply the Arrhenius temperature dependence The rate constant is a function of absolute temperature. $$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$ ### Step 4 — Combine the equations into a single expression Substituting the Arrhenius expression into the rate equation yields the **standard KTU kinetic model**: $$\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT} \cdot (1 - \alpha)$$ ### Step 5 — Separate variables and integrate Rearranging and integrating from $\alpha = 0$ at $t = 0$ to $\alpha$ at time $t$: $$\int_{0}^{\alpha} \frac{d\alpha}{1 - \alpha} = \int_{0}^{t} A e^{-E_a / RT} \, dt$$ $$-\ln(1 - \alpha) = A \int_{0}^{t} e^{-E_a / RT} \, dt$$ ### Step 6 — Linear form for experimental verification Taking the natural logarithm of both sides produces the **Kissingers/Akahira-Sunose linearization**: $$\ln\left[\frac{-\ln(1 - \alpha)}{T^2}\right] = \ln\left(\frac{AR}{\beta E_a}\right) - \frac{E_a}{RT}$$ Where $\beta = dT/dt$ is the constant heating rate. A plot of $\ln[-\ln(1-\alpha)/T^2]$ versus $1/T$ yields a straight line with slope $-E_a/R$ from which the activation energy can be determined. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Reference Values for KTU:** > - **Biomass:** $E_a \approx 80 - 110$ kJ/mol > - **Plastic (PE):** $E_a \approx 200 - 250$ kJ/mol > - **Tyre rubber:** $E_a \approx 130 - 170$ kJ/mol ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Demand for Pyrolysis Reactor A frequently asked 14-mark problem in KTU 2024 involves computing the total heat input for a pyrolysis reactor. ### Step 1 — Identify the energy demands Total energy required = sensible heating of dry feedstock + sensible heating of moisture + latent heat of vaporization of moisture + heat for endothermic devolatilization. $$Q_{\text{total}} = Q_{\text{sensible,dry}} + Q_{\text{moisture,sensible}} + Q_{\text{latent}} + Q_{\text{pyrolysis}}$$ ### Step 2 — Expand each term Assuming moisture content $M$ (wet basis, fraction): $$Q_{\text{total}} = m_{\text{dry}} c_{p,\text{dry}} (T_f - T_i) + m_{\text{water}} c_{p,\text{water}} (100 - T_i) + m_{\text{water}} h_{fg} + Q_{\text{pyrolysis}}$$ ### Step 3 — Substitute mass relationships Let $m_{\text{feed}} = m_{\text{dry}} + m_{\text{water}}$ and $M = m_{\text{water}} / m_{\text{feed}}$: $$m_{\text{dry}} = m_{\text{feed}} (1 - M)$$ $$m_{\text{water}} = m_{\text{feed}} \cdot M$$ ### Step 4 — Final combined expression $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{\text{total}} &= m_{\text{feed}} \Big[ (1 - M) c_{p,\text{dry}} (T_f - T_i) \\ &\quad + M \, c_{p,\text{water}} (100 - T_i) \\ &\quad + M \, h_{fg} \\ &\quad + (1 - M) \, q_{\text{pyrolysis}} \Big] \end{aligned} $$ Where $q_{\text{pyrolysis}} \approx 0.8 - 1.2$ MJ/kg is the specific heat of devolatilization. ### Step 5 — Numerical illustration Consider **1 kg of wet sawdust** with $M = 0.10$, $T_i = 25$ °C, $T_f = 500$ °C, $c_{p,\text{dry}} = 1.6$ kJ/kg·K, $c_{p,\text{water}} = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K, $h_{fg} = 2260$ kJ/kg, $q_{\text{pyrolysis}} = 1000$ kJ/kg. $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{\text{sensible,dry}} &= 0.9 \times 1.6 \times 475 = 684 \text{ kJ} \\ Q_{\text{moisture,sensible}} &= 0.1 \times 4.18 \times 75 = 31.35 \text{ kJ} \\ Q_{\text{latent}} &= 0.1 \times 2260 = 226 \text{ kJ} \\ Q_{\text{pyrolysis}} &= 0.9 \times 1000 = 900 \text{ kJ} \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{Q_{\text{total}} = 684 + 31.35 + 226 + 900 \approx 1841 \text{ kJ per kg of feed}}$$ > [!TIP] > **Engineering Insight:** This 1.84 MJ/kg is **roughly 8% of the energy content** of the resulting bio-oil (~22 MJ/kg), confirming pyrolysis's excellent energy efficiency. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Pyrolysis vs. Combustion vs. Gasification | Criterion | Pyrolysis | Combustion | Gasification | |---|---|---|---| | Oxygen | None / trace | Excess | Sub-stoichiometric | | ER Value | 0 | > 1.0 | 0.2 – 0.4 | | Temperature | 300 – 900 °C | 800 – 1500 °C | 700 – 1200 °C | | Primary Product | Char + Oil + Gas | Heat + Flue gas | Syngas | | Air Emissions | Low (no NOₓ at low T) | High CO₂, NOₓ, SOₓ | Moderate | | Energy Self-Sustaining? | No (endothermic) | Yes (exothermic) | Marginal | | Carbon Conversion | Partial | Complete | High | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Pyrolysis Process — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow The following Mermaid block diagram represents the functional flow of an industrial-scale pyrolysis plant, detailing mass and energy streams between the major processing units. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Feedstock Receipt
and Pre-sorting] --> B[Shredding and
Size Reduction] B --> C[Drying Unit
100 to 150 degree C] C --> D[Pyrolysis Reactor
300 to 900 degree C
No Oxygen] D --> E[Char Collection
Cyclone and Screw Cooler] D --> F[Quench and Condensation
Train at 25 to 50 degree C] D --> G[Non-condensable
Gas Cleaning and Storage] F --> H[Bio-oil Storage
and Stabilization] E --> I[Char Upgrading
Activation or Briquetting] G --> J[Energy Recovery
Boiler and CHP] J --> K[Recycled Heat to
Pyrolysis Reactor] H --> L[Bio-oil Dispatch
to Refining] I --> M[Biochar Market
Agriculture or Filter] J --> N[Power Export
Grid or Captive] ``` ## 4.2 Classification Tree of Pyrolysis Types ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Pyrolysis Classification
by Operating Parameters] --> P1[Slow Pyrolysis
Carbonization] P --> P2[Fast Pyrolysis
Bio-oil Focus] P --> P3[Flash Pyrolysis
Ultra-rapid Heating] P --> P4[Vacuum Pyrolysis
Low Pressure] P --> P5[Intermediate Pyrolysis
Haloclean / Auger] P1 --> P1A[Heating Rate 0.1 to 1 C per sec] P1 --> P1B[Temp 300 to 500 C] P1 --> P1C[Char Yield 30 to 35 percent] P2 --> P2A[Heating Rate 10 to 200 C per sec] P2 --> P2B[Temp 500 to 650 C] P2 --> P2C[Bio-oil Yield up to 75 percent] P2 --> P2D[Reactor: Fluidized Bed] P3 --> P3A[Heating Rate above 1000 C per sec] P3 --> P3B[Temp 800 to 1000 C] P3 --> P3C[Residence time below 1 sec] P4 --> P4A[Pressure 5 to 30 kPa] P4 --> P4B[Temp 300 to 450 C] P4 --> P4C[Long residence time] P5 --> P5A[Temp 500 to 650 C] P5 --> P5B[Residence time 0.5 to 1 sec] P5 --> P5C[Reactor: Auger or Rotary] ``` ## 4.3 Pyrolysis Reactor Comparative Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SLOW[Slow Pyrolysis] S1[Fixed Bed Reactor] --> S2[Char Dominant Output] end subgraph FAST[Fast Pyrolysis] F1[Bubbling Fluidized Bed] --> F2[Bio-oil Dominant Output] F3[Circulating Fluidized Bed] --> F2 end subgraph FLASH[Flash Pyrolysis] FL1[Rotating Cone Reactor] --> FL2[Bio-oil and Gas] end subgraph VAC[Vacuum Pyrolysis] V1[Vacuum Chamber Reactor] --> V2[High Quality Bio-oil] end ``` ## 4.4 Mass and Energy Flow Diagram of Pyrolysis Plant ```mermaid flowchart TD IN[Feedstock Input
1000 kg] --> R[Reactor] R --> C1[Char 300 kg] R --> C2[Bio-oil 450 kg] R --> C3[Non-condensable Gas 250 kg] R -.Heat In.-> H[Heat Source] C1 --> M1[Char Application] C2 --> M2[Bio-oil Refining] C3 --> M3[Boiler or CHP] M3 -.Recovered Heat.-> H ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering note:** The mass percentages shown are indicative for **fast pyrolysis of lignocellulosic biomass** at ~550 °C. Actual yields depend on feedstock composition, moisture, and ash content. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define pyrolysis and distinguish it from combustion. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]` **Model Answer:** Pyrolysis is the **thermochemical decomposition of organic material in the absence of oxygen** at temperatures of **300–900 °C**, producing char, bio-oil, and non-condensable gases. Combustion, in contrast, is the **exothermic oxidation of fuel in excess oxygen**, yielding CO₂, H₂O, and heat. The key distinctions are summarized in the table below. | Feature | Pyrolysis | Combustion | |---|---|---| | Oxygen | Absent / trace | Excess | | Energy balance | Endothermic | Exothermic | | Products | Char, oil, gas | CO₂, H₂O, ash | | Temperature | 300–900 °C | 800–1500 °C | --- ### Question 2: List the three main products of biomass pyrolysis and one end-use of each. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Model Answer:** | Product | End-use Application | |---|---| | **Char / Biochar** | Soil amendment, carbon sequestration, activated carbon precursor | | **Bio-oil** | Boiler fuel, diesel substitute after upgrading, chemical feedstock | | **Non-condensable gases (CO, H₂, CH₄)** | Combustion for process heat, internal energy recovery, syngas | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks): Pyrolysis Process Analysis `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified]` #### (a) Classify pyrolysis into different types based on operating conditions. Discuss the effect of heating rate, temperature, and residence time on product yield. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** Pyrolysis is classified into **five principal types** based on the operating regime of heating rate, final temperature, and vapour residence time. The complete classification is given below. **1. Slow Pyrolysis (Carbonization)** - Heating rate: **0.1 – 1 °C/s** - Temperature: **300 – 500 °C** - Residence time: **several minutes to hours** - Product yield: char **30–35 %**, bio-oil **20–30 %**, gas **20–30 %** - Application: biochar production, traditional charcoal making **2. Fast Pyrolysis** - Heating rate: **10 – 200 °C/s** - Temperature: **500 – 650 °C** - Vapour residence time: **< 2 s** - Product yield: bio-oil **up to 75 %**, char **12–15 %**, gas **10–15 %** - Reactor: bubbling or circulating fluidized bed **3. Flash Pyrolysis** - Heating rate: **> 1000 °C/s** - Temperature: **800 – 1000 °C** - Vapour residence time: **< 0.5 s** - Product yield: bio-oil **up to 80 %**, high gas fraction **4. Vacuum Pyrolysis** - Pressure: **5 – 30 kPa** - Temperature: **300 – 450 °C** - Product: high-quality bio-oil with low oxygen content **5. Intermediate Pyrolysis** - Heating rate: **1 – 10 °C/s** - Temperature: **500 – 650 °C** - Residence time: **0.5 – 1 s** - Reactor: auger or Haloclean type **Effect of Process Parameters:** | Parameter | Effect on Char | Effect on Bio-oil | Effect on Gas | |---|---|---|---| | ↑ Temperature | Decreases | Peaks at 500–600 °C | Increases sharply | | ↑ Heating Rate | Decreases | Increases | Marginal increase | | ↑ Residence Time | Marginal | Decreases (cracking) | Increases | | ↓ Particle Size | Decreases | Increases | Marginal | --- #### (b) With a neat block diagram, describe the various steps involved in the pyrolysis of biomass. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** The pyrolysis process flow consists of **six sequential stages** as illustrated in the block diagram below. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Feedstock Preparation
Sorting, Shredding] --> B[Drying
100 to 150 degree C] B --> C[Pyrolysis Zone
300 to 900 degree C, No O2] C --> D[Char Separation
Cyclone] C --> E[Quenching and Condensation
25 to 50 degree C] C --> F[Gas Cleaning
Scrubbing and Filtration] D --> P1[Char Storage and Upgrading] E --> P2[Bio-oil Storage] F --> P3[Syngas Energy Recovery] ``` **Stage-by-stage description:** **1. Feedstock Preparation:** Municipal solid waste or biomass is sorted to remove recyclables and inert materials, then shredded to **< 50 mm particle size** for uniform heating. **2. Drying:** Moisture is reduced from **10–60 %** to **< 10 %** using waste heat from the syngas. This stage consumes about **10–15 %** of total energy. **3. Pyrolysis Zone:** The dried feedstock is heated to **300–900 °C** in an oxygen-free reactor. Devolatilization and primary cracking occur here. The endothermic heat demand is met by external burners or partial combustion of syngas. **4. Char Separation:** Solid char is separated from the vapour stream using cyclones operating at **> 99 %** collection efficiency. The char is rapidly cooled to prevent further decomposition. **5. Quenching and Condensation:** Vapours are rapidly cooled in condensers to **25–50 °C**, converting condensable fractions into bio-oil. Rapid quenching is critical — slow cooling causes secondary cracking and loss of oil yield. **6. Gas Cleaning and Energy Recovery:** Non-condensable gases (CO, H₂, CH₄, CO₂, C₂H₄) are scrubbed of tars and particulates, then either recycled to provide reactor heat or exported for power generation. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** > Students frequently lose **2–3 marks** by: > - Omitting the **quenching step** and treating it as part of the reactor. > - Confusing **vapour residence time** with **solid residence time**. > - Forgetting to state the **oxygen-free condition** when describing the reactor. --- ### Question B (14 Marks): Pyrolysis Kinetics and Applications `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]` #### (a) Derive the first-order kinetic model for biomass pyrolysis and explain how the activation energy is determined experimentally. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Define the conversion fraction:** $$\alpha = \frac{m_0 - m(t)}{m_0 - m_{\infty}}$$ **Step 2 — First-order rate expression:** $$\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = k(1 - \alpha)$$ **Step 3 — Apply Arrhenius temperature dependence:** $$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$ **Step 4 — Combine into a single differential equation:** $$\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT} (1 - \alpha)$$ **Step 5 — Convert to temperature domain using** $\beta = dT/dt$: $$\frac{d\alpha}{dT} = \frac{A}{\beta} e^{-E_a / RT} (1 - \alpha)$$ **Step 6 — Separate variables and integrate:** $$\int_{0}^{\alpha} \frac{d\alpha}{1 - \alpha} = \frac{A}{\beta} \int_{T_0}^{T} e^{-E_a / RT} dT$$ $$-\ln(1 - \alpha) = \frac{A R T^2}{\beta E_a} e^{-E_a / RT}$$ **Step 7 — Linearize for experimental determination:** $$\ln\left[\frac{-\ln(1 - \alpha)}{T^2}\right] = \ln\left(\frac{AR}{\beta E_a}\right) - \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}$$ **Experimental Determination:** A TGA (thermogravimetric analyzer) experiment is conducted at multiple constant heating rates (e.g., 5, 10, 20, 50 K/min). The mass-loss data is converted to $\alpha(T)$ values. A plot of $\ln[-\ln(1-\alpha)/T^2]$ versus $1/T$ yields a **straight line**. The slope of the line is $-E_a/R$, from which the activation energy is calculated: $$E_a = -(\text{slope}) \times R$$ The pre-exponential factor $A$ is obtained from the intercept. > Standard values for biomass: $E_a = 80 - 110$ kJ/mol. --- #### (b) Discuss the major industrial applications of pyrolysis products with suitable examples. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** Pyrolysis is one of the most versatile waste-to-energy technologies because **all three product streams have commercial value.** The major applications are summarized below. **1. Biochar Applications:** - **Soil amendment** — improves water retention, cation exchange capacity, and microbial activity. Application rate: **5–20 tonnes/hectare**. - **Carbon sequestration** — biochar is stable for **100–1000 years**, making it a recognized climate-change mitigation strategy. - **Activated carbon precursor** — biochar activated with steam or KOH produces **> 1000 m²/g BET surface area** for water and air purification. **2. Bio-oil Applications:** - **Direct combustion** in boilers and industrial furnaces. - **Upgraded transport fuel** — through hydrodeoxygenation (HDO), catalytic cracking, and esterification, bio-oil can be refined into **green gasoline and diesel**. - **Chemical feedstock** — contains phenols, furans, levoglucosan, acetic acid for pharmaceutical and resin industries. - **Binders** in pelletization of biomass for energy densification. **3. Syngas Applications:** - **Internal energy recovery** — burned to supply heat to the pyrolysis reactor, making the process **self-sustaining**. - **Combined Heat and Power (CHP)** — gas engines or gas turbines for electricity generation. - **Syngas platform** — CO + H₂ mixture for Fischer-Tropsch synthesis of liquid fuels. **4. Special Waste Applications:** - **Tyre pyrolysis** — recovers **carbon black, steel wire, pyrolysis oil, and gas** from end-of-life tyres. Global capacity > **4 million tonnes/year**. - **Plastic pyrolysis** — converts mixed polyolefins (PE, PP, PS) into **pyrolysis fuel oil (PFO)** with diesel-grade calorific value. - **E-waste pyrolysis** — recovery of precious metals and safe destruction of brominated flame retardants. - **Sewage sludge pyrolysis** — produces **P-rich biochar** for fertilizer use and eliminates pathogens. > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall:** > Students often write "pyrolysis is used for energy generation" without specifying *which* product is the energy carrier. Always distinguish: char → solid fuel / soil use; bio-oil → liquid fuel / chemical; gas → internal heat / power. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Pyrolysis** ### Core Definitions - ☐ Pyrolysis = **thermal decomposition in absence of oxygen** (ER ≤ 0.2). - ☐ Distinguish pyrolysis, combustion, and gasification by **oxygen availability and product slate**. - ☐ Pyrolysis is **endothermic** below 600 °C; mildly exothermic above 700 °C. ### Process Stages - ☐ Three stages: **Drying (≤ 150 °C) → Devolatilization (150–500 °C) → Secondary Reactions (500–900 °C)**. - ☐ Hemicellulose degrades first (220–315 °C), cellulose next (315–400 °C), lignin over a wide range. - ☐ Rapid **vapour quenching** is critical to preserve bio-oil yield. ### Pyrolysis Types (Master Table) - ☐ **Slow:** char maximum (30–35 %), 0.1–1 °C/s, 300–500 °C. - ☐ **Fast:** bio-oil maximum (up to 75 %), 10–200 °C/s, 500–650 °C. - ☐ **Flash:** > 1000 °C/s, 800–1000 °C, residence < 0.5 s. - ☐ **Vacuum:** 5–30 kPa, 300–450 °C, high-quality bio-oil. - ☐ **Intermediate:** auger reactor, balanced products. ### Key Equations to Memorize - ☐ First-order kinetic model: $d\alpha/dt = A e^{-E_a/RT} (1 - \alpha)$. - ☐ Linearized form: $\ln[-\ln(1-\alpha)/T^2] = \ln(AR/\beta E_a) - E_a/RT$. - ☐ Energy balance: $Q = mc_p\Delta T + m_w h_{fg} + q_{\text{pyrolysis}}$. - ☐ Mass balance: feed = char + oil + gas (100 % closure required). ### Reactor Types - ☐ **Fixed bed / kiln** → slow pyrolysis. - ☐ **Bubbling fluidized bed (BFB)** → fast pyrolysis, scalable. - ☐ **Circulating fluidized bed (CFB)** → high-throughput fast pyrolysis. - ☐ **Rotating cone** → flash pyrolysis, compact design. - ☐ **Auger / screw** → intermediate pyrolysis, dry feedstock. - ☐ **Vacuum reactor** → high-quality bio-oil. ### Industrial Applications - ☐ **Biochar** → soil amendment, carbon sequestration, activated carbon. - ☐ **Bio-oil** → boiler fuel, upgraded transport fuel, chemical feedstock. - ☐ **Syngas** → internal heating, CHP, Fischer-Tropsch synthesis. - ☐ **Tyre pyrolysis** → carbon black + oil + steel recovery. - ☐ **Plastic pyrolysis** → pyrolysis fuel oil (PFO). ### Standard Numerical Values - ☐ Energy demand: **~ 1.5 – 2.0 MJ/kg** of dry biomass. - ☐ Bio-oil HHV: **16 – 22 MJ/kg** (vs. diesel 45 MJ/kg). - ☐ Char HHV: **20 – 32 MJ/kg**. - ☐ Syngas HHV: **4 – 18 MJ/Nm³** depending on composition. - ☐ Bio-oil yield: **up to 75 wt %** (fast, lignocellulosic). - ☐ Activation energy biomass: **80 – 110 kJ/mol**. > [!TIP] > **Final KTU Strategy Tip:** Always draw a **Mermaid block diagram** in 14-mark answers — examiners explicitly reward "neat labelled diagrams" with **1–2 extra marks** beyond the content score. ---

topics.title: Gasification: Principles, Types, and Benefits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT836 - WASTE TO ENERGY TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gasification: Principles, Types, and Benefits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Gasification** is a thermochemical conversion process that transforms carbonaceous feedstocks (biomass, municipal solid waste, agricultural residues, coal, or industrial waste) into a combustible gaseous fuel known as **Producer Gas** or **Synthesis Gas (Syngas)**. The process occurs at elevated temperatures (typically **800 °C to 1200 °C**) under a **sub-stoichiometric supply of oxygen, air, steam, or a mixture of these** as the gasifying medium. The end-products are primarily **Carbon Monoxide (CO), Hydrogen (H₂), Carbon Dioxide (CO₂), Methane (CH₄), and Nitrogen (N₂)** along with minor quantities of tar, char, and ash.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** Gasification is defined as the *incomplete combustion* of solid fuels where the oxygen supplied is **less than the stoichiometric requirement** for complete combustion. The chemical energy of the original solid fuel is converted into the chemical energy of the resulting combustible gas.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Controlled Charcoal Furnace"

Imagine you have a backyard charcoal pit used for **traditional ironing (in Kerala villages, the "kombukuthi")**. When you smother the burning charcoal with a thin layer of ash and limit air intake through a small hole, the charcoal does **not burn with flames** (no complete combustion). Instead, it slowly releases a blue-ish, flammable gas that can be lit at the outlet — this is **producer gas**.

**Mapping to Engineering:**
- The **charcoal** = Solid biomass/MSW feedstock
- The **restricted air** = Sub-stoichiometric oxygen
- The **blue flame gas** = Producer gas (CO + H₂)
- The **ash layer** = Reactor's bed material / temperature moderator

Just as the ironing charcoal gives you a cleaner, controllable gaseous fuel instead of direct fire, an industrial gasifier extracts much more usable energy from waste than simple incineration ever could.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Combustion:**
> - **Combustion (Incineration):** Excess air (λ > 1) → produces heat + flue gases (CO₂, H₂O, N₂)
> - **Gasification:** Deficient air (λ ≈ 0.2 to 0.4) → produces combustible gases (CO, H₂, CH₄)

---

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Process Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Equivalence Ratio (ER) | **0.2 – 0.4** | dimensionless |
| Operating Temperature | **800 – 1200** | °C |
| Heating Value of Producer Gas | **4 – 18** | MJ/Nm³ |
| Cold Gas Efficiency (CGE) | **60 – 80** | % |
| Carbon Conversion Efficiency | **70 – 95** | % |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Equivalence Ratio (ER)** is the **single most important control parameter** in gasification. ER = (Actual Air/Fuel ratio) / (Stoichiometric Air/Fuel ratio). For gasification, ER < 1.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Physical Principle

Gasification leverages the **thermodynamic instability of carbon** at high temperatures when oxygen is restricted. When only **20–40 %** of the stoichiometric oxygen is supplied, the carbon atoms preferentially combine with hydrogen to form **CO and H₂** rather than fully oxidizing to **CO₂ and H₂O**. This is because the equilibrium of the **Boudouard reaction** shifts towards CO formation at temperatures above **700 °C**.

### 2.2 The Four Sequential Reaction Zones (Logical Steps)

The gasification reactor conceptually (and physically, in fixed-bed systems) divides into **four distinct thermal zones**, each governed by a dominant chemical reaction:

**Step 1 — Drying Zone (100 °C – 200 °C)**
- The wet feedstock loses its **moisture** as water vapor driven off by the rising hot gases.
- This is an **endothermic** physical process.
- Why: Wet fuel reduces the calorific value of the producer gas and wastes thermal energy as latent heat.

**Step 2 — Pyrolysis / Devolatilization Zone (200 °C – 600 °C)**
- The dry biomass decomposes into **char (solid carbon)**, **tars**, **light hydrocarbons (CH₄, C₂H₆)**, **CO, CO₂, and H₂**.
- This is the **primary source of volatile matter** that becomes part of the syngas.
- Why: Pyrolysis is endothermic and acts as the chemical "pre-cracker" for the fuel.

**Step 3 — Combustion / Oxidation Zone (700 °C – 1200 °C)**
- The char partially burns with the limited oxygen supplied:
- Reactions are **highly exothermic**, providing the heat for the rest of the reactor.
- This zone's temperature is the **highest** in the system.

**Step 4 — Reduction Zone (800 °C – 1000 °C)**
- The hot CO₂ and H₂O from the combustion zone react with the remaining hot char:
- These are the **most critical reactions** because they generate the combustible CO and H₂ that define the gasifier's product.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Reaction Name | Chemical Equation | Enthalpy (ΔH @ 25 °C) | Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Partial Oxidation of Carbon | $\text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}$ | **−111 MJ/kmol** | Exothermic |
| 2 | Complete Oxidation of Carbon | $\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$ | **−394 MJ/kmol** | Exothermic |
| 3 | Boudouard Reaction | $\text{C} + \text{CO}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{CO}$ | **+172 MJ/kmol** | Endothermic |
| 4 | Water–Gas Reaction | $\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + \text{H}_2$ | **+131 MJ/kmol** | Endothermic |
| 5 | Water–Gas Shift (Methanation) | $\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2$ | **−41 MJ/kmol** | Exothermic |
| 6 | Methane Formation | $\text{C} + 2\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_4$ | **−75 MJ/kmol** | Exothermic |

| # | Process Efficiency Formula | Mathematical Form | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cold Gas Efficiency (CGE) | $\text{CGE} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{gas}} \times \text{LHV}_{\text{gas}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \times \text{LHV}_{\text{fuel}}} \times 100$ | % |
| 2 | Carbon Conversion Efficiency (CCE) | $\text{CCE} = \dfrac{\text{C}_{\text{in gas}}}{\text{C}_{\text{in fuel}}} \times 100$ | % |
| 3 | Equivalence Ratio (ER) | $\text{ER} = \dfrac{(\text{Air}/\text{Fuel})_{\text{actual}}}{(\text{Air}/\text{Fuel})_{\text{stoichiometric}}}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Gas Yield | $\eta_{\text{yield}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{gas}}}{m_{\text{fuel}}}$ | Nm³/kg |
| 5 | Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{\text{Energy in product gas}}{\text{Energy in feedstock}} \times 100$ | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** These formulas are used by process engineers at plants like **NTPC Vidyut Vyapar Nigam (India)** and the **IIT Delhi Biomass Gasifier** to optimize syngas output. Cold Gas Efficiency above **70 %** is considered commercially viable.

### 2.4 The Equivalence Ratio — The "Master Knob"

The single most influential operating parameter is **ER**.

- If **ER < 0.2**: Insufficient heat is generated, temperature drops, char conversion falls, **drying/pyrolysis dominate**.
- If **0.2 < ER < 0.4**: **Optimal gasification window.** Combustion zone provides just enough heat for endothermic reduction reactions.
- If **ER > 0.5**: System drifts toward **combustion**, CO and H₂ are oxidized further to CO₂ and H₂O, and heating value of gas drops sharply.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air Requirement for Carbon

To understand the gasification process, we must first derive the stoichiometric oxygen demand for the simplest feedstock — pure carbon.

**Step 1:** Write the balanced complete combustion equation.

$$\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$$

**Step 2:** Determine the molar mass relationships.

$$1 \text{ kmol C (12 kg)} \equiv 1 \text{ kmol O}_2 (32 \text{ kg})$$

**Step 3:** Convert oxygen mass to air mass using air composition (≈ 23.2 % O₂ by mass).

$$\text{Air mass} = \dfrac{\text{O}_2 \text{ mass}}{0.232} = \dfrac{32}{0.232} = 137.93 \text{ kg air / 32 kg O}_2$$

**Step 4:** Calculate per kg of carbon.

$$\text{Stoichiometric Air} = \dfrac{137.93}{12} \approx 11.49 \text{ kg air / kg C}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This means that burning 1 kg of pure carbon completely requires 11.49 kg of air. In gasification, we deliberately supply only ~2.3 to 4.6 kg air per kg carbon (i.e., ER = 0.2 to 0.4).

### 3.2 Derivation: Producer Gas Composition Using Material Balance

Consider a gasifier fed with **1 kg of carbon (C)** and **air at ER = 0.3**.

**Step 1:** Calculate actual oxygen supplied.

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ supplied} = 0.3 \times \dfrac{32}{12} = 0.8 \text{ kg O}_2 / \text{kg C}$$

**Step 2:** Convert to kmol basis.

$$n_{\text{O}_2} = \dfrac{0.8}{32} = 0.025 \text{ kmol O}_2$$

**Step 3:** Assume the dominant reactions proceed as:
- $25 \%$ of C undergoes partial oxidation: $\text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}$
- $75 \%$ of C undergoes Boudouard + Water–Gas: $\text{C} + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}$, $\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2$

**Step 4:** Solve the carbon balance for a simplified equilibrium.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total C in} &= 1 \text{ kg} \equiv \dfrac{1}{12} = 0.0833 \text{ kmol} \\
\text{C consumed in partial oxidation} &= 2 \times n_{\text{O}_2} = 2 \times 0.025 = 0.05 \text{ kmol} \\
\text{C remaining for Boudouard + Water-Gas} &= 0.0833 - 0.05 = 0.0333 \text{ kmol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Assume 50 % of remaining C reacts via Water–Gas, 50 % via Boudouard.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CO from Boudouard} &= 2 \times 0.0167 = 0.0334 \text{ kmol} \\
\text{H}_2 \text{ from Water-Gas} &= 0.0167 \text{ kmol} \\
\text{CO from Water-Gas} &= 0.0167 \text{ kmol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6:** Total producer gas composition (excluding N₂ dilution):

$$\text{CO}_{\text{total}} = 0.0334 + 0.0167 = 0.0501 \text{ kmol} \approx 60.1 \text{ mol \%}$$

$$\text{H}_2 = 0.0167 \text{ kmol} \approx 20.0 \text{ mol \%}$$

$$\text{N}_2 \text{ from air} = 0.025 \times \dfrac{79}{21} = 0.0940 \text{ kmol}$$

**Step 7:** Total dry gas produced.

$$n_{\text{gas}} = 0.0501 + 0.0167 + 0.0940 = 0.1608 \text{ kmol}$$

**Step 8:** Normalized composition of producer gas:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CO} &= \dfrac{0.0501}{0.1608} \times 100 \approx 31.2 \, \% \\
\text{H}_2 &= \dfrac{0.0167}{0.1608} \times 100 \approx 10.4 \, \% \\
\text{N}_2 &= \dfrac{0.0940}{0.1608} \times 100 \approx 58.4 \, \%
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3 Heating Value Calculation

**Step 1:** LHV of pure CO = **12.6 MJ/Nm³**; LHV of H₂ = **10.8 MJ/Nm³**.

**Step 2:** Convert mol fractions to volume fractions (at NTP, mol = vol).

$$V_{\text{CO}} = 0.312 \text{ Nm}^3, \quad V_{\text{H}_2} = 0.104 \text{ Nm}^3 \text{ per Nm}^3 \text{ gas}$$

**Step 3:** Energy content per Nm³ of producer gas.

$$Q = (0.312 \times 12.6) + (0.104 \times 10.8) = 3.93 + 1.12 = 5.05 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3$$

> **Conversion Logic:** A typical air-blown biomass producer gas delivers **4–6 MJ/Nm³**, which is about **15–20 %** of natural gas (≈ 35 MJ/Nm³). Hence producer gas is a **low-BTU gas**, unsuitable for direct grid distribution but excellent for combined heat & power (CHP) engines.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of ER on Gas Quality

| Equivalence Ratio | Temperature (°C) | Gas LHV (MJ/Nm³) | CGE (%) | Char (%) | Tar (g/Nm³) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.20 | 750 | **6.2** | 68 | High | High |
| 0.25 | 850 | **5.5** | 72 | Moderate | Moderate |
| 0.30 | 950 | **5.0** | 75 | Low | Low |
| 0.35 | 1050 | **4.4** | 73 | Very Low | Very Low |
| 0.45 | 1200 | **3.0** | 60 | Negligible | Negligible |

> **Insight for KTU:** Note that **LHV peaks at ER ≈ 0.20** but **CGE peaks at ER ≈ 0.30**. This is because higher ER burns more fuel (raising CGE) but also dilutes syngas with N₂ (lowering LHV).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Gasification Process Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **block-level functional architecture** of a generic downdraft gasifier, which is the most common type for agricultural waste in India and the focus of KTU Module 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Feedstock Hopper"] --> A2["Screw Feeder"]
    A2 --> A3["Drying Zone 100-200 C"]
    A3 --> A4["Pyrolysis Zone 200-600 C"]
    A4 --> A5["Combustion Zone 700-1200 C"]
    A5 --> A6["Reduction Zone 800-1000 C"]
    A6 --> A7["Ash Removal Grate"]
    A7 --> A8["Ash Disposal"]
    B1["Air Blower"] --> B2["Air Nozzle Ring"]
    B2 --> A5
    A6 --> C1["Cyclone Separator"]
    C1 --> C2["Particulate Filter"]
    C2 --> C3["Scrubber Cooler"]
    C3 --> C4["Tar Condenser"]
    C4 --> C5["Producer Gas Outlet"]
    C5 --> D1["Internal Combustion Engine"]
    D1 --> D2["Electrical Generator"]
    D2 --> D3["Electricity Grid"]
    A5 --> E1["Waste Heat Boiler"]
    E1 --> E2["Hot Water Steam"]
    E2 --> E3["District Heating"]
```

### 4.2 Classification Architecture of Gasifier Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G1["Gasifier Types"]
    G1 --> G2["Fixed Bed Gasifier"]
    G1 --> G3["Fluidized Bed Gasifier"]
    G1 --> G4["Entrained Flow Gasifier"]
    G2 --> G2A["Updraft Counter-Current"]
    G2 --> G2B["Downdraft Co-Current"]
    G2 --> G2C["Cross Draft"]
    G3 --> G3A["Bubbling Fluidized Bed BFB"]
    G3 --> G3B["Circulating Fluidized Bed CFB"]
    G4 --> G4A["Slagging Entrained Flow"]
    G4 --> G4B["Non-Slagging Entrained Flow"]
```

### 4.3 Decision Matrix — Selecting the Right Gasifier

| Feedstock Property | Recommended Gasifier | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Dry biomass (sawdust, rice husk) | **Downdraft** | Tar cracks in hot combustion zone; clean gas |
| High moisture biomass (MSW) | **Updraft** | Top-down hot gas pre-dries wet fuel |
| Fine, uniform particles | **Fluidized Bed (BFB)** | Excellent heat & mass transfer |
| High-ash Indian coal | **Circulating Fluidized Bed** | Handles ash without slagging |
| Pure cellulose / powders | **Entrained Flow** | Very high throughput, slagging operation |

### 4.4 Working Principle Block — Downdraft Gasifier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1["Biomass Inlet Top"] --> F2["Drying Section"]
    F2 --> F3["Pyrolysis Section"]
    F3 --> F4["Combustion Throat"]
    F4 --> F5["Reduction Section"]
    F5 --> F6["Char Bed"]
    F6 --> F7["Grate"]
    F7 --> F8["Ash Outlet Bottom"]
    A1["Air Inlet Sides"] --> F4
    F5 --> G1["Producer Gas Outlet Sides"]
    F3 -.->|"Tars and Vapors descend"| F4
    F4 -.->|"High temp cracks tars"| F5
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **downdraft design** is universally recommended for biomass because the hot combustion zone sits directly below the pyrolysis zone, forcing all tars to pass through the hottest region and crack into lighter gases — yielding a relatively tar-free producer gas suitable for IC engines.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Define gasification. How does it differ from incineration?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Gasification** is a thermochemical process in which carbonaceous solid fuels (biomass, MSW, coal) are converted into a combustible gaseous product (producer gas / syngas) at high temperatures (800–1200 °C) by controlled partial oxidation using **sub-stoichiometric air, oxygen, steam, or a mixture** as the gasifying agent.
>
> **Difference from Incineration (Combustion):**
>
> | Aspect | Gasification | Incineration |
> |---|---|---|
> | Air Supply | Sub-stoichiometric (ER ≈ 0.2–0.4) | Excess air (ER > 1) |
> | Main Product | Combustible gas (CO + H₂) | Heat + flue gases (CO₂ + H₂O) |
> | Temperature | 800–1200 °C | 1000–1400 °C |
> | Energy Recovery | Chemical (gas) | Thermal (heat) |

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **State and explain the water–gas reaction with its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **water–gas reaction** (also called the steam–carbon reaction) is:
>
> $$\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = +131 \text{ MJ/kmol}$$
>
> It is **strongly endothermic** and requires temperatures above **800 °C** to proceed at an appreciable rate. Steam is injected into the gasifier to enhance **H₂ production** and raise the heating value of the producer gas. The H₂ thus produced can be separated and used as a clean fuel or chemical feedstock. **[3 marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Describe the **principles and chemistry of gasification** with the help of the **four sequential reaction zones**. Enumerate the **six primary chemical reactions** that take place inside the gasifier. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Classify **gasifiers** into major types. Compare **downdraft, updraft, and fluidized bed gasifiers** with respect to tar content, suitability for IC engines, moisture tolerance, and feedstock size. **[7 marks]**

---

##### Model Solution for Q.A (a):

The gasification process occurs in **four sequential zones** inside the reactor:

**Zone 1 — Drying (100–200 °C):**
The incoming wet feedstock encounters the hot gases rising from below. Moisture is evaporated as steam. This is purely a **physical process** and consumes sensible heat from the producer gas. Wet biomass (> 30 % moisture) drastically reduces gasifier efficiency.

**Zone 2 — Pyrolysis / Devolatilization (200–600 °C):**
The dry fuel decomposes thermally. Volatile matter escapes as **tars, light hydrocarbons (CH₄, C₂H₄, C₂H₆), CO, CO₂, and H₂**. The remaining solid residue is **char (fixed carbon + ash)**. Pyrolysis is **endothermic** and is responsible for 70–90 % of the producer gas mass.

**Zone 3 — Combustion / Oxidation (700–1200 °C):**
Limited oxygen supplied through the air nozzle reacts exothermically with the char:

$$\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad (-394 \text{ MJ/kmol})$$

$$\text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO} \quad (-111 \text{ MJ/kmol})$$

This zone is the **thermal heart** of the gasifier; its heat sustains the entire process.

**Zone 4 — Reduction (800–1000 °C):**
The hot combustion gases (CO₂ and H₂O) react with the descending char:

$$\text{C} + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO} \quad (+172 \text{ MJ/kmol}) \quad \text{[Boudouard]}$$

$$\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2 \quad (+131 \text{ MJ/kmol}) \quad \text{[Water-Gas]}$$

$$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad (-41 \text{ MJ/kmol}) \quad \text{[Water-Gas Shift]}$$

$$\text{C} + 2\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 \quad (-75 \text{ MJ/kmol}) \quad \text{[Methanation]}$$

The CO and H₂ generated here are the **primary combustible constituents** of the producer gas. **[7 marks — full breakdown given]**

---

##### Model Solution for Q.A (b):

**Major types of gasifiers:**

1. **Fixed Bed** (Updraft, Downdraft, Cross Draft)
2. **Fluidized Bed** (Bubbling — BFB, Circulating — CFB)
3. **Entrained Flow** (Slagging, Non-slagging)

**Comparative Table:**

| Parameter | Updraft | Downdraft | Fluidized Bed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gas–Solid Flow | Counter-current | Co-current | Mixed/Fluidized |
| Tar Content in Gas | **Very High** (10–100 g/Nm³) | **Low** (< 1 g/Nm³) | Low–Moderate |
| Suitability for IC Engine | Not suitable | **Excellent** | Good |
| Moisture Tolerance | **High** (up to 50 %) | Low (must dry to < 20 %) | Moderate |
| Feedstock Size | Coarse chunks OK | Uniform small chips | Fine particles |
| Scale | Small to Medium | Small to Medium | Medium to Large |
| Turndown Ratio | Poor | Moderate | **Excellent** |

**Conclusion:** For **Indian rural electrification using agricultural waste**, the **downdraft gasifier** is preferred because its tar level is low enough for direct use in diesel dual-fuel engines. For **wet MSW**, the **updraft** is preferred. For **power plants > 1 MW**, the **fluidized bed** is the industry standard. **[7 marks]**

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2 Alternate]**

**(a)** Explain the **principle of producer gas generation**. Discuss the effect of **equivalence ratio (ER)** on the producer gas composition and its calorific value. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A downdraft gasifier is fed with **100 kg/hr of biomass** (proximate analysis: 8 % moisture, 42 % fixed carbon, 38 % volatile matter, 12 % ash).
