The producer gas flow rate is **180 Nm³/hr** with LHV of **5.2 MJ/Nm³**. The biomass LHV is **17 MJ/kg**. Calculate: (i) **Gas Yield**, (ii) **Cold Gas Efficiency**, and (iii) **Carbon Conversion Efficiency** assuming 85 % of carbon is gasified. **[7 marks]**

---

##### Model Solution for Q.B (a):

**Principle of Producer Gas Generation:**

Producer gas is generated by **partial combustion and reduction** of solid carbonaceous fuel in a controlled oxygen-deficient atmosphere. The oxygen supplied is **insufficient for complete combustion**; hence the carbon is partially oxidized to CO and the resulting CO₂ and H₂O are reduced back to CO and H₂ over hot char. The gas mixture leaving the gasifier, after cooling and cleaning, is called **producer gas**, primarily containing **CO, H₂, N₂**, with minor CH₄, CO₂, and H₂O.

**Effect of Equivalence Ratio (ER):**

$$\text{ER} = \dfrac{(\text{Air}/\text{Fuel})_{\text{actual}}}{(\text{Air}/\text{Fuel})_{\text{stoichiometric}}}$$

- **Low ER (≈ 0.20):** Less O₂ → combustion zone cooler → reduction reactions dominate → **CO and H₂ concentrations are high**, but char conversion is incomplete.
- **Moderate ER (≈ 0.30):** Optimal balance — sufficient heat, good reduction → **peak CGE**, moderate tar content.
- **High ER (> 0.45):** Excess O₂ → CO and H₂ get re-oxidized to CO₂ and H₂O → **calorific value drops** sharply; tar and char decrease because of more complete combustion.

**Calorific Value vs ER:**
- LHV typically drops from **6.5 MJ/Nm³ at ER = 0.20** to **3.5 MJ/Nm³ at ER = 0.45**.
- However, **Gas Yield** (Nm³/kg fuel) increases up to ER ≈ 0.40.

Thus, ER must be tuned to a **sweet spot (~0.25–0.30)** for engine applications. **[7 marks]**

---

##### Model Solution for Q.B (b):

**Given Data:**
- Feedstock mass flow: $\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} = 100$ kg/hr
- Feedstock LHV: $\text{LHV}_{\text{fuel}} = 17$ MJ/kg
- Producer gas flow: $\dot{V}_{\text{gas}} = 180$ Nm³/hr
- Producer gas LHV: $\text{LHV}_{\text{gas}} = 5.2$ MJ/Nm³
- Carbon in fuel: 42 %
- Carbon gasified: 85 %

**Step 1 — Carbon mass in fuel:**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{C}} = 100 \times 0.42 = 42 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step 2 — Carbon in producer gas (assuming all gasified C becomes CO):**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{C, gas}} = 42 \times 0.85 = 35.7 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step 3 — Moles of carbon in gas:**

$$n_{\text{C}} = \dfrac{35.7}{12} = 2.975 \text{ kmol/hr}$$

**Step 4 — Volume of CO at NTP (22.4 Nm³/kmol):**

$$V_{\text{CO}} = 2.975 \times 22.4 = 66.64 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{hr}$$

---

**(i) Gas Yield:**

$$\eta_{\text{yield}} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{\text{gas}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}}} = \dfrac{180}{100} = 1.80 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{kg}$$

---

**(ii) Cold Gas Efficiency:**

$$\text{CGE} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{\text{gas}} \times \text{LHV}_{\text{gas}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \times \text{LHV}_{\text{fuel}}} \times 100$$

$$\text{CGE} = \dfrac{180 \times 5.2}{100 \times 17} \times 100 = \dfrac{936}{1700} \times 100 \approx 55.06 \text{ \%}$$

---

**(iii) Carbon Conversion Efficiency:**

$$\text{CCE} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{C, gas}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{C, fuel}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{35.7}{42} \times 100 = 85.00 \text{ \%}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The CCE directly equals the carbon gasified fraction in this simplified case (since all gasified carbon is assumed to enter the producer gas). A CCE of 85 % is **commercially acceptable** for a downdraft gasifier. **[7 marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Don't confuse ER with excess air ratio (λ).** ER = 1/λ. Students frequently interchange these and lose 2 marks.
> - **Don't write "combustion" instead of "partial oxidation"** when describing the oxidation zone. Combustion implies complete burning; gasification requires *incomplete* combustion.
> - In numerical questions, **always state the formula first, then substitute**, otherwise the valuation key deducts marks for the methodology step.
> - When asked to compare gasifiers, **tabular comparison always scores better** than narrative paragraphs (examiners allocate 2 marks for "comparison table" specifically).
> - **Units must be carried through every calculation step.** Writing 5.2 instead of 5.2 MJ/Nm³ in the substitution step loses 1 mark per occurrence.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Gasification is **incomplete combustion** at ER ≈ 0.2–0.4, producing **CO + H₂ rich producer gas**.
- ✅ Four zones in order: **Drying → Pyrolysis → Combustion → Reduction**.
- ✅ Six key reactions: partial & complete oxidation, **Boudouard**, **Water–Gas**, **Water–Gas Shift**, **Methanation**.
- ✅ **Boudouard reaction** is endothermic and requires T > 700 °C; it is the dominant source of CO.
- ✅ **Equivalence Ratio (ER)** is the master control knob — affects temperature, LHV, tar, and char.
- ✅ **Downdraft gasifier** = best for biomass + IC engine (low tar); **Updraft** = best for wet fuels; **Fluidized bed** = best for large-scale power.
- ✅ Cold Gas Efficiency (CGE) = $\dfrac{\dot{V}_{\text{gas}} \times \text{LHV}_{\text{gas}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \times \text{LHV}_{\text{fuel}}} \times 100$ — target **> 70 %** for commercial operation.
- ✅ Typical producer gas LHV with air: **4–6 MJ/Nm³**; with oxygen/steam: **10–18 MJ/Nm³**.
- ✅ Nitrogen from air acts as a **diluent** in air-blown gasifiers, lowering calorific value.
- ✅ **Carbon Conversion Efficiency (CCE)** quantifies how much feed-carbon becomes gas-carbon; > 85 % is acceptable.
- ✅ Gasification benefits include: higher electrical efficiency (30–40 % vs 15–20 % for direct combustion), lower emissions (NOx, SOx), syngas for **Fischer-Tropsch** liquid fuels.
- ✅ For KTU numericals: **always carry units, show the formula, then substitute, then state final answer with units**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparative Analysis of Pyrolysis and Gasification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT836 - WASTE TO ENERGY TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Comparative Analysis of Pyrolysis and Gasification # 📘 MODULE 2 — COMPARATIVE ANALYSIS OF PYROLYSIS AND GASIFICATION ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Pyrolysis — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pyrolysis** is the **thermochemical decomposition of organic (carbonaceous) feedstock in the complete absence of oxygen (or in such limited oxygen that combustion cannot occur) at temperatures typically in the range of 300 °C to 900 °C.** The process breaks long-chain hydrocarbon molecules into smaller, more useful molecules through the application of heat alone. Because oxidation is suppressed, the carbon in the feed does *not* convert into CO₂ and H₂O — instead, it is partitioned into three product phases: a **solid char (biochar)**, a **condensable liquid (bio-oil / tar)**, and a **non-condensable gas (pyro-gas / light hydrocarbons)**. **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** > The student must distinguish pyrolysis from gasification on the basis of oxygen partial pressure, temperature window, residence time, and product distribution. ### 1.2 Gasification — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Gasification** is the **thermochemical conversion of a carbonaceous feedstock into a combustible gaseous fuel (producer gas / syngas) through partial oxidation at sub-stoichiometric air (or oxygen) supply, typically in the temperature range of 700 °C to 1200 °C.** Here, the equivalence ratio (ER = actual air / stoichiometric air) is deliberately kept between **0.20 and 0.40** — just enough oxygen to sustain endothermic gas-forming reactions, but far too little to allow complete combustion to CO₂ and H₂O. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stove vs. Tandoor" Picture 🍳 Imagine you are cooking in your kitchen: | Concept | Real-World Analogy | What's Happening | |---|---|---| | **Combustion** | Gas stove with full flame | Plenty of air → complete burning to CO₂ + H₂O | | **Pyrolysis** | Sealed pressure cooker on slow flame | No oxygen reaches the food → it chars, releases oil, vapours, and a smoky gas | | **Gasification** | Charcoal sigdi / traditional tandoor with restricted air inlet | Just enough air to keep the charcoal glowing red → most carbon becomes CO + H₂ instead of CO₂ + H₂O | > [!NOTE] > **Quick Memory Hook:** *Pyrolysis is "cooking without air" → produces all three phases (solid + liquid + gas). Gasification is "cooking with too little air" → produces mainly gas.* ### 1.4 Standard Metrics at a Glance | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | |---|---|---| | Equivalence Ratio (Gasification) | ER | **0.20 – 0.40** | | Pyrolysis Temperature | T_pyro | **300 – 900 °C** | | Gasification Temperature | T_gas | **700 – 1200 °C** | | Stoichiometric O₂ per kg C | — | **2.67 kg O₂ / kg C** | | Stoichiometric air per kg C | — | **11.5 kg air / kg C** | | Air contains O₂ (mass) | — | **23.2 %** | | Air contains O₂ (volume) | — | **21.0 %** | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Pyrolysis Pyrolysis proceeds through **four sequential stages** when a solid biomass particle is heated: **Step 1 — Moisture Removal (Drying) — T < 150 °C** - Free and bound water evaporates. - Endothermic; consumes about **2.26 MJ / kg of water** (latent heat of vaporization). **Step 2 — Devolatilization (Primary Pyrolysis) — 200 °C < T < 500 °C** - Hemicellulose decomposes at ~ 220 – 315 °C. - Cellulose decomposes at ~ 315 – 400 °C. - Lignin decomposes slowly from ~ 160 °C up to 900 °C. - Products released: condensable vapours (acetic acid, methanol, tar precursors), non-condensable gases (CO, CO₂, CH₄), and a carbonaceous **char**. **Step 3 — Secondary Reactions (Cracking & Reforming) — T > 500 °C** - Primary vapours undergo **thermal cracking**: $$ \text{Tar} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{Lighter hydrocarbons} + \text{Char} + \text{H}_2 $$ - Higher temperature + longer residence time → more gas, less oil. **Step 4 — Char Consolidation — T > 600 °C** - Residual solid carbon forms **biochar** with high fixed-carbon content (typically **60 – 80 %** on dry ash-free basis). ### 2.2 Operational Breakdown — Gasification Gasification has **four zones** in a typical downdraft gasifier: **Zone I — Drying Zone (T < 150 °C)** - Moisture evaporates; biomass enters at ambient temperature. **Zone II — Pyrolysis Zone (150 – 600 °C)** - The feedstock is devolatilized, releasing char + gases (just like Step 2 of pyrolysis above). **Zone III — Oxidation / Combustion Zone (800 – 1200 °C)** - Limited air reacts exothermically with char: $$ \text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ $$ \text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO} \quad \Delta H = -111.4 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ - This zone provides the heat that drives the *endothermic* reactions in Zone IV. **Zone IV — Reduction Zone (600 – 900 °C)** - The hot gases from Zone III react with the descending char: $$ \text{C} + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO} \quad \text{(Boudouard)} \quad \Delta H = +172.5 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ $$ \text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2 \quad \text{(Water–Gas)} \quad \Delta H = +131.4 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ $$ \text{C} + 2\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 \quad \text{(Methanation)} \quad \Delta H = -74.8 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ $$ \text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \text{(Water–Gas Shift)} \quad \Delta H = -41.2 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations > [!NOTE] > **The following formulas are examination-critical.** Memorize the units along with each equation. | # | Formula | Description / Units | |---|---|---| | 1 | $m_{O_2,\text{stoich}} = 2.67 \cdot m_C$ | Stoichiometric O₂ per kg of carbon (kg O₂ / kg C) | | 2 | $m_{\text{air,stoich}} = \dfrac{2.67 \cdot m_C}{0.232}$ | Stoichiometric dry air per kg of C (kg air / kg C) | | 3 | $ER = \dfrac{m_{\text{air,actual}}}{m_{\text{air,stoich}}}$ | Equivalence ratio (dimensionless). Gasification: **0.20 – 0.40** | | 4 | $\eta_{\text{cold}} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{sg} \cdot LHV_{sg}}{\dot{m}_{\text{feed}} \cdot LHV_{\text{feed}}} \times 100\%$ | Cold Gas Efficiency (%) | | 5 | $\eta_{\text{hot}} = \eta_{\text{cold}} + \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{\text{sensible}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{feed}} \cdot LHV_{\text{feed}}} \times 100\%$ | Hot Gas Efficiency (includes sensible heat) | | 6 | $HHV_{sg} = 0.126\,\%CO + 0.126\,\%H_2 + 0.358\,\%CH_4 \;\; [\text{MJ/Nm}^3]$ | Heating value of dry syngas from volumetric composition | | 7 | $\text{Air–Fuel Ratio (mass)} = \dfrac{m_{\text{air}}}{m_{\text{feed}}}$ | Mass basis (kg/kg) — typical gasification: **1.5 – 2.5** | | 8 | $\text{Carbon Conversion} = \dfrac{m_{C,\text{in}} - m_{C,\text{char}}}{m_{C,\text{in}}} \times 100\%$ | % carbon converted to gas | | 9 | $\Delta H_{\text{vap}} = 2.26 \; \text{MJ/kg}$ | Latent heat of vaporization of water | ### 2.4 Real-World Utility - **Pyrolysis** bio-oil can be upgraded to **transportation fuel**, biochar used as **soil amendment / activated carbon precursor**. - **Gasification** syngas is used in **IC engines, gas turbines, FT-diesel synthesis, methanol synthesis**, and **SOFC fuel cells**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** *Pyrolysis is preferred when a multi-product slate (char + oil + gas) is desired. Gasification is preferred when a single, clean gaseous fuel is needed for power or chemical synthesis.* # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Stoichiometric Air Requirement for Gasification of a Generic Biomass **Problem Setup:** A biomass feedstock of the ultimate analysis (dry basis, mass fractions) is given. We need to determine the minimum theoretical air required to combust *all* the carbon, hydrogen, and sulphur completely. Let the dry feed contain (per kg dry basis): - Carbon: $w_C$, Hydrogen: $w_H$, Oxygen: $w_O$, Sulphur: $w_S$, Nitrogen: $w_N$, Ash: $w_A$ - Where $w_C + w_H + w_O + w_S + w_N + w_A = 1$ ### Step 1 — Oxygen demanded by each element **Carbon:** $$ \text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 $$ 1 kmol C (12 kg) consumes 1 kmol O₂ (32 kg). So: $$ m_{O_2,C} = w_C \times \dfrac{32}{12} = \dfrac{8}{3}\, w_C \;\; [\text{kg O}_2 / \text{kg dry feed}] $$ **Hydrogen:** $$ \text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} $$ 2 kg H₂ consumes 16 kg O₂. So: $$ m_{O_2,H} = w_H \times \dfrac{16}{2} = 8\, w_H \;\; [\text{kg O}_2 / \text{kg dry feed}] $$ **Sulphur:** $$ \text{S} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2 $$ 32 kg S consumes 32 kg O₂. So: $$ m_{O_2,S} = w_S \times \dfrac{32}{32} = w_S \;\; [\text{kg O}_2 / \text{kg dry feed}] $$ ### Step 2 — Subtract the oxygen already present in the fuel Biomass itself carries oxygen in its organic matrix. This *internal* oxygen is assumed available for combustion: $$ m_{O_2,\text{intrinsic}} = w_O \times \dfrac{32}{32} = w_O \;\; [\text{kg O}_2 / \text{kg dry feed}] $$ ### Step 3 — Net external O₂ required $$ m_{O_2,\text{net}} = \dfrac{8}{3}\,w_C + 8\,w_H + w_S - w_O $$ ### Step 4 — Convert to stoichiometric air Air contains **23.2 %** O₂ by mass, so: $$ m_{\text{air,stoich}} = \dfrac{m_{O_2,\text{net}}}{0.232} = \dfrac{\tfrac{8}{3}\,w_C + 8\,w_H + w_S - w_O}{0.232} $$ ### Step 5 — Actual air for gasification (apply ER) $$ m_{\text{air,actual}} = ER \times m_{\text{air,stoich}} \quad ; \quad ER = 0.25 - 0.35 \; \text{(typical for gasification)} $$ ### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Stoichiometric Air for Sawdust **Given (dry basis, per kg sawdust):** - $w_C = 0.483$, $w_H = 0.060$, $w_O = 0.395$, $w_S = 0.002$, $w_N = 0.005$, $w_A = 0.055$ - Moisture content (wet basis) = **10 %** → dry feed = 0.90 of total feed **Step 1 — Compute each term:** $$ \dfrac{8}{3}\,w_C = \dfrac{8}{3} \times 0.483 = 1.288 \; \text{kg O}_2 $$ $$ 8\,w_H = 8 \times 0.060 = 0.480 \; \text{kg O}_2 $$ $$ w_S = 0.002 \; \text{kg O}_2 $$ $$ w_O = 0.395 \; \text{kg O}_2 $$ **Step 2 — Net external O₂:** $$ m_{O_2,\text{net}} = 1.288 + 0.480 + 0.002 - 0.395 = 1.375 \; \text{kg O}_2 / \text{kg dry feed} $$ **Step 3 — Stoichiometric air (per kg dry feed):** $$ m_{\text{air,stoich}} = \dfrac{1.375}{0.232} = 5.927 \; \text{kg air / kg dry feed} $$ **Step 4 — Account for moisture (per kg wet feed):** $$ m_{\text{air,stoich, wet}} = 5.927 \times 0.90 = 5.334 \; \text{kg air / kg wet feed} $$ **Step 5 — Actual air for gasification at ER = 0.30:** $$ m_{\text{air,actual}} = 0.30 \times 5.334 = 1.600 \; \text{kg air / kg wet feed} $$ ### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Cold Gas Efficiency **Given:** - Feed rate $\dot{m}_{\text{feed}} = 100 \; \text{kg/hr}$, $LHV_{\text{feed}} = 16.0 \; \text{MJ/kg}$ - Syngas yield $\dot{V}_{sg} = 220 \; \text{Nm}^3/\text{hr}$ - Syngas LHV $LHV_{sg} = 5.5 \; \text{MJ/Nm}^3$ **Computation:** $$ \eta_{\text{cold}} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{sg} \cdot LHV_{sg}}{\dot{m}_{\text{feed}} \cdot LHV_{\text{feed}}} \times 100\% $$ $$ \eta_{\text{cold}} = \dfrac{220 \times 5.5}{100 \times 16.0} \times 100\% = \dfrac{1210}{1600} \times 100\% = 75.6\% $$ ### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > **This 12-point comparison matrix is the most frequently asked KTU 2024 question on this topic.** Memorize all rows. | # | Parameter | Pyrolysis | Gasification | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Oxygen availability | **Absent / trace** | **Sub-stoichiometric (ER 0.2–0.4)** | | 2 | Operating temperature | **300 – 900 °C** | **700 – 1200 °C** | | 3 | Primary products | **Char, bio-oil, pyro-gas** | **Producer gas / syngas + ash** | | 4 | Product state at outlet | Solid + Liquid + Gas | Predominantly Gas | | 5 | Energy content of product | Bio-oil ≈ 17–22 MJ/kg | Syngas ≈ 4–6 MJ/Nm³ | | 6 | Air requirement | None | 1.5 – 2.5 kg/kg feed | | 7 | Tar yield | **High** (especially slow pyrolysis) | **Low** (cracked at high T) | | 8 | Residence time | Seconds → hours (varies) | Seconds → minutes | | 9 | Process complexity | Moderate | High (needs gas cleaning) | | 10 | Carbon conversion | 30 – 70 % (rest stays in char) | 70 – 95 % | | 11 | Cold-gas efficiency | N/A | 60 – 80 % | | 12 | Typical end-use | Charcoal, fuel oil, chemicals | Power generation, FT fuel, methanol | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pyrolysis Process ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Biomass Feed
Drying Zone] --> A2[Devolatilization
200 to 500 C] A2 --> A3[Secondary Cracking
500 to 800 C] A3 --> A4[Char Consolidation
Above 600 C] A4 --> A5[Biochar Output
Solid 25 to 40 percent] A3 --> A6[Bio-oil Condenser
Liquid 20 to 60 percent] A3 --> A7[Non-condensable Gas
Gas 15 to 30 percent] A7 --> A8[Pyro-gas to Flare
or Recycle] A6 --> A9[Bio-oil Storage Tank] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Downdraft Gasifier ```mermaid flowchart TD B1[Biomass Feedstock
Top Hopper] --> B2[Drying Zone
Below 150 C] B2 --> B3[Pyrolysis Zone
150 to 600 C] B3 --> B4[Oxidation Zone
800 to 1200 C] B4 --> B5[Reduction Zone
600 to 900 C] B5 --> B6[Producer Gas
Outlet CO H2 CH4] B6 --> B7[Gas Cleaning
Cyclone Scrubber] B7 --> B8[Syngas to IC Engine
or Turbine] B4 --> B9[Ash and Slag
Bottom Discharge] ``` ## 4.3 Side-by-Side Process Comparison Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Pyrolysis Pathway] --> P2[No Air or
Inert N2] P2 --> P3[Heat Only
300 to 900 C] P3 --> P4[Three Phase
Output] P4 --> P5[Char] P4 --> P6[Bio-oil] P4 --> P7[Pyro-gas] G1[Gasification Pathway] --> G2[Sub-stoich Air
ER 0.2 to 0.4] G2 --> G3[Heat Plus Air
700 to 1200 C] G3 --> G4[Predominantly
Gaseous Output] G4 --> G5[Syngas
CO and H2 Rich] G4 --> G6[Ash / Slag] ``` ## 4.4 Reactor-Type Selection Map (Pyrolysis vs Gasification) ```mermaid flowchart TD R0{Target Product?} R0 -->|Char dominant| R1[Slow Pyrolysis
300 to 500 C
Long Residence] R0 -->|Oil dominant| R2[Fast Pyrolysis
500 to 800 C
Short Residence] R0 -->|Gas dominant| R3[Gasification
800 to 1200 C
Fixed or Fluid Bed] R1 --> R1a[Fixed Bed Reactor] R2 --> R2a[Fluidized Bed Reactor] R3 --> R3a[Downdraft or
Updraft Gasifier] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define pyrolysis. List the three main products obtained and state the operating temperature range.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 m):** Pyrolysis is the thermal decomposition of organic matter in the *absence of air/oxygen* at temperatures typically **300 – 900 °C**. - **Products (1 m):** Solid **biochar**, liquid **bio-oil**, and non-condensable **pyro-gas**. - **Temperature range (1 m):** 300 – 900 °C, with peak devolatilization between 400 – 600 °C. --- > **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *What is a gasifying agent? Name any four with their typical applications.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 m):** A gasifying agent is the oxidizing medium supplied in sub-stoichiometric quantity to convert solid carbonaceous fuel into combustible gas. - **Four agents (2 m):** 1. **Air** — most common; cheapest; gives low heating-value gas (4–6 MJ/Nm³). 2. **Pure Oxygen** — gives medium heating-value gas (10–18 MJ/Nm³). 3. **Steam** — produces H₂-rich syngas; used for hydrogen production. 4. **CO₂** — recycles combustion product; reduces net emissions. 5. *(Bonus)* Hydrogen — produces CH₄-rich gas via methanation. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Question (14 Marks) > **Choose ONE of the following alternatives.** ### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]* **(a)** *Compare pyrolysis and gasification on the basis of any **seven** technical parameters. Tabulate your answer.* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 m — 1 mark per parameter, neatly tabulated):** | # | Parameter | Pyrolysis | Gasification | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Oxygen | Absent | Limited (ER 0.2–0.4) | | 2 | Temperature | 300 – 900 °C | 700 – 1200 °C | | 3 | Main products | Char + oil + gas | Syngas + ash | | 4 | Product state | All 3 phases | Mainly gas | | 5 | Air required | None | 1.5 – 2.5 kg/kg | | 6 | Tar yield | High | Low | | 7 | End-use | Fuel/chemical feedstock | Power / synthetic fuel | **(b)** *With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **downdraft gasifier**. State its advantages over updraft gasifier.* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 m):** **Working (4 m):** The downdraft gasifier has **four zones** vertically arranged. Biomass enters at the top and air is admitted through side nozzles located about midway down. The flow of gas and solid is *co-current* (both downward). 1. **Drying zone** (top): Moisture evaporates as biomass descends. 2. **Pyrolysis zone**: At 150 – 600 °C, the dry biomass devolatilizes to char + gas + tar vapour. 3. **Oxidation (combustion) zone**: Air enters here; char burns with O₂ to give CO₂ + heat. Temperature reaches 1000 – 1200 °C. 4. **Reduction zone**: Hot gases pass through the hot char bed; endothermic reactions (Boudouard, water–gas) convert CO₂ + H₂O into CO + H₂. Producer gas exits from the bottom along with ash. **Advantages over updraft (3 m):** - Tar content of gas is **much lower** (~ 0.5 g/Nm³ vs 50 g/Nm³) because all tars must pass through the hot oxidation zone. - Suitable for **IC engines** without elaborate gas cleaning. - More **compact** design; higher specific throughput. - Faster **start-up** time. --- ### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023]* **(a)** *Explain the **chemistry of gasification** with the help of the **five principal reactions**. State the role of the reduction zone.* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 m — 1 mark each for reaction + 2 marks for reduction zone role):** The five principal reactions are: 1. **Partial combustion (oxidation zone):** $$ \text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO} \quad \Delta H = -111.4 \; \text{kJ/mol} $$ *Exothermic — provides process heat.* 2. **Complete combustion:** $$ \text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -393.5 \; \text{kJ/mol} $$ *Exothermic — major heat source.* 3. **Boudouard reaction (reduction zone):** $$ \text{C} + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO} \quad \Delta H = +172.5 \; \text{kJ/mol} $$ *Endothermic — converts CO₂ into useful CO.* 4. **Water–gas reaction (reduction zone):** $$ \text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = +131.4 \; \text{kJ/mol} $$ *Endothermic — primary H₂ source.* 5. **Methanation:** $$ \text{C} + 2\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 \quad \Delta H = -74.8 \; \text{kJ/mol} $$ *Exothermic — small contribution to gas heating value.* **Role of reduction zone (2 m):** It is the "gas-making" heart of the gasifier. The endothermic reactions 3 and 4 absorb the heat released by the exothermic reactions 1 and 2 in the oxidation zone above, and convert the hot combustion products (CO₂, H₂O) into combustible CO and H₂. Without this zone, the gas would be mostly inert CO₂ + N₂ with negligible heating value. **(b)** *A biomass of composition **C = 45 %, H = 6 %, O = 42 %, S = 0.5 %, N = 1 %, Ash = 5.5 %** (dry basis) is gasified with air at **ER = 0.30**. Calculate the **stoichiometric air requirement** per kg dry feed and the **actual air supplied** to the gasifier.* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 m):** **Given (per kg dry feed):** - $w_C = 0.45$, $w_H = 0.06$, $w_O = 0.42$, $w_S = 0.005$, $w_N = 0.01$, $w_A = 0.055$ **Step 1 — O₂ demand by carbon (2 m):** $$ m_{O_2,C} = \dfrac{8}{3} \times 0.45 = 1.200 \; \text{kg O}_2 $$ **Step 2 — O₂ demand by hydrogen (1 m):** $$ m_{O_2,H} = 8 \times 0.06 = 0.480 \; \text{kg O}_2 $$ **Step 3 — O₂ demand by sulphur (1 m):** $$ m_{O_2,S} = 1 \times 0.005 = 0.005 \; \text{kg O}_2 $$ **Step 4 — Subtract intrinsic O₂ (1 m):** $$ m_{O_2,\text{intrinsic}} = 0.420 \; \text{kg O}_2 $$ $$ m_{O_2,\text{net}} = 1.200 + 0.480 + 0.005 - 0.420 = 1.265 \; \text{kg O}_2 / \text{kg dry feed} $$ **Step 5 — Stoichiometric air (1 m):** $$ m_{\text{air,stoich}} = \dfrac{1.265}{0.232} = 5.453 \; \text{kg air / kg dry feed} $$ **Step 6 — Actual air supplied (1 m):** $$ m_{\text{air,actual}} = ER \times m_{\text{air,stoich}} = 0.30 \times 5.453 = 1.636 \; \text{kg air / kg dry feed} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting to subtract intrinsic oxygen** ($w_O$) — costs 1 mark. > 2. **Using 21 % instead of 23.2 %** for mass-basis O₂ fraction in air. KTU expects **mass basis = 23.2 %**, volume basis = 21 %. Mixing them up is a common -1 mark mistake. > 3. **Forgetting the multiplication by 8/3** for carbon (i.e., writing 32/12 = 2.67 directly without converting to kg/kg basis properly). > 4. **Not explicitly stating ER assumption** when computing actual air — KTU wants the ER value visible in the solution. --- ## 5.3 📝 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pin this list in your memory the night before the exam.** - ✅ **Pyrolysis** = no oxygen, 300–900 °C, three products (char + oil + gas). - ✅ **Gasification** = limited oxygen (ER 0.2–0.4), 700–1200 °C, mainly syngas. - ✅ **Equivalence Ratio (ER)** = (actual air) / (stoichiometric air); gasifier uses ER < 1. - ✅ **Stoichiometric air formula:** $$ m_{\text{air,stoich}} = \dfrac{\tfrac{8}{3}\,w_C + 8\,w_H + w_S - w_O}{0.232} $$ - ✅ **Mass % of O₂ in air = 23.2 %**, volume % = 21 %. - ✅ **Five principal gasification reactions** — memorize signs of ΔH (exo vs endo). - ✅ **Boudouard** and **Water–Gas** reactions occur in the **reduction zone** and are **endothermic**. - ✅ **Combustion zone** reactions are **exothermic**; provide heat to the reduction zone. - ✅ **Cold gas efficiency** = output gas energy / input feed energy. - ✅ **Downdraft gasifier** has **co-current** flow and **low tar** output → preferred for IC engines. - ✅ **Updraft gasifier** has **counter-current** flow and **high tar** output → better for direct heating. - ✅ **Bio-oil heating value** ≈ 17–22 MJ/kg; **syngas LHV** ≈ 4–6 MJ/Nm³ (air-blown). - ✅ **Pyrolysis** is **endothermic** overall (heat must be supplied externally). - ✅ **Auto-thermal gasification** is possible because the exothermic oxidation zone sustains the endothermic reduction zone.

topics.title: Overview of Biological Conversion Technologies in WTE
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT836 - WASTE TO ENERGY TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Overview of Biological Conversion Technologies in WTE

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Biological Conversion Technologies** in Waste-to-Energy (WTE) refer to a class of biochemical processes that utilize the metabolic activity of microorganisms (bacteria, archaea, fungi, and algae) to convert organic fractions of solid, liquid, and gaseous waste streams into useful forms of energy, primarily **biogas (CH₄ + CO₂)**, **bioethanol**, **biodiesel**, **biohydrogen**, and **microbial heat**, while simultaneously stabilizing the waste.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Biological conversion technologies encompass all processes where living organisms or their enzymatic systems catalyze the breakdown or transformation of biodegradable waste substrates into energy carriers. These operate under mild conditions (typically **20 °C to 60 °C** and near-atmospheric pressure), distinguishing them from thermochemical routes (incineration, pyrolysis, gasification) discussed earlier in the course.

The overarching stoichiometric umbrella reaction for biological conversion of a generic organic substrate can be summarized as:

$$ \text{Organic Waste} + \text{Microorganisms} \xrightarrow{\text{Enzymes}} \text{Energy Carrier} + \text{Residue} + \text{Heat} $$

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Cow's Stomach"**
> Think of biological conversion as feeding your waste to a herd of invisible "cows" (microbes). Just as a cow chews grass (cellulose) in four stomach chambers and produces methane burps (biogas) and dung (digestate), microbes consume organic waste in sealed tanks called **bioreactors** and burp out methane-rich biogas while leaving behind a nutrient-rich slurry. Faster than a cow, more efficient, and you can collect the gas for cooking, electricity, or vehicle fuel!

The **core philosophy** is simple: nature has been recycling biomass for **3.5 billion years**. Instead of burning waste and releasing carbon as CO₂, we harness controlled microbial digestion to capture energy in cleaner molecular forms.

## 1.3 Classification of Biological Conversion Pathways

Biological WTE technologies are broadly classified into **three families** based on the presence or absence of oxygen and the end-product sought:

| Pathway | Oxygen Status | Primary Energy Product | Typical Microbes | Operating Temperature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Anaerobic Digestion (AD)** | Strictly absent | Biogas (CH₄: 50–70%) | Methanogenic archaea | Mesophilic (35 °C) / Thermophilic (55 °C) |
| **Aerobic Composting** | Continuously supplied | Heat + stabilized compost | Bacillus, Actinomycetes, Fungi | Ambient to 70 °C |
| **Fermentation** | Limited / Anaerobic | Ethanol, H₂, organic acids | Yeast (*Saccharomyces*), Clostridia | 30–40 °C |
| **Landfill Gas Recovery** | Limited natural O₂ | CH₄ (40–60%) | Mixed consortia | Ambient (in-situ) |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiners' Anchor Point:** Any question on Module 3 essentially boils down to identifying **which pathway suits which waste type**. Municipal Sewage Sludge → AD; Food Waste → AD/Aerobic Composting; Lignocellulosic crop residue → Fermentation (after pretreatment).

## 1.4 Why Biological Conversion? Engineering Motivation

- Operates at **low temperature and pressure** → low CAPEX & OPEX compared to incinerators.
- Handles **wet waste** (60–95% moisture) efficiently, where incineration is impractical.
- Simultaneously **stabilizes** the waste, kills pathogens, and produces a nutrient-rich **digestate/bio-slurry** usable as fertilizer.
- Achieves a **negative carbon balance** when integrated with proper land use (carbon sequestration in soil).
- Aligns with **Sustainable Development Goals (SDG 7, 11, 13)** — KTU frequently frames questions in this SDG context.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Industrial Metrics Every KTU Student Must Memorize:**
> - **Biogas Yield:** 0.2 – 0.5 m³ per kg of volatile solids (VS) added
> - **Methane Content:** 50 – 70 % by volume
> - **Energy Content of Biogas:** **18 – 28 MJ/m³** (≈ 5 – 8 kWh/m³)
> - **Electrical Conversion Efficiency (CHP):** 35 – 42 %
> - **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT):** 15 – 40 days
> - **Organic Loading Rate (OLR):** 1 – 6 kg VS/m³·day
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Anaerobic Digestion (AD)

AD is the most heavily weighted topic in Module 3. It proceeds through **four sequential biochemical stages**, each mediated by a distinct microbial guild:

### Stage 1 — Hydrolysis
- **Function:** Complex biopolymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) are cleaved into monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids) by extracellular enzymes (amylases, proteases, lipases).
- **Rate-limiting step** for particulate / lignocellulosic waste.
- Generic reaction:
$$ (C_6H_{10}O_5)_n + nH_2O \xrightarrow{\text{Cellulase}} n \, C_6H_{12}O_6 $$

### Stage 2 — Acidogenesis
- **Function:** Monomers are fermented into volatile fatty acids (VFAs — acetic, propionic, butyric), alcohols, CO₂, and H₂.
- Mediated by facultative and obligate anaerobes.
- Example:
$$ C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2 \, CH_3CH_2OH + 2 \, CO_2 $$
$$ C_6H_{12}O_6 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2 \, CH_3COOH + 2 \, CO_2 + 4H_2 $$

### Stage 3 — Acetogenesis
- **Function:** VFAs and alcohols are converted into acetate, H₂, and CO₂ by acetogenic bacteria (syntrophs).
- **Why critical:** These microbes are thermodynamically unfavorable; they require H₂ partial pressure to be kept low (< 10⁻⁴ atm), which is why methanogens must consume H₂ simultaneously (interspecies H₂ transfer).

### Stage 4 — Methanogenesis
- **Function:** Acetate, H₂/CO₂, and other C₁ compounds are converted to CH₄ by methanogenic archaea.
- Two dominant pathways:
$$ CH_3COOH \rightarrow CH_4 + CO_2 \quad \text{(acetoclastic, ~70% of CH₄)}$$
$$ CO_2 + 4H_2 \rightarrow CH_4 + 2H_2O \quad \text{(hydrogenotrophic, ~30%)}$$

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of Aerobic Composting

Unlike AD, composting is an **exothermic aerobic** process with three temperature phases:

| Phase | Temperature | Active Microbes | Duration |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mesophilic** | 20 – 45 °C | Bacteria (mesophiles) | 1 – 3 days |
| **Thermophilic** | 45 – 70 °C | Thermophilic bacteria, actinomycetes | 2 – 4 weeks |
| **Cooling & Maturation** | < 40 °C | Fungi, mesophiles return | 4 – 12 weeks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Reaching > 55 °C is essential to kill pathogens (E. coli, Salmonella) and weed seeds — this is why windrow turners and aerated static piles are engineered with temperature feedback loops.

## 2.3 Fermentation Routes to Biofuels

### 2.3.1 Bioethanol Production
- **Substrates:** Sugarcane molasses, corn starch, starchy food waste, lignocellulosic biomass.
- **Microbe:** *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* (yeast) for hexoses; *Zymomonas mobilis* for higher yield.
- **Core reaction (fermentation):**
$$ C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Yeast}} 2 \, C_2H_5OH + 2 \, CO_2 \quad (\Delta G = -235 \, kJ/mol) $$
- **Theoretical yield:** 0.51 g ethanol per g glucose → **51 %** by mass; real industrial yield: ~90–95 % of theoretical.

### 2.3.2 Biohydrogen Production
- **Dark fermentation** by Clostridia:
$$ C_6H_{12}O_6 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 4H_2 + 2CH_3COOH + 2CO_2 $$
- **Photo fermentation** by purple non-sulfur bacteria:
$$ CH_3COOH + 2H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{Light}} 4H_2 + 2CO_2 $$
- **Theoretical max:** 4 mol H₂ / mol glucose (dark) = 33 % energy recovery; practical: 1–2 mol H₂/mol.

### 2.3.3 Biodiesel via Transesterification (Biological Context)
Although transesterification is a chemical step, **biological production of oil** (microbial lipids from oleaginous yeasts like *Yarrowia lipolytica* or *Rhodotorula glutinis* grown on food waste) places it under biological WTE.
- Oleaginous microbes accumulate > 20 % lipids by dry cell weight.
- Lipids → transesterified with methanol → Fatty Acid Methyl Esters (FAME) = biodiesel.

## 2.4 Landfill Gas (LFG) Recovery

A passive AD process occurring inside municipal solid waste (MSW) landfills. The five phases of landfill stabilization:

| Phase | Duration | Dominant Gas |
|---|---|---|
| I — Initial Aerobic | Days | CO₂, N₂ |
| II — Acidogenic | Weeks–months | H₂, CO₂, VFA |
| III — Methanogenic Unsteady | Months–years | CH₄ rising |
| IV — Methanogenic Steady | Years (peak) | CH₄ (40–60 %), CO₂ (35–50 %) |
| V — Maturation | Decades | CH₄ decline, O₂ ingress |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU-ESE favorites. Memorize the units and boundary conditions.**

| # | Formula / Expression | Physical Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Y_{CH_4} = \dfrac{0.35 \times (\text{COD removed})}{1{,}000}$ | Methane yield from COD balance | m³ CH₄/day |
| 2 | $\text{OLR} = \dfrac{Q \times S_0}{V}$ | Organic Loading Rate | kg VS/m³·day |
| 3 | $\text{HRT} = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | Hydraulic Retention Time | days |
| 4 | $\text{SRT} = \dfrac{V \times X}{Q \times X_e}$ | Solids Retention Time | days |
| 5 | $C/N \text{ ratio} = 20\!:\!30$ (optimum) | C:N of feed | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\eta_{CHP} = \dfrac{P_{elec} + P_{thermal}}{Q_{biogas} \times LHV_{biogas}}$ | Combined Heat & Power efficiency | % |
| 7 | $E_{biogas} = V_{CH_4} \times 35.8 \, MJ/m^3$ | Energy in methane (LHV) | MJ |
| 8 | $\text{TS} = \dfrac{M_{dry}}{M_{wet}} \times 100\%$ | Total Solids content | % |
| 9 | $\text{VS} = \dfrac{M_{VS}}{M_{TS}} \times 100\%$ | Volatile Solids (organic fraction) | % |
| 10 | $\text{BMP} = \dfrac{V_{CH_4,STP}}{m_{VS,added}}$ | Biochemical Methane Potential | mL CH₄/g VS |
| 11 | $Y_{ethanol} = 0.51 \times Y_{glucose}$ | Theoretical ethanol yield | g/g |
| 12 | $\Delta H_{CH_4\,combustion} = -890 \, kJ/mol$ | Energy released on CH₄ burning | kJ/mol |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipeline Trick:** When asked "calculate the energy potential of X tonnes of food waste":
> Energy (MJ) = Mass × VS% × BMP (m³ CH₄/tonne VS) × 35.8 MJ/m³
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations

### 3.1.1 Derivation of Theoretical Methane Yield from Substrate Composition

For a generic organic substrate with molecular formula $C_aH_bO_cN_d$, Buswell's equation gives the complete stoichiometry of complete anaerobic oxidation to methane:

$$
C_aH_bO_cN_d + \left(a - \dfrac{b}{4} - \dfrac{c}{2} + \dfrac{3d}{4}\right)H_2O \longrightarrow \left(\dfrac{a}{2} + \dfrac{d}{8} - \dfrac{c}{4}\right)CH_4
+ \left(\dfrac{a}{2} - \dfrac{d}{8} + \dfrac{c}{4}\right)CO_2 + d \, NH_3
$$

This is the **foundational derivation** every KTU examiner expects a student to be able to write for carbohydrates, proteins, and lipids.

**Worked Example 1: Glucose** ($C_6H_{12}O_6$, so a=6, b=12, c=6, d=0)
Substituting into Buswell's coefficients:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CH}_4 \text{ coefficient} &= \frac{6}{2} + 0 - \frac{6}{4} = 3 - 1.5 = 1.5 \\
\text{CO}_2 \text{ coefficient} &= \frac{6}{2} - 0 + \frac{6}{4} = 3 + 1.5 = 4.5
\end{aligned}
$$

The water coefficient:

$$
a - \frac{b}{4} - \frac{c}{2} + \frac{3d}{4} = 6 - 3 - 3 + 0 = 0
$$

Hence the reaction reduces to the famous textbook form:

$$
C_6H_{12}O_6 \longrightarrow 3 \, CH_4 + 3 \, CO_2
$$

Notice that glucose **does not require water** in the net reaction — a special case because the H/O ratio already matches that of methane + carbon dioxide (4:1).

**Worked Example 2: A Lipid** — Tripalmitin ($C_{51}H_{98}O_6$, a=51, b=98, c=6, d=0)
Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CH}_4 \text{ coefficient} &= \frac{51}{2} + 0 - \frac{6}{4} = 25.5 - 1.5 = 24 \\
\text{CO}_2 \text{ coefficient} &= \frac{51}{2} - 0 + \frac{6}{4} = 25.5 + 1.5 = 27 \\
\text{H}_2\text{O coefficient} &= 51 - 24.5 - 3 + 0 = 23.5
\end{aligned}
$$

Reaction:
$$
C_{51}H_{98}O_6 + 23.5 \, H_2O \longrightarrow 24 \, CH_4 + 27 \, CO_2
$$

This is **why lipids have ~3× higher methane yield per mass than carbohydrates** — a key KTU question trap.

### 3.1.2 Derivation of the Biogas Energy Potential Equation

Starting from a known feed mass $M$ (kg) with volatile solids fraction $VS$ (decimal) and specific methane yield $BMP$ (m³ CH₄ per kg VS added):

**Step 1:** Calculate total VS in feed.
$$
M_{VS} = M \times VS \quad [\text{kg VS}]
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate methane volume at standard conditions (STP: 0 °C, 101.325 kPa).
$$
V_{CH_4} = M_{VS} \times BMP \quad [\text{m}^3 \, CH_4]
$$

**Step 3:** Convert methane volume to energy using its lower heating value (LHV = 35.8 MJ/m³).
$$
E_{CH_4} = V_{CH_4} \times 35.8 \quad [\text{MJ}]
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to electrical equivalent assuming 35 % CHP efficiency.
$$
E_{elec} = E_{CH_4} \times 0.35 \quad [\text{MJ}]
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ).
$$
E_{kWh} = \frac{E_{elec}}{3.6} \quad [\text{kWh}]
$$

### 3.1.3 Numerical Demonstration (Worked Calculation)

**Problem (typical KTU style):** A hotel generates 1.2 tonnes/day of food waste with 22 % TS and 90 % VS (of TS). The BMP is 0.42 m³ CH₄/kg VS. A CHP unit operates at 38 % electrical efficiency. Calculate the daily electricity output in kWh.

**Given:**
- $M$ = 1200 kg/day
- $TS$ = 0.22, $VS$ = 0.90 (of TS)
- $BMP$ = 0.42 m³ CH₄/kg VS
- $\eta_{CHP}$ = 0.38

**Step-by-step:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{TS} &= 1200 \times 0.22 = 264 \, \text{kg/day} \\
M_{VS} &= 264 \times 0.90 = 237.6 \, \text{kg VS/day} \\
V_{CH_4} &= 237.6 \times 0.42 = 99.792 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day} \\
E_{CH_4} &= 99.792 \times 35.8 = 3572.55 \, \text{MJ/day} \\
E_{elec} &= 3572.55 \times 0.38 = 1357.57 \, \text{MJ/day} \\
E_{kWh} &= \frac{1357.57}{3.6} = 377.1 \, \text{kWh/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** If a question gives VS as a fraction of wet mass, do **not** multiply by TS again. The wording "VS = 90 % of TS" is different from "VS = 90 % of total mass". Read the stem carefully.

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix

Selecting a biological WTE technology requires a structured decision logic. The matrix below is built for **KTU-style scenario problems**:

| Waste Characteristic | Best Biological Route | Why? | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|
| Food waste, kitchen waste (high moisture, easily degradable) | **Wet Anaerobic Digestion** | Fast hydrolysis, high BMP (~0.4 m³ CH₄/kg VS) | Inhibited by high NH₃ if protein-rich |
| Sewage sludge (municipal WWTP) | **Mesophilic AD** | Pathogen kill, energy recovery, sludge volume reduction | Long HRT (~20 d) |
| Lignocellulosic agri-residue (rice straw, bagasse) | **SSF / SHF Fermentation → 2G Ethanol** | Abundant & renewable, doesn't compete with food | Needs pretreatment, low yield |
| Used cooking oil / FOG | **Transesterification to Biodiesel** | Direct route, simple | Glycerol by-product management |
| Cattle dung / poultry litter | **Plug-flow / CSTR Biogas** | Indigenous familiarity, decentralized | High fiber, low C/N |
| Garden / yard trimmings | **Aerobic Composting** | Aerobic, no gas collection needed | Slow, requires turning |
| Mixed MSW (landfilled) | **Landfill Gas Recovery** | In-situ, no pre-sorting | Slow, declining yield over decades |
| Wastewater with high BOD | **Microbial Fuel Cell (MFC)** | Direct electricity from substrate | Very low power density |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Logic Anchor:** When a question asks "which technology is best suited for X waste?" the answer is *not* always biogas. Match the **dominant biochemical limitation** to the route that overcomes it.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Anaerobic Digestion Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feedstock: Organic Waste] --> B[Pre-treatment: Shredding, Mixing]
    B --> C[Bioreactor / Digester]
    C --> D[Hydrolysis Stage]
    D --> E[Acidogenesis Stage]
    E --> F[Acetogenesis Stage]
    F --> G[Methanogenesis Stage]
    G --> H[Biogas Storage]
    H --> I[CHP Unit Electricity and Heat]
    G --> J[Digestate Separator]
    J --> K[Liquid Fraction: Biofertilizer]
    J --> L[Solid Fraction: Soil Conditioner]
    C --> M[Process Control Sensors]
    M --> N[Temperature pH VFA Alkalinity]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Biological Conversion Technology Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```
