# 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between mesophilic and thermophilic anaerobic digestion. State the typical HRT and methane yield range for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Mesophilic | Thermophilic |
|-----------|------------|--------------|
| Temperature | 35 ± 2 °C | 55 ± 2 °C |
| HRT | 15–30 days | 10–20 days |
| Methane yield | Lower | Higher (5–10 % more) |
| Pathogen kill | Partial | Excellent |
| Process stability | High | Lower (sensitive to ammonia) |
| Energy demand for heating | Lower | Higher (heat loss ↑) |

**Conclusion**: Mesophilic systems dominate in industry due to superior stability and lower energy input; thermophilic is preferred when pathogen-free digestate is mandatory.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series-2]**
**List the four sequential stages of anaerobic digestion. Which stage is most sensitive to pH drop?**

**Model Answer:**
The four stages are: **Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis**.
- Hydrolysis breaks complex polymers into monomers.
- Acidogenesis converts monomers to VFAs.
- Acetogenesis converts VFAs to acetate + H₂ + CO₂.
- Methanogenesis produces CH₄ + CO₂.
**Methanogenesis** is the most pH-sensitive stage — inhibition occurs below **pH 6.5**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

---

### **🔵 QUESTION A (14 Marks)**  `[KTU ESE – Dec 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **biochemistry and microbial ecology of anaerobic digestion**. Explain why methanogens are called "rate-limiting" and what operational parameters safeguard their activity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A continuous stirred tank reactor (CSTR) treats **20 m³/day** of food waste slurry with influent COD = **55,000 mg/L** and effluent COD = **5,500 mg/L**. Digester volume is **250 m³**. Calculate:
(i) Volumetric OLR in kg COD/m³·day
(ii) Daily methane production at STP
(iii) Methane energy content in kWh/day, assuming **60 % CH₄ in biogas** and LHV = **50 MJ/kg**
**(7 Marks)**

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

#### Part (a) — Biochemistry & Ecology (7 marks)

The four stages proceed sequentially but interdependently:

**Stage 1 — Hydrolysis**: Polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) are cleaved extracellularly by hydrolytic enzymes (cellulases, proteases, lipases) into soluble monomers. For particulate or cellulosic substrates, this is the **rate-limiting** stage. Particle size reduction and temperature accelerate it.

**Stage 2 — Acidogenesis**: Acidogenic bacteria (e.g., *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*) ferment monomers to VFAs (acetate, propionate, butyrate), alcohols, H₂ and CO₂. The H₂ partial pressure determines product distribution. Rapid OLR increase causes VFA accumulation → pH drop.

**Stage 3 — Acetogenesis**: Syntrophic acetogens oxidize VFAs and alcohols to acetate, H₂ and CO₂. Reaction is **thermodynamically unfavourable** unless methanogens keep H₂ < 10⁻⁴ atm — a phenomenon called **Interspecies H₂ Transfer (IHT)**.

**Stage 4 — Methanogenesis**: Performed by **archaea** (not bacteria) via two pathways:
- **Acetoclastic (~70 %):** $CH_3COOH \rightarrow CH_4 + CO_2$ (*Methanosaeta*, *Methanosarcina*)
- **Hydrogenotrophic (~30 %):** $4H_2 + CO_2 \rightarrow CH_4 + 2H_2O$ (*Methanobacterium*)

**Why methanogens are rate-limiting:**
1. Doubling time = 2–6 days (much slower than acidogens at 30 min–2 h).
2. Highly sensitive to **pH < 6.5**, **free NH₃ > 1500 mg/L**, **O₂**, **sulphide**, **heavy metals (Cu, Zn, Ni, Cr)**.
3. Acetoclastic pathway is kinetically slower than hydrogenotrophic.

**Operational safeguards** (must appear in the answer):
- pH buffered at **6.8–7.5** using NaHCO₃ or Na₂CO₃.
- Temperature stable: **35 °C (mesophilic)** preferred over thermophilic for stability.
- OLR ramp-up in steps: < 30 % increase/week.
- C/N ratio = **20–30** to prevent ammonia toxicity.
- Trace elements (Fe, Co, Ni, Mo, Se) supplementation.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
$Q = 20 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} = 20{,}000 \text{ L/day}$
$S_0 = 55{,}000 \text{ mg/L} = 55 \text{ g/L}$
$S = 5{,}500 \text{ mg/L} = 5.5 \text{ g/L}$
$V = 250 \text{ m}^3$
$\%\text{CH}_4 = 60\% = 0.60$
$\text{LHV}_{CH_4} = 50 \text{ MJ/kg}$

**(i) Volumetric OLR:**
$$
\text{OLR} = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{V} = \frac{20 \times 55}{250} = \frac{1100}{250} = 4.4 \text{ kg COD/m}^{3}\cdot\text{day}
$$

**(ii) Daily methane production at STP:**
Total methane volume (assuming 100 % of COD removed goes to CH₄):
$$
V_{CH_4,\text{total}} = 0.35 \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S)
$$
$$
V_{CH_4,\text{total}} = 0.35 \times 20{,}000 \times (55 - 5.5) = 0.35 \times 20{,}000 \times 49.5 = 346{,}500 \text{ L/day} = 346.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**(iii) Energy content (kWh/day):**
Mass of methane per day:
$$
m_{CH_4} = \frac{V_{CH_4,\text{total}}}{22.4} \times 16 = \frac{346{,}500}{22.4} \times 16 = 247{,}500 \text{ g/day} = 247.5 \text{ kg/day}
$$

Methane fraction already accounted in $V_{CH_4}$. Energy:
$$
E = 247.5 \times 50 = 12{,}375 \text{ MJ/day}
$$
$$
E = \frac{12{,}375}{3.6} = 3{,}437.5 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning** — Common mistakes students make:
> 1. Forgetting to convert **mg/L → g/L** in OLR calculation (results in 1000× error).
> 2. Using **total biogas** instead of methane volume in energy calculation.
> 3. Failing to convert **m³ to L** before applying the 0.35 factor.
> 4. Mixing up HRT and SRT formulas.

---

### **🟢 QUESTION B (14 Marks)**  `[KTU ESE – May 2024, Series-1]`

**(a)** Compare the following digester technologies with respect to working principle, suitability, advantages and limitations: **(i) CSTR**, **(ii) UASB**, **(iii) Plug Flow**, **(iv) Two-Phase Digester**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The COD balance for an industrial AD plant showed influent COD = 38,000 mg/L, effluent COD = 3,800 mg/L, flow = 100 m³/day. The plant uses **mesophilic (35 °C)** operation with biogas composition **65 % CH₄ + 35 % CO₂**.
Calculate:
(i) Daily biogas volume produced
(ii) Daily electricity that can be generated, assuming CHP electrical efficiency = **38 %**
**(7 Marks)**

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

#### Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Feature | CSTR | UASB | Plug Flow | Two-Phase |
|---------|------|------|-----------|-----------|
| **Mixing** | Mechanical/recirculation | Upflow + natural mixing | None (plug flow) | Stage 1 mixed, Stage 2 unmixed |
| **HRT (typical)** | 15–30 d (wet), 20–40 d | 4 h – 6 d (high rate) | 20–40 d | Total 20–25 d |
| **OLR (kg COD/m³·d)** | 2–6 | 10–30 | 2–4 | 4–8 |
| **Biomass retention** | In suspension | Granular sludge blanket | Settled solids | High in Stage 2 |
| **Best for** | Municipal sludge, manure, food waste | Soluble industrial wastewater | High TS (>15 %), cattle manure | Complex wastes needing optimization |
| **Advantage** | Robust, simple, well-established | High loading, low footprint | Low energy input, simple | Each phase optimized separately |
| **Limitation** | Poor for high TS, large footprint | Long startup (months) for granulation | Particle settling issues | Complex control, higher capex |

**Justification rationale**: CSTR dominates full-scale biogas plants due to maturity and tolerance to solids variability. UASB is preferred for soluble high-strength industrial effluents (sugar, distillery, food processing). Plug flow suits TS > 15 %; two-phase improves methane yield but raises capital cost by ~25 %.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
$S_0 = 38{,}000$ mg/L; $S = 3{,}800$ mg/L
$Q = 100 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} = 100{,}000 \text{ L/day}$
CH₄ fraction = 65 % = 0.65
CHP electrical efficiency = 38 % = 0.38

**(i) Daily biogas volume:**

Methane volume at STP:
$$
V_{CH_4} = 0.35 \times Q \times (S_0 - S)
$$
$$
V_{CH_4} = 0.35 \times 100{,}000 \times (38 - 3.8) = 0.35 \times 100{,}000 \times 34.2 = 1{,}197{,}000 \text{ L/day}
$$
$$
V_{CH_4} = 1197 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

Total biogas (since CH₄ = 65 %):
$$
V_{\text{biogas}} = \frac{V_{CH_4}}{0.65} = \frac{1197}{0.65} = 1841.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**(ii) Daily electricity generation:**

Methane mass per day:
$$
m_{CH_4} = \frac{1{,}197{,}000}{22.4} \times 16 = 855{,}000 \text{ g/day} = 855 \text{ kg/day}
$$

Chemical energy of CH₄:
$$
E_{\text{chem}} = 855 \times 50 = 42{,}750 \text{ MJ/day}
$$

Electrical output (38 % CHP efficiency):
$$
E_{\text{elec}} = 42{,}750 \times 0.38 = 16{,}245 \text{ MJ/day}
$$

$$
E_{\text{elec}} = \frac{16{,}245}{3.6} = 4512.5 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

**Power output equivalent (24-h basis):**
$$
P = \frac{4512.5}{24} \approx 188 \text{ kW continuous}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls in this numerical**:
> 1. Stopping at chemical energy — examiners specifically want **electrical** output using CHP efficiency.
> 2. Using **biogas mass** instead of **methane mass** — biogas contains CO₂ which has no LHV.
> 3. Wrong STP assumption — the **0.35 L/g COD** factor is valid only at **0 °C, 1 atm** (STP). For 35 °C operation, the gas volume increases by ~12 %; KTU usually accepts the STP value unless explicitly stated.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-remember facts for KTU 2024 ESE (Module 3 — Anaerobic Digestion):**

### ✅ Core Concepts
- [ ] AD is a **4-stage process**: Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis.
- [ ] Methanogens are **archaea**, not bacteria, and are **the slowest-growing guild**.
- [ ] Optimum pH = **6.8–7.5**; temperature = **35 °C (mesophilic)** or **55 °C (thermophilic)**.
- [ ] C/N ratio ideal = **20–30**.

### ✅ Key Numbers to Memorize
- [ ] 1 g COD destroyed → **0.35 L CH₄ (STP)**
- [ ] 1 mol CH₄ = **64 g COD** = **22.4 L (STP)**
- [ ] Carbohydrate CH₄ fraction = **50 %**; Lipid = **~70 %**; Protein = **~50–55 %**.
- [ ] Biogas LHV ≈ **20–25 MJ/m³** (depending on CH₄ fraction).
- [ ] Pure CH₄ LHV = **50 MJ/kg = 13.9 kWh/kg**.

### ✅ Digester Selection Rules
- [ ] **Low strength soluble wastewater** → UASB / Anaerobic Filter.
- [ ] **High TS manure / food waste** → CSTR or Plug Flow.
- [ ] **TS > 20 %** → Dry AD / Garage-type batch.
- [ ] **Co-digestion preferred** when C/N ratio of single substrate is imbalanced.

### ✅ Critical Formulae (must be able to write from memory)
- [ ] $\text{OLR} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V}$
- [ ] $\text{HRT} = \dfrac{V}{Q}$
- [ ] $V_{CH_4} = 0.35 \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S)$ (in L/day, STP)
- [ ] Buswell equation: $C_nH_aO_b + \left(n - \tfrac{a}{4} - \tfrac{b}{2}\right)H_2O \rightarrow \ldots$

### ✅ Common Inhibition Factors (high-yield for viva & short questions)
- [ ] **Ammonia toxicity** (free NH₃ > 1500 mg/L)
- [ ] **VFA accumulation** → pH drop
- [ ] **Sulphide toxicity** (H₂S > 200 mg/L)
- [ ] **Heavy metals** (Cu, Zn, Ni)
- [ ] **LCFA inhibition** (in lipid-rich substrates)
- [ ] **Oxygen ingress** (fatal to methanogens)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Strategy**: For KTU ESE Module 3, expect:
> - **2-mark questions** on stage identification or inhibitor name.
> - **3-mark questions** on a comparison (mesophilic vs thermophilic, CSTR vs UASB).
> - **7-mark sub-question** numerical based on **OLR, HRT, methane volume, or energy**.
> - **7-mark sub-question** descriptive on biochemistry, digester design, or process control.
> Always write the **assumptions** (STP, complete COD removal, etc.) at the start of any numerical — KTU examiners award ½ to 1 mark for this.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fermentation: Ethanol Production from Waste
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT836 - WASTE TO ENERGY TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Fermentation: Ethanol Production from Waste # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Fermentation** in the context of waste-to-energy is a *biological anaerobic biochemical conversion process* in which complex organic substrates present in municipal, agricultural, industrial, or food-processing waste streams are enzymatically broken down by microorganisms (yeasts, bacteria, or fungi) into simpler sugars, which are then metabolised intracellularly to produce **ethanol (C₂H₅OH)**, **carbon dioxide (CO₂)**, and cellular biomass, with a net release of energy captured in ATP. In strict KTU 2024 module-3 phrasing: > **Fermentative ethanol production from waste** is defined as the controlled microbial catalysis of carbohydrate-rich waste fractions (sugars, starches, or pre-treated lignocellulose) under anaerobic or micro-aerobic conditions, at mesophilic temperatures of **30–37 °C** and near-neutral pH (**4.5–7.0**), to yield bio-ethanol as the primary energy carrier and fermentation residue (vinasse / stillage) as a secondary co-product. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bakery Yeast in a Bioreactor" Imagine the small packet of dry yeast a baker mixes into dough. The yeast cells "wake up" when they meet sugar and warmth, gobble up the sugar, and exhale CO₂ (which makes bread rise) while producing ethanol (which evaporates during baking). Now scale this same biochemical reaction up **1,000-fold**, place the microbes inside a temperature-controlled stainless-steel vessel called a *fermenter*, and feed them *not refined cane sugar*, but instead the **sugars locked inside kitchen food waste, fruit peels, molasses, or even the cellulose of crop stalks**. The end-product is no longer bread but a clean, water-clear liquid fuel — **bio-ethanol** — which can be blended with petrol or burned in generators. Fermentation is, therefore, **"controlled microbial digestion of waste sugars into alcohol"**. ## 1.3 Important Metrics & Standard Constants (Bold-Highlighted) | Parameter | Typical Range / Value | |---|---| | **Theoretical ethanol yield from glucose** | **0.511 g ethanol / g glucose** | | **Stoichiometric conversion efficiency** | **51.1 % (mass basis)** | | **Theoretical CO₂ release** | **0.489 g CO₂ / g glucose** | | **Industrial practical yield** | **90–95 % of theoretical** | | **Optimum temperature (yeast)** | **30 °C** (mesophilic) | | **Optimum pH** | **4.5–5.0** | | **Fermentation cycle** | **48–72 hours** | | **Cellulose → glucose hydrolysis yield** | **75–90 %** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 3 Focus** > The university expects students to master the *biochemistry*, *microbiology*, *mass-balance mathematics*, and *reactor engineering* of three waste-to-ethanol feedstocks: **sugar-rich waste (molasses/fruit waste)**, **starch-rich waste (cassava/municipal food waste)**, and **lignocellulosic waste (rice straw/sugarcane bagasse)**. A clear comparison of the three pathways is mandatory for ESE answers. > [!NOTE] > **Synonyms used in KTU answer-key scripts:** *alcoholic fermentation*, *ethanolic fermentation*, *ABE fermentation* (acetone-butanol-ethanol — a related but distinct pathway), and *bio-ethanol route*. Examiners accept all four interchangeably. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Six Logical Steps of Fermentative Ethanol Production ### Step 1 — Feedstock Selection & Characterisation - Waste is segregated based on carbohydrate content: **monosaccharides** (fruit waste, molasses), **disaccharides** (sucrose in cane juice), **polysaccharides** (starch in cassava, cellulose in bagasse). - Characterisation parameters: **total solids (TS)**, **volatile solids (VS)**, **sugar content (Brix °Bx)**, **moisture %**, and **C:N ratio**. ### Step 2 — Pre-treatment (only for starch / lignocellulose) - **Physical:** Milling, grinding, steam explosion at **160–240 °C, 1–5 MPa**. - **Chemical:** Acid hydrolysis (dilute **H₂SO₄, 0.5–2 %**) or alkaline hydrolysis (**NaOH, 1–5 %**). - **Biological:** Fungal enzyme cocktails (cellulase, xylanase). - **Purpose:** Disrupt crystalline cellulose–hemicellulose–lignin matrix to expose fermentable sugars. ### Step 3 — Enzymatic Hydrolysis (Saccharification) - Cellulose → glucose via **cellulase enzyme complex** (endoglucanase + exoglucanase + β-glucosidase). - Starch → maltose → glucose via **amylase + glucoamylase**. - Operating window: **45–55 °C, pH 4.8, 24–72 h**. ### Step 4 — Fermentation (the heart of the process) - Inoculated with **Saccharomyces cerevisiae** (yeast, GRAS status) or **Zymomonas mobilis** (bacterium). - Carried out in **batch, fed-batch, or continuous stirred-tank reactor (CSTR)**. - Conditions strictly **anaerobic / micro-aerobic**, pH 4.5–5.0, T = 30 °C. ### Step 5 — Distillation & Dehydration - **Beer column** (first distillation) → ~95 % v/v ethanol azeotrope. - **Molecular sieve dehydration** → anhydrous fuel-grade ethanol (**>99.5 % v/v**). ### Step 6 — Effluent Management - Spent wash / vinasse / stillage is rich in N, P, K → used as **liquid bio-fertiliser** or sent to **anaerobic digester** for biogas co-generation. ## 2.2 The Biochemistry — Why and How It Works The Embden–Meyerhof–Parnas (EMP) glycolytic pathway inside yeast cells converts one mole of glucose into two moles of ethanol and two moles of CO₂, regenerating **2 moles of NAD⁺** and producing **2 moles of ATP**: $$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \;\xrightarrow{\text{yeast enzymes}}\; 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} \;+\; 2\,\text{CO}_2 \;+\; \Delta G \;(\text{exergonic}) $$ **Why anaerobic?** Under oxygen, yeast shifts to *aerobic respiration*, producing CO₂ + H₂O instead of ethanol (Pasteur effect). Anaerobic conditions force the cell to dump excess reducing equivalents (NADH) onto acetaldehyde, regenerating NAD⁺ — and ethanol is the by-product of that safety valve. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 2\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\text{CO}_2$ | Stoichiometric reaction | — | | 2 | $Y_{\text{theor}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{ethanol}} \times 2}{M_{\text{glucose}}} = \dfrac{2 \times 46}{180} = 0.5111$ | Theoretical mass yield | g EtOH / g glucose | | 3 | $\eta_{\text{process}} = \dfrac{Y_{\text{actual}}}{Y_{\text{theor}}} \times 100\,\%$ | Process efficiency | % | | 4 | $\text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{22}\text{O}_{11} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\,\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$ | Sucrose hydrolysis | — | | 5 | $\text{P}_{\text{EtOH}} = \text{V}_{\text{beer}} \times \rho_{\text{EtOH}} \times \text{ABV}\,\% $ | Ethanol production rate | L or kg/h | | 6 | $\text{Y}_{\text{p/s}} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta S}$ | Product yield coefficient (g EtOH / g substrate consumed) | g/g | | 7 | $\mu = \dfrac{\ln(N_t / N_0)}{t}$ | Specific growth rate of yeast | h⁻¹ | | 8 | $\text{DS} = \dfrac{\text{sugars released (g)}}{\text{biomass loaded (g)}} \times 100\,\%$ | Degree of saccharification | % | | 9 | $\text{Energy recovery} = \dfrac{\text{LHV}_{\text{EtOH}} \times m_{\text{EtOH}}}{\text{LHV}_{\text{waste}} \times m_{\text{waste}}} \times 100\,\%$ | Energy efficiency | % | | 10 | $\text{By-product:CO}_2 = 0.956 \times m_{\text{EtOH}}$ | Mass of CO₂ released per kg EtOH | kg CO₂ / kg EtOH | > [!NOTE] > In every KTU answer, examiners specifically look for the **0.511 constant**. Forgetting it is the most common mark-losing error in derivation problems. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Transportation sector** — E10, E20, E85 blending mandates under India's **Ethanol Blending Programme (EBP), 2025 target: 20 %**. - **Pharmaceutical industry** — Ethanol is the most widely used solvent for hand sanitisers, syrups, and tinctures. - **Power generation** — Ethanol can directly fuel **E85 flex-fuel gensets** or solid-oxide fuel cells. - **Chemical feedstock** — Ethylene, ethyl acetate, acetic acid. - **Waste management** — Converts *negative-value* organic waste (food waste, market waste) into *positive-value* liquid fuel, simultaneously solving sanitation problems. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Ethanol Yield from Glucose (KTU Hot Topic) **Given:** Hexose sugar fermentation reaction: $$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} \;+\; 2\,\text{CO}_2 $$ **Step 1 — Write down molecular masses.** - Glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) = (6×12) + (12×1) + (6×16) = **180 g/mol** - Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) = (2×12) + (6×1) + (1×16) = **46 g/mol** - CO₂ = 12 + (2×16) = **44 g/mol** **Step 2 — Mass of ethanol produced per mole of glucose.** Since 1 mole of glucose yields 2 moles of ethanol: $$ m_{\text{EtOH}} = 2 \times 46 \;=\; 92 \;\text{g} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the theoretical mass yield (Y_theor).** $$ Y_{\text{theor}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{EtOH}}}{m_{\text{glucose}}} = \dfrac{92}{180} = 0.5111 \;\frac{\text{g EtOH}}{\text{g glucose}} $$ **Step 4 — Convert to percentage.** $$ Y_{\text{theor}} = 0.5111 \times 100\,\% \;=\; \boxed{51.11\,\%} $$ **Step 5 — Compute theoretical CO₂ release per gram of glucose.** $$ m_{\text{CO}_2} = 2 \times 44 \;=\; 88 \;\text{g CO}_2 \text{ per 180 g glucose} $$ $$ \text{CO}_2 \text{ yield} = \dfrac{88}{180} = 0.489 \;\frac{\text{g CO}_2}{\text{g glucose}} $$ **Step 6 — Sanity check (mass conservation).** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Reactant mass} &= 180 \;\text{g (glucose)} \\ \text{Products mass} &= 92 + 88 = 180 \;\text{g} \;\;\checkmark \end{aligned} $$ The law of conservation of mass holds. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Actual Ethanol Yield from Molasses Waste **Given data:** - Molasses feed = **10,000 kg/day** with **50 % (w/w) total sugar** as sucrose. - Sucrose is hydrolysed by yeast invertase: 1 mole sucrose → 2 moles glucose + 1 mole fructose. - **Actual fermentation efficiency** = **90 %** of theoretical. **Step 1 — Total sucrose in feed.** $$ m_{\text{sucrose}} = 10{,}000 \times 0.50 = 5{,}000 \;\text{kg/day} $$ **Step 2 — Hydrolysis of sucrose to monosaccharides.** Hydrolysis reaction: $$ \text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{22}\text{O}_{11} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 $$ Molar masses: sucrose = **342 g/mol**, glucose = **180 g/mol**. Mass conversion factor: $$ \text{glucose from sucrose} = \dfrac{2 \times 180}{342} = 1.0526 \;\frac{\text{kg glucose}}{\text{kg sucrose}} $$ **Step 3 — Total fermentable glucose produced.** $$ m_{\text{glucose}} = 5{,}000 \times 1.0526 = 5{,}263 \;\text{kg/day} $$ **Step 4 — Theoretical ethanol mass.** $$ m_{\text{EtOH, theor}} = 5{,}263 \times 0.5111 = 2{,}690 \;\text{kg/day} $$ **Step 5 — Actual ethanol produced at 90 % efficiency.** $$ m_{\text{EtOH, actual}} = 2{,}690 \times 0.90 = 2{,}421 \;\text{kg/day} $$ **Step 6 — Volume of ethanol (for transport / blending calculations).** Using density of ethanol $\rho_{\text{EtOH}} = 0.789 \;\text{kg/L}$: $$ V_{\text{EtOH}} = \dfrac{2{,}421}{0.789} = \boxed{3{,}068 \;\text{L/day}} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy Recovery Efficiency **Lower Heating Values (LHV):** - LHV of ethanol = **29.7 MJ/kg** - LHV of dry molasses = **17 MJ/kg** - Molasses feed (dry basis) = 5,000 kg/day. **Step 1 — Total chemical energy input.** $$ E_{\text{in}} = 5{,}000 \times 17 = 85{,}000 \;\text{MJ/day} $$ **Step 2 — Total chemical energy output (as ethanol).** $$ E_{\text{out}} = 2{,}421 \times 29.7 = 71{,}903 \;\text{MJ/day} $$ **Step 3 — Energy recovery efficiency.** $$ \eta_{\text{energy}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{out}}}{E_{\text{in}}} \times 100\,\% = \dfrac{71{,}903}{85{,}000} \times 100\,\% $$ $$ \boxed{\eta_{\text{energy}} = 84.6\,\%} $$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Three Waste-to-Ethanol Pathways | Parameter | Sugar Waste (Molasses / Fruit) | Starch Waste (Cassava / Food Waste) | Lignocellulosic Waste (Bagasse / Straw) | |---|---|---|---| | **Pre-treatment required?** | No (direct) | Yes — gelatinisation + liquefaction | Yes — harsh (steam explosion / acid) | | **Hydrolysis needed?** | No | Yes — enzymatic (amylase) | Yes — enzymatic (cellulase) | | **Microbe** | *S. cerevisiae* | *S. cerevisiae* | *S. cerevisiae* or recombinant *Z. mobilis* | | **Fermentation time** | **24–48 h** | **48–72 h** | **72–120 h** | | **Theoretical yield** | **0.511 g/g** | **0.566 g/g starch** (≈ 1.11 × 0.511) | **0.511 g/g glucose** (after hydrolysis) | | **Practical yield (% theor)** | **90–95 %** | **85–92 %** | **70–85 %** | | **Capital cost** | **Low** | Medium | **High** | | **Status** | Commercial (India, Brazil) | Pilot / Demo | Research / First-of-a-kind | | **By-product** | Vinasse | Protein-rich DDGS | Lignin residue | | **KTU examiner weightage** | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Degree of Saccharification (Lignocellulose) A pre-treated rice-straw slurry containing **100 g dry biomass** is enzymatically hydrolysed and yields **68 g of glucose** in 72 h. Calculate **degree of saccharification (DS)** and **overall ethanol yield**. **Step 1 — Cellulose content of rice straw (assumed).** Cellulose ≈ **35 %** of dry rice straw. $$ m_{\text{cellulose}} = 100 \times 0.35 = 35 \;\text{g} $$ **Step 2 — Theoretical glucose if 100 % cellulose converted.** $$ m_{\text{glucose, theor}} = 35 \times \dfrac{180}{162} = 35 \times 1.111 = 38.89 \;\text{g} $$ (Factor 180/162 accounts for one water molecule added per glycosidic bond broken.) **Step 3 — Degree of saccharification.** $$ \text{DS} = \dfrac{68}{38.89} \times 100\,\% = 174.8\,\% $$ > [!WARNING] > A value >100 % indicates **hemicellulose xylan and arabinan are *also* being hydrolysed**, which is normal. Recompute using *total* polysaccharide content (cellulose + hemicellulose ≈ 60 %), not cellulose alone. **Step 3 (corrected)** — Total polysaccharide = 60 g. $$ \text{DS}_{\text{corrected}} = \dfrac{68}{60 \times 1.111} \times 100\,\% = \dfrac{68}{66.67} \times 100\,\% = 102\,\% \;\;\text{(acceptable within experimental error)} $$ **Step 4 — Ethanol mass (assuming 90 % fermentation efficiency).** $$ m_{\text{EtOH}} = 68 \times 0.511 \times 0.90 = 31.3 \;\text{g EtOH per 100 g dry rice straw} $$ # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Ethanol from Sugar-Rich Waste (KTU Most-Favoured Question) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Waste Feedstock
Molasses or Fruit Pulp] --> B[Screening and
Dilution to 15-20 Brix] B --> C[pH Adjustment
to 4.5-5.0 with H2SO4] C --> D[Nutrient Addition
Urea and DAP] D --> E[Heat Sterilisation
90 degree C for 30 min] E --> F[Cooling to 30 degree C] F --> G[Inoculation with
Saccharomyces cerevisiae
5 percent v/v] G --> H[Fermentation Reactor
Anaerobic CSTR
30 degree C, 48-72 h] H --> I[Beer Output
8-12 percent v/v ethanol] I --> J[Centrifugation
Yeast Recovery] J --> K[Distillation Column 1
Beer Column
95 percent v/v ethanol] K --> L[Distillation Column 2
Rectifying Column
96 percent v/v ethanol] L --> M[Molecular Sieve
Dehydration
99.5 percent v/v Fuel Grade] M --> N[Anhydrous Ethanol
Storage Tank] H --> O[CO2 Release
Captured for Carbonation] J --> P[Spent Wash
Vinasse to Biogas Plant
or Liquid Fertiliser] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Lignocellulosic Waste-to-Ethanol ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FEED[Feedstock Preparation Block] A1[Lignocellulosic Waste
Bagasse or Straw] --> A2[Size Reduction
Hammer Mill 2-5 mm] A2 --> A3[Drying to 10 percent moisture] end subgraph PRE[Pre-treatment Block] A3 --> B1[Steam Explosion
180-220 degree C, 15-30 bar] B1 --> B2[Neutralisation with Lime] B2 --> B3[Washing and Solid Liquid Separation] end subgraph SACCH[Saccharification Block] B3 --> C1[Enzymatic Hydrolysis
Cellulase 15 FPU per g] C1 --> C2[Fermentable Sugar Broth
Glucose and Xylose] end subgraph FER[Fermentation Block] C2 --> D1[SSF or SHF Reactor
30 degree C, pH 5.0] D1 --> D2[CO2 Vent] D1 --> D3[Beer 8 percent ethanol] end subgraph DOWN[Downstream Block] D3 --> E1[Beer Stripping] E1 --> E2[Rectification to 95 percent] E2 --> E3[Molecular Sieve to 99.8 percent] E3 --> E4[Anhydrous Bio Ethanol] end subgraph UTIL[Co-product Block] B3 --> F1[Lignin Residue] F1 --> F2[Boiler Combustion
Process Steam] E1 --> F3[Stillage or Vinasse] F3 --> F4[Animal Feed or Biogas] end ``` ## 4.3 Microbial Metabolism Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Glucose enters Yeast Cell
via GLUT transporter] --> S2[Glycolysis
EMP Pathway] S2 --> S3[Pyruvate
2 molecules per glucose] S3 --> S4[Pyruvate Decarboxylase
removes CO2] S4 --> S5[Acetaldehyde] S5 --> S6[Alcohol Dehydrogenase
NADH donates electrons] S6 --> S7[Ethanol produced
NAD+ regenerated] S7 --> S8[Ethanol diffuses out
of cell into broth] ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define *alcoholic fermentation*. Write the balanced biochemical reaction and state the role of the enzyme *pyruvate decarboxylase*. **Model Answer (3 marks):** Alcoholic fermentation is the **anaerobic microbial conversion of sugars into ethanol and CO₂** catalysed primarily by the yeast *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*. Balanced reaction: $$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \;\xrightarrow{\text{yeast}}\; 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} \;+\; 2\,\text{CO}_2 \;+\; 2\,\text{ATP} $$ *Pyruvate decarboxylase* (with cofactor **thiamine pyrophosphate, TPP**) catalyses the **irreversible decarboxylation of pyruvate to acetaldehyde**, releasing CO₂ and forming a hydroxyethyl-TPP intermediate. **[3 marks]** --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** List any **three lignocellulosic waste feedstocks** suitable for bio-ethanol production and state the **single most critical pre-treatment step** required. **Model Answer:** 1. Sugarcane bagasse 2. Rice straw 3. Corn stover / sawdust **Most critical pre-treatment step:** *Disruption of the lignin seal and decrystallisation of cellulose* — typically achieved via **steam explosion at 180–220 °C** or **dilute acid hydrolysis**, without which cellulase enzymes cannot access the polysaccharide chains. **[3 marks]** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** With a neat flowchart, describe the **process of bio-ethanol production from sugarcane molasses**. Clearly identify the role of the *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* yeast. **[7 marks]** **(b)** A sugar mill generates **8,000 kg/day** of molasses with **48 % (w/w) total sugars as sucrose**. The plant achieves **88 % fermentation efficiency**. Determine: (i) The mass of ethanol produced per day, and (ii) The volume of anhydrous ethanol produced (density = 0.789 kg/L). **[7 marks]** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Flowchart description (7 marks):** 1. **Feedstock preparation** — Molasses is diluted with water to **15–20 °Brix** (15–20 % sugar w/w) and pH adjusted to **4.5–5.0** using dilute H₂SO₄. Nutrients (urea, di-ammonium phosphate) added. (1 mark) 2. **Sterilisation** — Heat to **90 °C for 30 minutes** to kill wild contaminants. (0.5 mark) 3. **Cooling and inoculation** — Cooled to **30 °C**; active dry yeast *S. cerevisiae* (5 % v/v) added. *Role of yeast:* it secretes **invertase** (hydrolyses sucrose to glucose + fructose) and ferments monosaccharides via **EMP pathway** to ethanol + CO₂ under anaerobic conditions. (1.5 marks) 4. **Fermentation** — Anaerobic batch fermentation in closed CSTR for **48–72 h**, temperature controlled at 30 °C. CO₂ vented and captured. (1 mark) 5. **Post-fermentation** — Centrifugation to recover yeast (recycled). "Beer" now contains 8–12 % ethanol. (0.5 mark) 6. **Distillation** — Two-column distillation: beer column → 95 % azeotrope; rectifying column → 96 %; molecular sieve → 99.5 %+ anhydrous fuel-grade ethanol. (1.5 marks) 7. **By-product** — Spent wash (vinasse) → biofertiliser or anaerobic digester for biogas. (1 mark) **Part (b) — Numerical solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Mass of sucrose in molasses:** $$ m_{\text{sucrose}} = 8{,}000 \times 0.48 = 3{,}840 \;\text{kg/day} \quad (1 \text{ mark}) $$ **Step 2 — Mass of glucose equivalent after sucrose hydrolysis:** Hydrolysis: C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁ + H₂O → 2 C₆H₁₂O₆ $$ m_{\text{glucose}} = 3{,}840 \times \dfrac{2 \times 180}{342} = 3{,}840 \times 1.0526 = 4{,}042 \;\text{kg/day} \quad (2 \text{ marks}) $$ **Step 3 — Theoretical ethanol mass:** $$ m_{\text{EtOH, theor}} = 4{,}042 \times 0.5111 = 2{,}066 \;\text{kg/day} \quad (2 \text{ marks}) $$ **Step 4 — Actual ethanol at 88 % efficiency:** $$ m_{\text{EtOH, actual}} = 2{,}066 \times 0.88 = \boxed{1{,}818 \;\text{kg/day}} \quad (1 \text{ mark}) $$ **Step 5 — Volume of anhydrous ethanol:** $$ V_{\text{EtOH}} = \dfrac{1{,}818}{0.789} = \boxed{2{,}304 \;\text{L/day}} \quad (1 \text{ mark}) $$ --- ### Question B — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Compare the **three major feedstock categories** (sugar, starch, lignocellulose) for waste-to-ethanol conversion in terms of (i) pre-treatment requirement, (ii) yield, (iii) cost. **[7 marks]** **(b)** Explain the **biochemistry of the Embden–Meyerhof–Parnas (EMP) pathway**, highlighting the role of **NAD⁺/NADH balance**, and derive the **theoretical ethanol yield from 1 kg of glucose**. **[7 marks]** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Comparison table (7 marks):** | Aspect | Sugar Waste | Starch Waste | Lignocellulosic Waste | |---|---|---|---| | (i) Pre-treatment | **Not needed** | Mild (gelatinisation at 70–90 °C) | Severe (steam explosion 180–220 °C) | | (ii) Yield | Highest — **90–95 %** of theoretical | High — **85–92 %** | Lower — **70–85 %** due to recalcitrance | | (iii) Cost | Lowest (capex) | Medium (enzymes cost) | Highest (capex + enzymes + pre-treatment) | | Examples | Molasses, fruit pulp | Cassava peel, food waste | Bagasse, rice straw | | **Score** | 1+1+1 = **3** marks | 1+1+1 = **3** marks | 1 mark | (Note: distribute marks evenly — any equivalent tabular comparison scoring 7 marks is acceptable.) **Part (b) — EMP pathway and derivation (7 marks):** **EMP Pathway (3 marks):** | Step | Enzyme | Product | |---|---|---| | 1. Glucose → Glucose-6-P | Hexokinase | Uses 1 ATP | | 2. G6P → Fructose-6-P | Phosphoglucose isomerase | — | | 3. F6P → F1,6BP | Phosphofructokinase | Uses 1 ATP | | 4. F1,6BP → G3P + DHAP | Aldolase | Splits 6-C into two 3-C | | 5. G3P → 1,3-BPG | Glyceraldehyde-3-P dehydrogenase | **Reduces NAD⁺ to NADH**, produces 1 ATP later per G3P | | 6. 1,3-BPG → 3-PG → 2-PG → PEP | Multiple | — | | 7. PEP → Pyruvate | Pyruvate kinase | Produces 1 ATP per pyruvate | | 8. Pyruvate → Acetaldehyde + CO₂ | Pyruvate decarboxylase | — | | 9. Acetaldehyde → Ethanol | Alcohol dehydrogenase | **Re-oxidises NADH to NAD⁺** | **NAD⁺/NADH balance (1.5 marks):** Step 5 consumes NAD⁺ (×2 per glucose). Step 9 regenerates NAD⁺ (×2 per glucose). Net balance = **zero**, allowing glycolysis to continue under anaerobic conditions. Without this regeneration, glycolysis would stall. **Derivation (2.5 marks):** $$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2 $$ $$ Y = \dfrac{2 \times 46}{180} = \dfrac{92}{180} = \boxed{0.5111 \;\text{g EtOH / g glucose} = 51.11\,\%} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Forgetting the water molecule in sucrose hydrolysis.** Sucrose (342 g) yields 2 × glucose (360 g). Students often write 2 × 180 = 360 g sucrose and forget to divide by 342. This single error loses **2 marks** in part (b). > 2. **Confusing ethanol density with water density.** Always use **ρ = 0.789 kg/L**, not 1 kg/L, when converting mass to volume of ethanol. > 3. **Not mentioning the role of NAD⁺/NADH** in biochemistry questions. KTU key specifically awards **1.5 marks** for the redox balance. > 4. **Skipping the second-stage distillation** in process-flowchart questions. Beer column + rectifier + molecular sieve = **3 separate units**; bundling them loses marks. > 5. **Lignocellulose answers missing "lignin removal"** — examiners specifically ask *why* pre-treatment is needed, and "lignin seal" must appear in the answer. ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist - [x] Fermentation = **anaerobic microbial conversion** of sugars → ethanol + CO₂. - [x] Master organism: ***Saccharomyces cerevisiae*** (yeast, GRAS). - [x] Master reaction: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2$. - [x] Master constant: **Theoretical yield = 0.511 g EtOH / g glucose (51.11 %)**. - [x] Industrial practical yield = **90–95 %** of theoretical for sugar waste; **70–85 %** for lignocellulose. - [x] Optimum **pH = 4.5–5.0**; optimum **temperature = 30 °C**. - [x] Process chain (sugar route): **Feedstock → Dilution → Sterilisation → Inoculation → Fermentation → Centrifugation → Distillation → Dehydration → Fuel-grade EtOH**. - [x] Process chain (lignocellulosic route): **Feedstock → Size reduction → Pre-treatment (steam explosion) → Enzymatic hydrolysis → Fermentation → Distillation → Dehydration**. - [x] Lignocellulose is the **future** — abundant but recalcitrant; pre-treatment is the bottleneck. - [x] By-products: **CO₂** (captured), **vinasse/stillage** (fertiliser/biogas), **DDGS** (animal feed), **lignin** (boiler fuel). - [x] Indian policy hook: **EBP 2025 — 20 % ethanol blending** in petrol. - [x] Energy efficiency of waste-to-ethanol ≈ **80–90 %** on an LHV basis. - [x] Co-generation synergy: **Vinasse → biogas → electricity**; **Lignin → boiler → process steam** → improves overall plant economics. - [x] Always mention **NAD⁺/NADH regeneration** in EMP pathway answers. - [x] Always convert mass to volume using **ρ_EtOH = 0.789 kg/L**.

topics.title: Composting and Other Biological Treatments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT836 - WASTE TO ENERGY TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Composting and Other Biological Treatments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🌱 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Composting — The Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Standard):**
> *Composting is the controlled biological decomposition and stabilization of organic solid waste under aerobic (oxygen-rich) conditions by a mixed microbial population (bacteria, fungi, actinomycetes), producing a humus-like end product rich in humic substances, suitable as a soil conditioner and slow-release nutrient source.*

In simple words — composting is **engineered forest-floor decay**. Nature would take 2 to 5 years to break a pile of leaves into dark soil; composting replicates and accelerates that natural decay to **21 to 60 days** by giving microbes the perfect environment.

## 1.2 The Other "Biological Treatments" Family

Biological treatment is an umbrella term covering every process that uses **microorganisms (bacteria, fungi, worms, enzymes)** to convert waste into useful products:

| Biological Treatment | Oxygen Status | End Product |
|---|---|---|
| **Composting** | Aerobic | Humus / Soil conditioner |
| **Anaerobic Digestion (AD)** | Strictly Anaerobic | Biogas (CH₄ + CO₂) + Digestate |
| **Vermicomposting** | Aerobic (mesophilic) | Vermicast (worm castings) |
| **Biodrying** | Aerobic (controlled) | Dried RDF (Refuse Derived Fuel) |
| **Fermentation** | Anaerobic (controlled) | Ethanol / Organic acids |

## 1.3 The Kitchen-Bin Analogy (Intuition Builder)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Your Kitchen Compost Bin:**
> Imagine you keep a small box of vegetable peels under your sink. Within a week, it gets warm (microbes working), shrinks in volume, and turns into dark earthy material. **That is composting in miniature.** Nature's cleanup crew (bacteria, fungi) eats the "lunch" (organic carbon) and exhales CO₂ + heat, leaving behind a stable, nutrient-rich "dessert" (humus).
> - **Too wet → smells like rotten egg** (anaerobic, sour)
> - **Too dry → microbes go dormant** (nothing happens)
> - **Just right → earthy forest smell** (composting success!)

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics (Must-Memorize)

- **C : N Ratio (Carbon : Nitrogen)** = **25 : 1 to 30 : 1** (ideal)
- **Moisture Content** = **50 % to 60 %** (by weight)
- **pH** = **6.5 to 8.0**
- **Free Airspace (FAS)** = **35 % to 45 %**
- **C : P Ratio** = **75 : 1 to 150 : 1**
- **Bulk Density** = **400 to 700 kg/m³**
- **Oxygen Concentration in Pile** = **> 5 % (preferably 10 – 18 %)**
- **Particle Size** = **< 50 mm** (optimum 25 – 40 mm)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Microbiology of Composting (Who's Doing the Work?)

The decomposition is performed by a **consortium of microbes** that succeed each other in a temperature-dependent relay:

**Step 1 — Mesophilic Bacteria (15 °C → 40 °C)**
- *Bacillus, Pseudomonas, Enterobacter*
- Attack simple sugars, starches, amino acids
- Generate heat as a metabolic by-product

**Step 2 — Thermophilic Bacteria & Actinomycetes (40 °C → 65 °C)**
- *Thermus, Streptomyces, Thermomonospora*
- Break down proteins, fats, cellulose, hemicellulose
- **This stage kills pathogens and weed seeds** (a key KTU point)

**Step 3 — Mesophilic Re-establishment (Cooling Phase) → 25 °C → 40 °C**
- Fungi return (*Aspergillus, Trichoderma, Penicillium*)
- Lignin degradation begins

**Step 4 — Curing & Maturation (Ambient Temperature)**
- Slow humification
- Formation of stable humic acids

## 2.2 The Three Temperature Phases (Critical for KTU)

The single most-tested concept in this module is the **temperature curve**:

$$\text{Phase 1: Mesophilic} \rightarrow \text{Phase 2: Thermophilic (peak)} \rightarrow \text{Phase 3: Cooling} \rightarrow \text{Phase 4: Curing}$$

The thermophilic phase (≥ 55 °C for **at least 3 days**) is the **sanitization window** — it must be retained to comply with sanitation regulations (USEPA / IS 16208).

## 2.3 Carbon-to-Nitrogen (C : N) Ratio — The Master Variable

Microbes need **25 – 30 parts carbon for every 1 part nitrogen** to build proteins and grow. If the ratio is off:

- **C : N > 35 : 1** → Microbes cannot grow fast enough → **slow composting**
- **C : N < 20 : 1** → Excess nitrogen → ammonia (NH₃) is released → **odor and nitrogen loss**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The C : N ratio of common feedstocks must be memorized:
> - **Sawdust = 500 : 1**, **Dry leaves = 60 : 1**, **Food waste = 15 : 1**, **Cow manure = 20 : 1**, **Sewage sludge = 8 : 1**

## 2.4 Anaerobic Digestion (AD) — Companion Biological Process

Where composting is aerobic, **anaerobic digestion** happens in the **complete absence of oxygen**, in a sealed reactor called a **digester**. The four-stage biochemical pathway is:

$$\text{Organics} \xrightarrow{\text{Hydrolysis}} \text{Monomers} \xrightarrow{\text{Acidogenesis}} \text{VFAs} \xrightarrow{\text{Acetogenesis}} \text{Acetate + H_2} \xrightarrow{\text{Methanogenesis}} \text{CH}_4 + \text{CO}_2$$

The output is **biogas** (typically **60 % CH₄, 40 % CO₂, trace H₂S**) and a nutrient-rich liquid called **digestate**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are tested in KTU numericals. Memorize the form, the variable meanings, and the units.

| # | Formula (LaTeX-safe) | Meaning / Variables | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C/N_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{\sum m_i \cdot (C/N)_i}{\sum m_i}$ | Weighted C : N of mixed feedstocks | ratio (dimensionless) |
| 2 | $M_{\text{add}} = M_{\text{wet}} \cdot \dfrac{M_{\text{target}} - M_{\text{current}}}{100 - M_{\text{target}}}$ | Mass of water to add for target moisture | kg |
| 3 | $C_{\text{decomp}}(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$ | First-order carbon decay | kg C / kg VS |
| 4 | $Y_{CH_4} = 0.40 \cdot \left( \dfrac{A}{2} + \dfrac{B}{8} - \dfrac{C}{4} - \dfrac{3D}{8} \right)$ | **Buswell Equation** — methane yield from CₐHᵦOᵧNᵟ | m³ CH₄ / kg VS |
| 5 | $V_{\text{biogas}} = Y_{CH_4} \cdot M_{VS} \cdot \eta_{\text{rec}}$ | Total biogas volume | m³ |
| 6 | $O_2^{\text{demand}} = 1.5 \cdot C_{\text{oxidized}}$ | Theoretical oxygen for aerobic oxidation | kg O₂ |
| 7 | $HRT = \dfrac{V_{\text{reactor}}}{Q_{\text{feed}}}$ | Hydraulic Retention Time | days |
| 8 | $OLR = \dfrac{Q_{\text{feed}} \cdot S_0}{V_{\text{reactor}}}$ | Organic Loading Rate | kg VS / m³ · d |
| 9 | $\eta_{\text{digestion}} = \dfrac{VS_{\text{in}} - VS_{\text{out}}}{VS_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | Volatile Solids reduction efficiency | % |
| 10 | $\rho_{\text{pile}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{pile}}}{V_{\text{pile}}}$ | Pile bulk density | kg/m³ |

**Notes for KTU exam:**
- In the Buswell equation, A, B, C, D are the subscripts of Carbon, Hydrogen, Oxygen, Nitrogen in the empirical waste formula CₐHᵦOᵧNᵟ
- The factor **0.40** is the theoretical CH₄ mass yield at STP per kg of VS
- $k$ is the first-order rate constant; typical values: 0.2 / d (food waste), 0.05 / d (manure)

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Solid Waste Management** — ISWA estimates composting diverts **30 – 60 %** of MSW from landfills
- **Carbon Sequestration** — 1 tonne compost ≈ **0.3 t CO₂-eq** avoided
- **Renewable Energy** — AD produces **0.3 – 0.5 m³ biogas / kg VS**
- **Agriculture** — replaces **100 % synthetic N-fertilizer** at typical application rates

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📐 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Weighted C : N Ratio of a Mixed Feedstock

Suppose a municipal composting facility receives **two feedstocks** with given masses and C : N values. KTU often asks the simple weighted formula; here is the full derivation.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of a ratio:**

$$C/N = \dfrac{m_C}{m_N}$$

**Step 2 — For each feedstock $i$, with mass $m_i$:**

$$(C/N)_i = \dfrac{m_{C,i}}{m_{N,i}}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply both sides by $m_i$ and sum over all feedstocks:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \cdot (C/N)_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \cdot \dfrac{m_{C,i}}{m_{N,i}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{m_i \cdot m_{C,i}}{m_{N,i}}$$

For a **two-component** mixture where $m_N$ is identical across feedstocks (an acceptable simplification for many KTU problems), the denominator simplifies to $m_{N,\text{total}}$:

$$C/N_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{\sum m_i \cdot (C/N)_i}{\sum m_i}$$

> **Why this works:** We are essentially taking a *mass-weighted average* of the individual C : N values. Just as we average the height of students in a class (weight = mass), we average the C : N of feedstocks (weight = their respective masses).

### Worked Numerical Example

A composting plant mixes **800 kg of dry leaves (C : N = 60 : 1)** with **400 kg of food waste (C : N = 15 : 1)**. Find the C : N of the mix.

**Solution:**

$$C/N_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{800 \times 60 + 400 \times 15}{800 + 400}$$

$$C/N_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{48{,}000 + 6{,}000}{1{,}200} = \dfrac{54{,}000}{1{,}200}$$

$$\boxed{C/N_{\text{mix}} = 45 : 1}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Since 45 : 1 > 30 : 1, the mix is **too carbon-rich**. We must add nitrogen (e.g., **urea**, food waste, or green manure) to bring it into the **25 : 1 – 30 : 1** window.

## 3.2 Derivation: Buswell Equation for Methane Yield

The Buswell equation is the **golden formula** for KTU anaerobic digestion problems. It gives the *theoretical* methane yield from any organic compound of empirical formula **CₐHᵦOᵧNᵈ**.

**Step 1 — Write the general stoichiometry:**

$$\text{C}_a\text{H}_b\text{O}_c\text{N}_d + \left(a - \dfrac{b}{4} - \dfrac{c}{2} + \dfrac{3d}{4}\right)\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \left(\dfrac{a}{2} + \dfrac{b}{8} - \dfrac{c}{4} - \dfrac{3d}{8}\right)\text{CH}_4 + \left(\dfrac{a}{2} - \dfrac{b}{8} + \dfrac{c}{4} + \dfrac{3d}{8}\right)\text{CO}_2 + d\,\text{NH}_3$$

**Step 2 — Identify the moles of CH₄ produced per mole of substrate:**

$$n_{CH_4} = \dfrac{a}{2} + \dfrac{b}{8} - \dfrac{c}{4} - \dfrac{3d}{8}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to mass-based yield using molecular weights:**

- Molar mass of CH₄ = **16 g/mol**
- Molar mass of substrate = $M = 12a + b + 16c + 14d$ g/mol

The methane mass yield (g CH₄ / g substrate):

$$Y_{CH_4}^{\text{mass}} = \dfrac{16 \cdot \left(\dfrac{a}{2} + \dfrac{b}{8} - \dfrac{c}{4} - \dfrac{3d}{8}\right)}{12a + b + 16c + 14d}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to STP volume (1 mol gas = 22.4 L at STP = 0.0224 m³):**

$$Y_{CH_4}^{\text{vol}} = \dfrac{0.0224 \cdot 16 \cdot \left(\dfrac{a}{2} + \dfrac{b}{8} - \dfrac{c}{4} - \dfrac{3d}{8}\right)}{12a + b + 16c + 14d}$$

**Step 5 — Standard KTU simplified form (VS-basis, coefficient 0.40):**

$$Y_{CH_4} = 0.40 \cdot \left(\dfrac{a}{2} + \dfrac{b}{8} - \dfrac{c}{4} - \dfrac{3d}{8}\right) \quad \text{m}^3\,\text{CH}_4/\text{kg VS}$$

### Worked Numerical Example (Buswell)

Glucose has empirical formula **C₆H₁₂O₆** (so $a = 6, b = 12, c = 6, d = 0$). Calculate the theoretical methane yield per kg of glucose at STP.

**Solution:**

$$\dfrac{a}{2} = \dfrac{6}{2} = 3.000$$

$$\dfrac{b}{8} = \dfrac{12}{8} = 1.500$$

$$\dfrac{c}{4} = \dfrac{6}{4} = 1.500$$

$$\dfrac{3d}{8} = 0$$

$$n_{CH_4} = 3.000 + 1.500 - 1.500 - 0 = 3.000 \;\text{mol CH}_4 \text{ per mol glucose}$$

Volume at STP:

$$V_{CH_4} = 3.000 \times 22.4 = 67.2 \;\text{L} = 0.0672 \;\text{m}^3$$

Per kg of glucose (M = 180 g/mol → 1000/180 = 5.556 mol/kg):

$$Y_{CH_4} = 5.556 \times 0.0672 = 0.3733 \;\text{m}^3\,\text{CH}_4/\text{kg}$$

$$\boxed{Y_{CH_4}^{\text{glucose}} \approx 0.373 \;\text{m}^3\,\text{CH}_4/\text{kg VS}}$$

> **Sanity Check:** This matches the textbook value of **0.373 m³ CH₄/kg glucose** at STP. ✓

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Biological Treatments

| Parameter | Composting | Anaerobic Digestion | Vermicomposting | Biodrying |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Oxygen** | Required (aerobic) | Strictly absent | Required (aerobic) | Required (limited) |
| **Temperature** | 55 – 65 °C | 35 – 55 °C (mesophilic) or 50 – 60 °C (thermophilic) | 20 – 30 °C | 45 – 60 °C |
| **Duration** | 21 – 60 days | 15 – 40 days (HRT) | 60 – 90 days | 7 – 21 days |
| **End Product** | Humus | Biogas + Digestate | Vermicast | Dried RDF |
| **Energy Output** | None directly | **High** (electricity/heat) | None | Calorific fuel |
| **C : N Requirement** | 25 : 1 – 30 : 1 | 20 : 1 – 30 : 1 | 20 : 1 – 25 : 1 | 25 : 1 – 40 : 1 |
| **Moisture Range** | 50 – 60 % | 80 – 95 % (wet) | 70 – 80 % | 40 – 55 % |
| **Pathogen Kill** | Excellent (thermophilic) | Good to Excellent | Limited | Excellent |
| **Capital Cost** | Low | High | Very Low | Medium |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Composting Process Flow

