topics.title: Chemical Reactions Involved
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT836 - WASTE TO ENERGY TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Chemical Reactions Involved

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Chemical Reactions in Waste-to-Energy (WtE)

In the context of **Waste to Energy Technology**, the term **"Chemical Reactions Involved"** refers to the complete set of thermochemical, biochemical, and physiochemical transformations that convert heterogeneous waste feedstocks (municipal solid waste, agricultural residues, industrial sludge, plastics, and biomass) into usable energy carriers such as **heat, syngas, bio-methane, bio-ethanol, biodiesel, and bio-char**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** This topic forms the *theoretical backbone* of all conversion routes studied in Module 4. The student is expected to identify, balance, and interpret reactions for **Combustion, Pyrolysis, Gasification, Anaerobic Digestion, Transesterification, and Fermentation**.

## 1.2 The Two Broad Families of Reactions

| **Family** | **Energy Input** | **Environment** | **Output** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Thermochemical** | High temperature (300 – 1200 °C) | Limited / no oxygen | Heat, Syngas, Bio-oil, Char |
| **Biochemical** | Ambient / mild temperature | Anaerobic / aqueous | Biogas, Bioethanol, Biodiesel |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Kitchen Fire to the Bioreactor"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:**
> Imagine a kitchen. When you **burn** dry wood completely in a fireplace, you get maximum heat but only ash and CO₂ — this is **Combustion**. If you bake the same wood in a sealed oven (no air), the wood chars, releases vapors that condense to a brown liquid (bio-oil), and leaves black char — this is **Pyrolysis**. If you supply just a *whiff* of air (less than stoichiometric) and steam to the oven, you get a combustible gas mixture (CO + H₂) — this is **Gasification**. Now place wet food waste in an airtight jar with microbes; weeks later it bubbles out methane — this is **Anaerobic Digestion**. The microbes act like a slow, cold "burner" doing the same chemistry nature would do in centuries.

## 1.4 Why Chemical Reactions Matter in WtE

- They govern the **energy yield (calorific value)** of the process.
- They determine the **composition of the product gas/liquid** (which decides downstream use).
- They control **emission formation** (NOx, SOx, dioxins, CO, CH₄ slip).
- They define the **operating envelope** (T, P, residence time, equivalence ratio).

## 1.5 Stoichiometric Basis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Ultimate (Elemental) Analysis** of waste is denoted as **CₓHᵧO_zNₐSᵦClᵧAsh**, expressed in mass % on a dry basis. Every chemical reaction is written **per kg of waste** or **per mole of carbon**, and the **stoichiometric air requirement** is derived from the elemental composition.

For a generic waste **CₐHᵦOᵧNᵟS_ε**, the complete combustion reaction is:

$$C_aH_bO_cN_dS_e + \left(a + \frac{b}{4} + e - \frac{c}{2}\right)O_2 \rightarrow a\,CO_2 + \frac{b}{2}\,H_2O + \frac{d}{2}\,N_2 + e\,SO_2$$

The minimum moles of O₂ required (i.e., **stoichiometric oxygen**) is the multiplier on O₂ in the above equation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Six Core Reaction Families Studied in Module 4

The KTU Module 4 syllabus groups the chemical reactions into six operational families. Each is a *gateway* to a specific reactor type and product.

### 2.1.1 Combustion Reactions (Oxidation)

Combustion is the **complete oxidation** of carbonaceous matter in **excess air** at **800 – 1200 °C**. The reactions are fast, highly exothermic, and produce **CO₂ + H₂O** as the major flue gas components.

**Primary reactions (C, H, S, N oxidation):**

$$C_{(s)} + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 \quad \Delta H = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O \quad \Delta H = -241.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2 \quad \Delta H = -296.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$N_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2NO \text{ (Thermal NOx, T > 1300 °C)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Combustion Exothermic?**
> The C=O and O=H bonds in CO₂ and H₂O are among the strongest in chemistry. Forming them *releases* the energy difference between the weak C–C, C–H, and O=O bonds in the fuel and the strong bonds in the products.

**The Combustion of a Generic Waste Molecule:**

$$C_xH_yO_zN_aS_b + \left(x + \frac{y}{4} + b - \frac{z}{2}\right)O_2 \rightarrow x\,CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,H_2O + \frac{a}{2}\,N_2 + b\,SO_2$$

### 2.1.2 Pyrolysis Reactions (Thermal Decomposition, No Oxygen)

Pyrolysis is the **thermal cracking** of biomass in the **complete absence of oxygen** (or with less than stoichiometric air) at **300 – 800 °C**. The three products — **char, bio-oil (tar), and non-condensable gases** — come from three competing reaction paths:

| **Reaction Type** | **Temperature Range** | **Dominant Product** |
|---|---|---|
| **Slow Pyrolysis** | 300 – 500 °C, long residence | Char (≈35 %) |
| **Fast Pyrolysis** | 500 – 800 °C, short residence | Bio-oil (≈75 %) |
| **Gasification Pyrolysis** | > 700 °C, very long residence | Gas (≈80 %) |

**The Generalized Pyrolysis Stoichiometry:**

$$Biomass \xrightarrow{\Delta,\;no\,O_2} Char_{(C)} + Tar_{(C_xH_yO_z)} + Gas_{(CO,\,CO_2,\,CH_4,\,H_2)}$$

**Component-wise Pyrolysis (Crucial for KTU 14-mark answers):**

$$Cellulose \xrightarrow{300-375\,°C} Volatiles + Char$$

$$Hemicellulose \xrightarrow{220-315\,°C} Volatiles + Char \text{ (more reactive)}$$

$$Lignin \xrightarrow{160-900\,°C} Char + \text{phenols, aromatic tars}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Lignin yields the **maximum char (≈ 40 %)** because of its aromatic, highly cross-linked structure. Cellulose gives the **maximum bio-oil**.

### 2.1.3 Gasification Reactions (Partial Oxidation + Steam Reforming)

Gasification uses **20 – 40 % of the stoichiometric air** (i.e., Equivalence Ratio **ER = 0.2 – 0.4**) at **700 – 1200 °C** to produce a combustible gas called **Producer Gas / Syngas** (CO + H₂ + CH₄ + CO₂ + N₂).

**The Seven Cardinal Gasification Reactions (HIGH-YIELD for KTU):**

| **#** | **Reaction Name** | **Equation** | **ΔH (kJ/mol)** | **Type** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Partial Oxidation (POX) | C + ½ O₂ → CO | **−111** | Exothermic |
| 2 | Complete Combustion | C + O₂ → CO₂ | −393.5 | Exothermic |
| 3 | Boudouard Reaction | C + CO₂ ⇌ 2CO | **+172** | Endothermic |
| 4 | Water-Gas Reaction | C + H₂O ⇌ CO + H₂ | **+131** | Endothermic |
| 5 | Water-Gas Shift (WGS) | CO + H₂O ⇌ CO₂ + H₂ | **−41** | Exothermic |
| 6 | Methanation | C + 2H₂ → CH₄ | −75 | Exothermic |
| 7 | Steam Reforming | CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3H₂ | +206 | Endothermic |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three endothermic reactions (Boudouard, Water-Gas, Steam Reforming)?**
> These reactions **consume heat** to break strong bonds. They are responsible for **converting solid carbon into gaseous products** — without them, gasification would just be smoldering combustion. The heat for these endothermic reactions is supplied by exothermic POX and Combustion.

**Overall Gasification of Carbon (mass balance check):**

$$2C + O_2 + H_2O \rightarrow 2CO + H_2$$

### 2.1.4 Anaerobic Digestion (AD) — Four-Stage Biochemistry

AD converts **wet organic waste** (sewage sludge, food waste, animal manure) into **biogas (≈ 60 % CH₄ + 40 % CO₂)** using a consortium of microbes at **mesophilic (35 – 40 °C)** or **thermophilic (50 – 55 °C)** conditions in the **complete absence of oxygen**.

**Stage 1 — Hydrolysis:**

Complex polymers are broken into monomers by extracellular enzymes:

$$\left(C_6H_{10}O_5\right)_n + n\,H_2O \xrightarrow{Enzymes} n\,C_6H_{12}O_6$$

**Stage 2 — Acidogenesis (Fermentation):**

Monomers (sugars, amino acids) are converted to volatile fatty acids (VFAs), alcohols, H₂, and CO₂:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2\,CH_3CH_2OH + 2\,CO_2$$

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3\,CH_3COOH \quad (Acetic\ Acid)$$

**Stage 3 — Acetogenesis:**

VFAs and alcohols are oxidized to acetate, H₂, and CO₂:

$$CH_3CH_2OH + H_2O \rightarrow CH_3COOH + 2\,H_2$$

$$CH_3CH_2COOH + 2\,H_2O \rightarrow CH_3COOH + CO_2 + 3\,H_2$$

**Stage 4 — Methanogenesis (Two Pathways):**

The terminal step where methane is produced by *Methanogenic archaea*:

**Pathway A — Acetoclastic (≈ 70 % of CH₄):**

$$CH_3COOH \rightarrow CH_4 + CO_2$$

**Pathway B — Hydrogenotrophic (≈ 30 % of CH₄):**

$$CO_2 + 4\,H_2 \rightarrow CH_4 + 2\,H_2O$$

**The Buswell Equation (Overall AD stoichiometry for CₓHᵧO_zNₐSᵦ):**

$$C_xH_yO_zN_aS_b + \left(x - \frac{y}{4} - \frac{z}{2} + \frac{3a}{4} + \frac{b}{2}\right)H_2O \rightarrow \left(\frac{x}{2} - \frac{y}{8} + \frac{z}{4} - \frac{3a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}\right)CH_4 + \left(\frac{x}{2} + \frac{y}{8} - \frac{z}{4} + \frac{3a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\right)CO_2 + a\,NH_3 + b\,H_2S$$

For pure glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆, x = 6, y = 12, z = 6, a = b = 0):

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3\,CH_4 + 3\,CO_2$$

This is the **most-tested equation in KTU for AD questions**.

### 2.1.5 Transesterification (Biodiesel Synthesis)

Biodiesel is produced by reacting **triglycerides** (from waste cooking oil, oil cakes, algae) with a **short-chain alcohol** (methanol or ethanol) in the presence of an **alkali catalyst** (NaOH/KOH) or **acid catalyst** (H₂SO₄).

**The General Transesterification Reaction:**

$$Triglyceride + 3\,R\text{-}OH \xrightleftharpoons{\text{Catalyst}} 3\,R'\text{-}COOR + Glycerol$$

Where R is the alkyl group of the alcohol and R' is the fatty acid chain.

**The Specific Reaction (Waste Cooking Oil → Biodiesel):**

$$C_{57}H_{104}O_6 + 3\,CH_3OH \xrightarrow{NaOH,\;60\,°C} 3\,C_{19}H_{36}O_2 + C_3H_8O_3$$

Triglyceride + Methanol → Fatty Acid Methyl Ester (FAME / Biodiesel) + Glycerol

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The 3:1 molar ratio of alcohol to oil is the **stoichiometric ratio**. In practice, a **6:1 to 12:1 ratio** is used to drive the equilibrium *forward* (Le Chatelier's Principle) and complete the reaction in finite time.

### 2.1.6 Fermentation (Bioethanol Production)

Bioethanol is produced by **enzymatic hydrolysis** of cellulose/hemicellulose to sugars, followed by **yeast fermentation**.

**Step 1 — Hydrolysis:**

$$\left(C_6H_{10}O_5\right)_n + n\,H_2O \xrightarrow{Cellulase} n\,C_6H_{12}O_6$$

**Step 2 — Fermentation by *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*:**

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{Zymase} 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2 \quad \Delta H = -92 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Theoretical yield** of ethanol from glucose = 0.51 g ethanol / g glucose (from stoichiometry: 2 × 46 / 180 = 0.511).
> **Practical yield** in industry ≈ 0.45 – 0.48 g/g due to microbial growth and by-product formation.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU question on this topic.**

| **Quantity** | **Formula / Equation** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Stoichiometric O₂ for CₐHᵦOᵧ | $n_{O_2} = a + \frac{b}{4} + e - \frac{c}{2}$ | mol O₂ per mol fuel |
| Stoichiometric air | $n_{air} = \frac{n_{O_2}}{0.21}$ | mol air per mol fuel |
| Air-Fuel Ratio (mass) | $AFR = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}}$ | kg/kg |
| Equivalence Ratio (ER) | $ER = \frac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$ | ER < 1 → lean, ER > 1 → rich |
| Calorific Value (HHV, dry) | $HHV = 338C + 1442H - 180O$ (Dulong) | kJ/kg, C,H,O in % mass |
| Gasification efficiency | $\eta = \frac{LHV_{syngas} \times \dot{V}_{syngas}}{LHV_{biomass} \times \dot{m}_{biomass}}$ | dimensionless, 0 – 1 |
| Methane yield (Buswell, glucose) | $Y_{CH_4} = 0.373 \text{ Nm}^3/kg\ glucose$ | at STP |
| Ethanol yield (theoretical) | $Y_{EtOH} = 0.511 \text{ kg/kg\ glucose}$ | from stoichiometry |
| Biodiesel yield (mass) | $Y_{FAME} = \frac{M_{FAME}}{M_{oil}} \times 3$ | mass fraction ≈ 1.0 |
| Activation Energy (Arrhenius) | $k = A\,e^{-E_a/RT}$ | k in s⁻¹, Eₐ in J/mol |

## 2.3 Engineering Utility Summary

| **Reaction Family** | **Engineering Output** | **Real-World Use** |
|---|---|---|
| Combustion | Steam → Electricity | MSW incinerators (Kerala – Brahmapuram, Delhi – Okhla) |
| Pyrolysis | Bio-char, Bio-oil | Soil amendment, liquid fuel |
| Gasification | Syngas (CO + H₂) | Gas engines, Fischer-Tropsch diesel |
| Anaerobic Digestion | Biogas (CH₄) | Cooking, CNG, electricity |
| Transesterification | Biodiesel (FAME) | Diesel engine blend (B20, B100) |
| Fermentation | Bioethanol | Petrol blend (E5, E10, E85) |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Air for a Typical MSW Composition

**Problem Statement (KTU Standard):**
A municipal solid waste has the following ultimate analysis (by mass, dry basis): C = 40 %, H = 6 %, O = 30 %, N = 1 %, S = 1 %, Ash = 22 %, Moisture = 10 %. Calculate:
(a) the stoichiometric oxygen required per kg of waste (as-received)
(b) the stoichiometric air required per kg of waste (as-received)
(c) the theoretical CO₂ flue gas volume per kg of waste at STP

### Step 1 — Convert Mass % to Mass per kg of dry waste

Take **1 kg of dry waste** (we'll add moisture later). The mass of each element is:

$$
m_C = 0.40 \text{ kg}, \quad m_H = 0.06 \text{ kg}, \quad m_O = 0.30 \text{ kg}, \quad m_N = 0.01 \text{ kg}, \quad m_S = 0.01 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 2 — Convert Mass to Moles

Use atomic masses: C = 12, H = 1, O = 16, N = 14, S = 32.

$$
n_C = \frac{0.40}{12} = 0.03333 \text{ kmol}
$$

$$
n_H = \frac{0.06}{1} = 0.060 \text{ kmol}
$$

$$
n_O = \frac{0.30}{16} = 0.01875 \text{ kmol}
$$

$$
n_N = \frac{0.01}{14} = 0.000714 \text{ kmol}
$$

$$
n_S = \frac{0.01}{32} = 0.000313 \text{ kmol}
$$

### Step 3 — Write the Generalized Combustion Equation

For the formula CₐHᵦOᵧNᵟS_ε (per kmol of fuel), with a = 0.0333, b = 0.060, c = 0.01875, d = 0.000714, e = 0.000313:

$$
C_aH_bO_cN_dS_e + \left(a + \frac{b}{4} + e - \frac{c}{2}\right)O_2 \rightarrow a\,CO_2 + \frac{b}{2}\,H_2O + \frac{d}{2}\,N_2 + e\,SO_2
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Stoichiometric O₂ Coefficient

$$
n_{O_2,\,stoich} = a + \frac{b}{4} + e - \frac{c}{2}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
n_{O_2,\,stoich} = 0.03333 + \frac{0.060}{4} + 0.000313 - \frac{0.01875}{2}
$$

$$
n_{O_2,\,stoich} = 0.03333 + 0.01500 + 0.000313 - 0.009375
$$

$$
n_{O_2,\,stoich} = 0.03927 \text{ kmol O}_2 \text{ per kg dry waste}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Mass of O₂

$$
m_{O_2} = n_{O_2} \times 32 = 0.03927 \times 32 = 1.2566 \text{ kg O}_2 \text{ per kg dry waste}
$$

### Step 6 — Account for Moisture (As-Received Basis)

Per kg of as-received waste, dry waste = (1 − moisture) = 0.90 kg.

$$
m_{O_2,\,as\text{-}received} = 1.2566 \times 0.90 = 1.131 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{kg wet waste}
$$

### Step 7 — Stoichiometric Air

Air contains 21 % O₂ and 79 % N₂ by volume; mass-wise, O₂ is 23.2 % of air. Using molar basis:

$$
n_{air,\,stoich} = \frac{n_{O_2}}{0.21} = \frac{0.03927}{0.21} = 0.1870 \text{ kmol air/kg dry}
$$

In mass terms:

$$
m_{air} = n_{O_2} \times \frac{100}{23.2} = 1.2566 \times \frac{100}{23.2} = 5.416 \text{ kg air/kg dry waste}
$$

For as-received:

$$
m_{air,\,wet} = 5.416 \times 0.90 = 4.874 \text{ kg air/kg wet waste}
$$

### Step 8 — CO₂ Volume per kg Waste (STP, 22.4 Nm³/kmol)

$$
V_{CO_2} = n_C \times 22.4 = 0.03333 \times 22.4 = 0.7467 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{kg dry}
$$

For wet waste:

$$
V_{CO_2,\,wet} = 0.7467 \times 0.90 = 0.672 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{kg wet}
$$

### Final Answer Summary

| **Quantity** | **Value (per kg wet waste)** |
|---|---|
| Stoichiometric O₂ | **1.131 kg** |
| Stoichiometric Air | **4.874 kg** |
| CO₂ Flue Gas | **0.672 Nm³** |

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Methane Yield from Glucose using Buswell Equation

**Problem Statement:**
Calculate the volume of methane (at STP) and CO₂ produced from the complete anaerobic digestion of **1 kg of glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆)**.

### Step 1 — Apply the Buswell Equation

For C₆H₁₂O₆ (x=6, y=12, z=6, a=b=0):

$$
C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3\,CH_4 + 3\,CO_2
$$

This can be verified by mass balance: C: 6 = 3 + 3 ✓, H: 12 = 12 (from 3 CH₄) ✓, O: 6 = 6 (from 3 CO₂) ✓.

### Step 2 — Moles of Glucose in 1 kg

$$
n_{glucose} = \frac{1000}{180} = 5.556 \text{ mol} = 0.005556 \text{ kmol}
$$

### Step 3 — Moles of CH₄ and CO₂ Produced

$$
n_{CH_4} = 3 \times 0.005556 = 0.01667 \text{ kmol}
$$

$$
n_{CO_2} = 3 \times 0.005556 = 0.01667 \text{ kmol}
$$

### Step 4 — Volume at STP (22.414 m³/kmol)

$$
V_{CH_4} = 0.01667 \times 22.414 = 0.3736 \text{ Nm}^3
$$

$$
V_{CO_2} = 0.01667 \times 22.414 = 0.3736 \text{ Nm}^3
$$

### Step 5 — Energy Content of Biogas

Assuming LHV of CH₄ = 35.8 MJ/Nm³:

$$
E_{biogas} = 0.3736 \times 35.8 = 13.37 \text{ MJ/kg glucose}
$$

### Final Answer Summary

| **Product** | **Moles** | **Volume at STP** |
|---|---|---|
| CH₄ | 0.01667 kmol | **0.3736 Nm³** |
| CO₂ | 0.01667 kmol | **0.3736 Nm³** |
| **Total Biogas** | 0.03333 kmol | **0.7472 Nm³** |
| **Energy Yield** | — | **13.37 MJ** |

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Biodiesel Yield from Waste Cooking Oil

**Problem Statement:**
1 kg of waste cooking oil (triglyceride, average molecular weight = 884 g/mol) is transesterified with methanol (M = 32 g/mol) using NaOH catalyst. Calculate the mass of FAME (biodiesel, average M = 296 g/mol) produced.

### Step 1 — Reaction Stoichiometry

$$
TG + 3\,CH_3OH \rightarrow 3\,FAME + Glycerol
$$

### Step 2 — Moles of Triglyceride in 1 kg

$$
n_{TG} = \frac{1000}{884} = 1.131 \text{ mol}
$$

### Step 3 — Moles of FAME Produced

$$
n_{FAME} = 3 \times n_{TG} = 3 \times 1.131 = 3.394 \text{ mol}
$$

### Step 4 — Mass of FAME Produced

$$
m_{FAME} = n_{FAME} \times 296 = 3.394 \times 296 = 1004.6 \text{ g} \approx 1.005 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 5 — Mass of Glycerol By-product

$$
n_{glycerol} = n_{TG} = 1.131 \text{ mol}
$$

$$
m_{glycerol} = 1.131 \times 92 = 104.1 \text{ g}
$$

### Step 6 — Stoichiometric Methanol Required

$$
n_{MeOH,\,stoich} = 3 \times 1.131 = 3.394 \text{ mol}
$$

$$
m_{MeOH,\,stoich} = 3.394 \times 32 = 108.6 \text{ g}
$$

**In practice, twice this (≈ 217 g) is used to ensure completion.**

### Final Answer Summary

| **Stream** | **Mass (per kg oil)** |
|---|---|
| Biodiesel (FAME) | **1.005 kg** |
| Glycerol | **0.104 kg** |
| Methanol (stoich) | 0.109 kg |
| Methanol (practical) | 0.20 – 0.25 kg |

---

| O₂ / Air | Excess | None | Sub-stoich | None | None (catalyst) | None (yeast) |
| Catalyst | None | None | None / bed | Microbes | NaOH / H₂SO₄ | Enzymes (Zymase) |

| Energy Output | Direct heat | Liquid/solid fuel | Gaseous fuel | Gaseous fuel | Liquid fuel | Liquid fuel |
| Scale of Plant | Large (MW) | Small/Med | Med (MW) | Small/Large | Small/Med | Med/Large |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State the Buswell equation for anaerobic digestion of a generic substrate CₓHᵧO_zNₐSᵦ. Apply it to find the methane yield from 1 kg of glucose.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Buswell equation gives the stoichiometric products of complete anaerobic digestion:

$$C_xH_yO_zN_aS_b + \left(x - \frac{y}{4} - \frac{z}{2} + \frac{3a}{4} + \frac{b}{2}\right)H_2O \rightarrow \left(\frac{x}{2} - \frac{y}{8} + \frac{z}{4} - \frac{3a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}\right)CH_4 + \left(\frac{x}{2} + \frac{y}{8} - \frac{z}{4} + \frac{3a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\right)CO_2 + a\,NH_3 + b\,H_2S$$

For glucose C₆H₁₂O₆ (x=6, y=12, z=6, a=b=0):

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3\,CH_4 + 3\,CO_2$$

Moles of glucose = 1000/180 = 5.556 mol. Moles of CH₄ = 3 × 5.556 = 16.67 mol. Volume at STP = 16.67 × 22.4 × 10⁻³ = **0.373 Nm³ CH₄ per kg glucose**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List the seven primary chemical reactions occurring in a biomass gasifier. Identify the endothermic ones.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. Partial Oxidation: C + ½O₂ → CO (exo)
2. Complete Combustion: C + O₂ → CO₂ (exo)
3. Boudouard: C + CO₂ ⇌ 2CO (**endo**, +172 kJ/mol)
4. Water-Gas: C + H₂O ⇌ CO + H₂ (**endo**, +131 kJ/mol)
5. Water-Gas Shift: CO + H₂O ⇌ CO₂ + H₂ (exo)
6. Methanation: C + 2H₂ → CH₄ (exo)
7. Steam Reforming: CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3H₂ (**endo**, +206 kJ/mol)

**The three endothermic reactions are 3, 4, and 7.**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A municipal solid waste has the following ultimate analysis (mass %, dry basis): C = 38 %, H = 5 %, O = 28 %, N = 2 %, S = 1 %, Ash = 26 %. If the moisture content is 15 %, calculate:
(i) Stoichiometric oxygen required (kg/kg wet waste)
(ii) Stoichiometric air required (Nm³/kg wet waste)
(iii) Theoretical flue gas volume (Nm³/kg wet waste)

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with balanced chemical equations the **two pathways of methanogenesis** in anaerobic digestion. Which pathway is dominant and why?

### Model Answer to Q-A (a):

**Step 1 — Per kg of dry waste, moles of each element:**

$$
n_C = \frac{380}{12} = 31.67 \text{ mol}, \quad n_H = \frac{50}{1} = 50 \text{ mol}, \quad n_O = \frac{280}{16} = 17.50 \text{ mol}
$$

$$
n_N = \frac{20}{14} = 1.429 \text{ mol}, \quad n_S = \frac{10}{32} = 0.3125 \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric O₂:**

$$
n_{O_2} = n_C + \frac{n_H}{4} + n_S - \frac{n_O}{2} = 31.67 + 12.50 + 0.3125 - 8.75 = 35.73 \text{ mol O}_2/\text{kg dry}
$$

Mass of O₂ = 35.73 × 32 × 10⁻³ = **1.143 kg O₂/kg dry waste**.

Per kg wet waste (× 0.85) = 1.143 × 0.85 = **0.972 kg O₂/kg wet waste**. ✓ (i)

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric Air in Nm³:**

Moles of air = 35.73/0.21 = 170.1 mol/kg dry → 170.1 × 0.85 = 144.6 mol/kg wet.

Volume = 144.6 × 22.4 × 10⁻³ = **3.239 Nm³ air/kg wet waste**. ✓ (ii)

**Step 4 — Flue Gas Volume (products + excess N₂):**

CO₂ produced = 31.67 × 0.85 = 26.92 mol
H₂O produced = (50/2) × 0.85 = 21.25 mol
SO₂ produced = 0.3125 × 0.85 = 0.266 mol
N₂ from air = 4 × 35.73 × 0.85 = 121.5 mol (since air is 79/21 ≈ 3.76 N₂ per O₂)

Total flue gas = 26.92 + 21.25 + 0.266 + 121.5 = 169.9 mol/kg wet.
Volume = 169.9 × 22.4 × 10⁻³ = **3.806 Nm³ flue gas/kg wet waste**. ✓ (iii)

### Model Answer to Q-A (b):

Methanogenesis is the terminal stage of anaerobic digestion where acetate and H₂/CO₂ are converted to CH₄ by *Methanogenic archaea*.

**Pathway A — Acetoclastic Methanogenesis (dominant, ~70 %):**

$$
CH_3COOH \rightarrow CH_4 + CO_2
$$

Acetate is split directly into methane and carbon dioxide. This pathway dominates because **acetate is the most abundant intermediate** produced in acidogenesis and acetogenesis, and *Methanosarcina* and *Methanosaeta* species are well-adapted to use it.

**Pathway B — Hydrogenotrophic Methanogenesis (~30 %):**

$$
CO_2 + 4\,H_2 \rightarrow CH_4 + 2\,H_2O
$$

Hydrogenotrophic methanogens consume H₂ and CO₂, keeping the partial pressure of H₂ low and **thermodynamically driving** the acetogenesis reactions forward (otherwise they would be endergonic). The two pathways are thus **synergistic**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of balanced chemical equations, explain the **process of transesterification** for biodiesel production from waste cooking oil. State the role of the catalyst and explain why an excess of methanol is used in practice.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **seven reactions of biomass gasification** with their enthalpy changes. Explain how the endothermic reactions are sustained in a gasifier.

### Model Answer to Q-B (a):

Transesterification is the reaction of a triglyceride (the main component of waste cooking oil) with a short-chain alcohol (methanol) in the presence of a catalyst to produce **Fatty Acid Methyl Esters (FAME, biodiesel)** and **glycerol**.

**The General Reaction:**

$$
Triglyceride + 3\,CH_3OH \xrightleftharpoons{NaOH/KOH} 3\,FAME + Glycerol
$$

**Specific Example (with triolein):**

$$
C_{57}H_{104}O_6 + 3\,CH_3OH \xrightarrow{NaOH,\;60\,°C} 3\,C_{19}H_{36}O_2 + C_3H_8O_3
$$

The reaction proceeds in **three consecutive reversible steps**, each replacing one fatty acid chain:

**Step 1:** TG + CH₃OH → Diglyceride + FAME
**Step 2:** DG + CH₃OH → Monoglyceride + FAME
**Step 3:** MG + CH₃OH → Glycerol + FAME

**Role of Catalyst:**
The NaOH/KOH catalyst (typically 0.5 – 1 % by mass of oil) reacts with methanol to form **sodium methoxide (CH₃O⁻Na⁺)**, which is the *active species* that attacks the carbonyl carbon of the triglyceride, breaking the C–O bond and releasing FAME. Without the catalyst, the reaction is prohibitively slow (≥ 250 °C needed).

**Why Excess Methanol:**
The stoichiometric ratio is 3:1 (methanol:oil). In practice, a ratio of **6:1 to 12:1** is used because:
- The reaction is **reversible** — excess methanol shifts equilibrium *forward* (Le Chatelier).
- It ensures **complete conversion** of triglycerides to FAME (target ≥ 96.5 % as per EN 14214).
- It compensates for methanol losses due to evaporation at 60 °C.

After the reaction, excess methanol is recovered by distillation and recycled.

### Model Answer to Q-B (b):

**The Seven Gasification Reactions:**

| **#** | **Reaction** | **ΔH (kJ/mol)** | **Type** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | C + ½O₂ → CO | −111 | Exothermic |
| 2 | C + O₂ → CO₂ | −393.5 | Exothermic |
| 3 | C + CO₂ ⇌ 2CO | +172 | Endothermic |
| 4 | C + H₂O ⇌ CO + H₂ | +131 | Endothermic |
| 5 | CO + H₂O ⇌ CO₂ + H₂ | −41 | Exothermic |
| 6 | C + 2H₂ → CH₄ | −75 | Exothermic |
| 7 | CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3H₂ | +206 | Endothermic |

**How Endothermic Reactions are Sustained:**
The three endothermic reactions (Boudouard, Water-Gas, Steam Reforming) require a continuous **heat input of +131 to +206 kJ/mol**. This heat is provided in-situ by the **partial oxidation (Rxn 1) and complete combustion (Rxn 2)** of a fraction of the carbon in the same reactor. Approximately 20 – 40 % of the carbon is combusted to release the heat needed to drive the endothermic reactions. This is why the **equivalence ratio (ER) is kept at 0.2 – 0.4** in a gasifier (less than stoichiometric). The heat release is balanced against the heat consumption to maintain the reactor at **800 – 1200 °C**, the temperature range where the rate constants of the endothermic reactions become significant (high T favors endothermic reactions per Le Chatelier). The **water-gas shift (Rxn 5)** is mildly exothermic and acts as a "H₂ amplifier," adjusting the H₂:CO ratio of the syngas.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Don't forget the nitrogen in ultimate analysis.** Many students ignore N₂ from the fuel in flue gas calculations. Nitrogen from the fuel *also* goes to flue gas as N₂ (1/2 mol N₂ per mol N in fuel).
> 2. **Forgetting the moisture in "as-received" basis** when the problem states moisture %. Always multiply by (1 − moisture).
> 3. **Boudouard, Water-Gas, and Steam Reforming are the THREE endothermic reactions** — students often list only the first two. Steam Reforming of CH₄ is also endothermic.
> 4. **The Buswell equation** is asked more often than the "simple" glucose → 3CH₄ + 3CO₂. Practice plugging in values for a, b, c, d, e.
> 5. **In transesterification**, write the *three consecutive steps* if the question asks for "the mechanism" — partial credit is given for the stepwise mechanism.
> 6. **For ER**: ER < 1 = lean / gasification / partial combustion; ER > 1 = rich / incomplete combustion. **Do not confuse** with λ (excess air ratio), which is the inverse.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and revise 5 minutes before the exam.**

- [ ] **Combustion** = excess O₂, high T, products CO₂ + H₂O, releases ~20 – 30 MJ/kg.
- [ ] **Pyrolysis** = no O₂, T = 300 – 800 °C, products = char + bio-oil + gas.
- [ ] **Gasification** = sub-stoich air + steam, T = 700 – 1200 °C, product = syngas.
- [ ] **ER (Equivalence Ratio)** for combustion = 1; for gasification = 0.2 – 0.4; for pyrolysis = 0.
- [ ] **Seven Gasification Reactions** — memorize: **POX, Combustion, Boudouard, Water-Gas, WGS, Methanation, Steam Reforming**.
- [ ] **Endothermic gasification reactions**: #3, #4, #7. Heat is supplied by #1 and #2.
- [ ] **Anaerobic Digestion** has **4 stages**: Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis.
- [ ] **Acetoclastic** methanogenesis dominates (~70 % of CH₄): CH₃COOH → CH₄ + CO₂.
- [ ] **Hydrogenotrophic** methanogenesis: CO₂ + 4H₂ → CH₄ + 2H₂O.
- [ ] **Buswell equation** for glucose: C₆H₁₂O₆ → 3 CH₄ + 3 CO₂ (molar ratio 1:1).
- [ ] **CH₄ yield from glucose** = 0.373 Nm³/kg (theoretical).
- [ ] **Transesterification** uses 3:1 stoichiometric alcohol:oil; **6:1 to 12:1 in practice**.
- [ ] **Catalyst** in transesterification = NaOH/KOH (alkali) for refined oils; H₂SO₄ (acid) for high FFA oils.
- [ ] **Biodiesel yield** from triglyceride ≈ 1.0 kg FAME per kg oil (mass basis).
- [ ] **Fermentation** by *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*: C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2 C₂H₅OH + 2 CO₂.
- [ ] **Theoretical ethanol yield** = 0.511 g/g glucose.
- [ ] **Dulong formula** for HHV: HHV = 338C + 1442H − 180O (kJ/kg, mass %).
- [ ] **Stoichiometric O₂** formula (per mol of CₐHᵦOᵧNₐSᵦ): $a + b/4 + e - c/2$.
- [ ] **Air is 21 % O₂ by volume, 23.2 % O₂ by mass, 3.76 mol N₂ per mol O₂.**
- [ ] **Cellulose** yields max bio-oil; **Lignin** yields max char.
- [ ] **Hydrogenotrophic methanogens** keep H₂ partial pressure low, making acetogenesis thermodynamically feasible.
- [ ] **CHP (Combined Heat and Power)** is the typical end-use for biogas from AD.
- [ ] **B100, B20** are biodiesel blend notations; **E5, E10, E85** are ethanol blend notations.
- [ ] **GHG mitigation** by AD avoids CH₄ release to atmosphere (CH₄ has GWP = 28).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Catalysts Used (Acid, Base, and Enzyme Catalysts)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT836 - WASTE TO ENERGY TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Catalysts Used (Acid, Base, and Enzyme Catalysts)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Catalyst (KTU 2024 Definition):** A substance that increases the rate of a chemical reaction by lowering the activation energy barrier without being consumed in the overall reaction. In the context of Waste to Energy (WTE) processes, catalysts are essential for transesterification, hydrolysis, gasification, pyrolysis, and biogas upgradation reactions.

In Waste to Energy Technology, three principal classes of catalysts are employed to convert waste biomass, used cooking oils, municipal solid waste, and agricultural residues into usable energy vectors (biodiesel, biogas, syngas, and bioethanol):

| S.No. | Catalyst Class | Chemical Nature | Phase |
|-------|----------------|-----------------|-------|
| 1 | **Acid Catalysts** | Proton donors (Bronsted) / Electron pair acceptors (Lewis) | Homogeneous & Heterogeneous |
| 2 | **Base (Alkali) Catalysts** | Proton acceptors / Hydroxide donors | Homogeneous & Heterogeneous |
| 3 | **Enzyme (Biocatalysts)** | Proteins (typically lipases) | Homogeneous (biochemical) |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Toll Booth and the Express Lane"**
> Imagine a highway where every vehicle (reactant molecule) must pass a toll booth (energy barrier) to reach its destination (product). Without a toll collector, vehicles pile up. A **catalyst acts as an express lane operator** — it opens a faster, alternative route so molecules can cross the barrier more easily, but the operator (catalyst) doesn't get consumed or carried away by the traffic. The total amount of energy required for the journey (reaction) drops significantly, and the traffic flow (reaction rate) increases dramatically.

- **Acid Catalysts** = "The Strict Acidic Gatekeeper" → donates protons (H⁺) to weaken bonds in reactant molecules (e.g., breaking triglyceride ester bonds in waste oil).
- **Base Catalysts** = "The Alkaline Matchmaker" → grabs acidic protons, creating negatively charged nucleophiles (methoxide ion) that attack triglyceride molecules violently and rapidly.
- **Enzyme Catalysts** = "The Molecular Locksmith" → a biological protein (lipase) physically cradles the reactant in its active site like a lock and key, perfectly positioning molecules for reaction under mild conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric in WTE:** Catalyst loading is typically expressed in **weight percent (wt%)** relative to the oil/fat mass, with values ranging from **0.5 wt% to 5 wt%** for most industrial biodiesel production.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Acid Catalysts — Detailed Analysis

#### 2.1.1 Homogeneous Acid Catalysts

The most industrially common homogeneous acid catalysts are **sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄)**, **hydrochloric acid (HCl)**, and **p-toluenesulfonic acid**.

- **Mechanism:** The acid donates a proton (H⁺) to the carbonyl oxygen of the triglyceride, making the carbon more electrophilic. Methanol then attacks this activated carbon, leading to stepwise breakdown into fatty acid methyl esters (FAME) and glycerol.
- **Operating Conditions:** Temperature of **60–70 °C**, reaction time of **1–4 hours**, molar ratio (alcohol:oil) of **20:1 to 30:1**, catalyst loading of **1–5 wt%**.
- **Advantages:** Can handle feedstocks with high **Free Fatty Acid (FFA) content (>2 wt%)**, simultaneously performing esterification and transesterification.
- **Disadvantages:** Slow reaction rate, corrosive to equipment, requires neutralization and washing, generates large volumes of wastewater.

#### 2.1.2 Heterogeneous Acid Catalysts

Common examples include **sulfonated zirconia (SO₄²⁻/ZrO₂)**, **Amberlyst-15 (sulfonated polystyrene resin)**, **H-ZSM-5 zeolite**, **nafion**, **sulfated tin oxide**, and **heteropolyacids (HPA)**.

- **Mechanism:** Surface Bronsted acid sites (–SO₃H) or Lewis acid sites (metal cations) protonate the carbonyl oxygen of the triglyceride.
- **Advantages:** Reusable, easier separation, no soap formation, eco-friendly.
- **Disadvantages:** Lower activity compared to homogeneous acids, mass transfer limitations, requires higher temperatures (**150–250 °C**).

### 2.2 Base (Alkali) Catalysts — Detailed Analysis

#### 2.2.1 Homogeneous Base Catalysts

The most widely used are **sodium hydroxide (NaOH)**, **potassium hydroxide (KOH)**, and **sodium methoxide (NaOCH₃)**.

- **Mechanism:** The base reacts with methanol to form methoxide ion (CH₃O⁻), a strong nucleophile that attacks the carbonyl carbon of the triglyceride. This forms a tetrahedral intermediate that collapses to yield FAME and diglyceride, which continues the cascade.
- **Operating Conditions:** Temperature of **60–70 °C**, reaction time of **30–90 minutes**, molar ratio of **6:1**, catalyst loading of **0.5–1.5 wt%**.
- **Advantages:** **~4000 times faster** than acid catalysis under mild conditions, high conversion (>98%) in short time.
- **Disadvantages:** Highly sensitive to FFA and water content; if FFA > 2 wt%, saponification occurs producing soap, which consumes catalyst, reduces yield, and complicates separation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The **soap formation reaction** is:
> $$\text{RCOOH} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{RCOONa} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
> This is why **two-step acid-base catalysis** is preferred for waste cooking oil with high FFA — first acid esterifies FFA to biodiesel, then base transesterifies the remaining triglycerides.

#### 2.2.2 Heterogeneous Base Catalysts

Examples include **CaO (calcium oxide)**, **MgO (magnesium oxide)**, **BaO**, **SrO**, **hydrotalcites**, **anion exchange resins**, and **KF/γ-Al₂O₃**.

- **Advantages:** Reusable for multiple cycles, no soap formation, easier product separation, no wastewater.
- **Disadvantages:** Leaching of active phase, mass transfer limitations, requires higher temperatures and pressures.

### 2.3 Enzyme (Biocatalyst) Catalysts — Detailed Analysis

The most common enzyme catalysts are **lipases** (triacylglycerol acylhydrolases, EC 3.1.1.3), sourced from:
- *Candida antarctica* (Novozym 435)
- *Rhizomucor miehei* (Lipozyme RM IM)
- *Pseudomonas cepacia*
- *Thermomyces lanuginosus*
- *Burkholderia cepacia*

- **Mechanism:** The lipase follows the **interfacial activation mechanism** — its active site is covered by a flexible amphipathic lid (α-helix). At the oil-water interface, the lid opens, exposing the catalytic triad (Ser-His-Asp/Glu). The serine residue attacks the carbonyl carbon, forming an acyl-enzyme intermediate. Methanol then attacks this intermediate, releasing FAME and regenerating the enzyme.
- **Operating Conditions:** Temperature of **30–50 °C**, pH of **6.0–8.0**, reaction time of **4–24 hours**, molar ratio of **3:1 to 6:1**, enzyme loading of **5–20 wt%**.
- **Advantages:** High specificity, no soap formation, works with high FFA feedstocks, mild operating conditions, biodegradable, reusable for 100+ cycles.
- **Disadvantages:** High initial cost, slow reaction rate, susceptible to methanol deactivation (requires stepwise methanol addition).

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Limitation of Enzymes:** Short-chain alcohols (especially methanol) can strip the essential water layer from the enzyme surface, causing **protein denaturation and irreversible inactivation**. This is why **stepwise methanol addition in 3 stages** is recommended industrially.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|-------|---------------------|------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Conversion Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{\text{biodiesel, actual}}}{m_{\text{biodiesel, theoretical}}} \times 100$ | % | Catalyst performance |
| 2 | Reaction Rate (Pseudo-first order) | $r = k \cdot C_A$ | mol·L⁻¹·s⁻¹ | Kinetic modeling |
| 3 | Arrhenius Equation | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | s⁻¹ | Temperature effect |
| 4 | Activation Energy (Base catalysis) | $E_a \approx 10\text{–}25$ | kJ/mol | Fast kinetics |
| 5 | Activation Energy (Acid catalysis) | $E_a \approx 40\text{–}80$ | kJ/mol | Slow kinetics |
| 6 | Activation Energy (Enzyme) | $E_a \approx 15\text{–}30$ | kJ/mol | Mild conditions |
| 7 | Michaelis-Menten Equation | $v = \dfrac{V_{max} \cdot [S]}{K_m + [S]}$ | mol·L⁻¹·s⁻¹ | Enzyme kinetics |
| 8 | Saponification Value | $SV = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \cdot N \cdot 56.1}{m}$ | mg KOH/g | Oil quality check |
| 9 | Acid Value | $AV = \dfrac{V \cdot N \cdot 56.1}{m}$ | mg KOH/g | FFA content |
| 10 | Molar Ratio (Methanol:Oil) | $MR = \dfrac{n_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH}}}{n_{\text{oil}}}$ | dimensionless | Process optimization |
| 11 | Catalyst Loading | $CL = \dfrac{m_{\text{catalyst}}}{m_{\text{oil}}} \times 100$ | wt% | Process design |
| 12 | Theoretical Biodiesel Yield | $Y_t = \dfrac{m_{\text{oil}}}{M_{\text{oil}}} \times M_{\text{FAME}} \times 3$ | g | Stoichiometry |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants Used:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), Faraday-like equivalents for acid-base titration use **56.1 g/mol** (molecular weight of KOH).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Yield of Biodiesel from Triglyceride

**Starting with the general transesterification reaction:**

A triglyceride (TG) reacts with 3 moles of methanol (MeOH) in the presence of a catalyst to produce 3 moles of fatty acid methyl ester (FAME) and 1 mole of glycerol (GLY).

$$\text{TG} + 3\,\text{MeOH} \xrightarrow{\text{Catalyst}} 3\,\text{FAME} + \text{GLY}$$

**Step 1: Establish the molar relationship.**

From stoichiometry:

$$n_{\text{FAME}} = 3 \cdot n_{\text{TG}}$$

This means 1 mole of triglyceride theoretically produces exactly 3 moles of FAME.

**Step 2: Express in terms of mass.**

Using $n = m/M$, where $m$ is mass and $M$ is molecular weight:

$$n_{\text{FAME}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{FAME}}}{M_{\text{FAME}}}$$

**Step 3: Set up the theoretical yield equation.**

$$m_{\text{FAME, theoretical}} = 3 \cdot n_{\text{TG}} \cdot M_{\text{FAME}}$$

$$m_{\text{FAME, theoretical}} = 3 \cdot \left(\dfrac{m_{\text{oil}}}{M_{\text{oil}}}\right) \cdot M_{\text{FAME}}$$

**Step 4: Rearrange for the final form used in the cheat sheet.**

$$\boxed{Y_t = \dfrac{3 \cdot m_{\text{oil}} \cdot M_{\text{FAME}}}{M_{\text{oil}}}}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The factor of **3** arises directly from the 3 fatty acid chains per triglyceride molecule, which is why biodiesel yield is theoretically limited to ~100% mass of oil (slightly less due to glycerol removal, which has mass ~10% of original oil).

### 3.2 Derivation: Pseudo-First-Order Kinetics for Base Catalysis

The transesterification reaction can be approximated as pseudo-first-order when methanol is in large excess.

**Step 1: Rate law.**

$$-\dfrac{dC_{\text{TG}}}{dt} = k \cdot C_{\text{TG}}$$

**Step 2: Separation of variables.**

$$\dfrac{dC_{\text{TG}}}{C_{\text{TG}}} = -k\, dt$$

**Step 3: Integration from $t = 0$ to $t = t$.**

$$\int_{C_{\text{TG},0}}^{C_{\text{TG},t}} \dfrac{dC_{\text{TG}}}{C_{\text{TG}}} = -\int_{0}^{t} k\, dt$$

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{C_{\text{TG},t}}{C_{\text{TG},0}}\right) = -k \cdot t$$

**Step 4: Exponentiate to obtain concentration profile.**

$$C_{\text{TG},t} = C_{\text{TG},0} \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$$

**Step 5: Express in terms of conversion $X$.**

Since conversion $X = 1 - \dfrac{C_{\text{TG},t}}{C_{\text{TG},0}}$:

$$\boxed{X(t) = 1 - e^{-k \cdot t}}$$

**Conversion Logic:** This equation is the **engineering workhorse** for reactor design — it tells the engineer that doubling the time gives exponentially more conversion, and the rate constant $k$ depends on temperature via the Arrhenius equation $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$.

### 3.3 Derivation: Michaelis-Menten Kinetics for Enzyme Catalysis

**Step 1: Enzyme-substrate complex formation.**

$$E + S \underset{k_{-1}}{\overset{k_1}{\rightleftharpoons}} ES \xrightarrow{k_2} E + P$$

**Step 2: Apply steady-state approximation to [ES].**

$$\dfrac{d[ES]}{dt} = k_1 [E][S] - k_{-1}[ES] - k_2[ES] = 0$$

**Step 3: Solve for [ES].**

$$[ES] = \dfrac{k_1 [E][S]}{k_{-1} + k_2} = \dfrac{[E][S]}{K_m}$$

where $K_m = (k_{-1} + k_2) / k_1$ is the **Michaelis constant**.

**Step 4: Use enzyme mass balance $[E]_0 = [E] + [ES]$.**

$$[ES] = \dfrac{[E]_0 [S]}{K_m + [S]}$$

**Step 5: Initial reaction rate $v_0 = k_2 [ES]$.**

Define $V_{max} = k_2 [E]_0$ (maximum rate when all enzyme is bound).

$$\boxed{v_0 = \dfrac{V_{max} \cdot [S]}{K_m + [S]}}$$

**Conversion Logic:** When $[S] \ll K_m$, the rate is first-order; when $[S] \gg K_m$, the rate saturates at $V_{max}$ (zero-order). This explains why adding **excess methanol does NOT increase enzyme rate** — it merely wastes substrate and may deactivate the enzyme.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Evaluation of Catalysts

| Performance Parameter | Homogeneous Acid | Homogeneous Base | Heterogeneous Acid | Heterogeneous Base | Enzyme |
|------------------------|------------------|------------------|--------------------|--------------------|--------|
| Reaction Temperature (°C) | 60–70 | 60–70 | 150–250 | 60–200 | 30–50 |
| Reaction Time | 1–4 h | 30–90 min | 5–10 h | 1–3 h | 4–24 h |
| Molar Ratio (MeOH:Oil) | 20:1–30:1 | 6:1 | 20:1 | 6:1–12:1 | 3:1–6:1 |
| Catalyst Loading (wt%) | 1–5 | 0.5–1.5 | 1–10 | 1–5 | 5–20 |
| FFA Tolerance | High (>20%) | Very Low (<2%) | High | Low | Very High |
| Water Tolerance | Moderate | Very Low | Moderate | Low | Moderate |
| Reusability (cycles) | 1 (single use) | 1 (single use) | 50–200 | 50–200 | 100+ |
| Conversion (%) | 90–95 | 95–99 | 80–90 | 85–95 | 80–95 |
| Reaction Rate | Slow | Very Fast | Slow | Moderate | Slow–Moderate |
| Equipment Corrosion | Severe | Mild | Mild | Mild | None |
| Wastewater Generation | High | High | Low | Low | Negligible |
| Capital Cost | Low | Low | High | Moderate | Very High |
| Operating Cost | High | Low | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate |
| Soap Formation | No | Yes (if FFA high) | No | No | No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Decision Rule:** If feedstock is **refined vegetable oil** → use **homogeneous base catalysis**. If feedstock is **waste cooking oil with high FFA** → use **two-step acid-base catalysis** or **enzyme catalysis** or **heterogeneous acid catalysis**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Catalyst Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CATALYSTS IN WASTE TO ENERGY] --> B[ACID CATALYSTS]
    A --> C[BASE CATALYSTS]
    A --> D[ENZYME CATALYSTS]

    B --> B1[Homogeneous Acid]
    B --> B2[Heterogeneous Acid]

    B1 --> B1A[H2SO4 Sulfuric Acid]
    B1 --> B1B[HCl Hydrochloric Acid]
    B1 --> B1C[p-TsOH p-Toluenesulfonic Acid]

    B2 --> B2A[Amberlyst 15 Sulfonated Resin]
    B2 --> B2B[Sulfated Zirconia SO4 ZrO2]
    B2 --> B2C[H ZSM5 Zeolite]
    B2 --> B2D[Heteropolyacids HPA]

    C --> C1[Homogeneous Base]
    C --> C2[Heterogeneous Base]

    C1 --> C1A[NaOH Sodium Hydroxide]
    C1 --> C1B[KOH Potassium Hydroxide]
    C1 --> C1C[NaOCH3 Sodium Methoxide]

    C2 --> C2A[CaO Calcium Oxide]
    C2 --> C2B[MgO Magnesium Oxide]
    C2 --> C2C[Hydrotalcite Mg Al LDH]
    C2 --> C2D[KF gamma Al2O3]

    D --> D1[Lipases from Fungi]
    D --> D2[Lipases from Bacteria]
    D --> D3[Lipases from Yeasts]

    D1 --> D1A[Candida antarctica Novozym 435]
    D1 --> D1B[Rhizomucor miehei Lipozyme RM IM]
    D1 --> D1C[Thermomyces lanuginosus]

    D2 --> D2A[Pseudomonas cepacia]
    D2 --> D2B[Burkholderia cepacia]

    D3 --> D3A[Candida rugosa]
    D3 --> D3B[Geotrichum candidum]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Process Flow for Two-Step Acid-Base Catalysis (High FFA Waste Oil)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Waste Cooking Oil] --> P2[FFA Analysis Acid Value]
    P2 --> P3{FFA Content?}

    P3 -->|FFA less than 2 percent| P4[Single Step Base Transesterification]
    P3 -->|FFA greater than 2 percent| P5[Step 1 Acid Esterification]
    P3 -->|FFA greater than 20 percent| P9[Enzyme Catalysis]

    P5 --> P5A[Add H2SO4 1 to 5 wt%]
    P5A --> P5B[MeOH Ratio 20 to 30 to 1]
    P5B --> P5C[Temperature 60 C, 1 to 2 h]
    P5C --> P6[FFA Converted to FAME]

    P6 --> P7[Step 2 Base Transesterification]
    P7 --> P7A[Add NaOH 0.5 to 1.5 wt%]
    P7A --> P7B[MeOH Ratio 6 to 1]
    P7B --> P7C[Temperature 60 C, 1 h]
    P7C --> P8[Crude Biodiesel]

    P4 --> P8
    P9 --> P8

    P8 --> P10[Glycerol Separation]
    P10 --> P11[Water Washing]
    P11 --> P12[Drying and Purification]
    P12 --> P13[Pure Biodiesel FAME]

    P8 --> P8A[Soap Removal via Centrifugation]
    P8A --> P11

    style P3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P8 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P13 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P9 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Comparative Decision Tree for Catalyst Selection

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1[Feedstock Type Selection] --> Q2{Quality of Oil Feed?}

    Q2 -->|Refined Oil| Q3[Use Homogeneous Base NaOH KOH]
    Q2 -->|Crude or Used Oil| Q4{FFA Content?}

    Q4 -->|Low FFA less than 2 percent| Q5[Use Homogeneous Base with Pre-treatment]
    Q4 -->|High FFA 2 to 20 percent| Q6[Use Two Step Acid Base Process]
    Q4 -->|Very High FFA greater than 20 percent| Q7[Use Heterogeneous Acid or Enzyme]

    Q3 --> R1[Optimal: 60 C, 1 h, 98 percent yield]
    Q5 --> R2[Optimal: 60 C, 1 h, 95 percent yield]
    Q6 --> R3[Optimal: 2 h total, 95 percent yield]
    Q7 --> R4[Optimal: 40 C, 8 h, 90 percent yield]

    Q1 --> S1[Priority: Cost]
    Q1 --> S2[Priority: Purity]
    Q1 --> S3[Priority: Sustainability]

    S1 --> S3A[Choose Homogeneous Base]
    S2 --> S3B[Choose Enzyme Catalysis]
    S3 --> S3C[Choose Heterogeneous or Enzyme]

    style Q4 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```
