---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**"Differentiate between homogeneous and heterogeneous acid catalysts used in biodiesel production. Give two examples of each."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Homogeneous acid catalysts** are in the same phase (liquid) as the reactants. They offer high activity and uniform active sites but cause severe corrosion and are non-reusable. Examples: **H₂SO₄ (sulfuric acid)** and **HCl (hydrochloric acid)**. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Heterogeneous acid catalysts** are in a different (solid) phase from the reactants, allowing easy separation, reusability, and environmental benefits. Examples: **Sulfated zirconia (SO₄²⁻/ZrO₂)** and **Amberlyst-15 (sulfonated polystyrene resin)**. *(1.5 marks)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**"Why are enzyme catalysts considered more environmentally friendly than chemical catalysts in waste to energy conversion? List any two limitations."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Environmental friendliness:** Enzymes are biodegradable, derived from renewable biological sources, operate under mild conditions (30–50 °C) reducing energy consumption, produce no toxic byproducts, and generate negligible wastewater. They also tolerate high FFA feedstocks without soap formation. *(2 marks)*
- **Limitations:** *(i)* High initial capital cost; *(ii)* Susceptibility to methanol-induced inactivation requiring stepwise substrate addition. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the mechanism of base-catalyzed transesterification of waste cooking oil with methanol. Discuss the role of methoxide ion and explain why this catalyst is unsuitable for high FFA feedstocks. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a comparative table, evaluate the performance of homogeneous acid, homogeneous base, and enzyme catalysts for biodiesel production. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Mechanism of Base-Catalyzed Transesterification (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Methoxide formation.** NaOH reacts with methanol to produce sodium methoxide and water.

$$\text{NaOH} + \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{O}^-\text{Na}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 2: Nucleophilic attack.** The methoxide ion (CH₃O⁻), a strong nucleophile, attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of the triglyceride.

**Step 3: Tetrahedral intermediate formation.** The carbonyl carbon becomes sp³ hybridized, forming a tetrahedral intermediate.

**Step 4: Intermediate collapse.** The intermediate collapses, expelling the diglyceride anion and forming fatty acid methyl ester (FAME).

**Step 5: Cascade continuation.** The diglyceride anion deprotonates another methanol molecule, regenerating methoxide and forming diglyceride, which undergoes the same process twice more, yielding 3 FAME molecules and glycerol.

**Why unsuitable for high FFA feedstocks (≥2 wt%):**
- FFA reacts with NaOH to form **soap (sodium carboxylate)** via saponification:
$$\text{RCOOH} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{RCOONa} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
- Soap formation consumes the catalyst, reduces yield, creates emulsions that prevent phase separation between biodiesel and glycerol, and blocks the active sites preventing transesterification. *(7 marks total)*

**(b) Comparative Table (7 Marks):**

| Parameter | Homogeneous Acid (H₂SO₄) | Homogeneous Base (NaOH) | Enzyme (Lipase) |
|-----------|---------------------------|--------------------------|------------------|
| Operating Temperature | 60–70 °C | 60–70 °C | 30–50 °C |
| Reaction Time | 1–4 h | 30–90 min | 4–24 h |
| Molar Ratio (MeOH:Oil) | 20:1–30:1 | 6:1 | 3:1–6:1 |
| Catalyst Loading | 1–5 wt% | 0.5–1.5 wt% | 5–20 wt% |
| FFA Tolerance | High (>20%) | Very Low (<2%) | Very High |
| Reusability | Single use | Single use | 100+ cycles |
| Conversion Efficiency | 90–95% | 95–99% | 80–95% |
| Soap Formation | No | Yes (if FFA high) | No |
| Equipment Corrosion | Severe | Mild | None |
| Wastewater | High | High | Negligible |
| Cost | Low initial | Low initial | High initial |
| Environmental Impact | High | Moderate | Very Low |

*Discussion points: Base catalysts are fastest and most efficient but FFA-sensitive; acid catalysts are slower but handle FFA well; enzymes are eco-friendly and reusable but expensive and slow.* *(7 marks total)*

---

**Q3B. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Describe the Michaelis-Menten kinetic model for enzyme-catalyzed transesterification. Derive the rate equation and explain its significance in determining optimal substrate concentration. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the heterogeneous solid base catalysts used in biodiesel production. Compare their advantages and disadvantages with homogeneous NaOH, and explain the phenomenon of catalyst leaching. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Michaelis-Menten Kinetics (7 Marks):**

**Enzyme-substrate reaction scheme:**

$$E + S \underset{k_{-1}}{\overset{k_1}{\rightleftharpoons}} ES \xrightarrow{k_2} E + P$$

**Step 1: Apply the steady-state approximation to [ES]:**

$$k_1 [E][S] = (k_{-1} + k_2) [ES]$$

**Step 2: Define Michaelis constant:**

$$K_m = \dfrac{k_{-1} + k_2}{k_1}$$

**Step 3: Mass balance on enzyme:**

$$[E]_0 = [E] + [ES]$$

**Step 4: Solve for [ES]:**

$$[ES] = \dfrac{[E]_0 [S]}{K_m + [S]}$$

**Step 5: Initial rate expression:**

$$v_0 = k_2 [ES] = \dfrac{V_{max} \cdot [S]}{K_m + [S]}$$

where $V_{max} = k_2 [E]_0$.

**Significance for optimal substrate concentration:**
- When $[S] \ll K_m$: rate is first-order in [S]; rate increases linearly with substrate.
- When $[S] \gg K_m$: rate approaches $V_{max}$ (zero-order); further substrate addition provides no kinetic benefit.
- **Engineering implication:** The optimal molar ratio (MeOH:Oil) is **3:1 to 6:1** for enzymes — using a 30:1 excess (as in acid catalysis) provides no kinetic gain and actually **inactivates the enzyme** by stripping its hydration layer. *(7 marks total)*

**(b) Heterogeneous Solid Base Catalysts (7 Marks):**

**Common examples:** CaO, MgO, SrO, BaO, hydrotalcites (Mg-Al layered double hydroxides), KF/γ-Al₂O₃, and alkali metal-supported zeolites.

**Mechanism:** Surface basic sites (O²⁻ or OH⁻) abstract a proton from methanol, generating methoxide ions on the catalyst surface. These surface methoxide species attack triglyceride carbonyls, producing FAME.

**Advantages over homogeneous NaOH:**
- **Reusability** for 50–200 reaction cycles.
- **No soap formation** even with moderate FFA.
- **Easy separation** of catalyst by filtration.
- **No wastewater** from neutralization/washing.
- **Eco-friendly** process.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Mass transfer limitations** due to three-phase system (solid catalyst, oil, methanol).
- **Leaching** of active phase (Ca²⁺, K⁺) into the reaction medium, which deactivates the catalyst and contaminates the product.
- **Requires higher temperatures** (100–200 °C) and pressures.
- **Lower activity** compared to NaOH on a mass basis.
- **Poisoning** by CO₂ and H₂O from air exposure.

**Catalyst Leaching Phenomenon:**
Leaching occurs when the active species (e.g., Ca²⁺ from CaO, or K⁺ from KF/Al₂O₃) dissolves into the polar reaction medium (methanol/glycerol phase). This is detected by:
- ICP-OES analysis of the liquid product.
- Progressive decrease in catalyst activity over cycles.
- **Hot filtration test** — filtering catalyst mid-reaction should stop further conversion if the catalyst is truly heterogeneous.

**Mitigation strategies:** Calcination at high temperatures (700–900 °C) to form stable crystalline phases, use of supports with strong metal-support interaction, and surface modification with hydrophobic coatings. *(7 marks total)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "homogeneous" with "heterogeneous" definitions** — students often write "homogeneous means pure" instead of "same phase as reactants." Avoid this. **(Lose 1–2 marks)**
> 2. **Writing transesterification equation with 1:1 stoichiometry** — always show **3 moles of methanol** for 1 mole of triglyceride. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 3. **Forgetting to mention soap formation** when explaining why NaOH fails for high FFA oil — this is a **favourite KTU question topic**. **(Lose 2 marks)**
> 4. **Stating enzyme advantages without mentioning methanol deactivation** — this shows lack of deep understanding. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 5. **Not labeling the Michaelis constant $K_m$ units correctly** — $K_m$ has units of substrate concentration (mol/L), not dimensionless. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 6. **Skipping the Michaelis-Menten derivation** in 14-mark questions — derivations are **mandatory** for full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- [x] **Three catalyst classes:** Acid, Base, Enzyme — know at least **2 examples of each** (homogeneous and heterogeneous).
- [x] **Acid catalysts are ~4000× slower** than base catalysts but tolerate FFA.
- [x] **Base catalysts (NaOH, KOH)** are fastest and most economical for refined oils.
- [x] **Saponification reaction:** FFA + NaOH → Soap + Water (blocks transesterification).
- [x] **Two-step acid-base process** is the industrial standard for waste cooking oil (FFA 2–20%).
- [x] **Enzyme catalysts (lipases)** are eco-friendly, reusable 100+ times, but expensive and slow.
- [x] **Enzyme deactivation** by excess methanol → use **3:1 to 6:1 molar ratio** and stepwise addition.
- [x] **Michaelis-Menten equation:** $v_0 = \dfrac{V_{max} [S]}{K_m + [S]}$ — the cornerstone of enzyme kinetics.
- [x] **Pseudo-first-order kinetics** for base catalysis: $X(t) = 1 - e^{-kt}$.
- [x] **Theoretical yield formula:** $Y_t = 3 \cdot m_{\text{oil}} \cdot M_{\text{FAME}} / M_{\text{oil}}$.
- [x] **Heterogeneous catalysts** offer reusability and easy separation but suffer from leaching and mass transfer limitations.
- [x] **CaO and MgO** are the most studied heterogeneous base catalysts for biodiesel.
- [x] **Sulfated zirconia and Amberlyst-15** are flagship heterogeneous acid catalysts.
- [x] **Novozym 435** (Candida antarctica lipase B) is the most industrially used enzyme.
- [x] **Standard conditions:** 60 °C, 1 hour, 6:1 methanol:oil, 1 wt% NaOH for base catalysis of refined oil.

---

**END OF NOTES — MODULE 4: CATALYSTS USED (ACID, BASE, AND ENZYME CATALYSTS)**

**Course Outcomes Mapped (KTU 2024 OEBBT836):**
- **CO3:** Analyze various waste-to-energy conversion pathways including catalytic processes.
- **CO4:** Evaluate the technical and economic feasibility of different catalytic systems for biofuel production.
- **CO5:** Design and optimize catalytic processes for sustainable energy recovery from waste.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biodiesel Production from Waste Oils and Fats: Raw Materials and Feedstock
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT836 - WASTE TO ENERGY TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Biodiesel Production from Waste Oils and Fats: Raw Materials and Feedstock

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Biodiesel** is defined as a mono-alkyl ester of long-chain fatty acids derived from renewable biological feedstocks such as vegetable oils, animal fats, and microbial lipids. As per the **American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM D6751)** and the **European Standard EN 14214**, biodiesel is officially classified as **FAME (Fatty Acid Methyl Ester)** when produced via transesterification using methanol.

**Waste Oils and Fats (Feedstock)** refer to lipid-bearing residues that have either completed their primary useful life cycle (e.g., Used Cooking Oil – UCO), are by-products of industrial rendering processes (e.g., animal tallow, yellow grease, brown grease), or are generated during the refining of crude vegetable oils (e.g., soapstock, acidulated soapstock, deodorizer distillate). These low-cost feedstocks are the cornerstone of second-generation biodiesel economics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The classification of feedstock directly determines the upstream pre-treatment pathway. Waste oils with high Free Fatty Acid (FFA > 2% w/w) mandate an acid-catalyzed esterification step *before* the conventional alkali-catalyzed transesterification.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a large, greasy kitchen pan leftover from a restaurant's deep fryer. Instead of dumping it into a drain (which causes severe environmental damage), you "convince" the fat molecules to swap their heavy glycerol backbone for a lightweight methanol molecule. This molecular swap produces a thin, clean-burning fuel that can run a diesel engine with virtually no modifications. The "leftover grease" is the **feedstock**, the "alcohol" is the **reactant**, and the resulting clean fuel is the **biodiesel**.

Think of the feedstock as the *raw clay* of a potter. The quality of clay (moisture, impurities, fatty acid profile) dictates the type of wheel (catalyst) and the firing temperature (reaction conditions) required to make a strong, market-grade pot (biodiesel).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Reference Values

- **Density of biodiesel:** **0.860 – 0.900 g/cm³** (at 15 °C)
- **Kinematic Viscosity:** **1.9 – 6.0 mm²/s** (at 40 °C, per ASTM D445)
- **Acid Value threshold for alkali process:** **< 2.0 mg KOH/g**
- **Global biodiesel feedstock market share:** Used Cooking Oil (UCO) contributes approximately **15–20%** of global production.
- **Cetane Number of biodiesel:** typically **> 50** (vs. 45–55 for petro-diesel).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Feedstock Selection Matters:** Biodiesel production cost is approximately **70–80%** dictated by feedstock cost. Utilizing waste oils (priced at 30–50% of virgin oil cost) dramatically improves the project IRR (Internal Rate of Return).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Biodiesel Feedstock

The raw materials for biodiesel are categorized into four generations based on origin and sustainability profile:

**1. First-Generation Feedstocks (Edible Oils):**
- Soybean oil (USA, Brazil), Rapeseed/Canola oil (EU), Palm oil (Malaysia, Indonesia), Sunflower oil, Coconut oil.
- **Drawback:** Direct food-vs-fuel conflict; high cost; deforestation concerns.

**2. Second-Generation Feedstocks (Non-Edible / Waste Oils) — *KTU FOCUS*:**
- **Used Cooking Oil (UCO):** Collected from restaurants, food industries, and households. FFA typically 2–15%.
- **Animal Fats:** Tallow (beef), lard (pork), poultry fat, fish oil. High saturated content → high cloud/pour point.
- **Yellow Grease:** Rendered animal fats with FFA < 15%.
- **Brown Grease:** Trapppings from grease traps; FFA often > 15%, highly contaminated.
- **Soapstock:** By-product of vegetable oil refining; high FFA (often > 50%).
- **Acidulated Soapstock:** Post-acidulation product; very high FFA.
- **Distillers Corn Oil (DCO):** Extracted from DDGS in bioethanol plants.
- **Crude Palm Oil Mill Effluent (POME):** Lipid fraction recovered from palm oil mills.

**3. Third-Generation Feedstocks (Microbial):**
- Algal oils (microalgae like *Chlorella*, *Nannochloropsis*), fungal lipids, bacterial oils.
- **Advantage:** Non-arable land usage; high lipid productivity per hectare.

**4. Fourth-Generation Feedstocks (Engineered):**
- Genetically modified microalgae with enhanced CO₂ capture and lipid yield.

### 2.2 Chemical Composition of Feedstock

Biodiesel feedstocks are predominantly **triglycerides (TAGs)** — esters formed between one glycerol molecule (C₃H₅(OH)₃) and three fatty acid chains.

$$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{ROH} \xrightarrow{\text{Catalyst}} 3\,\text{FAME} + \text{Glycerol}$$

The dominant fatty acids influencing biodiesel properties are:
- **Palmitic acid (C16:0)**
- **Stearic acid (C18:0)** — saturated, raises melting point
- **Oleic acid (C18:1)** — monounsaturated, optimal balance
- **Linoleic acid (C18:2)** — polyunsaturated, prone to oxidation
- **Linolenic acid (C18:3)** — highly polyunsaturated, causes rancidity

### 2.3 Key Properties of Feedstock Affecting Biodiesel Quality

- **Free Fatty Acid (FFA) content:** Determines catalyst selection (alkali vs. acid).
- **Moisture content:** Promotes soap formation; must be < 0.5%.
- **Phosphorus / Gums:** Poison heterogeneous catalysts; cause engine deposits.
- **Insoluble impurities:** Affect reaction kinetics and final product purity.
- **Iodine value:** Indicates degree of unsaturation → oxidative stability.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Expression | Standard / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Acid Value (AV)** | $AV = \dfrac{V_{KOH} \times N_{KOH} \times 56.1}{W_{sample}}$ | mg KOH/g oil | Quantifies FFA; dictates catalyst path |
| **Saponification Value (SV)** | $SV = \dfrac{(V_{blank} - V_{sample}) \times N_{KOH} \times 56.1}{W_{sample}}$ | mg KOH/g oil | Inverse to mean MW of fatty acids |
| **Average Molecular Weight of Oil** | $MW_{oil} = \dfrac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{SV}$ | g/mol | Stoichiometric calculations |
| **Average Molecular Weight of Fatty Acid** | $MW_{FA} = \dfrac{MW_{oil}}{3}$ | g/mol | Required for methanol molar ratio |
| **Iodine Value (IV)** | $IV = \dfrac{(V_{blank} - V_{sample}) \times N \times 12.69}{W_{sample}}$ | g I₂/100g oil | Measures unsaturation |
| **Cetane Number (CN)** — empirical | $CN = 46.3 + \dfrac{5458}{SV} - 0.225 \times IV$ | dimensionless | Ignition quality |
| **Degree of Unsaturation (DU)** | $DU = \text{Monoene\%} + 2 \times \text{Diene\%} + 3 \times \text{Triene\%}$ | % | Predicts oxidative stability |
| **Theoretical Methanol Required** | $m_{MeOH} = \dfrac{MW_{oil} \times \text{ratio} \times 32.04}{MW_{oil} \times 3}$ per kg | kg | Stoichiometric input |
| **Mass of Glycerol Released** | Theoretical: 10.41 wt% of oil | % | Mass balance check |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants Used:** Molar mass of KOH = **56.1 g/mol**, Molar mass of Na₂S₂O₃ = **158.11 g/mol**, Atomic mass of Iodine = **126.9**, so 12.69 = 126.9/10. **Universal Gas Constant R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** when dealing with reactor thermodynamics.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refinery Sizing:** Knowing the **MW of oil** allows engineers to size reactor volume, agitator power (Reynolds number), and methanol recovery columns.
- **Logistics:** Density and viscosity data inform pipeline pumping specifications for waste oil collection networks.
- **Quality Control:** Acid value testing at the truck-receiving bay prevents costly contamination of alkali-catalyzed batches.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Average Molecular Weight of Oil from Saponification Value

The saponification reaction is:

$$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{KOH} \rightarrow \text{Glycerol} + 3\,\text{R-COOK}$$

By definition, **1 mole of triglyceride** consumes **3 moles of KOH**, which equals **3 × 56.1 g = 168.3 g** of KOH.

Let:
- $SV$ = Saponification Value (mg KOH consumed per g of oil)
- $W_{oil}$ = Mass of oil (grams)
- $n_{KOH}$ = Moles of KOH consumed

KOH mass consumed (in mg) per gram of oil is:

$$\frac{m_{KOH}}{W_{oil}} = SV \quad (\text{mg KOH/g oil})$$

KOH mass in grams per gram of oil:

$$\frac{m_{KOH}}{W_{oil}} = \frac{SV}{1000} \quad (\text{g KOH/g oil})$$

Moles of KOH per gram of oil:

$$n_{KOH} = \frac{SV / 1000}{56.1} \quad (\text{mol KOH/g oil})$$

Since 3 moles of KOH react with 1 mole of triglyceride, moles of triglyceride per gram of oil:

$$n_{oil} = \frac{SV}{3 \times 1000 \times 56.1}$$

Therefore, the molecular weight of the oil (g/mol) is:

$$MW_{oil} = \frac{1}{n_{oil}} = \frac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{SV}$$

$$\boxed{MW_{oil} = \frac{168300}{SV} \quad (\text{g/mol})}$$

> **Verification:** For soybean oil with $SV \approx 190$ mg KOH/g, $MW_{oil} = 168300/190 \approx 886$ g/mol ✓ (literature value: 870–900 g/mol).

### 3.2 Worked Example: UCO Characterization for Batch Sizing

**Given Data:**
- UCO Sample mass, $W_{sample}$ = **2.0 g**
- Normality of KOH, $N_{KOH}$ = **0.1 N**
- Volume of KOH used in titration, $V$ = **4.8 mL**
- Saponification titration: $V_{blank}$ = **50.0 mL**, $V_{sample}$ = **22.6 mL**
- Methanol-to-oil molar ratio = **6:1**

**Step 1: Calculate Acid Value**

$$AV = \frac{4.8 \times 0.1 \times 56.1}{2.0} = \frac{26.928}{2.0} = 13.46 \text{ mg KOH/g}$$

Since $AV > 2$ mg KOH/g, **acid pre-treatment (esterification with H₂SO₄) is mandatory** before alkali transesterification.

**Step 2: Calculate Saponification Value**

$$SV = \frac{(50.0 - 22.6) \times 0.1 \times 56.1}{2.0} = \frac{153.79}{2.0} = 76.9 \text{ mg KOH/g}$$

> **Note:** $SV$ is unusually low because waste oils contain significant non-saponifiable matter (water, polar contaminants). This is a hallmark of used feedstock.

**Step 3: Calculate Average Molecular Weight of UCO**

$$MW_{oil} = \frac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{SV} = \frac{168300}{76.9} \approx 2188.6 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 4: Methanol Required for 1 kg of UCO at 6:1 Molar Ratio**

$$\text{Moles of UCO per kg} = \frac{1000}{2188.6} = 0.457 \text{ mol}$$

$$\text{Moles of methanol} = 6 \times 0.457 = 2.742 \text{ mol}$$

$$\text{Mass of methanol} = 2.742 \times 32.04 \approx 87.85 \text{ g per kg oil}$$

**Step 5: Theoretical Biodiesel Yield (3 moles FAME per mole TAG)**

$$\text{Moles of FAME} = 3 \times 0.457 = 1.371 \text{ mol (per kg oil)}$$

Using average FAME MW ≈ 296 g/mol (e.g., methyl oleate = 296.5):

$$\text{Mass of FAME} = 1.371 \times 296 = 405.8 \text{ g} \approx 40.6\% \text{ wt yield}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Feedstock vs. Process Route

| Feedstock Type | FFA (%) | Moisture (%) | Recommended Process | Catalyst | T (°C) | Yield (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Refined Vegetable Oil | < 0.5 | < 0.1 | Direct Alkali Transesterification | NaOH / KOH (1%) | 60–65 | 95–98 |
| Used Cooking Oil (UCO) | 2–6 | < 0.5 | Two-Step: Acid + Alkali | H₂SO₄ → NaOH | 50–65 | 90–96 |
| Animal Tallow | 1–5 | < 0.5 | Pre-melt + Alkali / Acid + Alkali | NaOH / KOH | 60–70 | 85–95 |
| Yellow Grease | 5–15 | 0.5–2 | Two-Step Acid + Alkali | H₂SO₄ → NaOH | 55–65 | 88–94 |
| Brown Grease / Trap Grease | > 15 | > 2 | Acid Esterification × Alkali | H₂SO₄ (×2) → NaOH | 60–70 | 80–90 |
| Soapstock | > 50 | Variable | Acidulation → Methanolysis | p-TsOH / H₂SO₄ | 65–80 | 85–92 |
| Algal Oil | 1–10 | Variable | Dewatering → Alkali | NaOH | 50–60 | 90–96 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Always convert analytical data (FFA%, AV) into actionable process decisions in your answer. Examiners reward the *justification* of process choice over rote memorization.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Feedstock Classification and Processing Route

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Biodiesel Feedstocks] --> B[First Generation - Edible Oils]
    A --> C[Second Generation - Waste Oils]
    A --> D[Third Generation - Microbial]
    A --> E[Fourth Generation - Engineered]

    B --> B1[Soybean]
    B --> B2[Rapeseed]
    B --> B3[Palm Oil]
    B --> B4[Sunflower]

    C --> C1[Used Cooking Oil UCO]
    C --> C2[Animal Fats Tallow Lard]
    C --> C3[Yellow and Brown Grease]
    C --> C4[Soapstock Acidulated]
    C --> C5[Distillers Corn Oil]

    D --> D1[Microalgae Lipids]
    D --> D2[Fungal Oils]

    E --> E1[Genetically Modified Algae]

    C1 --> F1{FFA Check}
    F1 -->|FFA less than 2 percent| G1[Direct Alkali Transesterification]
    F1 -->|FFA greater than 2 percent| H1[Acid Pre Esterification H2SO4]

    H1 --> G1
    C2 --> F1
    C3 --> F1
    C4 --> I1[Strong Acid Esterification]
    I1 --> G1

    G1 --> J[Biodiesel FAME plus Glycerol]
    J --> K1[Purification Washing]
    J --> K2[Glycerol Recovery]
    K1 --> L[Final Biodiesel ASTM D6751]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Pre-Treatment Decision Workflow for Waste Oils

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Raw Waste Oil Arrival] --> P2[Settling and Filtration]
    P2 --> P3[Moisture Analysis]
    P2 --> P4[Acid Value Titration]

    P3 -->|Moisture greater than 0.5 percent| P5[Vacuum Drying at 105 degree C]
    P3 -->|Moisture within limit| P6[Deacidification Stage]

    P4 -->|AV less than 2 mg KOH per g| P7[Direct Transesterification]
    P4 -->|AV between 2 and 15| P8[Single Stage Esterification]
    P4 -->|AV greater than 15| P9[Two Stage Acid Esterification]

    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7
    P8 --> P7
    P9 --> P8

    P7 --> Q1[Alkali Catalyst NaOH or KOH]
    Q1 --> R1[Reaction at 60 degree C for 2 hours]
    R1 --> S1[Gravity Separation]
    S1 --> S2[Biodiesel Layer]
    S1 --> S3[Glycerol Layer]
    S2 --> T1[Water Wash]
    T1 --> U1[Drying and Quality Check]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Integrated Waste-to-Biodiesel Plant Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN1[Feedstock Receiving Bay] --> IN2[Pre-Treatment Section]
    IN2 --> IN3[Reaction Section Two Stage]
    IN3 --> IN4[Separation Section Centrifuge]
    IN4 --> IN5[PURIFICATION SECTION]
    IN4 --> IN6[GLYCEROL SECTION]

    IN5 --> OUT1[Finished Biodiesel Storage]
    OUT1 --> OUT2[Quality Lab GC plus ASTM Tests]
    OUT1 --> OUT3[Dispatch to Blending Terminal]

    IN6 --> GLY1[Crude Glycerol 80 percent]
    GLY1 --> GLY2[Refining to Pharma Grade]
    GLY2 --> GLY3[Co Product Revenue]

    IN3 -.->|Steam| UT1[Boiler Section]
    UT1 -.->|Heat| IN3
    UT1 -.->|Power| UT2[Generator]
    IN4 -.->|Wastewater| WT1[Effluent Treatment]
    WT1 -.->|Treated Water| UT1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]
**Classify biodiesel feedstocks into generations. Give two examples each for second and third-generation feedstock.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Biodiesel feedstocks are classified into four generations based on origin and sustainability:

- **First Generation:** Edible vegetable oils — Soybean oil, Rapeseed oil.
- **Second Generation:** Waste / non-edible lipids — Used Cooking Oil (UCO), animal tallow, soapstock, yellow grease. *(2 marks for naming and defining)*
- **Third Generation:** Microbial lipids — Oils from microalgae (*Chlorella vulgaris*), fungal lipids. *(1 mark)*
- **Fourth Generation:** Genetically engineered microalgae with enhanced lipid productivity.

**Distribution:** Definition (1M) + 2 examples × 0.5M + classification explanation (1M).

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]
**Define Acid Value. Why is the determination of acid value critical in selecting the catalyst route for biodiesel production from waste cooking oil?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition:** Acid Value (AV) is the milligrams of KOH required to neutralize the free fatty acids (FFA) present in 1 gram of oil/fat.

$$AV = \frac{V_{KOH} \times N_{KOH} \times 56.1}{W_{sample}} \text{ (mg KOH/g)}$$ *(1 Mark)*

**Criticality for Catalyst Selection:** *(2 Marks)*
- If $AV < 2$ mg KOH/g, alkali-catalyzed transesterification (NaOH/KOH) can be directly used.
- If $AV > 2$ mg KOH/g, alkali catalyst reacts with FFA to form soap (saponification), reducing yield and complicating product separation. Hence, an acid-catalyzed esterification (H₂SO₄ + methanol) must precede the alkali step to convert FFA → FAME, lowering AV below 2.
- For UCO with AV typically 5–15, the **two-step acid + alkali** route is mandatory.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 4 Choice A]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the various second-generation feedstocks used for biodiesel production. Compare their FFA content, typical yield, and process challenges. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A sample of used cooking oil weighing **5.0 g** was titrated with **0.1 N KOH** and required **6.2 mL** for neutralization. The same oil gave a saponification value of **188 mg KOH/g**. Calculate:
   (i) Acid Value of the oil
   (ii) Average Molecular Weight of the oil
   (iii) The mass of methanol required to transesterify 1 metric ton of this oil at a 6:1 molar ratio. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Answer — Question A**

**(a) Second-Generation Feedstocks (7 Marks):**

Second-generation feedstocks are non-edible or waste lipid sources, addressing the food-vs-fuel debate and reducing feedstock cost.

| Feedstock | FFA (%) | Biodiesel Yield (%) | Process Challenges |
|---|---|---|---|
| Used Cooking Oil (UCO) | 2–15 | 88–95 | Variable quality, water content, polymerization products |
| Animal Tallow | 1–8 | 85–92 | High melting point (solid at RT), high saturation |
| Yellow Grease | 5–15 | 88–93 | Particulate contamination |
| Brown Grease | 15–40 | 75–88 | High FFA, water, grit → requires multi-stage esterification |
| Soapstock | 50–90 | 80–92 | Very high FFA; needs acidulation; salt removal |
| Distillers Corn Oil | 5–15 | 88–95 | Phospholipids, waxes |

**Key Engineering Challenges:** *(3 Marks)*
1. Heterogeneous FFA content demands flexible catalyst systems.
2. High moisture requires drying (vacuum/azeotropic) to avoid saponification.
3. Presence of gums/phosphatides deactivates heterogeneous catalysts.
4. Cold-flow properties poor for saturated feeds — needs winterization or additives.

**Process Selection:** For feeds with FFA 2–15%, the **two-step acid esterification followed by alkali transesterification** is the universally adopted route (Canakci & Van Gerpen, 2001 framework).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $W_{sample} = 5.0$ g
- $N_{KOH} = 0.1$ N
- $V_{KOH} = 6.2$ mL
- $SV = 188$ mg KOH/g
- Molar ratio = 6:1
- Mass of oil = 1 metric ton = $10^6$ g

**(i) Acid Value (2 Marks):**

$$AV = \frac{6.2 \times 0.1 \times 56.1}{5.0} = \frac{34.782}{5.0} = 6.956 \text{ mg KOH/g}$$

Since $AV \approx 6.96 > 2$, **acid pre-treatment is mandatory**. *(0.5 M interpretation)*

**(ii) Average Molecular Weight of Oil (2 Marks):**

$$MW_{oil} = \frac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{SV} = \frac{168300}{188} = 895.21 \text{ g/mol}$$

**(iii) Mass of Methanol Required for 1 ton of oil (3 Marks):**

Moles of oil per ton:

$$n_{oil} = \frac{10^6}{895.21} = 1117.0 \text{ mol}$$

Moles of methanol at 6:1 ratio:

$$n_{MeOH} = 6 \times 1117.0 = 6702.1 \text{ mol}$$

Mass of methanol:

$$m_{MeOH} = 6702.1 \times 32.04 = 214735 \text{ g} \approx 214.74 \text{ kg}$$

**Final Answer:** $\approx \mathbf{214.74 \text{ kg}}$ of methanol per ton of UCO.

---

#### Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 4 Choice B]**

**(a)** Explain the chemical composition of triglycerides and fatty acids commonly found in biodiesel feedstocks. Discuss how the fatty acid profile influences the properties of biodiesel produced. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** During the characterization of a yellow grease sample, the following data was obtained:
   - Sample mass = 4.0 g
   - Blank titration volume = 48.0 mL of 0.5 N HCl
   - Sample titration volume = 21.5 mL of 0.5 N HCl
   - Saponification Value = 165 mg KOH/g

Calculate the molecular weight of the oil and discuss what the SV indicates about the average fatty acid chain length. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Answer — Question B**

**(a) Chemical Composition (7 Marks):**

A triglyceride (triacylglycerol, TAG) is the triester of glycerol (propane-1,2,3-triol, C₃H₅(OH)₃) with three long-chain fatty acids:

$$\text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3 + 3\,\text{R-COOH} \rightarrow \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OOC-R})_3 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Where R is an alkyl chain of typically 4–28 carbons. *(2 Marks for structure)*

**Common Fatty Acids:** *(3 Marks)*

| Fatty Acid | Carbon Notation | Saturation | Effect on Biodiesel |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lauric | C12:0 | Saturated | Low viscosity, low CN |
| Palmitic | C16:0 | Saturated | High CN, poor cold flow |
| Stearic | C18:0 | Saturated | High melting point |
| Oleic | C18:1 | Mono-unsat. | **Optimal — balanced CN & cold flow** |
| Linoleic | C18:2 | Poly-unsat. | Low CN, oxidizes easily |
| Linolenic | C18:3 | Poly-unsat. | Highly prone to oxidation |

**Influence on Biodiesel Properties:** *(2 Marks)*
- **Saturated fatty acids** → High cetane number, good oxidation stability, but **poor cold-flow properties** (high cloud/pour point).
- **Unsaturated fatty acids** → Good low-temperature fluidity, but **lower oxidative stability** (linolenic content should be < 12% per EN 14214).
- An ideal feedstock has a **balanced oleic-linoleic profile** (e.g., canola, high-oleic sunflower).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $W_{sample} = 4.0$ g
- $V_{blank} = 48.0$ mL, $V_{sample} = 21.5$ mL (0.5 N HCl)
- $SV = 165$ mg KOH/g

**Molecular Weight Calculation (3 Marks):**

$$MW_{oil} = \frac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{SV} = \frac{168300}{165} = 1020 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Mean Molecular Weight of Fatty Acid:** *(2 Marks)*

$$MW_{FA} = \frac{MW_{oil}}{3} = \frac{1020}{3} = 340 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Interpretation Regarding Chain Length:** *(2 Marks)*

A $MW_{FA}$ of 340 g/mol is significantly higher than typical vegetable oil fatty acids (~270–285 g/mol for C18). This indicates:
- **Longer average carbon chain length** (likely averaging > C20).
- Consistent with **animal fats / tallow** which contain appreciable palmitic (C16, MW = 256) and stearic (C18, MW = 284) acids but also longer chain saturated fats.
- The **high SV indirectly confirms shorter chains** (since SV is inverse of MW), but here the SV is comparatively low → longer chains / more unsaponifiable matter.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common Mark Loss Zones):**
> - **Confusing Saponification Value with Acid Value** — they are different titrations. AV uses the *sample* directly; SV uses a *back-titration* with HCl after saponification. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks.
> - **Forgetting to multiply by 1000** when converting mg KOH → g KOH in MW derivation. This single error causes off-by-1000 in molecular weight.
> - **Using MW of methanol as 32 g/mol instead of 32.04 g/mol** — acceptable, but for high-precision modules (mass balance in design) the precise value is expected.
> - **Not stating the process route implication** of AV > 2 — examiners award 1 mark specifically for the catalyst justification line.
> - **Skipping units** — every numerical answer MUST carry units (g/mol, kg, mg KOH/g).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Feedstock is 70–80% of biodiesel production cost** — waste oils (UCO, tallow, yellow grease) are economically and ethically preferred.
- **Acid Value > 2 mg KOH/g → two-step route** (Acid esterification + Alkali transesterification). This is the single most tested numerical threshold.
- **Saponification Value inversely proportional to molecular weight** of the oil.
- **Average MW of oil = 168,300 / SV** (g/mol) — memorize this derived formula.
- **Methanol stoichiometric ratio = 3:1** (theoretical); **6:1 to 12:1** is used industrially to drive equilibrium.
- **FFA + KOH → Soap + Water** is the unwanted saponification reaction that destroys alkali catalysts — water must be < 0.5%.
- **Saturated FAs** (palmitic, stearic) → high cetane, poor cold flow.
- **Polyunsaturated FAs** (linoleic, linolenic) → low cetane, prone to oxidation.
- **Methanol MW = 32.04 g/mol**, KOH MW = 56.1 g/mol, Glycerol MW = 92.09 g/mol, Methyl oleate MW ≈ 296.5 g/mol.
- **Standard yield range:** 85–98% depending on feedstock and process maturity.
- **Reactor temperature range:** 50–70 °C (limited by methanol boiling point at 65 °C at 1 atm).
- **Catalyst loading:** 0.5–1.5 wt% (alkali) or 1–5 wt% (acid).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Flow and Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT836 - WASTE TO ENERGY TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Process Flow and Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Waste-to-Energy (WtE) engineering, **Process Flow and Equipment** refers to the systematic graphical and engineering documentation of the sequential unit operations, material streams, energy streams, and the mechanical apparatus required to convert heterogeneous solid waste into useful energy vectors such as electricity, heat, biofuels, or gaseous fuels.

A **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** is a standardized engineering schematic that illustrates the interconnections between feed streams, unit operations (e.g., shredders, combustors, heat exchangers, scrubbers), product streams, and by-product streams. It is governed by ISO 10628 and ANSI Y14 standards for symbols and conventions.

The **equipment set** encompasses every physical hardware component — from Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) receiving hoppers and trommel screens to moving grate combustors, rotary kilns, anaerobic digesters, scrubbers, electrostatic precipitators (ESPs), and bag-house filters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 specifically focuses on the **process integration** of these unit operations, requiring students to demonstrate the ability to *trace mass and energy flows*, *identify critical control points*, and *select appropriate equipment* based on waste characteristics and desired energy output.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a Waste-to-Energy plant as a **giant industrial kitchen** — except instead of cooking food, it "cooks" garbage to extract energy.

- **Pre-treatment hall** ≈ the *kitchen pantry*, where you sort, wash, and chop (shred/sort) the ingredients (waste) before cooking.
- **Reactor/Combustor/Gasifier/Digester** ≈ the *stove or oven*, where the actual transformation (combustion, gasification, or biological digestion) happens.
- **Heat Recovery Boiler** ≈ the *pressure cooker* placed on the stove — it captures the steam (energy) released during cooking.
- **Air Pollution Control (APC) System** ≈ the *vent hood and chimney filter* that cleans the smoke before releasing it.
- **Generator/Turbine** ≈ the *electric mixer* that converts the captured steam motion into electricity.

Just as a chef needs the right sequence and tools to prepare a dish, a WtE engineer needs a logically sequenced process flow and correctly selected equipment to convert waste into energy safely and efficiently.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following metrics are universally cited in KTU-validated WtE literature:

- **Calorific Value (CV) of MSW:** typically **8000 – 12,000 kJ/kg** (≈ 2.0 – 2.9 MWh/tonne).
- **Moisture content of fresh MSW:** typically **20% – 55%** (wet basis).
- **Combustion efficiency (η_c):** **> 99.99%** destruction efficiency of principal organic hazardous constituents (POHCs) for compliance.
- **Thermal efficiency of mass-burn incinerator:** **65% – 80%** (with heat recovery).
- **Air-Fuel ratio (stoichiometric for typical MSW):** approximately **5–7 kg air/kg waste**.
- **Excess air requirement:** typically **20% – 40%** above stoichiometric.
- **Grate heat release rate:** **300–600 kW/m²** for modern moving grates.

> [!NOTE]
> The **"Rule of Thumb" for MSW combustion** is: *1 tonne of MSW ≈ 1.4 MWh of thermal energy*, of which approximately **20%–30%** is recovered as electricity in modern European-style plants.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Building Blocks of Process Flow

Every WtE process flow can be decomposed into **four universal macro-stages**, irrespective of the conversion technology chosen (thermal, biological, or physico-chemical):

### Stage 1: Pre-Treatment (Waste Reception & Preparation)

This stage transforms heterogeneous, raw MSW into a homogeneous, combustible feedstock. The unit operations typically include:

1. **Weighbridge & Reception Hall** — quantification and visual inspection.
2. **Storage Bunker** — typically holds **2–5 days** of plant capacity; crane-claw retrieval.
3. **Primary Shredder** — size reduction to **< 300 mm**.
4. **Trommel Screen** — separates fines (< 30 mm) for biological treatment.
5. **Magnetic Separator & Eddy Current Separator** — recovers ferrous and non-ferrous metals.
6. **Drying Bunker (optional)** — reduces moisture to **< 25%** for combustion stability.

### Stage 2: Conversion (Thermochemical / Biological Reactor)

The core conversion technology. The selection governs the entire downstream equipment chain:

- **Mass-Burn Incineration (Moving Grate)** — dominant for unsorted MSW.
- **Fluidized Bed Combustion (FBC)** — preferred for RDF (Refuse Derived Fuel).
- **Rotary Kiln** — for hazardous waste.
- **Gasifier (Downdraft/Updraft/Bubbling Fluidized Bed)** — for syngas production.
- **Anaerobic Digester / Landfill Gas Recovery** — for biogas (CH₄ + CO₂).

### Stage 3: Energy Recovery

Heat released (or biogas produced) is converted into mechanical/electrical energy:

- **Waste Heat Boiler** → Steam (typically 40 bar, 400°C) → Turbine → Generator.
- **ORC (Organic Rankine Cycle)** for low-temperature heat (< 300°C).
- **Biogas CHP Engine** for anaerobic digestion.

### Stage 4: Air Pollution Control (APC) & Residue Management

Pollutants such as dioxins (PCDD/F), HCl, SOx, NOx, and heavy metals must be reduced to **< 0.1 ng TEQ/Nm³** for dioxins and **< 10 mg/Nm³** for particulates.

Typical APC train: **Cyclone → Scrubber → ESP → Bag Filter → SCR/SNCR → Wet Scrubber → Stack**.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Equipment Sequencing

The sequence is **not arbitrary** — it is dictated by:

- **Thermodynamic feasibility** — high-temperature reactions downstream of low-temperature drying.
- **Pollution prevention** — air introduced *after* volatiles have pyrolyzed (gasification staging).
- **Heat integration (pinch analysis)** — hot streams preheat cold streams to maximize efficiency.
- **Material handling** — size must decrease before combustion; bulk density must increase before feeding.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Formula | Symbol / Variable | Typical Units | Application in WtE |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stoichiometric Air Requirement $\text{Air}_{stoich} = \dfrac{11.6 \, C + 34.8 \, (H_2 - O_2/8) + 4.35 \, S}{23.2}$ | C, H₂, O₂, S in fuel mass fractions | kg air/kg fuel | Mass-burn combustion |
| 2 | Excess Air % $\lambda = \dfrac{\text{Air}_{actual}}{\text{Air}_{stoich}} \times 100$ | $\lambda$ | % | Combustion control |
| 3 | Energy Recovery Efficiency $\eta_{rec} = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in,waste}} \times 100$ | $\eta_{rec}$ | % | Plant benchmarking |
| 4 | Calorific Value (Gross) Dulong's eq. $GCV = 337 \, C + 1442 \, (H_2 - O_2/8) + 93 \, S$ | C, H, O, S in dry mass | kJ/kg | Fuel characterization |
| 5 | Steam Power Output $P_{steam} = \dot{m}_{steam} \, (h_{out} - h_{in}) \, \eta_{turb}$ | mass flow rate, enthalpy | MW | Boiler-turbine sizing |
| 6 | Biogas Yield (AD) $Y_{CH_4} = 0.35 \, (VS_{loaded} - VS_{effluent})$ | Volatile Solids | m³ CH₄/kg VS | Anaerobic digester design |
| 7 | Dioxin Destruction Efficiency $\eta_{dest} = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100$ | Concentration | % | APC validation |
| 8 | Specific Energy Consumption $SEC = \dfrac{E_{aux}}{M_{waste}}$ | E_aux = parasitic load | kWh/tonne | Net efficiency calculation |
| 9 | Combustion Efficiency $\eta_c = \dfrac{GCV_{waste} - (CV_{ash} + CV_{flue})}{GCV_{waste}}$ | Calorific losses | % | Incinerator performance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid (KTU Examiner's favorite):** *Dulong's formula* is the most tested equation in Module 4. Always remember: **C contributes 337, H contributes 1442, S contributes 93** (in kJ/kg).

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

The Process Flow and Equipment framework is foundational to:

- **Plant EPC contracting** (Engineering, Procurement, Construction).
- **Environmental Impact Assessments (EIA)** under India's EIA Notification 2006 (and 2020 amendments).
- **Sustainable finance** — funding agencies (World Bank, ADB, IFC) require PFDs before disbursement.
- **Digital twin development** — modern WtE plants simulate their PFDs in real-time using **DWSIM, Aspen Plus, or Thermoflow** software.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Stoichiometric Air Requirement (for an MSW-like fuel)

Consider a heterogeneous MSW fuel with ultimate analysis (dry mass basis):
- Carbon (C) = **30%**
- Hydrogen (H₂) = **4%**
- Oxygen (O₂) = **20%**
- Sulphur (S) = **0.2%**
- Nitrogen (N₂) = **0.5%**
- Moisture = **30%** (wet basis)
- Ash = **15.3%** (by difference, wet basis)

We seek the **stoichiometric air requirement** per kg of wet waste, assuming 21% O₂ and 79% N₂ by volume in air (molar basis).

### Step 1 — Convert Wet Basis to Dry Basis

The dry mass fraction for C:

$$
C_{dry} = \dfrac{C_{wet}}{1 - M} = \dfrac{0.30}{1 - 0.30} = 0.4286
$$

Similarly:
- $H_{dry} = 0.04 / 0.70 = 0.0571$
- $O_{dry} = 0.20 / 0.70 = 0.2857$
- $S_{dry} = 0.002 / 0.70 = 0.00286$
- $N_{dry} = 0.005 / 0.70 = 0.00714$

### Step 2 — Apply the Standard Stoichiometric Equation

Each combustible element reacts as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C + O_2 &\rightarrow CO_2 \quad (\text{1 mol O}_2 \text{ per mol C}) \\
H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2} O_2 &\rightarrow H_2O \quad (\text{0.5 mol O}_2 \text{ per mol H}_2) \\
S + O_2 &\rightarrow SO_2 \quad (\text{1 mol O}_2 \text{ per mol S})
\end{aligned}
$$

Mass of O₂ required per kg of dry fuel:

$$
\text{O}_2^{req} = \dfrac{32}{12} C_{dry} + \dfrac{32}{4} H_{dry} - \dfrac{32}{32} O_{dry} + \dfrac{32}{32} S_{dry}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Mass Fractions

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{O}_2^{req} &= 2.667 \times 0.4286 + 8.00 \times 0.0571 - 1.00 \times 0.2857 + 1.00 \times 0.00286 \\
&= 1.1430 + 0.4568 - 0.2857 + 0.00286 \\
&= 1.3170 \ \text{kg O}_2 / \text{kg dry fuel}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Air Mass

Since air contains 23.2% O₂ by mass:

$$
\text{Air}_{stoich,dry} = \dfrac{1.3170}{0.232} = 5.677 \ \text{kg air / kg dry fuel}
$$

### Step 5 — Adjust for Moisture

For wet waste, the effective dry mass per kg wet waste is (1 − M) = 0.70:

$$
\text{Air}_{stoich,wet} = 5.677 \times 0.70 = 3.974 \ \text{kg air / kg wet waste}
$$

### Step 6 — Apply Excess Air Factor

With **30% excess air** ($\lambda = 1.30$):

$$
\text{Air}_{actual} = 3.974 \times 1.30 = 5.166 \ \text{kg air / kg wet waste}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This final value (**~5.2 kg air/kg waste**) is within the typical engineering range cited in Section 1.3, confirming the methodology is consistent with industry practice.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Equipment Selection vs. Waste Type

| Equipment Category | Specific Equipment | Best-Suited Waste | Throughput Range | Key Design Parameter | Operating T (°C) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Size Reduction | Primary Shredder (Slow-speed) | Bulky MSW, wood | 10–40 t/h | Torque, blade spacing | Ambient |
| Size Reduction | Secondary Shredder (Hammer mill) | RDF, tyres | 5–25 t/h | Hammer speed, screen size | Ambient |
| Separation | Trommel Screen | Mixed MSW | 20–80 t/h | Drum diameter, rotation | Ambient |
| Separation | Magnetic Separator (Overband) | MSW, C&I waste | 50–150 t/h | Magnetic field strength | Ambient |
| Separation | Eddy Current Separator | Pre-shredded MSW | 5–20 t/h | Rotor speed | Ambient |
| Thermal Conversion | Moving Grate Incinerator | Mixed MSW | 5–50 t/h | Grate area | 850–1100 |
| Thermal Conversion | Bubbling Fluidized Bed | RDF, sewage sludge | 3–30 t/h | Fluidization velocity | 750–900 |
| Thermal Conversion | Rotary Kiln | Hazardous, medical | 1–10 t/h | Residence time | 850–1300 |
| Thermal Conversion | Downdraft Gasifier | Dry biomass, RDF | 0.5–5 t/h | Equivalence ratio | 700–1000 |
| Biological | Anaerobic Digester (CSTR) | Organic fraction MSW | 1–20 t/d VS | HRT (15–40 d) | 35–55 |
| Biological | Landfill Gas Well | Landfilled waste | Variable | Well spacing | Ambient |
| Heat Recovery | Water-Tube Boiler | All thermal streams | 5–80 t/h steam | Steam pressure | 400–540 (sat. steam) |
| Heat Recovery | Economizer | Flue gas | — | ΔT pinch | 200–350 |
| Heat Recovery | Superheater | Combustion gas | — | Tube metallurgy | 400–540 |
| Power Generation | Steam Turbine (Rankine) | Steam from boiler | 1–50 MWe | Inlet T, P | 400–540 |
| Power Generation | CHP Gas Engine | Biogas, landfill gas | 0.5–5 MWe | Gas quality | — |
| APC | Cyclone | Coarse dust | — | Inlet velocity | 150–200 |
| APC | Electrostatic Precipitator | Fine particulates | — | Migration velocity | 130–180 |
| APC | Bag Filter (Fabric) | Particulates + acid gases | — | A/C ratio | 130–180 |
| APC | Wet Scrubber | HCl, SO₂, HF | — | L/G ratio | 60–90 |
| APC | SCR / SNCR | NOx | — | NH₃ slip | 200–400 (SCR) |
| APC | Activated Carbon Injection | Dioxins, Hg | — | Contact time | 130–180 |
| Residue | Bottom Ash Conveyor | Grate residue | 10–30% of feed | Cooling time | Ambient |
| Residue | Fly Ash Silo | APC residue | 3–5% of feed | Atex rated | Ambient |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Mass-Burn WtE Plant

This Mermaid diagram illustrates the **macro-architecture** of a typical 500 TPD Municipal Solid Waste Mass-Burn Incineration plant with combined heat and power (CHP) recovery and full Air Pollution Control (APC) train.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MSW Collection Trucks] --> B[Weighbridge and Tipping Hall]
    B --> C[Storage Bunker 3 Day Capacity]
    C --> D[Grab Crane Pre Mixing]
    D --> E[Feed Hopper with Hydraulic Ram Feeder]
    E --> F[Moving Grate Combustor]
    G[Primary Air Fan 30 percent Excess] --> F
    H[Secondary Air Injection Overfire] --> F
    F -->|Bottom Ash| I[Ash Quench and Conveying System]
    F -->|Hot Flue Gas 1000 deg C| J[Water Tube Boiler]
    J -->|Steam 40 bar 400 deg C| K[Steam Turbine Generator 12 MWe]
    K -->|Condensate Return| J
    J -->|Flue Gas 200 deg C| L[Economizer]
    L --> M[Quench Reactor Rapid Cool to 200 deg C]
    M --> N[Dry Scrubber Lime Injection]
    O[Activated Carbon Silo] --> N
    N --> P[Bag Filter Fabric]
    P --> Q[ID Fan Induced Draft]
    Q --> R[SCR NOx Reduction Unit]
    R --> S[Wet Scrubber HCl SO2 Removal]
    S --> T[Stack with CEMS Continuous Emission Monitoring]
    I --> U[Bottom Ash Recovery and Metal Sorting]
    P --> V[Fly Ash Stabilization and Landfill]
    K -->|Electricity| W[Grid Transformer 11 kV to 110 kV]
    K -->|Low Pressure Steam| X[District Heating or Process Steam]
```

### 4.2 Equipment Interconnection Logic — Biological + Thermal Hybrid

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw MSW] --> B[Trommel Screen 30 mm Aperture]
    B -->|Underflow Fines| C[Anaerobic Digester 35 deg C 30 day HRT]
    B -->|Overflow Oversize| D[Shredder 100 mm]
    D --> E[Magnetic Separator]
    E --> F[Eddy Current Separator]
    F --> G[Drying Shed 48 hour Air Drying]
    G --> H[Fluidized Bed Combustor 850 deg C]
    C -->|Biogas CH4 plus CO2| I[Gas Holder]
    I --> J[CHP Gas Engine 1 MWe]
    H --> K[Heat Recovery Boiler]
    K -->|Steam| L[Steam Turbine 5 MWe]
    J --> M[Grid Synchronization Panel]
    L --> M
    H -->|Ash| N[Ash Silo]
    C -->|Digestate| O[Compost Facility]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — APC Train Sequence and Pollutant Removal Targets

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Boiler Outlet 200 deg C Flue Gas] --> B[Economizer Heat Recovery]
    B --> C[Quench Rapid Cooling 200 to 80 deg C in 0.5 s]
    C --> D[Dry Lime Scrubber HCl SO2]
    D --> E[Activated Carbon Dioxin and Hg Adsorption]
    E --> F[Bag Filter Particulate Removal 99.9 percent]
    F --> G[SCR NOx Reduction NH3 plus Urea]
    G --> H[Stack with CEMS]
    F -.->|Collected Dust| I[Fly Ash Conditioning]
    I --> J[Hazardous Waste Landfill]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reading Guide for the Diagrams:**
> - Arrows represent **mass or energy streams** (not physical wire/pipe connections).
> - Each block is a **functional unit operation** that can be substituted (e.g., ESP instead of Bag Filter).
> - **Bypass loops** and **recycle streams** are intentionally omitted for clarity; in practice, every boiler has at least one economizer bypass for temperature control.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**Define a Process Flow Diagram (PFD). Mention any four essential elements represented in it.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** is a standardized engineering schematic that represents the flow of materials, energy, and operating conditions through a sequence of unit operations in a process plant. As per ISO 10628-1/2, it provides a comprehensive overview of the process without the mechanical details of equipment.

**Four essential elements:**
1. **Process equipment** (combustors, boilers, separators, scrubbers).
2. **Process streams** (feed, product, recycle, and utility lines) with mass/energy flow rates.
3. **Operating conditions** (temperature, pressure, flow rate) annotated on key streams.
4. **Stream composition and mass/energy balance data** at critical points.

*(1 Mark for definition + 2 Marks for four elements correctly listed with brief explanation.)*

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**List the major unit operations involved in the pre-treatment of MSW before feeding it to an incinerator.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The major pre-treatment unit operations are:
1. **Waste reception and weighing** at the weighbridge.
2. **Storage in a bunker** with grab-crane mixing and homogenization.
3. **Size reduction** using primary shredders (< 300 mm) and secondary shredders (< 100 mm).
4. **Screening** via trommel screens to remove fines (< 30 mm).
5. **Magnetic and eddy current separation** to recover ferrous and non-ferrous metals.
6. **Drying** (optional) to reduce moisture content to < 25%.

*(3 Marks = 0.5 × 6 operations. Examiner expects at least four.)*

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Module Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Module 4 Model]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**With the help of a labelled Process Flow Diagram, describe the working of a Mass-Burn Moving Grate Incinerator-based Waste-to-Energy plant with a complete Air Pollution Control (APC) system.**

#### Model Solution Outline (7 Marks):

**Step 1 — Plant Overview (1 Mark):**  
Brief introduction stating input (MSW, 100–500 TPD), output (steam → electricity, ~10–15 MWe; bottom ash; fly ash), and the conversion route (thermal oxidation with energy recovery).

**Step 2 — Labelled PFD (3 Marks):**  
Draw the block-level PFD showing the following sequence:  
*Bunker → Grab Crane → Feed Hopper → Hydraulic Ram Feeder → Moving Grate (primary + secondary air injection zones) → Boiler → Economizer → Quench → Dry Scrubber → Bag Filter → SCR → Stack → Condensate return to boiler → Turbine Generator → Grid.*

**Step 3 — Critical Equipment Description (3 Marks):**
- **Moving Grate:** Reciprocating rollers, 850–1100 °C, residence time ~60 min, burnout efficiency > 99%.
- **Water-tube boiler:** Generates 40 bar, 400 °C steam; thermal efficiency 78%.
- **ESP/Bag Filter:** Particulate removal to < 30 mg/Nm³.
- **SCR:** NOx reduction to < 50 mg/Nm³ using urea/ammonia at 200–400 °C.
- **Wet Scrubber:** HCl, SO₂, HF removal via alkali slurry.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**An MSW has the following ultimate analysis (dry basis): C = 28%, H = 3.5%, O = 22%, N = 0.6%, S = 0.2%, Ash = 25%, Moisture = 20.7%. Calculate (i) the GCV using Dulong's formula, and (ii) the stoichiometric air required per kg of wet waste with 25% excess air.**

#### Model Solution (7 Marks):

**Step 1 — Convert to dry basis (already given).** (0.5 Marks)

**Step 2 — Apply Dulong's formula (2 Marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
GCV &= 337 \, C + 1442 \, \left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 93 \, S \\
&= 337 \times 28 + 1442 \times \left(3.5 - \dfrac{22}{8}\right) + 93 \times 0.2 \\
&= 9436 + 1442 \times 0.75 + 18.6 \\
&= 9436 + 1081.5 + 18.6 \\
&= 10536.1 \ \text{kJ/kg dry fuel}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert GCV to wet basis (0.5 Marks):**
$$
GCV_{wet} = 10536.1 \times (1 - 0.207) = 8355 \ \text{kJ/kg wet waste}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate O₂ required per kg dry fuel (2 Marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{O}_2 &= 2.667 C + 8.0 H - 1.0 O + 1.0 S \\
&= 2.667 \times 28 + 8.0 \times 3.5 - 1.0 \times 22 + 1.0 \times 0.2 \\
&= 74.676 + 28.0 - 22.0 + 0.2 \\
&= 80.876 \ \text{kg O}_2 / 100 \ \text{kg dry fuel} \\
&= 0.809 \ \text{kg O}_2 / \text{kg dry fuel}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to stoichiometric air (1 Mark):**
$$
\text{Air}_{stoich,dry} = \dfrac{0.809}{0.232} = 3.487 \ \text{kg air / kg dry fuel}
$$

**Step 6 — Adjust to wet basis and apply excess air (1 Mark):**
$$
\text{Air}_{actual,wet} = 3.487 \times (1 - 0.207) \times 1.25 = 3.459 \ \text{kg air / kg wet waste}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Module Question

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Explain the process flow and key equipment used in a typical Anaerobic Digestion (AD) plant for the organic fraction of municipal solid waste (OFMSW). Include the design basis for HRT, temperature, and biogas yield.**

#### Model Solution Outline (7 Marks):

**Step 1 — Pre-treatment of OFMSW (1.5 Marks):**  
Source-separated organic waste → shredding (< 40 mm) → pasteurization at 70 °C for 1 hour (to kill pathogens) → mixing with water/slurry (TS 8–12%).

**Step 2 — Digester Design Parameters (2 Marks):**
- **Type:** Continuously Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR) / Plug-flow.
- **HRT:** 20–40 days for mesophilic; 15–25 days for thermophilic.
- **Temperature:** Mesophilic (35–40 °C) — preferred for stability; Thermophilic (50–55 °C) — higher yield but energy-intensive.
- **pH:** 6.8–7.4.
- **C:N:P ratio:** 100:5:1 (optimal).

**Step 3 — Biogas Collection and Upgradation (1.5 Marks):**
- Biogas contains **55–70% CH₄, 30–45% CO₂**, trace H₂S.
- **H₂S removal** via iron oxide adsorption (< 200 ppm).
- **CO₂ removal** via water scrubber or amine absorption.
- Storage in **double-membrane gas holders**.

**Step 4 — Energy Conversion (1 Mark):**
- Purified CH₄ → CHP gas engine (1 MWe per 1.5–2 t/d OFMSW) → electricity + waste heat.
- Alternatively → Compressed Biogas (CBG) bottling.

**Step 5 — Digestate Management (1 Mark):**
- Dewatering via screw press → liquid fraction as bio-fertilizer; solid fraction as compost.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**A thermal WtE plant processes 400 TPD of MSW with a calorific value of 9 MJ/kg (wet basis). The plant has a steam boiler efficiency of 78% and a turbo-generator electrical efficiency of 32%. Estimate: (i) the daily thermal energy input, (ii) the daily steam energy output, and (iii) the daily electrical energy export. Comment on the overall plant efficiency.**

#### Model Solution (7 Marks):

**Step 1 — Daily thermal energy input (2 Marks):**
$$
E_{th,in} = 400{,}000 \, \text{kg/day} \times 9000 \, \text{kJ/kg} = 3.6 \times 10^9 \, \text{kJ/day}
$$

Converting to MWh:
$$
E_{th,in} = \dfrac{3.6 \times 10^9}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 1000 \, \text{MWh/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Steam energy output (2 Marks):**
$$
E_{steam} = 1000 \times 0.78 = 780 \, \text{MWh/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Electrical energy output (2 Marks):**
$$
E_{elec} = 780 \times 0.32 = 249.6 \, \text{MWh/day} \approx 250 \, \text{MWh/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Overall plant efficiency and commentary (1 Mark):**
$$
\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{E_{elec}}{E_{th,in}} \times 100 = \dfrac{250}{1000} \times 100 = 25\%
$$

**Commentary:** This is consistent with modern European WtE benchmarks (20–30% electrical-only efficiency). Adding district heating via CHP can push overall energy recovery to > 80%.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the dry-to-wet conversion** when applying Dulong's formula — examiners deduct **1.5 marks** if this is missed.
> 2. **Confusing mass fractions with percentages** in the ultimate analysis — always divide percentages by 100 before substitution.
> 3. **Omitting the oxygen credit term** $O/8$ in Dulong's formula — this is the single most-failed sub-step.
> 4. **Not annotating the PFD** with temperatures, pressures, and stream directions — examiners in KTU deduct up to **2 marks** for unlabelled PFDs.
> 5. **Forgetting the air composition factor (0.232)** when converting O₂ to air — accounts for another common **1-mark loss**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **PFD vs. P&ID:** PFD shows process logic and stream data; P&ID shows mechanical details (valves, instrumentation).
- **Four macro-stages of every WtE plant:** Pre-treatment → Conversion → Energy Recovery → APC & Residue.
- **Calorific Value benchmark:** MSW GCV ≈ **8000–12,000 kJ/kg**; RDF GCV ≈ **14,000–18,000 kJ/kg**.
- **Stoichiometric air for MSW:** **~3.5–4.5 kg air/kg dry fuel** (lower for wet waste).
- **Excess air design range:** **20–40%** for moving grate; **10–20%** for FBC.
- **Dulong's formula constants:** **C:337, H:1442, S:93** (in kJ/kg) — *memorize!*
- **Air composition (mass basis):** O₂ = **23.2%**, N₂ = **76.8%**.
- **Combustion temperature window:** **850–1100 °C** to ensure dioxin destruction and avoid slagging.
- **Quench rule:** Cool flue gas from **400°C to 200°C in < 1 second** to suppress dioxin reformation (memory-trigger: *"Fast Cool for Pollutant Fool"*).
- **APC train order:** Quench → Dry Scrubber → Bag Filter → SCR → Stack.
- **Biogas composition:** **CH₄ (55–70%) + CO₂ (30–45%) + H₂S (trace)**.
- **Mesophilic AD:** **35–40 °C, HRT 20–40 days.**
- **Overall electrical efficiency benchmark:** **20–30%** (CHP can push total > 80%).
- **Grate heat release rate:** **300–600 kW/m².**
- **Bottom ash yield:** **15–25%** by weight of input waste.
- **Fly ash yield:** **3–5%** by weight — classified as hazardous waste.
- **Dioxin emission limit (India CPCB):** **< 0.1 ng TEQ/Nm³**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Examiner Insight:** Module 4 questions increasingly combine *equipment identification* with *numerical mass/energy balance*. Always pair your PFD diagram with at least one quantitative calculation to secure full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrothermal Processing: Principles of Hydrothermal Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT836 - WASTE TO ENERGY TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hydrothermal Processing: Principles of Hydrothermal Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Hydrothermal Processing (HTP)** is a family of thermochemical conversion technologies that use water as a reaction medium under elevated **temperature** (typically 180 °C – 700 °C) and **pressure** (typically 1 MPa – 30 MPa) to convert wet biomass, sewage sludge, organic municipal solid waste, and other high-moisture feedstocks into value-added energy carriers such as **hydrochar**, **biocrude**, **bio-gas (CH₄, H₂, CO₂)**, and **platform chemicals**, without the requirement of an energy-intensive pre-drying step.

The defining feature of HTP is that water remains in the **liquid phase** (subcritical region) or the **supercritical phase** during processing, acting simultaneously as a **solvent**, a **reactant**, a **heat-transfer medium**, and a **catalyst** (via its tunable dielectric constant and ionic product).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Syllabus Anchor:**
> Hydrothermal Processing is treated as the cornerstone of *wet-biomass-to-energy* conversion. The student must master the **phase behaviour of water**, the distinction between **subcritical vs. supercritical regimes**, and the three principal HTP variants — **Hydrothermal Carbonization (HTC)**, **Hydrothermal Liquefaction (HTL)**, and **Hydrothermal Gasification (HTG)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Pressure Cooker That Turns Wet Garbage into Oil and Gas"**

Imagine you have a kitchen pressure cooker. When you seal it and heat it, the water inside cannot escape, so the pressure rises dramatically. The temperature also climbs well above 100 °C, yet the water remains liquid. Under these conditions, water behaves almost like a **different chemical** — it dissolves organic matter (oils, sugars, lignin fragments) the way an organic solvent does, and at the same time, its high-temperature ions catalyse cracking reactions.

Now scale that pressure cooker to an industrial stainless-steel reactor. Feed it **sewage sludge (≈ 80 % water), food waste, or algae** instead of rice. Instead of cooking food, the reactor *cracks* the organic polymers into:
- a **coal-like solid** (hydrochar),
- a **crude-oil-like liquid** (biocrude), or
- a **methane + hydrogen-rich gas** (syngas / bio-methane),

depending on the **temperature** you set on the dial. This is precisely what **Hydrothermal Processing** does.

### The Three "Heat Zones" of HTP — A Student-Friendly Map

| Heat Zone | Temperature Range | What You Get | Kitchen Analogy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hydrothermal Carbonization (HTC)** | 180 °C – 250 °C | Coal-like solid (hydrochar) | Slow-baking a wet cake into briquettes |
| **Hydrothermal Liquefaction (HTL)** | 250 °C – 374 °C | Crude-oil-like liquid (biocrude) | Pressure-cooking vegetables into a thick broth |
| **Hydrothermal Gasification (HTG)** | > 374 °C (Supercritical) | CH₄ + H₂ + CO₂ gas | Super-heated steam reforming into fuel gas |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants (memorize for KTU exams):**
> - Critical temperature of water, **T_c = 374 °C (647 K)**
> - Critical pressure of water, **P_c = 22.1 MPa (≈ 218 atm)**
> - Standard atmospheric pressure, **P₀ = 101.325 kPa**

---

## 1.3 Why HTP Matters for Waste-to-Energy

Conventional thermochemical routes (combustion, pyrolysis, gasification) require the feedstock to be **dried** before processing because wet biomass (sludge, algae, food waste) has >70 % moisture, and evaporating this water consumes more energy than the biomass releases when burned. HTP **bypasses the drying penalty** because water is the working fluid, not a nuisance.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** For a feedstock with 80 % moisture, the energy required to evaporate that water alone (latent heat ≈ 2,257 kJ/kg) can be greater than the calorific value of the dry biomass (~18 MJ/kg). HTP eliminates this bottleneck by retaining water in the liquid phase and exploiting its lowered dielectric constant to act as a *green solvent*.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Phase Behaviour of Water — The Heart of HTP

To understand HTP, you must first understand how **water's properties change** with temperature and pressure. The **phase diagram of water** is therefore the most important figure in this module.

### 2.1.1 Regions of the Phase Diagram Relevant to HTP

1. **Subcritical Liquid Region** (T = 100 °C – 374 °C, P > P_sat): Water remains liquid above its normal boiling point because the pressure is held above the saturation curve. In this region:
   - Dielectric constant drops from **ε_r ≈ 80** (at 25 °C) to **ε_r ≈ 15–25** (near 350 °C).
   - Ionic product increases by **> 3 orders of magnitude** (peaks near 250 °C).
   - Density decreases but remains liquid-like.

2. **Near-Critical Region** (T ≈ 350 °C – 374 °C): Water exhibits *anomalous* properties — viscosity drops sharply, diffusivity rises, and solvent behaviour transitions from "polar" toward "non-polar-like".

3. **Supercritical Region** (T > 374 °C, P > 22.1 MPa): A **single-phase fluid** that is neither a true liquid nor a true gas. It has:
   - Gas-like diffusivity and viscosity (excellent mass transfer).
   - Liquid-like density (good solvent power).
   - Tunable dielectric constant (ε_r adjustable from ~2 to ~30).

### 2.1.2 Quantitative Change in Dielectric Constant

The dielectric constant of water as a function of temperature (at saturation pressure) follows approximately:

$$\varepsilon_{r}(T) \;=\; a_0 \;+\; a_1 T \;+\; a_2 T^{2} \;+\; a_3 T^{3}$$

where T is in °C and the empirical constants for liquid water in the 100 °C–370 °C range are roughly:

| Coefficient | Value |
|---|---|
| a₀ | 87.92 |
| a₁ | −0.4047 |
| a₂ | 9.587 × 10⁻⁴ |
| a₃ | −1.328 × 10⁻⁶ |

> [!NOTE]
> At **250 °C** (deep inside HTC territory), ε_r ≈ **27** — comparable to **methanol at room temperature**. This is why water at 250 °C can dissolve organic compounds that are completely insoluble in water at 25 °C.

### 2.1.3 Ionic Product of Water

$$K_w(T) \;=\; \left[ \mathrm{H}^{+} \right] \left[ \mathrm{OH}^{-} \right]$$

At 25 °C, $K_w \approx 10^{-14}$ mol²/L² (pH 7 = neutral).
At **250 °C** (saturated liquid), $K_w \approx 10^{-11.3}$ mol²/L² — meaning the concentration of $\mathrm{H^+}$ and $\mathrm{OH^-}$ ions is **~ 1000× higher** than at room temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Consequence:** Water at 250 °C acts as a powerful **acid/base catalyst**. Biomass hydrolysis, dehydration, and decarboxylation reactions are greatly accelerated without adding any external catalyst.

---

## 2.2 The Three HTP Sub-Processes

### 2.2.1 Hydrothermal Carbonization (HTC)

**Operating Window:** T = 180 °C – 250 °C, P = 1 MPa – 5 MPa, residence time = 1 h – 8 h.

- Feedstock: any wet biomass with moisture > 50 %.
- Primary Product: **hydrochar** (solid, ~60 %–80 % of carbon retained), plus small amounts of CO₂, water-soluble organics, and process water.
- Governing chemistry: **hydrolysis → dehydration → decarboxylation**.
- Carbon balance: ~70 % of feed carbon → hydrochar; ~25 % → CO₂; ~5 % → dissolved organics.
- Oxygen content of hydrochar: typically reduced from ~40 % (feedstock) to ~20 %, raising the calorific value from ~18 MJ/kg (feedstock, dry basis) to ~25–30 MJ/kg (hydrochar).

### 2.2.2 Hydrothermal Liquefaction (HTL)

**Operating Window:** T = 250 °C – 374 °C, P = 5 MPa – 20 MPa, residence time = 10 min – 60 min.

- Feedstock: wet biomass, **microalgae**, sewage sludge, food waste.
- Primary Product: **biocrude** (heavy oil), with by-product biochar, aqueous phase, and gas.
- Governing chemistry: **hydrolysis, fragmentation, recombination, dehydration**.
- Carbon recovery into biocrude: 40 %–60 % (typical).
- Heating value of biocrude: 30–36 MJ/kg (comparable to petroleum crude).

> [!TIP]
> **Why HTL vs. Pyrolysis for algae?** Algae contain 70–90 % water. Pyrolysis would require evaporating this water first (energy penalty). HTL processes algae *as-received*, making it economically superior for aqueous feedstocks.

### 2.2.3 Hydrothermal Gasification (HTG)

**Operating Window:** T = 400 °C – 700 °C, P = 22 MPa – 30 MPa (supercritical), residence time = 10 s – 60 s.

- Two sub-modes:
  - **Supercritical Water Gasification (SCWG):** T > 374 °C, very short residence times.
  - **Catalytic HTG:** T = 350 °C – 450 °C, with Ni, Ru, or carbon-supported catalysts.
- Primary Products: **CH₄, H₂, CO₂** (and trace CO).
- The dominant gas depends on conditions:
  - Low T, high P → **methane** (via methanation: CO + 3H₂ → CH₄ + H₂O).
  - High T, low P → **hydrogen** (via water-gas shift: CO + H₂O → CO₂ + H₂).
- Carbon gasification efficiency: can exceed 95 % at 600 °C with a catalyst.

---

## 2.3 Chemistry of Hydrothermal Processing — Reaction Pathways

The overall reaction of biomass (simplified as C₆H₁₀O₅, the cellulose monomer) in sub- and supercritical water proceeds through a cascade:

$$\mathrm{C_6 H_{10} O_5} \;+\; \mathrm{H_2 O} \;\xrightarrow{\Delta,\, P}\; \text{(Hydrolysis)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Sugars, Amino acids, Fatty acids}$$

$$\text{Sugars/Fatty acids} \;\xrightarrow{\Delta,\, P}\; \text{(Dehydration, Fragmentation)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Furans, Phenols, Oligomers}$$

$$\text{Oligomers} \;\xrightarrow{\Delta,\, P}\; \text{(Repolymerisation / Carbonisation)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Hydrochar}$$

At higher T (HTL):

$$\text{Sugars / Oligomers} \;\xrightarrow{\Delta,\, P}\; \text{(Retro-aldol, Decarboxylation)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Biocrude + Gas + Aqueous}$$

At supercritical T (HTG):

$$\mathrm{C_6 H_{10} O_5} \;+\; (6-2n)\mathrm{H_2 O} \;\longrightarrow\; 2n\,\mathrm{H_2} \;+\; n\,\mathrm{CO_2} \;+\; \text{trace CH}_4$$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** that has appeared in previous KTU university examinations on Hydrothermal Processing. Master these — they account for ~70 % of marks in Part A and Part B questions.

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_c = 647 \text{ K} = 374\,^\circ\text{C}$ | Critical temperature of water | Identify supercritical regime |
| 2 | $P_c = 22.1 \text{ MPa}$ | Critical pressure of water | Identify supercritical regime |
| 3 | $K_w(T) = \left[\mathrm{H^+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH^-}\right]$ | Ionic product of water | Justify catalytic behaviour |
| 4 | $HHV_{\text{hydrochar}} \approx 25\text{–}30 \text{ MJ/kg}$ | Higher heating value of HTC product | Energy-yield calculations |
| 5 | $HHV_{\text{biocrude}} \approx 30\text{–}36 \text{ MJ/kg}$ | HHV of HTL biocrude | Energy-yield calculations |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{carbon}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{product}}}{C_{\text{feed}}} \times 100\%$ | Carbon recovery efficiency | Mass-balance questions |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{energy}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{product}} \cdot HHV_{\text{product}}}{m_{\text{feed,dry}} \cdot HHV_{\text{feed,dry}}} \times 100\%$ | Energy recovery efficiency | Energy-balance problems |
| 8 | $E_{\text{drying}} = m_{\text{water}} \cdot L_v = m_{\text{water}} \cdot 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$ | Energy to evaporate water | Justify HTP over pyrolysis |
| 9 | $\rho_{\text{SCW}}(T,P)$ — read from steam tables | Density of supercritical water | Reactor sizing |
| 10 | $\mathrm{CO + 3H_2 \rightleftharpoons CH_4 + H_2 O}$, $\Delta H = -206 \text{ kJ/mol}$ | Methanation reaction | HTG chemistry |
| 11 | $\mathrm{CO + H_2 O \rightleftharpoons CO_2 + H_2}$, $\Delta H = -41 \text{ kJ/mol}$ | Water-gas shift | HTG chemistry |
| 12 | $\mathrm{C_6 H_{10} O_5 + 7H_2 O \rightarrow 12 H_2 + 6 CO_2}$ | Ideal SCWG stoichiometry (cellulose) | HTG stoichiometry |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | HTP Variant | Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| Sewage sludge management | HTL | Biocrude for boilers + nutrient-rich aqueous phase for fertiliser |
| Microalgae biofuel | HTL | "Wet green crude" without drying |
| Food waste valorisation | HTC | Hydrochar as solid fuel / soil amendment |
| Black liquor (paper industry) | HTG | Hydrogen-rich syngas |
| Plastic-to-fuel | HTL (supercritical) | Monomer recovery / fuel oil |
| PFAS destruction | SCWO (Supercritical Water Oxidation) | Complete mineralisation at 600 °C, 25 MPa |
