# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Mandate:** Every algebraic step below is written explicitly. No intermediate result is skipped. KTU examiners award **partial marks for every correct intermediate step**, so reproducing the full derivation is a high-yield strategy.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Justification for HTP over Pyrolysis

A KTU-favourite problem: **Show that hydrothermal processing is energetically preferable to pyrolysis for wet biomass.** We solve this rigorously.

### Statement of the Problem

A wet microalgae slurry has moisture content **M = 80 % (wet basis)**, dry-basis calorific value **HHV_dry = 21 MJ/kg**. Compare the **net energy yield** of:
- (a) Pyrolysis (requires drying), and
- (b) HTL (no pre-drying).

Assume:
- Latent heat of water, **L_v = 2257 kJ/kg**.
- Biocrude yield from HTL, **Y_oil = 35 % of dry mass**, with HHV_biocrude = 34 MJ/kg.
- Pyrolysis oil yield, **Y_py = 30 % of dry mass**, with HHV_py = 28 MJ/kg.

### Step 1 — Establish a Basis

Take **100 kg of wet algae slurry** as the basis.
- Mass of dry algae = 100 × (1 − 0.80) = **20 kg**.
- Mass of water = 100 × 0.80 = **80 kg**.

### Step 2 — Energy Required for Drying (Pyrolysis Path)

Energy to evaporate 80 kg of water (assuming water at 25 °C, simplified — ignores sensible heat):

$$E_{\text{dry}} \;=\; m_{\text{water}} \cdot L_v \;=\; 80 \text{ kg} \times 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{dry}} \;=\; 180{,}560 \text{ kJ} \;\approx\; 180.6 \text{ MJ}}$$

### Step 3 — Energy Content of the Dry Algae

$$E_{\text{dry\,algae}} \;=\; m_{\text{dry}} \cdot HHV_{\text{dry}} \;=\; 20 \text{ kg} \times 21 \text{ MJ/kg} \;=\; 420 \text{ MJ}$$

### Step 4 — Energy Content of Pyrolysis Products

$$E_{\text{py,oil}} \;=\; (20 \times 0.30) \text{ kg} \times 28 \text{ MJ/kg} \;=\; 6 \text{ kg} \times 28 \;=\; 168 \text{ MJ}$$

### Step 5 — Net Energy from Pyrolysis (subtracting drying penalty)

$$E_{\text{py,net}} \;=\; E_{\text{py,oil}} \;-\; E_{\text{dry}} \;=\; 168 \;-\; 180.6 \;=\; -12.6 \text{ MJ}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The pyrolysis path is NET-NEGATIVE for this feedstock.** You would spend more energy drying the algae than you recover from the oil. The same 100 kg of slurry actually *loses* 12.6 MJ when routed through pyrolysis+drying.

### Step 6 — Energy Content of HTL Biocrude

$$E_{\text{HTL,oil}} \;=\; (20 \times 0.35) \text{ kg} \times 34 \text{ MJ/kg} \;=\; 7 \text{ kg} \times 34 \;=\; 238 \text{ MJ}$$

### Step 7 — Energy Required to Heat Slurry from 25 °C to 350 °C (HTL)

Sensible heat (approximating $c_p$ of water ≈ 4.18 kJ/(kg·K)):

$$E_{\text{heat}} \;=\; m_{\text{slurry}} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

$$E_{\text{heat}} \;=\; 100 \text{ kg} \times 4.18 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)} \times (350 - 25) \text{ K}$$

$$E_{\text{heat}} \;=\; 100 \times 4.18 \times 325 \text{ kJ} \;=\; 135{,}850 \text{ kJ} \;\approx\; 135.9 \text{ MJ}$$

(Note: In practice, heat integration recovers ~40–60 % of this; we use the gross value here for a conservative estimate.)

### Step 8 — Net Energy from HTL

$$E_{\text{HTL,net}} \;=\; E_{\text{HTL,oil}} \;-\; E_{\text{heat}} \;=\; 238 \;-\; 135.9 \;=\; 102.1 \text{ MJ}$$

### Step 9 — Comparative Conclusion

$$\boxed{\dfrac{E_{\text{HTL,net}}}{E_{\text{py,net}}} \;=\; \dfrac{+102.1}{-12.6} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{HTL is energetically superior by a factor that is qualitatively } \textbf{decisive}}$$

| Metric | Pyrolysis | HTL |
|---|---|---|
| Pre-drying energy | 180.6 MJ | 0 MJ |
| Process heating energy | ≈ 50 MJ | 135.9 MJ |
| Energy in product oil | 168 MJ | 238 MJ |
| **Net energy yield** | **− 12.6 MJ** | **+ 102.1 MJ** |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** For feedstocks with >70 % moisture, **HTP is the only thermochemical route that yields net-positive energy**. Pyrolysis becomes viable only when feedstock moisture drops below ~30 %.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Carbon Mass Balance Across an HTL Reactor

A typical KTU problem provides mass-flow data for feed, biocrude, hydrochar, aqueous phase, and gas, and asks you to verify the balance.

### Step 1 — Define Variables

Let:
- $m_F$ = mass of dry feedstock entering per hour (kg/h)
- $m_O$ = mass of biocrude (kg/h)
- $m_C$ = mass of hydrochar (kg/h)
- $m_W$ = mass of aqueous phase organics (kg/h)
- $m_G$ = mass of gas (kg/h)

### Step 2 — Overall Mass Balance (steady state)

$$m_F \;=\; m_O \;+\; m_C \;+\; m_W \;+\; m_G \;+\; m_{\text{inorganics}}$$

### Step 3 — Elemental Carbon Balance

Let $C_i$ denote the mass fraction of carbon in stream *i*. Then:

$$m_F \cdot C_F \;=\; m_O \cdot C_O \;+\; m_C \cdot C_C \;+\; m_W \cdot C_W \;+\; m_G \cdot C_G$$

### Step 4 — Carbon Recovery Efficiency

$$\eta_{\text{carbon,oil}} \;=\; \dfrac{m_O \cdot C_O}{m_F \cdot C_F} \times 100\%$$

### Step 5 — Worked Example

Given: $m_F = 100 \text{ kg/h}$, $C_F = 0.48$, $m_O = 35 \text{ kg/h}$, $C_O = 0.75$.

$$\eta_{\text{carbon,oil}} \;=\; \dfrac{35 \times 0.75}{100 \times 0.48} \times 100\% \;=\; \dfrac{26.25}{48.00} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{\text{carbon,oil}} \;\approx\; 54.7\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This matches the typical **40 %–60 %** carbon-into-oil range for HTL reported in KTU-recommended literature.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Supercritical Water Gasification Stoichiometry

For cellulose C₆H₁₀O₅, the **ideal complete gasification** reaction (no char) in supercritical water is:

$$\mathrm{C_6 H_{10} O_5 \;+\; a\,H_2 O \;\longrightarrow\; b\,H_2 \;+\; c\,CO_2 \;+\; d\,CH_4}$$

To find the coefficients, we balance C, H, O atom by atom.

### Step 1 — Carbon Balance

$$\text{C: } 6 \;=\; c \;+\; d$$

### Step 2 — Hydrogen Balance

$$\text{H: } 10 \;+\; 2a \;=\; 2b \;+\; 4d$$

### Step 3 — Oxygen Balance

$$\text{O: } 5 \;+\; a \;=\; 2c$$

### Step 4 — Assume **No Methane** (highest H₂ yield condition, d = 0)

From C: $c = 6$.
From O: $5 + a = 12 \Rightarrow a = 7$.
From H: $10 + 14 = 2b \Rightarrow b = 12$.

$$\boxed{\mathrm{C_6 H_{10} O_5 \;+\; 7\,H_2 O \;\longrightarrow\; 12\,H_2 \;+\; 6\,CO_2}}$$

### Step 5 — Verify Atom Balance

| Atom | Reactant Side | Product Side |
|---|---|---|
| C | 6 | 6 ✓ |
| H | 10 + 14 = 24 | 2 × 12 = 24 ✓ |
| O | 5 + 7 = 12 | 2 × 6 = 12 ✓ |

### Step 6 — Enthalpy of Reaction

Standard enthalpies of formation (kJ/mol):

| Species | ΔH_f° |
|---|---|
| C₆H₁₀O₅ | − 948 |
| H₂O (l) | − 286 |
| H₂ | 0 |
| CO₂ | − 394 |

$$\Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \;=\; \left[ 12(0) + 6(-394) \right] - \left[ (-948) + 7(-286) \right]$$

$$\Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \;=\; \left[ -2364 \right] - \left[ -948 - 2002 \right]$$

$$\Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \;=\; -2364 \;-\; (-2950)$$

$$\boxed{\Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \;=\; +586 \text{ kJ/mol of cellulose}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The reaction is ENDOTHERMIC under standard conditions** — heat must be supplied. However, in supercritical water at 600 °C and 25 MPa, the effective enthalpy is much lower because the system is already hot; partial oxidation of a fraction of the feed (or external heat integration) typically supplies this energy.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary of HTP Sub-Processes

| Parameter | HTC | HTL | HTG |
|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature (°C) | 180 – 250 | 250 – 374 | 400 – 700 |
| Pressure (MPa) | 1 – 5 | 5 – 20 | 22 – 30 |
| Residence time | 1 – 8 h | 10 – 60 min | 10 – 60 s |
| Primary product | Hydrochar (solid) | Biocrude (liquid) | CH₄, H₂, CO₂ (gas) |
| Secondary products | Aqueous organics, CO₂ | Aqueous, char, gas | Trace tars |
| Carbon-to-product yield | 70 % solid | 40–60 % liquid | 80–95 % gas |
| Water state | Subcritical liquid | Subcritical liquid | Supercritical fluid |
| Catalytic role of water | Moderate | High | Very high |
| HHV of main product | 25–30 MJ/kg | 30–36 MJ/kg | 50–55 MJ/kg (CH₄) |
| Feedstock moisture | > 50 % | > 70 % | > 90 % |
| Reactor material | Stainless steel | Inconel / SS 316 | Inconel / Titanium |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define hydrothermal processing. List its three principal variants and state the temperature range associated with each.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Hydrothermal Processing (HTP)** is a thermochemical conversion route that uses **water as a solvent and reaction medium at elevated temperature (180 °C – 700 °C) and pressure (1 MPa – 30 MPa)** to convert wet biomass and organic wastes into solid, liquid, or gaseous energy carriers without pre-drying.
>
> The three principal variants are:
>
> | Variant | Temperature Range |
> |---|---|
> | Hydrothermal Carbonization (HTC) | 180 °C – 250 °C |
> | Hydrothermal Liquefaction (HTL) | 250 °C – 374 °C |
> | Hydrothermal Gasification (HTG) | > 374 °C (supercritical) |
>
> **(3 marks)** — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the three variants, 1 mark for correct temperature bands.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Why is the dielectric constant of water relevant to hydrothermal processing? What is its approximate value at 250 °C?**

**Model Answer:**

> The dielectric constant governs water's **solvent polarity**. At 25 °C, water's dielectric constant is **ε_r ≈ 80**, making it an excellent solvent for salts and polar compounds but a poor solvent for organic compounds.
>
> At **250 °C** (subcritical liquid water), the dielectric constant drops to **ε_r ≈ 27**, comparable to **methanol at room temperature**. This allows water to **dissolve organic compounds** that are otherwise insoluble, enabling the conversion of biomass fragments into a homogeneous reaction medium.
>
> **(3 marks)** — 1 mark for role of dielectric constant, 1 mark for value at 25 °C, 1 mark for value at 250 °C.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) guidelines**, Module 4 questions are framed with **internal choice**: a student must answer *either* Question A *or* Question B. Both are provided below with full model solutions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]

**(a)** With the help of a labelled **temperature–pressure phase diagram of water**, identify the operating regions of HTC, HTL, and HTG. State the critical constants of water and explain why HTG requires supercritical conditions.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Draw the P–T diagram mentally** (reproduce in the answer):
- Show the solid, liquid, vapour, and supercritical regions.
- Mark the critical point at T = 374 °C, P = 22.1 MPa.
- Shade three rectangles:
  - HTC zone (180–250 °C, 1–5 MPa) → liquid water region.
  - HTL zone (250–374 °C, 5–20 MPa) → liquid water region, near the saturation curve.
  - HTG zone (400–700 °C, 22–30 MPa) → supercritical region.

**Step 2 — Critical constants:**
- T_c = 374 °C (647 K).
- P_c = 22.1 MPa.
- Above this point, no distinction exists between liquid and vapour.

**Step 3 — Why supercritical conditions for HTG:**
- Above T_c, water becomes a **single-phase fluid** with both liquid-like density (good solvation) and gas-like diffusivity/viscosity (excellent mass transfer).
- The dielectric constant drops to **ε_r < 10**, allowing water to **dissolve non-polar gases like H₂, CH₄, and CO₂**, which is essential for complete gasification.
- The ionic product collapses, so ionic reactions cease; instead, **free-radical reactions** dominate, rapidly breaking C–C bonds into small gas molecules.
- Without these conditions, char-forming repolymerisation reactions dominate, preventing gasification.

**(b)** A wet algal biomass has **80 % moisture (wet basis)**, dry-basis HHV of **20 MJ/kg**, and a dry-basis carbon content of **48 %**. After HTL processing:
- Biocrude yield = 35 % of dry mass, HHV = 34 MJ/kg, carbon content = 75 %.
- Hydrochar yield = 15 % of dry mass, HHV = 25 MJ/kg, carbon content = 60 %.
- Remaining carbon leaves as gas and dissolved CO₂.

Calculate the **carbon recovery efficiency** into biocrude and the **net energy recovery** assuming 130 MJ of process heat is required per 100 kg of wet slurry. Comment on whether HTL is energetically viable.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Establish mass basis.**
- Take 100 kg of wet algae.
- Dry algae mass, $m_d = 100 \times (1 - 0.80) = 20 \text{ kg}$.

**Step 2 — Mass of products.**
- Biocrude mass, $m_O = 20 \times 0.35 = 7 \text{ kg}$.
- Hydrochar mass, $m_C = 20 \times 0.15 = 3 \text{ kg}$.

**Step 3 — Carbon recovery into biocrude.**
- Carbon in biocrude = $7 \times 0.75 = 5.25 \text{ kg}$.
- Carbon in feed = $20 \times 0.48 = 9.60 \text{ kg}$.

$$\eta_{\text{C,oil}} \;=\; \dfrac{5.25}{9.60} \times 100\% \;=\; 54.7\%$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Energy in biocrude + hydrochar.**
- $E_O = 7 \times 34 = 238 \text{ MJ}$.
- $E_C = 3 \times 25 = 75 \text{ MJ}$.
- $E_{\text{products,total}} = 313 \text{ MJ}$.

**Step 5 — Net energy recovery.**
- Process heat = 130 MJ.

$$E_{\text{net}} \;=\; E_{\text{products,total}} \;-\; E_{\text{process}} \;=\; 313 \;-\; 130 \;=\; \mathbf{+183 \text{ MJ}}$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 6 — Overall energy recovery efficiency (dry-basis feed).**

$$\eta_{\text{energy}} \;=\; \dfrac{313}{20 \times 20} \times 100\% \;=\; \dfrac{313}{400} \times 100\% \;=\; 78.3\%$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 7 — Comment.**
HTL is **energetically viable** for this feedstock, yielding +183 MJ of net energy per 100 kg of slurry with 78 % energy recovery. This result confirms that HTL is preferable to pyrolysis for high-moisture biomass.

**(1 mark)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a)** Explain the **change in dielectric constant and ionic product of water** as temperature increases from 25 °C to 400 °C. Discuss how these changes enable each HTP variant to produce distinct products.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Dielectric constant behaviour:**
- At 25 °C, ε_r ≈ 80 (strong polar solvent).
- ε_r decreases monotonically with T, reaching ≈ 27 at 250 °C, ≈ 15 near 350 °C, and ≈ 2–5 in the supercritical region.
- This is because **hydrogen-bonding network** breaks down with temperature.

**Step 2 — Ionic product behaviour:**
- K_w peaks near 250 °C at ≈ 10⁻¹¹·³ mol²/L² (≈ 1000× higher than at 25 °C).
- Beyond 250 °C, K_w drops sharply; in supercritical water, K_w < 10⁻²⁰ (essentially no ions).

**Step 3 — Linkage to HTP variants:**
- **HTC (180–250 °C):** Moderate ε_r (40–27) + high K_w → water is a **moderately polar solvent with strong acid/base catalysis**. Result: hydrolysis, dehydration, decarboxylation → **hydrochar**.
- **HTL (250–374 °C):** Lower ε_r (27–15) + still-significant K_w → water dissolves organic fragments; ionic catalysis continues but fragmentation dominates. Result: **biocrude**.
- **HTG (> 374 °C):** Very low ε_r (< 10) + negligible K_w → water behaves as a **non-polar solvent and free-radical medium**. Result: complete gasification to **CH₄, H₂, CO₂**.

**(b)** For supercritical water gasification of cellulose (C₆H₁₀O₅), write the **ideal complete gasification reaction** and balance it to yield **maximum hydrogen**. Calculate the **standard enthalpy of reaction** using standard enthalpies of formation. Comment on whether the reaction is endothermic or exothermic and its engineering implication.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the unbalanced reaction:**
$$\mathrm{C_6 H_{10} O_5 \;+\; a\,H_2 O \;\longrightarrow\; b\,H_2 \;+\; c\,CO_2}$$

(For maximum H₂, we assume zero CH₄.)

**Step 2 — Apply element balances** (as derived in §3.3):
- C: 6 = c ⇒ c = 6
- O: 5 + a = 2c = 12 ⇒ a = 7
- H: 10 + 2a = 2b ⇒ 10 + 14 = 2b ⇒ b = 12

$$\boxed{\mathrm{C_6 H_{10} O_5 \;+\; 7\,H_2 O \;\longrightarrow\; 12\,H_2 \;+\; 6\,CO_2}}$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Standard enthalpies of formation (kJ/mol):**

| Species | ΔH_f° |
|---|---|
| C₆H₁₀O₅ (s, cellulose) | − 948 |
| H₂O (l) | − 286 |
| H₂ (g) | 0 |
| CO₂ (g) | − 394 |

**Step 4 — Compute ΔH_rxn:**
$$\Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \;=\; \sum \Delta H_f^\circ (\text{products}) \;-\; \sum \Delta H_f^\circ (\text{reactants})$$

$$\Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \;=\; \left[12(0) + 6(-394)\right] - \left[(-948) + 7(-286)\right]$$

$$\Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \;=\; \left[-2364\right] - \left[-948 - 2002\right]$$

$$\Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \;=\; -2364 + 2950$$

$$\boxed{\Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \;=\; +586 \text{ kJ/mol of cellulose}}$$

**(3 marks)**

**Step 5 — Engineering implication:**
The reaction is **endothermic**, requiring heat input. In practice:
- Heat is supplied by partial oxidation of a small fraction of the feed (auto-thermal SCWG).
- Or by external heat integration with combustion of the product CH₄.
- Supercritical water acts as both solvent and heat-transfer fluid, distributing heat uniformly and rapidly.

**(2 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in HTP Questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating supercritical water (SCW) with steam.** Many students write *"HTG uses steam"* — this is **wrong**. HTG uses **supercritical water**, which is a single-phase fluid with neither gas nor liquid properties. Steam is a gas; SCW is neither.
>
> 2. **Confusing temperature boundaries of HTC, HTL, HTG.** Memorize:
>    - HTC: 180–250 °C
>    - HTL: 250–374 °C
>    - HTG: > 374 °C
>
> 3. **Forgetting that subcritical water needs to be pressurised.** At 300 °C, water is liquid only if P > 8.6 MPa (saturation pressure). If you forget to mention pressurisation, you lose 1–2 marks.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the dielectric constant explanation.** Examiners *love* to ask *"Why does water behave as a non-polar solvent at high temperature?"* Always cite the dielectric constant dropping from 80 to ~2.
>
> 5. **Mixing up HTC and HTL products.** HTC = SOLID (hydrochar). HTL = LIQUID (biocrude). HTG = GAS. Get this right.
>
> 6. **Not balancing the SCWG reaction.** Always show the C-balance, H-balance, and O-balance explicitly. KTU examiners award 1 mark per balanced element.
>
> 7. **Forgetting to subtract process heat from gross energy output** in HTL/HTG energy-balance problems. Net energy = gross product energy − process heat input.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and revise 30 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **Hydrothermal Processing (HTP)** = thermochemical conversion in **compressed water** at 180–700 °C, 1–30 MPa.
- ✅ **Critical point of water:** T_c = **374 °C**, P_c = **22.1 MPa**.
- ✅ **Three variants:**
  - **HTC** (180–250 °C) → **Hydrochar** (solid).
  - **HTL** (250–374 °C) → **Biocrude** (liquid).
  - **HTG** (>374 °C, supercritical) → **CH₄, H₂, CO₂** (gas).
- ✅ **Water's dielectric constant** drops from **80 → 2** as T rises from 25 °C → 600 °C; this is the *fundamental reason* HTP works on otherwise-insoluble organics.
- ✅ **Ionic product K_w** peaks near **250 °C**, ~10⁻¹¹·³ → enables acid/base catalysis without added catalysts.
- ✅ **No pre-drying** is required for HTP → major energy saving for > 70 % moisture feedstocks.
- ✅ **Key reactions:**
  - Hydrolysis, dehydration, decarboxylation (HTC).
  - Fragmentation + recombination (HTL).
  - Steam reforming + water-gas shift + methanation (HTG).
- ✅ **SCWG ideal stoichiometry (cellulose, max H₂):**
  $$\mathrm{C_6 H_{10} O_5 + 7H_2 O \rightarrow 12H_2 + 6CO_2}, \quad \Delta H = +586 \text{ kJ/mol}$$
- ✅ **Methanation:** $\mathrm{CO + 3H_2 \rightleftharpoons CH_4 + H_2 O}$ (exothermic, favoured at low T, high P).
- ✅ **Water-gas shift:** $\mathrm{CO + H_2 O \rightleftharpoons CO_2 + H_2}$ (mildly exothermic).
- ✅ **HHV values:** HTC hydrochar 25–30 MJ/kg; HTL biocrude 30–36 MJ/kg; HTG methane ~ 50 MJ/kg.
- ✅ **Energy justification:** For 80 % moisture algae, HTL gives **+102 MJ/100 kg** vs. **−12.6 MJ/100 kg** for pyrolysis+drying.
- ✅ **Reactor materials:** Stainless steel 316 for low-T HTP; **Inconel** or titanium for supercritical HTG.
- ✅ **Real-world applications:** Sewage sludge (HTL), microalgae (HTL), food waste (HTC), black liquor (HTG), PFAS destruction (SCWO).
- ✅ **Always report net energy** (product energy − process heat) in HTP questions, not gross product energy.

---

*End of Notes — Hydrothermal Processing: Principles of Hydrothermal Processing*
*Module 4 | OEBBT836 — Waste to Energy Technology | KTU 2024 Scheme*

topics.title: Supercritical Water and Its Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT836 - WASTE TO ENERGY TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Supercritical Water and Its Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Supercritical Water (SCW)** is defined as water existing at temperatures and pressures **above its thermodynamic critical point**, which occurs at a **critical temperature (T_c) of 374 °C (647.1 K)** and a **critical pressure (P_c) of 22.1 MPa (218 atm)**. Above this point, the distinct phases of liquid and vapor cease to exist, and water exists as a single homogeneous **supercritical fluid** with hybrid physicochemical properties.

In the context of **Waste to Energy (WTE) technology**, supercritical water serves as a unique reaction medium for two pivotal thermochemical conversion processes:

1. **Supercritical Water Gasification (SCWG)** — for hydrogen-rich syngas production from wet biomass and organic waste.
2. **Supercritical Water Oxidation (SCWO)** — for complete destruction of hazardous organic waste with energy recovery.

The critical point is the terminus of the liquid-vapor coexistence curve on a Pressure-Temperature (P-T) phase diagram, beyond which there is no distinction between liquid and gaseous phases.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of regular water (the liquid in your glass) and steam (the gas from a kettle) as two different "personalities" of water — they look different and behave very differently. Now imagine a magical "third state" where the water is neither fully liquid nor fully gas, but something in between. This is **supercritical water** — like a "super personality" of water that can dissolve oil like a liquid but spread through solids like a gas.

Imagine you are slowly heating water in a sealed, extremely strong container. As you cross 374 °C while keeping pressure above 22.1 MPa, the surface tension that separates liquid and vapor **vanishes**. The molecules move so fast that they cannot be compressed into a distinct liquid phase, yet they are dense enough to act as a solvent. This unique state is what makes SCW a "green" and highly reactive medium for converting wet wastes (which cannot be efficiently processed by conventional drying-based methods) into energy.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following physical constants are **board-exam mandatory** for KTU 2024 Scheme:

| Parameter | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Critical Temperature | T_c | **374.15 (≈ 374)** | °C (or 647.1 K) |
| Critical Pressure | P_c | **22.1** | MPa |
| Critical Density | ρ_c | **0.322** | g/cm³ |
| Critical Specific Volume | v_c | **3.11** | cm³/g |
| Molecular Formula | H₂O | 18.015 | g/mol |
| Dipole Moment | μ | **1.85** | Debye |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Students must memorize **T_c = 374 °C** and **P_c = 22.1 MPa** as these values appear almost every year in KTU examinations, either as direct recall (Part A) or in calculation-based Part B problems.

### 1.4 Historical & Engineering Context

The phenomenon of supercritical fluids was first observed by **Baron Charles Cagniard de la Tour in 1822** during acoustic experiments. However, the application of supercritical water to waste destruction was pioneered by **Glenn (Modell)** in the mid-1980s, leading to the modern SCWO and SCWG processes that form the backbone of advanced wet-waste-to-energy systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Phase Logic — Why Supercritical Water Exists

The existence of SCW can be understood by tracking three logical steps:

- **Step 1 — The Coexistence Curve:** On a P-T diagram, the liquid-vapor saturation curve has an **endpoint** at the critical point (T_c, P_c). Below this curve, liquid and vapor coexist in equilibrium.

- **Step 2 — Crossing the Critical Isotherm (T > T_c):** As temperature rises above T_c, the kinetic energy of water molecules overcomes the intermolecular hydrogen-bonding network. No amount of compression can liquefy the fluid — it remains a single supercritical phase.

- **Step 3 — Crossing the Critical Isobar (P > P_c):** Similarly, at pressures above P_c, even at lower temperatures, the density is too high to permit a clear vapor phase. The fluid becomes a "dense gas" with liquid-like solvation powers.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Question Answer:** SCW exists because the **Clausius-Clapeyron phase boundary** terminates at the critical point. Above this terminus, **∂P/∂T (saturation) becomes undefined**, meaning liquid and vapor become indistinguishable.

### 2.2 Properties of Supercritical Water — The Hybrid Nature

SCW possesses a fascinating blend of liquid-like and gas-like properties, which is the **central reason** it is used in WTE applications:

#### 2.2.1 Density (ρ)
- At critical point: **ρ_c = 0.322 g/cm³** (much lower than liquid water's 1.0 g/cm³, but much higher than steam's ~0.0006 g/cm³).
- Density is highly **tunable**: at 400 °C and 25 MPa, ρ ≈ 0.17 g/cm³; at 400 °C and 50 MPa, ρ ≈ 0.58 g/cm³.
- **Engineering Implication:** Adjusting pressure alone can change density by an order of magnitude, providing tunable solvation power.

#### 2.2.2 Viscosity (μ)
- Viscosity drops to **~1/10th of liquid water** (μ ≈ 0.03–0.07 mPa·s).
- High diffusivity combined with low viscosity creates excellent **mass-transfer conditions**, accelerating reaction kinetics for waste conversion.

#### 2.2.3 Dielectric Constant (ε)
- The most chemically significant property: drops from **ε ≈ 80 (liquid water at 25 °C)** to **ε ≈ 2–10 (in SCW region)**.
- This means SCW behaves like a **non-polar organic solvent**, capable of dissolving **hydrocarbons, oils, and gases** (which are insoluble in normal water).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Concept:** The drop in dielectric constant is the **single most important property** for SCWG/SCWO. It allows organic waste (e.g., sewage sludge, plastic waste, biomass) — normally hydrophobic and immiscible with water — to form a **single-phase homogeneous reaction mixture** with water, eliminating mass-transfer limitations.

#### 2.2.4 Diffusivity (D)
- Diffusivity increases by **1–2 orders of magnitude** compared to ambient water.
- Combined with low viscosity, this gives SCW a **"gas-like transport" property**, ideal for fast kinetics.

#### 2.2.5 Ion Product (K_w) and pH
- K_w drops dramatically: at 25 °C, K_w = 10⁻¹⁴; at 500 °C/25 MPa, K_w ≈ 10⁻²⁰.
- However, the **molar concentration of H⁺ and OH⁻** may still be significant in **absolute terms** due to lower density, allowing SCW to act as both an **acid and base catalyst** simultaneously.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_c = 647.1\ \text{K} = 374.15\ ^\circ\text{C}$ | Critical Temperature | SCW threshold |
| 2 | $P_c = 22.1\ \text{MPa}$ | Critical Pressure | SCW threshold |
| 3 | $\rho_c = 0.322\ \text{g/cm}^3$ | Critical Density | Phase identification |
| 4 | $\varepsilon_{SCW} \approx 2 - 10$ | Dielectric Constant of SCW | Solvation behavior |
| 5 | $\varepsilon_{\text{liquid, 25°C}} \approx 78.5$ | Dielectric Constant of Normal Water | Comparison |
| 6 | $\mu_{SCW} \approx 0.03 - 0.07\ \text{mPa·s}$ | Dynamic Viscosity of SCW | Mass-transfer calculation |
| 7 | $D_{SCW} \gg D_{\text{liquid water}}$ | Diffusivity (order 10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁵ cm²/s) | Reaction kinetics |
| 8 | $K_w = [\text{H}^+][\text{OH}^-] = 10^{-14}$ (at 25 °C) | Ion product of water | pH calculations |
| 9 | $K_w^{SCW} \approx 10^{-20}$ (at 500 °C, 25 MPa) | SCW ion product | Acid-base catalysis |
| 10 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu}$ | Reynolds Number (uses μ from SCW table) | Flow regime |
| 11 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot h_{SCW}$ | Enthalpy-based energy balance | Energy recovery |
| 12 | $E_a^{\text{obs}} \downarrow$ in SCW | Activation energy reduction | Reaction rate enhancement |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Rule Enforced:** In all tables above, mathematical absolute-value / condition notation uses `\vert` (e.g., $\vert T - T_c \vert$) instead of the raw pipe symbol `|` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Supercritical Water Gasification (SCWG):** Used in **biomass-to-hydrogen** plants in Germany, Japan, and the USA for converting wet sewage sludge and agricultural waste into H₂-rich syngas (H₂/CO ratio = 2:1 to 4:1).
- **Supercritical Water Oxidation (SCWO):** Deployed for **military chemical weapon destruction**, **pharmaceutical waste**, and **PCB-contaminated soil remediation** by companies like General Atomics and Chematur Engineering.
- **Hydrothermal Liquefaction (HTL):** A related near-critical process for **bio-crude oil** production from algae and wet organic feedstocks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Critical Constants from van der Waals Equation

The van der Waals equation of state for a real gas is:

$$\left( P + \dfrac{a}{V_m^2} \right)(V_m - b) = RT$$

where:
- $V_m$ = molar volume (L/mol)
- $a$ = intermolecular attraction constant
- $b$ = excluded volume constant
- $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K)

#### Step-by-Step Expansion

Expanding the equation in polynomial form of $V_m$:

$$P V_m^3 - (Pb + RT) V_m^2 + a V_m - a b = 0$$

This is a **cubic equation in $V_m$**, which can have **three real roots** at the critical point (corresponding to the liquid-like, gas-like, and supercritical volumes, all converging to a single value at the critical isotherm).

#### Critical Point Conditions

At the critical point, the cubic has a **triple root** at $V_m = V_c$, requiring both:

$$\left( \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial V_m} \right)_{T_c} = 0$$

$$\left( \dfrac{\partial^2 P}{\partial V_m^2} \right)_{T_c} = 0$$

#### Solving the System

Applying the first derivative condition:

$$\left( \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial V_m} \right)_T = \dfrac{RT}{(V_m - b)^2} - \dfrac{2a}{V_m^3}$$

Setting this to zero and applying the second derivative:

$$\left( \dfrac{\partial^2 P}{\partial V_m^2} \right)_T = -\dfrac{2RT}{(V_m - b)^3} + \dfrac{6a}{V_m^4} = 0$$

Solving both equations simultaneously yields the critical constants:

$$V_c = 3b$$

$$P_c = \dfrac{a}{27 b^2}$$

$$T_c = \dfrac{8 a}{27 R b}$$

#### Derivation of Critical Compressibility Factor

$$Z_c = \dfrac{P_c V_c}{R T_c} = \dfrac{(a/27b^2)(3b)}{R \cdot (8a/27Rb)}$$

$$Z_c = \dfrac{3a b / 27 b^2}{8a / 27 b} = \dfrac{a/(9b)}{8a/(27b)} = \dfrac{27 b}{9 \cdot 8 b} = \dfrac{27}{72} = 0.375$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The critical compressibility factor predicted by van der Waals is **Z_c = 3/8 = 0.375** for ALL substances, while experimentally Z_c ranges from 0.27 to 0.29 for most real gases. For water, **Z_c ≈ 0.229**, which demonstrates that van der Waals is a poor quantitative predictor but qualitatively correct.

### 3.2 Numerical: Calculating Density of SCW at Working Conditions

**Problem:** Calculate the density of supercritical water at T = 400 °C and P = 25 MPa using the **Peng-Robinson Equation of State (PR-EOS)**. Compare with the standard steam-table value.

The PR-EOS is:

$$P = \dfrac{RT}{V_m - b} - \dfrac{a \alpha}{V_m^2 + 2 b V_m - b^2}$$

with parameters for water (a_c = 0.5536 Pa·m⁶/mol², b = 3.04 × 10⁻⁵ m³/mol, κ = 0.7660).

#### Step 1: Compute α-function

$$\alpha = \left[ 1 + \kappa \left( 1 - \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{T_c}} \right) \right]^2$$

With $T/T_c = 673.15 / 647.1 = 1.0401$:

$$\sqrt{1.0401} = 1.0198$$

$$1 - 1.0198 = -0.0198$$

$$1 + (0.766)(-0.0198) = 0.9848$$

$$\alpha = (0.9848)^2 = 0.9698$$

#### Step 2: Substitute into PR-EOS

$$2.5 \times 10^7 = \dfrac{(8.314)(673.15)}{V_m - 3.04 \times 10^{-5}} - \dfrac{(0.5536)(0.9698)}{V_m^2 + 6.08 \times 10^{-5} V_m - 9.24 \times 10^{-10}}$$

#### Step 3: Iterative Solution

Using Newton-Raphson iteration (or consulting NIST data), the converged molar volume is:

$$V_m \approx 1.065 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3/\text{mol}$$

#### Step 4: Convert to Density

$$\rho = \dfrac{M}{V_m} = \dfrac{0.018015\ \text{kg/mol}}{1.065 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3/\text{mol}} \approx 169.2\ \text{kg/m}^3$$

$$\rho \approx 0.169\ \text{g/cm}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** This density (~0.17 g/cm³) is about **half the critical density** (0.322 g/cm³) and confirms that at 400 °C/25 MPa, SCW behaves more like a "dense gas" with substantial solvation power — the regime used in industrial SCWG reactors.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Property Comparison Table

| Property | Liquid Water (25 °C) | Steam (100 °C, 1 atm) | SCW (400 °C, 25 MPa) | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Density (g/cm³) | 1.000 | 0.0006 | **0.17** | Tunable solvation |
| Viscosity (mPa·s) | 0.89 | 0.012 | **0.03** | Low flow resistance |
| Dielectric Constant | 78.5 | 1.0 | **5–10** | Dissolves organics |
| Diffusivity (cm²/s) | 10⁻⁵ | 10⁻¹ | **10⁻³** | Fast kinetics |
| Thermal Conductivity (W/m·K) | 0.61 | 0.025 | **0.16** | Heat transfer |
| Ion Product K_w | 10⁻¹⁴ | 10⁻¹² | **10⁻²⁰** | Acid/base catalysis |
| Phase Behavior | Liquid | Gas | **Single phase** | No interphase transfer |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 P-T Phase Diagram of Water (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following diagram represents the **pressure-temperature phase architecture** of water, highlighting the location of the supercritical region relative to the standard liquid, vapor, and solid phases.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Starting Point: Water Phase Space P-T Diagram] --> B[Triple Point: 0.01 C and 611.657 Pa]
    B --> C[Liquid Region: 0 to 374 C, High Pressure]
    B --> D[Vapor Region: 0 to 374 C, Low Pressure]
    B --> E[Solid Region: Below 0 C, High Pressure]
    C --> F[Liquid-Vapor Coexistence Curve]
    D --> F
    F --> G[CRITICAL POINT: 374 C and 22.1 MPa]
    G --> H[SUPERCRITICAL REGION: T greater than 374 C AND P greater than 22.1 MPa]
    H --> H1[SCWG Operating Zone: 400 to 700 C, 25 to 35 MPa]
    H --> H2[SCWO Operating Zone: 400 to 650 C, 22 to 30 MPa]
    H1 --> H3[Applications: Biomass Gasification, Hydrogen Production]
    H2 --> H4[Applications: Hazardous Waste Destruction, Sewage Sludge Treatment]
    style G fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#900,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    style H fill:#ffd93d,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    style H1 fill:#6bcf7f,stroke:#2d5a2d,color:#fff
    style H2 fill:#6bcf7f,stroke:#2d5a2d,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — SCWO Reactor System

Since physical reactor drawings require complex process instrumentation, the following is a **block-level functional architecture flow** mapping the SCWO reactor system used in WTE plants:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Wet Organic Waste Feed Tank] --> P2[High Pressure Pump 25 MPa]
    P3[Compressed Air or H2O2 Oxidant Supply] --> P2
    P2 --> P4[Feed Preheater 200 to 300 C]
    P4 --> P5[SCWO Reactor 400 to 650 C, 22 to 30 MPa]
    P5 --> P6[Cooler and Heat Exchanger Energy Recovery]
    P6 --> P7[Pressure Let-down Valve]
    P7 --> P8[Gas-Liquid Separator]
    P8 --> P9[Clean Effluent Water]
    P8 --> P10[CO2 and N2 Off-gas]
    P5 --> Q1[Organic Compounds: H2O, CO2, N2, Mineral Acids]
    P5 --> Q2[Destruction Efficiency greater than 99.99 percent]
    P5 --> R1[Reaction Type: Free Radical Oxidation]
    P5 --> R2[Residence Time: 30 to 60 seconds]
    P6 --> R3[Heat Recovery: Steam Generation]
    style P5 fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#900,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    style Q2 fill:#6bcf7f,stroke:#2d5a2d,color:#fff
    style P9 fill:#4d96ff,stroke:#003366,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Property Variation vs. Temperature (Conceptual Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Supercritical Water Property Domains] --> S2[High Temperature Domain T greater than 400 C]
    S1 --> S3[Pressure Domain P greater than 22.1 MPa]
    S2 --> S4[Dielectric Constant drops from 80 to 2 to 10]
    S3 --> S5[Density tunable 0.05 to 0.8 g per cm3]
    S4 --> S6[Hydrocarbon Solubility ENABLES: Organic Waste Dissolution]
    S5 --> S7[Reactor Pressure Control ENABLES: Density Tuning for Selective Reactions]
    S6 --> S8[Outcome: Single Phase Homogeneous Reaction Medium]
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9[Engineering Benefit: No Mass Transfer Limitation]
    S9 --> S10[Final Benefit: Ultra Fast Reaction Kinetics for WTE]
    style S1 fill:#ffd93d,stroke:#b8860b,color:#000
    style S10 fill:#6bcf7f,stroke:#2d5a2d,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Define supercritical water. State its critical temperature and pressure values.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Supercritical Water (SCW)** is water that exists at temperatures and pressures above its thermodynamic critical point, where the distinction between liquid and vapor phases disappears, forming a single homogeneous supercritical fluid.
> - **Critical Temperature (T_c) = 374 °C (or 647.1 K)**
> - **Critical Pressure (P_c) = 22.1 MPa**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Why does supercritical water act as a good solvent for organic waste?**

**Model Answer:**

SCW acts as a good solvent for organic waste primarily because its **dielectric constant drops from ~80 (liquid water) to 2–10** in the supercritical region. This reduction makes SCW behave like a **non-polar organic solvent**, allowing it to dissolve hydrocarbons, oils, and organic compounds that are otherwise immiscible with normal water. Combined with its **gas-like diffusivity** and **low viscosity**, SCW forms a **single-phase homogeneous reaction medium** with organic waste, eliminating mass-transfer limitations and enabling ultra-fast reaction kinetics in WTE processes.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **P-T phase diagram of water** with a neat sketch. Clearly mark the critical point and the supercritical region. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **key physicochemical properties of supercritical water** that make it suitable for waste-to-energy applications. Compare its properties with those of liquid water and steam. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The P-T phase diagram of water is a two-dimensional representation showing the equilibrium phases as a function of pressure and temperature.

**Key Features of the Diagram:**

1. **Triple Point:** At T = 0.01 °C and P = 611.657 Pa, all three phases (solid, liquid, vapor) coexist in equilibrium.

2. **Solid-Liquid Boundary:** A nearly vertical line separating ice (left) from liquid water (right).

3. **Liquid-Vapor Coexistence Curve:** Extends from the triple point upward to the critical point. The Clausius-Clapeyron relation describes this curve.

4. **Critical Point:** The terminal point of the coexistence curve, located at:
   - $T_c = 374\ ^\circ\text{C}$
   - $P_c = 22.1\ \text{MPa}$

5. **Supercritical Region:** The area **above and to the right** of the critical point, where T > 374 °C AND P > 22.1 MPa. In this region, only a **single supercritical fluid phase** exists.

**Sketch Description (Mermaid ASCII representation):**

```
              PRESSURE (MPa)
                  |
              25 -| - - - - - - - - - - - -  SCW REGION
                  |                          /
              22.1| - - - - - - - - - - - / (Critical Point at 374 C, 22.1 MPa)
                  |                    /    \
                  |                  /       \   <-- Coexistence
                  |   LIQUID      /          \     Curve
                  |            /              \   VAPOR
                  |          /                 \
                  |        /                     \
              0.0006 ----/---- T = 100 C --------\
                  |    /  (Boiling at 1 atm)      \
                  |  /                              \
              0.000611| - TRIPLE POINT (0.01 C) ---\
                  |  /
                  |/_______________________________|________________ TEMPERATURE (C)
                  0   100   200    300  374  400    500     600
                                      CRITICAL
                                       POINT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Always draw the diagram with axes properly labeled, mark all three phase regions, the critical point, and clearly indicate the supercritical region. Mark the SCWG and SCWO operating zones for full marks.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Key Properties of SCW for WTE Applications:**

**Property 1: Dielectric Constant (ε)**
- Liquid water (25 °C): ε ≈ 78.5
- SCW (400 °C, 25 MPa): ε ≈ 5–10
- **Implication:** SCW acts like a non-polar organic solvent, dissolving organic wastes completely.

**Property 2: Density (ρ)**
- Liquid water: 1.0 g/cm³; SCW: 0.1–0.5 g/cm³ (tunable by pressure)
- **Implication:** Adjustable solvation power via pressure control.

**Property 3: Viscosity (μ)**
- Liquid water: 0.89 mPa·s; SCW: 0.03–0.07 mPa·s
- **Implication:** Gas-like flow behavior, low pumping costs.

**Property 4: Diffusivity (D)**
- Liquid water: ~10⁻⁵ cm²/s; SCW: ~10⁻³ cm²/s
- **Implication:** Enhanced mass transfer, faster reaction kinetics.

**Property 5: Ion Product (K_w)**
- Liquid water: 10⁻¹⁴; SCW: 10⁻²⁰
- **Implication:** Acts as acid/base catalyst, but ionic reactions are suppressed.

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Liquid Water | Steam | SCW |
|---|---|---|---|
| Phase | Liquid | Gas | Single supercritical |
| Solvent Polarity | Highly polar | Non-polar | **Tunable** |
| Organic Solubility | Poor | Variable | **Excellent** |
| Mass Transfer | Liquid-limited | Gas-limited | **Gas-like + liquid density** |

**Conclusion:** SCW's hybrid properties make it uniquely suitable for converting wet organic waste into energy via SCWG (hydrogen production) and SCWO (hazardous waste destruction).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the **critical constants** (T_c, P_c, V_c) from the **van der Waals equation of state**. Show that the critical compressibility factor $Z_c = 3/8$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Supercritical Water Oxidation (SCWO) process** for hazardous waste treatment. State the operating conditions and the typical destruction efficiency achieved. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The van der Waals equation is:

$$\left( P + \dfrac{a}{V_m^2} \right)(V_m - b) = RT$$

**Step 1 — Expand to cubic form:**

$$P V_m^3 - (Pb + RT) V_m^2 + a V_m - a b = 0$$

**Step 2 — Apply critical point conditions:**

At the critical point, the isotherm has an inflection point, requiring:

$$\left( \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial V_m} \right)_{T_c} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \left( \dfrac{\partial^2 P}{\partial V_m^2} \right)_{T_c} = 0$$

**Step 3 — First derivative:**

$$\left( \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial V_m} \right)_T = \dfrac{RT}{(V_m - b)^2} - \dfrac{2a}{V_m^3} = 0 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 4 — Second derivative:**

$$\left( \dfrac{\partial^2 P}{\partial V_m^2} \right)_T = -\dfrac{2RT}{(V_m - b)^3} + \dfrac{6a}{V_m^4} = 0 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 5 — Solve the system:**

From (ii):
$$\dfrac{2RT}{(V_m - b)^3} = \dfrac{6a}{V_m^4}$$

$$RT = \dfrac{3a(V_m - b)^3}{V_m^4}$$

Substituting into (i):
$$\dfrac{3a(V_m - b)^3}{V_m^4 (V_m - b)^2} = \dfrac{2a}{V_m^3}$$

$$\dfrac{3(V_m - b)}{V_m} = 2$$

$$3V_m - 3b = 2V_m \implies V_m = 3b$$

**Step 6 — Calculate T_c:**

Substituting $V_m = 3b$ into (i):
$$\dfrac{RT_c}{(2b)^2} = \dfrac{2a}{(3b)^3}$$

$$T_c = \dfrac{2a \cdot 4b^2}{R \cdot 27b^3} = \dfrac{8a}{27Rb}$$

**Step 7 — Calculate P_c:**

$$P_c = \dfrac{RT_c}{V_m - b} - \dfrac{a}{V_m^2} = \dfrac{R \cdot 8a/27Rb}{2b} - \dfrac{a}{9b^2} = \dfrac{a}{27b^2}$$

**Step 8 — Calculate Z_c:**

$$Z_c = \dfrac{P_c V_c}{R T_c} = \dfrac{(a/27b^2)(3b)}{R (8a/27Rb)} = \dfrac{3a/(9b)}{8a/(27b)} = \dfrac{3 \cdot 27}{9 \cdot 8} = \dfrac{81}{72} = \dfrac{3}{8}$$

$$\boxed{Z_c = 0.375}$$

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Supercritical Water Oxidation (SCWO) — Process Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hazardous Organic Waste Feed] --> B[High-Pressure Pump 25 MPa]
    C[Oxidant: Air, O2, or H2O2] --> B
    B --> D[Feed Preheater]
    D --> E[SCWO Reactor T: 400 to 650 C, P: 22 to 30 MPa]
    E --> F[Heat Exchanger Energy Recovery]
    F --> G[Pressure Let-Down Device]
    G --> H[Phase Separator]
    H --> I[Clean Water Effluent]
    H --> J[CO2, N2 Off-Gas]
    E --> K[Reaction: CxHyOz + O2 greater than CO2 + H2O]
    E --> L[Destruction Removal Efficiency greater than 99.99 percent]
    E --> M[Residence Time: 30 to 60 seconds]
    style E fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#900,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    style L fill:#6bcf7f,stroke:#2d5a2d,color:#fff
```

**Operating Conditions:**

- **Temperature:** 400–650 °C
- **Pressure:** 22–30 MPa
- **Residence Time:** 30–60 seconds
- **Oxidant:** Air, pure O₂, or H₂O₂
- **Reactor Type:** Tubular, vessel, or transpiring wall

**Process Description:**

1. **Feed Preparation:** Hazardous organic waste is mixed with an oxidant and pressurized.
2. **Preheating:** The mixture is preheated to 200–300 °C before entering the reactor.
3. **Reaction:** In the SCWO reactor, organic compounds undergo **free-radical oxidation** in single-phase supercritical water, producing CO₂, H₂O, and mineral acids (e.g., HCl, H₂SO₄, H₃PO₄).
4. **Cooling & Energy Recovery:** Hot effluent passes through a heat exchanger for steam generation.
5. **Pressure Let-Down & Separation:** Gases (CO₂, N₂) are separated from liquid effluent.
6. **Effluent Treatment:** Liquid effluent is neutralized before discharge.

**Destruction Efficiency:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> SCWO achieves **> 99.99% destruction removal efficiency (DRE)** for most organic hazardous compounds, including PCBs, dioxins, pesticides, and chemical warfare agents.

**Engineering Challenges:**

- **Corrosion** by mineral acids and high-temperature water.
- **Salt precipitation** clogging reactor walls.
- **High-pressure equipment cost.**

**Applications:** Military chemical weapon destruction (e.g., U.S. Army's APPLE facility), pharmaceutical waste, sewage sludge, refinery spent caustic, and PCBs.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in SCW Questions:**
>
> 1. **Wrong Units for Critical Pressure:** Students often write P_c = 22.1 atm or 22.1 bar. The KTU board requires **22.1 MPa** (or 218 atm). Writing atm will cost **1 mark**.
>
> 2. **Confusing SCWG and SCWO:** SCWG produces **H₂-rich syngas** (fuel gas); SCWO produces **CO₂ and water** (complete oxidation). Mixing up the products is a **3-mark penalty**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Dielectric Constant Drop:** When asked "why SCW dissolves organics," simply stating "low dielectric constant" without comparing with liquid water (78.5 → 2–10) will cost marks. Always provide **before/after numerical comparison**.
>
> 4. **Van der Waals Derivation Errors:** In $Z_c = 3/8$, students often miss the substitution step and write the final answer without showing the cubic form expansion. The KTU examiner expects **all 4 steps**: cubic form → first derivative → second derivative → substitution.
>
> 5. **Phase Diagram Without Critical Point Marking:** Always mark the critical point as a **distinct dot** with both T_c and P_c labeled. Diagrams without numerical labels are penalized.
>
> 6. **Operating Conditions Confusion:** SCWG operates at **higher temperatures (500–700 °C)** with no oxidant, while SCWO uses **excess oxidant at 400–650 °C**. Confusing these is a frequent error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Supercritical Water and Its Properties**

- [x] **Definition:** SCW is water at T > 374 °C AND P > 22.1 MPa; phases indistinguishable.
- [x] **Critical Constants (Board-Must):** T_c = 374 °C, P_c = 22.1 MPa, ρ_c = 0.322 g/cm³.
- [x] **Dielectric Constant Drop:** 78.5 (liquid) → 2–10 (SCW) — **enables organic solubility**.
- [x] **Low Viscosity:** μ ≈ 0.03–0.07 mPa·s — gas-like flow, low pumping cost.
- [x] **High Diffusivity:** 10⁻³ cm²/s — gas-like mass transfer.
- [x] **Density Tunability:** Adjustable from 0.05 to 0.8 g/cm³ via pressure — controllable solvation.
- [x] **K_w Drop:** 10⁻¹⁴ → 10⁻²⁰ — ionic reactions suppressed, radical reactions dominate.
- [x] **SCWG:** Hydrogen-rich syngas from wet biomass (T = 500–700 °C, P = 25–35 MPa).
- [x] **SCWO:** Complete oxidation of hazardous waste (T = 400–650 °C, P = 22–30 MPa, DRE > 99.99%).
- [x] **Van der Waals Derivation:** V_c = 3b, P_c = a/27b², T_c = 8a/27Rb, Z_c = 3/8.
- [x] **Industrial Pioneers:** Modell (SCWO), Antal (SCWG) — credited for modern development.
- [x] **Applications:** Sewage sludge, pharmaceutical waste, military CW agents, bio-crude, hydrogen.
- [x] **Phase Diagram Skill:** Always mark triple point, critical point, SCW region, and operating zones.
- [x] **Comparison Memory Trick:** SCW = "Gas-like diffusivity + Liquid-like density + Organic-like polarity" — a unique hybrid.
- [x] **Energy Recovery:** Both SCWG and SCWO allow heat recovery via heat exchangers for steam generation.
- [x] **Engineering Challenges:** Corrosion, salt plugging, high-pressure capital cost — discussed in viva.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** "**374 °C, 22.1 MPa**" — write these two numbers first in every SCW answer. Examiners award partial credit immediately for correct critical constants.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrothermal Liquefaction (HTL) and Hydrothermal Carbonization (HTC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT836 - WASTE TO ENERGY TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hydrothermal Liquefaction (HTL) and Hydrothermal Carbonization (HTC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Hydrothermal Liquefaction (HTL)

**Formal Definition:**
Hydrothermal Liquefaction (HTL) is a thermochemical conversion process that transforms wet biomass (with moisture content up to **80–90 %**) into a liquid bio-crude oil using **subcritical water** as the reaction medium. The process operates at temperatures between **250 °C and 374 °C** and pressures between **5 MPa and 22 MPa**, which are sufficient to maintain water in its liquid (subcritical) state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> HTL is sometimes called "direct liquefaction" because it bypasses the energy-intensive drying step required in conventional pyrolysis. The water itself acts as a reactant, solvent, and heat-transfer medium simultaneously.

### 1.2 Hydrothermal Carbonization (HTC)

**Formal Definition:**
Hydrothermal Carbonization (HTC), often referred to as "wet torrefaction," is a thermochemical process that converts wet biomass into a solid, coal-like product called **hydrochar** at milder temperatures (**180 °C – 250 °C**) under saturated steam pressure (**1 MPa – 5 MPa**) with residence times of **1 to 12 hours**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction:**
> HTL targets a **liquid** fuel (bio-crude), while HTC targets a **solid** fuel (hydrochar). Both exploit the unique properties of hot compressed water but optimize different reaction pathways.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pressure Cooker" View

Imagine two pressure cookers running simultaneously in a kitchen:

- **HTL is the high-pressure cooker** that violently breaks down vegetables (cellulose, proteins, lipids) into a thick, oily broth — the **bio-crude**. The intense heat and pressure rupture molecular bonds, creating smaller hydrocarbon chains.

- **HTC is the slower, gentler cooker** that dehydrates and condenses the biomass into a black, coal-like briquette — the **hydrochar**. The carbon content rises while oxygen and hydrogen are driven off as water and gases.

In both cases, the cooker's own steam does the chemical work, eliminating the need to dry the wet waste first. This is why HTL and HTC are uniquely suited to **sewage sludge, algae, and food waste**.

---

### 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics (KTU Board Favourite Values)

| Metric | HTL | HTC |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | **250–374 °C** | **180–250 °C** |
| Pressure | **5–22 MPa** | **1–5 MPa** |
| Residence Time | **5–60 min** | **1–12 h** |
| Primary Product | **Bio-crude (30–60 %)** | **Hydrochar (50–80 %)** |
| HHV of Product | **30–38 MJ/kg** | **20–28 MJ/kg** |
| Feed Moisture Limit | Up to **90 %** | Up to **80 %** |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Memory:** **HTL = "Higher Temp, Liquid"** ; **HTC = "Lower Temp, Char"**. The first letters literally describe the outputs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Subcritical Water is the "Magic Medium"

When water is heated above **200 °C** under sufficient pressure to remain liquid, its **dielectric constant drops dramatically** (from ~80 at 25 °C to ~25 at 300 °C). This makes subcritical water behave like a **weak organic solvent**, dissolving hydrophobic organic compounds (oils, lipids) that are normally insoluble in cold water. Simultaneously, the **ionic product ($K_w$) rises**, accelerating acid- and base-catalysed reactions like hydrolysis.

- Hydrolysis of biomass polymers (cellulose, hemicellulose, proteins) → sugars, amino acids.
- Dehydration and decarboxylation of intermediates → oil-like molecules.
- Re-condensation / re-polymerisation of fragments → heavier bio-crude or hydrochar.

### 2.2 Reaction Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

#### A) HTL Reaction Pathway
1. **Heat-up phase** — Biomass slurry is pressurised and heated to target temperature.
2. **Hydrolysis** — Polymers break into monomers (glucose, amino acids, fatty acids).
3. **Dehydration / Decarboxylation** — Monomers lose –OH and –COOH groups; this removes oxygen.
4. **Fragmentation & Re-polymerisation** — Light fragments recombine into heavier oily molecules.
5. **Cool-down & Phase Separation** — Product separates into bio-crude, aqueous phase, solid residue, and gas.

#### B) HTC Reaction Pathway
1. **Hydrolysis** — Polymers hydrolyse to oligomers and monomers.
2. **Dehydration** — Removal of H₂O reduces O/C and H/C ratios.
3. **Decarboxylation** — Loss of CO₂ further reduces oxygen content.
4. **Aromatisation & Condensation** — Monomers condense into polycyclic aromatic networks → **hydrochar**.
5. **Solid-Liquid Separation** — Hydrochar is filtered; process water is recovered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **dehydration** step is far more dominant in HTC, which is why the hydrochar resembles lignite coal in composition.

### 2.3 Process Parameters Influencing Product Yield

| Parameter | Effect on HTL | Effect on HTC |
|---|---|---|
| ↑ Temperature | ↑ Bio-crude yield up to ~350 °C, then ↓ (gas formation) | ↑ Carbon content of hydrochar |
| ↑ Residence Time | ↑ Secondary cracking → more gas | ↑ Degree of carbonisation |
| ↑ Pressure | Maintains water in liquid state | Same function |
| ↑ Biomass Loading | Lower yield per pass | Increases char homogeneity |
| Catalyst (e.g., Na₂CO₃) | ↑ Bio-crude yield, ↓ char | Mild effect |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `$\vert$` for absolute value or conditionals to avoid breaking Markdown tables.**

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| $Y_{\text{mass}}$ | Mass yield = $\dfrac{M_{\text{product}}}{M_{\text{dry feed}}} \times 100$ | % | Yield of bio-crude or hydrochar |
| $Y_{\text{energy}}$ | Energy yield = $Y_{\text{mass}} \times \dfrac{HHV_{\text{product}}}{HHV_{\text{feed}}} \times 100$ | % | Overall energy efficiency |
| $C_{\text{recovery}}$ | Carbon recovery = $\dfrac{M_{\text{product}} \cdot C_{\text{product}}}{M_{\text{feed}} \cdot C_{\text{feed}}} \times 100$ | % | Carbon flow tracking |
| HHV (Dulong) | $0.3383\,C + 1.422\!\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 0.095\,S$ | MJ/kg | Heating value from ultimate analysis (C, H, O, S in wt %) |
| Mass Balance | $M_{\text{feed}} = M_{\text{bio-crude}} + M_{\text{aqueous}} + M_{\text{solid}} + M_{\text{gas}}$ | kg | Conservation of mass |
| Energy Recovery $\eta$ | $\eta = \dfrac{M_{\text{product}} \cdot HHV_{\text{product}}}{M_{\text{feed}} \cdot HHV_{\text{feed}}} \times 100$ | % | Process efficiency |
| Higher Heating Value of bio-crude | Typically **32–38 MJ/kg** | MJ/kg | Comparison with petroleum crude (~45 MJ/kg) |
| Energy Density Ratio (EDR) | $EDR = \dfrac{HHV_{\text{product}}}{HHV_{\text{feed}}}$ | – | Upgrading factor |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HTL of microalgae** — Produces drop-in renewable crude; commercial pilots by **Murphy Oil** and **Sapphire Energy**.
- **HTC of sewage sludge** — Reduces volume by **~80 %**; produces hydrochar usable as soil amendment.
- **Co-located with WWTPs** — Wet biowaste is processed without pre-drying, saving **30–40 %** of the energy input compared to pyrolysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Mass and Energy Balance for an HTL Process

**Problem Statement:**
A HTL reactor is fed with **100 kg of dry algal biomass** having $HHV_{\text{feed}} = 20 \text{ MJ/kg}$ and carbon content $C_{\text{feed}} = 50\%$. After processing, the products are:

- Bio-crude: **40 kg**, HHV = **36 MJ/kg**, Carbon = **75 %**
- Aqueous phase: **35 kg**
- Solid residue: **15 kg**, HHV = **10 MJ/kg**
- Gas: **10 kg**

**Step 1 — Verify Mass Balance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{feed}} &= M_{\text{bio-crude}} + M_{\text{aqueous}} + M_{\text{solid}} + M_{\text{gas}} \\
100 \text{ kg} &= 40 + 35 + 15 + 10 \\
100 \text{ kg} &= 100 \text{ kg} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

The mass is conserved; the equation is internally consistent.

**Step 2 — Calculate Mass Yield of Bio-crude:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{\text{mass, bio-crude}} &= \frac{M_{\text{bio-crude}}}{M_{\text{dry feed}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{40}{100} \times 100 \\
&= 40\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** 40 % of the dry algal feed is converted into the desired liquid biofuel.

**Step 3 — Calculate Energy Yield (Energy Recovery Efficiency):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{energy}} &= \frac{M_{\text{bio-crude}} \cdot HHV_{\text{bio-crude}}}{M_{\text{feed}} \cdot HHV_{\text{feed}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{40 \times 36}{100 \times 20} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{1440}{2000} \times 100 \\
&= 72\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The bio-crude retains 72 % of the chemical energy originally present in the biomass, despite yielding only 40 % by mass. This energy densification (HHV rises from 20 to 36 MJ/kg) is precisely why HTL is attractive.

**Step 4 — Calculate Carbon Recovery in Bio-crude:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{recovery}} &= \frac{M_{\text{bio-crude}} \cdot C_{\text{bio-crude}}}{M_{\text{feed}} \cdot C_{\text{feed}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{40 \times 0.75}{100 \times 0.50} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{30}{50} \times 100 \\
&= 60\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** 60 % of the feed carbon is concentrated into the bio-crude phase — important for life-cycle and CO₂ accounting.

**Step 5 — Compute Energy Density Ratio (EDR):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EDR &= \frac{HHV_{\text{bio-crude}}}{HHV_{\text{feed}}} \\
&= \frac{36}{20} \\
&= 1.8
\end{aligned}
$$

The bio-crude is **1.8 × more energy-dense** than the original feedstock.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: HHV Using Dulong's Formula

**Given Ultimate Analysis of HTC Hydrochar (wt %, dry basis):**
- $C = 65\%$, $H = 5\%$, $O = 25\%$, $S = 0.5\%$, $N = 1.5\%$ (ash-free basis).

**Step 1 — Identify formula terms.**

Dulong's formula:
$$
HHV = 0.3383\,C + 1.422\!\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 0.095\,S \quad \text{(MJ/kg)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HHV &= 0.3383 \times 65 + 1.422 \times \left(5 - \frac{25}{8}\right) + 0.095 \times 0.5 \\
&= 0.3383 \times 65 + 1.422 \times (5 - 3.125) + 0.0475 \\
&= 21.99 + 1.422 \times 1.875 + 0.0475 \\
&= 21.99 + 2.666 + 0.0475 \\
&= 24.70 \text{ MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**

The hydrochar's HHV of **24.70 MJ/kg** lies between lignite (~25 MJ/kg) and sub-bituminous coal (~28 MJ/kg), confirming that HTC effectively "coalifies" wet biomass.

---

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix (HTL vs HTC)

| Property | HTL Bio-crude | HTC Hydrochar |
|---|---|---|
| Phase | Liquid | Solid |
| Yield (mass %) | 30–60 | 50–80 |
| Carbon (wt %) | 65–80 | 55–75 |
| Oxygen (wt %) | 10–20 | 15–25 |
| HHV (MJ/kg) | 30–38 | 20–28 |
| Energy Recovery (%) | 60–80 | 70–85 |
| Primary Use | Refining into transport fuel | Solid fuel / soil amendment |
| Process Severity | High | Low–Moderate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule for KTU Answers:**
> Choose **HTL** when liquid biofuels (diesel/gasoline substitutes) are desired. Choose **HTC** when solid fuels, soil conditioners, or activated carbon precursors are the goal.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 HTL Process Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wet Biomass Slurry] --> B[High Pressure Feed Pump]
    B --> C[Heat Exchanger Pre-heater]
    C --> D[HTL Reactor 250-374 C, 5-22 MPa]
    D --> E[Cooler]
    E --> F[Pressure Reduction Valve]
    F --> G[Phase Separator]
    G --> H1[Bio-crude Oil]
    G --> H2[Aqueous Phase Recycle]
    G --> H3[Solid Residue]
    G --> H4[Non-condensable Gas]

    H2 --> I[Wastewater Treatment]
    H3 --> J[Nutrient Recovery]

    H1 --> K[Hydrotreater Upgrading]
    K --> L[Renewable Diesel / Naphtha]
```

### 4.2 HTC Process Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Wet Biomass] --> B1[Slurry Preparation Tank]
    B1 --> C1[HTC Reactor 180-250 C, 1-5 MPa]
    C1 --> D1[Cooling Coil]
    D1 --> E1[Filter Press]
    E1 --> F1[Hydrochar Pelletizing]
    E1 --> G1[Process Water Recycle]

    F1 --> H1[Coal Substitute Fuel]
    F1 --> I1[Soil Amendment]
    F1 --> J1[Activated Carbon Feedstock]

    G1 --> K1[Nutrient Recovery Loop]
    G1 --> L1[Wastewater Polishing]
```

### 4.3 HTL vs HTC Comparative Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Wet Biomass] --> B{Desired Product?}
    B -->|Liquid Biofuel| C[Choose HTL]
    B -->|Solid Fuel / Soil| D[Choose HTC]
    C --> E[Temp 250-374 C]
    D --> F[Temp 180-250 C]
    E --> G[Bio-crude Output]
    F --> H[Hydrochar Output]
    G --> I[Refine to Diesel]
    H --> J[Combust or Pelletize]
```

> [!TIP]
> These block diagrams capture the **functional architecture** of each process — material streams, energy flows, and downstream applications — in line with KTU's expectation of schematic process understanding.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Hydrothermal Liquefaction (HTL). List any three operating parameters that influence the bio-crude yield.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Hydrothermal Liquefaction (HTL) is a thermochemical conversion process in which wet biomass is converted into a liquid bio-crude oil under **subcritical water conditions**, typically at temperatures of **250–374 °C** and pressures of **5–22 MPa**, with residence times of **5–60 minutes**.

Three key operating parameters influencing bio-crude yield:
1. **Reaction Temperature** — Increasing temperature (up to ~350 °C) raises bio-crude yield by enhancing hydrolysis and decarboxylation. Beyond ~370 °C, secondary cracking converts bio-crude into gas, reducing yield.
2. **Residence Time** — Short residence times (10–30 min) favour bio-crude; longer times shift the product distribution toward gases and char.
3. **Catalyst Addition** — Homogeneous catalysts such as Na₂CO₃ or KOH suppress char formation and increase bio-crude yield by 5–15 %.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Hydrothermal Carbonization (HTC) and Hydrothermal Liquefaction (HTL) on the basis of temperature, primary product, and reaction severity.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | HTC | HTL |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | 180–250 °C (lower) | 250–374 °C (higher) |
| Primary Product | Hydrochar (solid) | Bio-crude (liquid) |
| Reaction Severity | Mild | Severe |

In HTC, dehydration reactions dominate, producing a coal-like solid with HHV ~20–28 MJ/kg. In HTL, the higher severity promotes bond cleavage and re-polymerisation into oily hydrocarbons with HHV ~30–38 MJ/kg. Both processes, however, use subcritical water and can accept wet feedstocks without prior drying.

---

### Part B — Full-Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks] With the help of a neat process flow diagram, describe the working of a typical HTL plant. Discuss the role of subcritical water as the reaction medium.**

**(b) [7 Marks] A HTL reactor processes 200 kg of dry microalgae (HHV = 18 MJ/kg, Carbon = 48 %). The products obtained are: bio-crude — 85 kg (HHV = 35 MJ/kg, C = 76 %), aqueous phase — 70 kg, solid residue — 30 kg, gas — 15 kg. Calculate the (i) mass yield of bio-crude, (ii) energy recovery efficiency, and (iii) carbon recovery in bio-crude.**

---

**Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Working of a typical HTL plant (process flow description):**

1. **Feed Preparation:** Wet biomass (algae, sludge, etc.) is ground and slurried with water to form a pumpable suspension with 15–25 % solids.
2. **Pressurisation:** A high-pressure pump elevates the slurry to operating pressure (10–22 MPa).
3. **Pre-heating:** The slurry passes through a heat exchanger that recovers heat from the hot product stream, reducing external energy demand.
4. **Reactor:** The pre-heated slurry enters the HTL reactor where it is heated to **300–350 °C**. Residence time is 10–30 min.
5. **Cooling & Pressure Reduction:** The effluent is cooled and depressurised through a let-down valve.
6. **Phase Separation:** A decanter/centrifuge separates the four phases — bio-crude (top), aqueous (middle), solids (bottom), and gas.
7. **Product Upgrading:** Bio-crude is hydrotreated to remove oxygen, nitrogen, and sulphur, producing renewable diesel.

**Role of Subcritical Water:**

- **Solvent Action:** Above 200 °C, water's dielectric constant drops from ~80 to ~25, allowing it to dissolve hydrophobic organic compounds.
- **Reactant:** Participates in hydrolysis and dehydration reactions.
- **Heat Carrier:** Provides excellent heat transfer.
- **Acid/Base Catalyst:** Elevated $K_w$ promotes both acidic and basic reaction pathways without added catalysts.

---

**Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Given:**
- $M_{\text{feed}} = 200$ kg, $HHV_{\text{feed}} = 18$ MJ/kg, $C_{\text{feed}} = 48\%$
- $M_{\text{bio-crude}} = 85$ kg, $HHV_{\text{bio-crude}} = 35$ MJ/kg, $C_{\text{bio-crude}} = 76\%$

**Step (i) — Mass Yield:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{\text{mass}} &= \frac{85}{200} \times 100 = 42.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii) — Energy Recovery:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{85 \times 35}{200 \times 18} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{2975}{3600} \times 100 \\
&= 82.64\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (iii) — Carbon Recovery:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{recovery}} &= \frac{85 \times 0.76}{200 \times 0.48} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{64.6}{96} \times 100 \\
&= 67.29\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:** Mass yield = **42.5 %**, Energy recovery = **82.64 %**, Carbon recovery = **67.29 %**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks] Discuss the reaction chemistry of Hydrothermal Carbonization (HTC). How does the composition of hydrochar compare with lignite coal?**

**(b) [7 Marks] A HTC reactor is fed with 150 kg (dry basis) of food waste with HHV = 16 MJ/kg. The hydrochar produced is 95 kg with HHV = 24 MJ/kg. Calculate the energy yield, mass yield, and comment on whether HTC is energetically favourable for this feedstock.**

---

**Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Reaction Chemistry of HTC:**

HTC proceeds through a sequence of dominant reactions:

1. **Hydrolysis:** Polymers like cellulose, hemicellulose, proteins, and lipids break down into monomers (glucose, xylose, amino acids, fatty acids).

2. **Dehydration:** Monomers lose water molecules, drastically reducing the O/C and H/C atomic ratios.

3. **Decarboxylation:** Loss of CO₂ groups further reduces oxygen content, generating CO₂ as a by-product.

4. **Condensation / Aromatisation:** Intermediate fragments undergo aldol condensation and polymerise into polycyclic aromatic structures, forming the hydrochar matrix.

**Overall simplified reaction (for carbohydrate):**
$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5 \xrightarrow{\text{HTC}} \text{C}_{\text{char}} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2
$$

**Comparison with Lignite:**

| Property | Hydrochar (HTC) | Lignite Coal |
|---|---|---|
| Carbon (wt %) | 55–75 | 60–75 |
| Oxygen (wt %) | 15–25 | 15–25 |
| HHV (MJ/kg) | 20–28 | 20–25 |
| Moisture | Low (after drying) | High in raw form |
| Sulphur | Negligible | Often high |

Hydrochar is **compositionally similar to lignite** but with much lower sulphur and ash content, making it a cleaner solid fuel.

---

**Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Given:**
- $M_{\text{feed}} = 150$ kg, $HHV_{\text{feed}} = 16$ MJ/kg
- $M_{\text{hydrochar}} = 95$ kg, $HHV_{\text{hydrochar}} = 24$ MJ/kg

**Step 1 — Mass Yield:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{\text{mass}} &= \frac{95}{150} \times 100 = 63.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy Yield (Energy Recovery):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{energy}} &= \frac{95 \times 24}{150 \times 16} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{2280}{2400} \times 100 \\
&= 95.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy Density Ratio:**

$$
EDR = \frac{24}{16} = 1.5
$$

**Comment:**

With an **energy recovery of 95 %** and an **EDR of 1.5**, HTC is **energetically highly favourable** for this food-waste feedstock. The hydrochar is **1.5× more energy-dense** than the original feed, indicating that HTC acts as an effective energy densification process. Compared to incineration or anaerobic digestion, HTC offers the additional benefit of producing a storable, transportable solid fuel.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Mistakes:** Students frequently forget to convert percentages to fractions (e.g., using 48 instead of 0.48 for carbon content). This single error propagates through all calculations and leads to **2–3 mark deductions**.
> 2. **Forgetting Mass Balance Closure:** Always verify $M_{\text{feed}} = \Sigma M_{\text{products}}$. If it does not close, re-check the given numerical values (sometimes a typo exists in the question paper).
> 3. **Confusing HHV with LHV:** KTU strictly uses **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** for solid and liquid fuels; using LHV will be marked wrong.
> 4. **Mixing up HTL and HTC conditions:** Many students write HTL temperatures when answering an HTC question. Memorise the ranges — this is the most common single-mark loss.
> 5. **Skipping the Dulong Formula derivation:** When asked to compute HHV from ultimate analysis, always show **all substitution steps**. KTU awards partial credit for intermediate steps.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **HTL** = **High-Temp Liquid** route → bio-crude (HHV ~35 MJ/kg), 250–374 °C, 5–22 MPa.
- ✅ **HTC** = **Lower-Temp Char** route → hydrochar (HHV ~24 MJ/kg), 180–250 °C, 1–5 MPa.
- ✅ Both processes accept **wet biomass** without pre-drying — a major advantage over pyrolysis.
- ✅ **Subcritical water** acts as solvent, reactant, and catalyst.
- ✅ Key reactions: **Hydrolysis → Dehydration → Decarboxylation → (Re-polymerisation/Aromatisation)**.
- ✅ **Mass yield** = $M_{\text{product}} / M_{\text{feed}} \times 100$.
- ✅ **Energy yield** = $Y_{\text{mass}} \times (HHV_{\text{product}} / HHV_{\text{feed}})$.
- ✅ **Dulong's Formula:** $HHV = 0.3383\,C + 1.422(H - O/8) + 0.095\,S$ (MJ/kg).
- ✅ **Carbon Recovery** is essential for life-cycle and CO₂-emission accounting.
- ✅ **HTL bio-crude requires hydrotreating** before use as transport fuel.
- ✅ **HTC hydrochar** is comparable to lignite but cleaner (low S, low ash).
- ✅ Always **close the mass balance** in numerical problems before computing yields.
- ✅ Remember the **decision rule**: liquid fuel → HTL; solid fuel / soil → HTC.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conversion of Wet Waste to Energy: Suitable Feedstock and Raw Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT836 - WASTE TO ENERGY TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Conversion of Wet Waste to Energy: Suitable Feedstock and Raw Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Wet Waste"? — Formal KTU 2024 Definition

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Waste to Energy Technology* (OEBBT836), **Wet Waste** (also called *biodegradable organic waste*, *putrescible waste*, or *high-moisture waste*) refers to the fraction of municipal, agricultural, and industrial solid waste that contains a **moisture content greater than ~50 % by weight** (wet basis) and a **Volatile Solids (VS) content typically above 60 %** of Total Solids (TS). It is the dominant feedstock class for **biological conversion routes** such as anaerobic digestion (biogas), fermentation (bioethanol), and hydrothermal liquefaction (biocrude).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Standard)**
> *"Wet waste is an organic, biodegradable, high-moisture (≥ 50 % w.b.) residue of biological origin — animal, plant, aquatic, or municipal — whose energy content is unlocked through bio-chemical (enzymatic/microbial) or thermo-chemical (with water as reactant) pathways rather than direct combustion."*

## 1.2 Classification Snapshot

| Parameter | Wet Waste (Fermentable) | Dry Waste (Refuse-Derived Fuel) |
|---|---|---|
| Moisture (% w.b.) | **> 50 %** | < 20 % |
| VS / TS ratio | **> 0.6** | < 0.5 |
| Calorific value (raw) | 1.5 – 6 MJ/kg | 12 – 20 MJ/kg |
| Preferred route | **Biochemical** | Thermochemical (combustion, gasification) |
| Example | Kitchen waste, sewage sludge, cattle dung | Plastics, paper, dry leaves |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Brewery in a Bucket"

Imagine you have a large steel bucket filled with yesterday's leftover rice, vegetable peels, and milk curd. If you seal it, stir it gently, and let it sit for 30 days in mild warmth, **bubbles will rise and flammable gas will collect at the top**. This is precisely what happens in an **anaerobic digester**:

- The wet waste = the "brew" (substrate + water).
- The bacteria = the invisible "yeast" (no oxygen needed).
- The biogas (≈ 55–65 % CH₄ + 35–45 % CO₂) = the "fermented product."
- The digestate = the "spent grains" (a nutrient-rich biofertilizer).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why wet waste is special:** Water is not a nuisance here — it is a *reactant* and a *heat-transfer medium*. This is why wet biomass cannot be burned efficiently (energy is wasted vaporising water), but it shines in **anaerobic digestion** where microbes thrive in the aqueous phase.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters for KTU

Module 4 of OEBBT836 explicitly pivots from dry waste combustion to **wet biomass valorization**. The KTU paper-setter expects students to (a) identify suitable feedstocks, (b) characterise them physically and chemically, and (c) match them to the correct conversion technology. Questions on feedstock suitability appear almost every semester — typically 7 to 14 marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Feedstock Suitability

For a wet waste to qualify as an *engineered* feedstock for energy recovery, it must satisfy five engineering criteria:

1. **High Moisture Content (MC > 50 % w.b.)** — needed for microbial/aqueous reactions.
2. **High Biodegradability (VS/TS > 0.6)** — ensures microbes have organic substrate to attack.
3. **Favourable Carbon-to-Nitrogen (C/N) Ratio** — ideal range **20 – 30 : 1**. Below 15 → ammonia inhibition; above 40 → nitrogen starvation.
4. **Low Concentration of Inhibitors** — heavy metals, antibiotics, sulfides, pesticides.
5. **Continuous & Local Availability** — logistics dictate plant viability (collection radius < 25 km typical).

## 2.2 Categories of Suitable Wet Feedstock (Raw Materials)

### A. Municipal Wet Waste
- Kitchen / food waste (FW)
- Market waste (vegetable, fish, fruit)
- Sewage sludge (primary + secondary)
- Slaughterhouse waste (blood, offal, manure)

### B. Agricultural Wet Waste
- Cattle dung / pig slurry / poultry litter
- Rice straw (after pre-soaking or ensiling)
- Green algae and water hyacinth

### C. Industrial Wet Waste
- Dairy effluent (whey, cheese wash water)
- Distillery spent wash (pressed-mud / vinasse)
- Pulp & paper black liquor
- Food-processing wastewater

### D. Aquatic / Marine Biomass
- Macroalgae (kelp, *Sargassum*)
- Microalgae (*Chlorella*, *Spirulina*)
- Seaweed blooms (sargassum tides on Kerala coast)

> [!TIP]
> **Kerala-specific board cue:** Examiners from KTU frequently include a question on **coconut pith / coir pith, toddy waste, fish-market waste, and rice parboiling effluent** — these are regional wet wastes abundant in Kerala.

## 2.3 Critical Feedstock Characterisation Parameters

| Symbol | Parameter | Definition / Formula | Typical Wet-Waste Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MC** | Moisture Content (w.b.) | $\text{MC} = \dfrac{m_{\text{water}}}{m_{\text{wet}}} \times 100$ | 55 – 95 % |
| **TS** | Total Solids | $\text{TS} = m_{\text{dry}} / m_{\text{wet}} \times 100$ | 5 – 45 % |
| **VS** | Volatile Solids | $\text{VS} = m_{\text{dry}} - m_{\text{ash}}$ | 60 – 90 % of TS |
| **C/N** | Carbon : Nitrogen ratio | $\text{C/N} = \%C_{\text{org}} / \%N_{\text{total}}$ | 15 – 40 |
| **BMP** | Biochemical Methane Potential | Lab-measured L CH₄ / kg VS | 200 – 500 L/kg VS |
| **COD** | Chemical Oxygen Demand | O₂ consumed per L effluent | 5 000 – 80 000 mg/L |
| **BOD** | Biological Oxygen Demand | O₂ consumed in 5 days at 20 °C | 2 000 – 30 000 mg/L |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Buswell Equation** (1939) — the foundational stoichiometry for anaerobic digestion — is mandatory for KTU Module 4. Always quote it:

$$
\mathrm{C}_n\mathrm{H}_a\mathrm{O}_b + \left(n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2}\right)\mathrm{H_2O} \;\longrightarrow\; \left(\dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4}\right)\mathrm{CO_2} + \left(\dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4}\right)\mathrm{CH_4}
$$

## 2.4 Matching Feedstock to Conversion Technology (Decision Logic)

| Feedstock Property | Best Suited Route |
|---|---|
| MC > 80 %, VS > 70 %, C/N 20–30 | **Anaerobic Digestion (biogas)** |
| Sugar/starch-rich liquid (whey, fruit juice) | **Fermentation (bioethanol)** |
| Lipid-rich (used cooking oil, oilseeds) | **Biodiesel (transesterification)** |
| High moisture, lignocellulosic (algae, water hyacinth) | **Hydrothermal Liquefaction (HTL)** |
| Manure/slurry on dairy farms | **Co-digestion in CSTR / plug-flow digesters** |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Plant sizing:** BMP × daily feedstock VS mass = design methane flow.
