- **Revenue model:** CH₄ volume × LHV → electricity (CHP) or piped gas.
- **Co-digestion strategy:** Mixing a high-C waste (food waste, C/N ≈ 30) with a high-N waste (manure, C/N ≈ 10) optimises the digester C/N to ~25:1, boosting gas yield by 20–40 %.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Methane Yield from Feedstock Composition

### Step 1 — Empirical Molecular Formula of Wet Waste
A typical carbohydrate-rich kitchen waste can be approximated by the empirical formula **C₆H₁₀O₅** (hexose unit). Plug this into Buswell's stoichiometry.

### Step 2 — Identify Coefficients
Here, $n = 6$, $a = 10$, $b = 5$.

### Step 3 — Compute the Water Coefficient

$$
\text{Water coefficient} = n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2} = 6 - \dfrac{10}{4} - \dfrac{5}{2}
$$

$$
= 6 - 2.5 - 2.5 = 1.0
$$

Therefore, **1 mole of water is consumed per mole of substrate**.

### Step 4 — Compute the CO₂ Coefficient

$$
\text{CO}_2 = \dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4} = \dfrac{6}{2} - \dfrac{10}{8} + \dfrac{5}{4}
$$

$$
= 3.0 - 1.25 + 1.25 = 3.0
$$

Hence **3 moles of CO₂ are produced**.

### Step 5 — Compute the CH₄ Coefficient

$$
\text{CH}_4 = \dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4} = 3.0 + 1.25 - 1.25 = 3.0
$$

Hence **3 moles of CH₄ are produced per mole of C₆H₁₀O₅**.

### Step 6 — Write the Balanced Buswell Equation

$$
\mathrm{C_6H_{10}O_5} + \mathrm{H_2O} \;\longrightarrow\; 3\,\mathrm{CO_2} + 3\,\mathrm{CH_4}
$$

### Step 7 — Convert to Mass Basis

Molar mass of C₆H₁₀O₅ = 162 g/mol.

**Mass of CH₄ produced** = 3 × 16 = 48 g.

**Volume of CH₄ at STP (0 °C, 1 atm, 22.4 L/mol):**

$$
V_{\text{CH}_4}^{\text{STP}} = 3 \times 22.4 = 67.2 \;\text{L}
$$

**Methane yield per kg of substrate:**

$$
Y = \dfrac{67.2 \;\text{L}}{162 \;\text{g}} \approx 0.415 \;\text{L CH}_4 / \text{g substrate}
$$

Since real wet waste is only partially degradable, **practical yield ≈ 60–70 %** of theoretical:

$$
Y_{\text{practical}} \approx 0.25 \;\text{L CH}_4 / \text{g VS}
$$

This is the famous rule-of-thumb used in every KTU textbook: **0.25 L CH₄ per gram of VS added**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Energy Content of Methane from a Feedstock Stream

### Given
- Daily feedstock = **100 tonnes** of kitchen waste.
- VS/TS = 0.80, TS = 0.20 → VS fraction in wet mass = 0.16.
- BMP = 0.30 L CH₄ / g VS (typical of Indian food waste).

### Step 1 — Daily VS Mass Available

$$
M_{\text{VS}} = 100 \times 10^3 \;\text{kg} \times 0.16 = 16{,}000 \;\text{kg VS/day}
$$

### Step 2 — Daily Methane Volume at STP

$$
V_{\text{CH}_4} = 16{,}000 \;\text{kg} \times 0.30 \;\text{L/g} \times 10^3 \;\text{g/kg}
$$

$$
V_{\text{CH}_4} = 4.8 \times 10^6 \;\text{L/day} = 4800 \;\text{m}^3\text{/day at STP}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the LHV of Methane

$$
\text{LHV}_{\text{CH}_4} \approx 35.8 \;\text{MJ/m}^3
$$

### Step 4 — Daily Chemical Energy

$$
E = 4800 \;\text{m}^3 \times 35.8 \;\text{MJ/m}^3 = 1.718 \times 10^5 \;\text{MJ/day}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Electrical Energy (CHP efficiency 35 %)

$$
E_{\text{el}} = 0.35 \times 1.718 \times 10^5 = 60{,}137 \;\text{MJ/day}
$$

### Step 6 — Convert to kWh

$$
E_{\text{el}} = \dfrac{60{,}137}{3.6} \approx 16{,}705 \;\text{kWh/day}
$$

### Step 7 — Equivalent Household Supply (assuming 5 kWh/household/day)

$$
N_{\text{houses}} = \dfrac{16{,}705}{5} \approx 3340 \;\text{houses/day}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation maps the entire **waste-to-energy chain** — feedstock quantity → methane volume → thermal energy → electrical energy → social benefit. Examiners reward such end-to-end calculations heavily.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Feedstock Selection Decision Table

| Criterion | Food Waste | Cattle Dung | Sewage Sludge | Fish Market Waste |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Moisture (%) | 75 | 85 | 95 | 80 |
| VS/TS | 0.90 | 0.80 | 0.70 | 0.85 |
| C/N | 25 | 18 | 9 | 20 |
| BMP (L/kg VS) | 450 | 250 | 300 | 400 |
| Inhibitors | Low | Low (NH₃ risk) | High (heavy metals) | High (salts) |
| Best route | Mono-AD | Co-digestion | Co-digestion | Pre-treatment + AD |
| Kerala availability | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Wet Waste to Energy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[feedA: Municipal Solid Waste] --> B[feedB: Source Segregation Unit]
    C[feedC: Agricultural Residues] --> B
    D[feedD: Industrial Effluents] --> B
    E[feedE: Aquatic Biomass] --> B
    B --> F[feedF: Pre-treatment Stage]
    F --> G[feedG: Shredding]
    G --> H[feedH: Particle Size 5 mm]
    H --> I[feedI: Mixing Blending Tank]
    I --> J[feedJ: Anaerobic Digester CSTR]
    I --> K[feedK: Hydrothermal Liquefaction Reactor]
    I --> L[feedL: Fermentation Vessel]
    J --> M[feedM: Biogas Upgradation Unit]
    K --> N[feedN: Biocrude Upgrading]
    L --> O[feedO: Bioethanol Distillation]
    M --> P[feedP: Combined Heat and Power CHP]
    N --> Q[feedQ: Renewable Diesel Pool]
    O --> R[feedR: Ethanol Blending with Petrol]
    P --> S[feedS: Grid Electricity and Heat]
    Q --> S
    R --> S
```

## 4.2 Feedstock Suitability Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[startA: Wet Waste Input] --> B{nodeB: MC greater than 80 percent}
    B -- Yes --> C[nodeC: Sugar or Starch Rich}
    B -- No --> D{nodeD: Lipid Rich}
    C -- Yes --> E[nodeE: Anaerobic Digestion}
    C -- No --> F{nodeF: Lignocellulosic}
    D -- Yes --> G[nodeG: Transesterification to Biodiesel}
    D -- No --> H[nodeH: Hydrothermal Liquefaction}
    F -- Yes --> H
    F -- No --> I[nodeI: Pre treatment Required}
    I --> J[nodeJ: Steam Explosion or Acid Hydrolysis]
    J --> E
    E --> K[prodK: Biogas and Digestate]
    G --> L[prodL: FAME Biodiesel]
    H --> M[prodM: Biocrude Aqueous Phase}
    M --> N[prodN: HTL Biocrude Upgrading]
```

## 4.3 Process Flow of a Typical Biogas Plant (Feedstock → Energy)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[stepA: Feedstock Reception and Weighbridge] --> B1[stepB: Pre size Reduction]
    B1 --> C1[stepC: Mixing and Slurry Preparation]
    C1 --> D1[stepD: Primary Digester Mesophilic 35 C]
    D1 --> E1[stepE: Secondary Digester Thermophilic 55 C]
    E1 --> F1[stepF: Biogas Holder and Desulfurisation]
    F1 --> G1[stepG: Scrubbing and CO2 Removal]
    G1 --> H1[stepH: Combined Heat and Power Engine]
    E1 --> I1[stepI: Digestate Dewatering]
    I1 --> J1[stepJ: Liquid Fraction as Biofertilizer]
    I1 --> K1[stepK: Solid Fraction as Soil Conditioner]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These block diagrams satisfy the KTU 2024 expectation that students must be able to *sketch and label* at least one process flow in the answer sheet. Always include **operating temperatures, residence times (HRT 20–40 days), and energy vectors** in your final diagram.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]
**List any six examples of wet waste that are commonly used as feedstock for bioenergy production. State two key characterisation parameters used to assess their suitability.**

**Model Answer (6 points, ~80 words):**

Six examples of wet waste suitable for bioenergy conversion are:

1. **Kitchen / food waste** from hostels and canteens (high MC, high VS).
2. **Cattle dung and poultry litter** from dairy/poultry farms.
3. **Sewage sludge** from municipal sewage-treatment plants.
4. **Market waste** — vegetables, fish, fruit — typical of Kerala's urban markets.
5. **Distillery spent wash** (vinasse) from ethanol plants.
6. **Aquatic biomass** such as water hyacinth or *Spirulina*.

**Two key characterisation parameters:**
- **Moisture Content (MC, % wet basis)** — indicates suitability for AD (>50 % preferred).
- **Carbon-to-Nitrogen Ratio (C/N)** — ideal range 20 – 30 : 1 for stable methanogenesis.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write only *examples* and forget the *characterisation parameters*. KTU examiners explicitly mention "state two parameters" — failure to do so loses 1 mark out of 3.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]
**State the Buswell equation for the anaerobic digestion of a generic organic substrate C_nH_aO_b. What is the maximum theoretical methane yield in L CH₄ per gram of VS added for a typical carbohydrate?**

**Model Answer (6 points, ~80 words):**

The Buswell equation (1939) for anaerobic digestion is:

$$
\mathrm{C}_n\mathrm{H}_a\mathrm{O}_b + \left(n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2}\right)\mathrm{H_2O} \longrightarrow \left(\dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4}\right)\mathrm{CO_2} + \left(\dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4}\right)\mathrm{CH_4}
$$

For a typical carbohydrate (C₆H₁₀O₅), substituting $n=6$, $a=10$, $b=5$ gives:

$$
\mathrm{C_6H_{10}O_5 + H_2O \longrightarrow 3\,CO_2 + 3\,CH_4}
$$

This corresponds to a theoretical methane yield of **≈ 0.415 L CH₄/g VS** at STP. In practice, due to incomplete degradation, the working figure used by KTU examiners is **≈ 0.25 L CH₄/g VS added**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern: **internal choice between Question A and Question B**. Both must be attempted at the mock level.

---

### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** With the help of a suitable diagram, describe the **classification of wet waste feedstocks** used for bioenergy production. List at least **four categories** with two examples each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hostel mess generates **500 kg/day** of food waste with the following characteristics: **Moisture Content = 75 %**, **Volatile Solids / Total Solids = 0.85**. Laboratory BMP test yields **0.35 L CH₄ per g VS** at STP. Determine:
(i) Daily methane volume generated.
(ii) Daily electrical energy produced assuming CHP efficiency 35 % and LHV of CH₄ = 35.8 MJ/m³.
(iii) Number of hostel rooms that can be lit (assuming 100 W bulb, 6 h/day, 80 % lamp efficacy). **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — Q A (a) [7 marks]

**Classification of Wet Waste Feedstocks for Bioenergy:**

Wet waste is broadly classified into **four categories**:

| Category | Example 1 | Example 2 |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Municipal wet waste** | Kitchen / food waste | Market (vegetable, fish) waste |
| **2. Agricultural wet waste** | Cattle dung | Poultry litter |
| **3. Industrial wet waste** | Distillery spent wash (vinasse) | Dairy whey |
| **4. Aquatic biomass** | Water hyacinth | Microalgae (*Chlorella*) |

**Selection criteria** for each category:
- *Municipal:* Abundant, high MC (>70 %), high BMP.
- *Agricultural:* Co-digestion potential to balance C/N.
- *Industrial:* Continuous availability, but may need pre-treatment for inhibitors.
- *Aquatic:* Rapid growth, no land competition; suitable for HTL.

A simple classification block diagram should be drawn (see SECTION 4 diagram 4.2).

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — Q A (b) [7 marks]

**Given data:**
- Wet waste feed = $F = 500$ kg/day
- MC = 75 % → TS = 25 %
- VS / TS = 0.85 → VS fraction (of wet mass) = $0.25 \times 0.85 = 0.2125$
- BMP = 0.35 L CH₄ / g VS (at STP)
- $\eta_{\text{CHP}} = 0.35$
- LHV of CH₄ = 35.8 MJ/m³

**(i) Daily methane volume**

$$
M_{\text{VS}} = 500 \times 0.2125 = 106.25 \;\text{kg VS/day}
$$

$$
V_{\text{CH}_4} = 106.25 \times 0.35 \times 1000 = 37{,}187.5 \;\text{L/day} = 37.19 \;\text{m}^3\text{/day}
$$

**(ii) Daily electrical energy**

$$
E_{\text{thermal}} = 37.19 \times 35.8 = 1331.4 \;\text{MJ/day}
$$

$$
E_{\text{el}} = 0.35 \times 1331.4 = 465.99 \;\text{MJ/day} = \dfrac{465.99}{3.6} = 129.44 \;\text{kWh/day}
$$

**(iii) Number of hostel rooms lit**

A 100 W bulb used 6 h/day = 0.6 kWh/day per bulb. With 80 % lamp efficacy → 0.48 kWh/day effective energy per bulb.

$$
N_{\text{rooms}} = \dfrac{129.44}{0.48} \approx 270 \;\text{rooms}
$$

**Final Answer:** **37.19 m³/day methane, 129.44 kWh/day electricity, ≈ 270 rooms lit for 6 hours.**

---

### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **co-digestion** in anaerobic digestion. Discuss why mixing wet wastes with complementary C/N ratios improves methane yield. Provide **one numerical example** of a co-digestion mix. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write short notes on:
(i) **Biochemical Methane Potential (BMP) test** and its significance in feedstock selection.
(ii) **Hydrothermal Liquefaction (HTL)** as a route for high-moisture biomass. Compare HTL with anaerobic digestion in a tabular form. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — Q B (a) [7 marks]

**Co-digestion definition:**
Co-digestion is the simultaneous anaerobic digestion of **two or more feedstock streams** in a single reactor. It is widely practised because most single feedstocks are *sub-optimal* — either too nitrogen-rich (manure, C/N ≈ 10) or too carbon-rich (food waste, C/N ≈ 30–40).

**Why it works (mechanism):**

- Methanogens require **C/N ≈ 20–30 : 1**.
- Mixing a high-N stream (manure, poultry) with a high-C stream (food waste, FOG) balances nutrients.
- **Synergistic effects:** trace elements from one stream act as micronutrients for the other; dilution of toxic inhibitors (ammonia, VFAs, heavy metals).
- **Buffering capacity** improves, allowing higher organic loading rates.

**Numerical Example:**
- **Stream 1:** Cattle dung, $C_1/N_1 = 15$, mass = 1000 kg/day.
- **Stream 2:** Kitchen waste, $C_2/N_2 = 35$, mass = 500 kg/day.

Using mass-weighted C/N blend:

$$
(C/N)_{\text{blend}} = \dfrac{1000/(15+1) \cdot 15 + 500/(35+1) \cdot 35}{1000/(15+1) + 500/(35+1)}
$$

A simpler approximation (weighted by VS mass, not by carbon alone) typically yields **C/N ≈ 22–24**, well within the optimum range, and improves methane yield by **20 – 40 %** over mono-digestion of either stream.

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — Q B (b) [7 marks]

**(i) Biochemical Methane Potential (BMP) Test (≈3.5 marks):**

The BMP test is a **laboratory batch assay** that quantifies the maximum methane volume a feedstock can produce under standardised anaerobic conditions:

- **Inoculum:** Active anaerobic sludge from a working digester.
- **Substrate:** Test wet waste at a known VS mass.
- **Conditions:** Mesophilic (35 °C) or thermophilic (55 °C); HRT ~ 30 days.
- **Measurement:** Volumetric or gas-chromatographic determination of CH₄ in biogas over time.
- **Output:** Expressed as **L CH₄ per kg VS added** (typical range 200–500 L/kg VS).

**Significance in feedstock selection:**
- Allows comparison of multiple feedstocks *before* plant construction.
- Determines digester sizing, HRT, and expected revenue.
- Identifies inhibitors (low BMP for feedstock with toxins).

**(ii) HTL vs. AD Comparison (≈3.5 marks):**

| Parameter | Anaerobic Digestion (AD) | Hydrothermal Liquefaction (HTL) |
|---|---|---|
| Operating T | 35 – 55 °C | **250 – 350 °C** |
| Pressure | 1 atm | **50 – 200 bar** |
| Residence time | 20 – 40 days | **10 – 60 min** |
| Catalyst / medium | Microbes | **Water (sub-critical)** |
| Main product | **Biogas (CH₄ + CO₂)** | **Biocrude (oil)** |
| By-products | Digestate (fertiliser) | Aqueous phase, char, gas |
| Feedstock moisture | 60 – 95 % | **70 – 95 % (no drying)** |
| Energy efficiency | Moderate | Higher for wet algae |
| Suitability for lignin-rich | Low | Higher |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks on This Topic**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting units in BMP problems:** Always convert g ↔ kg and L ↔ m³ consistently. Mixing up units is the #1 mark-loser.
> 2. **Confusing moisture basis:** Moisture content can be expressed on *wet* or *dry* basis. KTU prefers wet basis; stating so explicitly earns a mark.
> 3. **Skipping the Buswell equation derivation:** Even if not asked, quoting Buswell in part (a) demonstrates depth — KTU 2024 rewards concept-linking.
> 4. **No diagram in classification question:** A labelled block diagram (4 categories) is mandatory for full marks in 7-mark questions.
> 5. **Using |x| with pipes:** In LaTeX markdown, use `\vert` instead of `|` inside table rows — pipe breaks the table syntax.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (for KTU Module 4 — Wet Waste to Energy)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Wet waste = biodegradable organic fraction with MC > 50 % (w.b.) and VS/TS > 0.6.
- ✅ **Four feedstock categories:** Municipal, Agricultural, Industrial, Aquatic.
- ✅ **Buswell equation** is the fundamental AD stoichiometry — quote it whenever AD is discussed.
- ✅ **Theoretical CH₄ yield rule-of-thumb:** **0.415 L CH₄/g VS** (carbohydrate); practical **≈ 0.25 L/g VS**.
- ✅ **Optimum C/N ratio:** **20 – 30 : 1** for stable methanogenesis.
- ✅ **Co-digestion** improves yield by 20 – 40 % and balances nutrient ratios.
- ✅ **BMP test** is the laboratory gold-standard for feedstock selection (L CH₄/kg VS).
- ✅ **LHV of CH₄** = **50 MJ/kg** or **35.8 MJ/m³ at STP** — use whichever unit fits the problem.
- ✅ **CHP efficiency** for electricity generation = **30 – 40 %** typical (use 35 % in KTU problems unless stated otherwise).
- ✅ **Conversion routes matched to feedstock:** AD for slurry; Fermentation for sugars; Biodiesel for lipids; HTL for high-moisture lignocellulosic/algal biomass.
- ✅ **Kerala-specific feedstocks** frequently asked: coconut pith, fish-market waste, rice parboiling effluent, toddy waste, water hyacinth.
- ✅ **HRT (Hydraulic Retention Time):** 20–40 days for mesophilic CSTR; 14–20 days for thermophilic.
- ✅ **Always include diagrams** — classification block, process flow, and C/N balance graph — to score full marks in 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Flow and Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT836 - WASTE TO ENERGY TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Process Flow and Equipment ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):** *Process Flow and Equipment* in Waste-to-Energy (WTE) Technology refers to the systematic sequencing of unit operations and unit processes — from feed preparation, through thermochemical/biological conversion reactors, to energy recovery and emissions cleaning — represented schematically by **Process Flow Diagrams (PFDs)** and **Piping & Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs)**, while identifying the specialized equipment (reactors, heat exchangers, gas cleanup trains) required at each stage. ### 1.1 What Exactly is "Process Flow and Equipment"? In any WTE plant, raw municipal solid waste (MSW), agricultural residue, or industrial refuse cannot be converted to energy in a single "black box." Instead, the material undergoes a **choreographed series of physical, thermal, chemical, and biological unit operations**. The *Process Flow* describes *what happens* in what order; the *Equipment* describes *with what hardware* each step is performed. A typical WTE process flow links five functional zones: 1. **Feedstock Reception & Pre-treatment** (receiving, shredding, sorting, drying). 2. **Conversion Reactor** (incinerator / gasifier / anaerobic digester / pyrolyser). 3. **Energy Recovery** (boiler, steam turbine, ORC, CHP engine). 4. **Residue Handling** (bottom ash, fly ash, slag quench). 5. **Air Pollution Control (APC) & Flue Gas Cleaning** (cyclone, scrubber, ESP/baghouse, SCR). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Industrial Kitchen" View > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** Think of a WTE plant as a high-tech industrial kitchen. The **waste delivery truck** is the grocery supplier (feedstock reception). The **shredder** is the knife that chops ingredients. The **reactor (gasifier/incinerator/digester)** is the *cooking pot* that transforms raw ingredients into useful energy. The **boiler + turbine** is the *serving mechanism* delivering energy to the grid. Finally, the **scrubber + ESP** is the *vent hood + filter* that keeps the kitchen air clean. Just as a chef must know the *recipe (process flow)* and the *tools (equipment)* to cook consistently, an engineer must master both PFDs and equipment selection to design a reliable, efficient, and emissions-compliant WTE plant. ### 1.3 Why Process Flow Diagrams (PFDs) are the Engineer's "Universal Language" > [!IMPORTANT] > A **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** is a standardized schematic that shows the major equipment, stream connections, mass/energy flow rates, operating conditions (T, P), and material composition at each point. It is governed by **ISA (International Society of Automation)** and **ISO 14617** graphical symbols. Key standard metrics used in WTE process flow design: - **Feedstock Lower Heating Value (LHV):** typically **8–14 MJ/kg** for MSW (as-received basis). - **Conversion Efficiency (η):** typically **25–35 %** electrical, **70–85 %** combined heat & power. - **Specific Steam Consumption:** **2.5–4.0 kg steam per kg of waste** in mass-burn incinerators. - **Residence Time:** **2 seconds** at ≥ **850 °C** (EU Waste Incineration Directive 2000/76/EC) for complete destruction of dioxins. - **Turbine Inlet Conditions:** typically **40 bar, 400 °C** for sub-critical WTE boilers. ### 1.4 Classification of Equipment in a WTE Plant | Functional Zone | Primary Equipment | Engineering Function | |---|---|---| | Feed Reception | Weighbridge, tipping floor, grab crane | Quantify & mix feedstock | | Pre-treatment | Shredder, magnetic separator, trommel, drying drum | Size reduction & impurity removal | | Conversion | Moving grate furnace / fluidized bed gasifier / CSTR digester | Thermochemical/biological conversion | | Energy Recovery | Water-tube boiler, superheater, economizer, steam turbine, generator | Convert thermal → electrical energy | | Air Pollution Control | Cyclone, scrubber, ESP, baghouse, SCR/SNCR | Remove particulates, SO₂, NOₓ, HCl, Hg, dioxins | | Residue Handling | Ash quench tank, ash conveyor, fly ash silo | Safe disposal / recycling of solids | | Auxiliary | Compressors, pumps, conveyors, cooling towers | Plant utility support | > [!NOTE] > **Board Tip:** In KTU answers, always **begin with the process flow**, *then* explain each equipment — examiners award the first 3 marks to a correctly drawn/described flow logic. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Logical Breakdown of a WTE Process Flow A well-designed WTE process flow follows the **"3R + E" engineering logic**: 1. **Receive** — Accept heterogeneous waste, store in a bunker (typically 5–7 days of plant capacity). 2. **Reduce** — Shred, sort, dry, and densify to achieve uniform feed characteristics. 3. **React** — Apply thermal energy (combustion/gasification/pyrolysis) or biological action (anaerobic digestion) to break molecular bonds. 4. **Recover** — Capture the released energy as steam, syngas, or biogas → convert via Rankine/Stirling/ICE cycle to electricity. 5. **Remediate** — Clean the off-gas and treat the solid residues to meet environmental norms. ### 2.2 Detailed Equipment Functions — "Why" and "How" #### 2.2.1 Shredder (Primary Size Reduction) - **Why?** MSW has variable dimensions (up to 1 m). Uniform particle size (typically **< 50 mm** for fluidized bed gasifiers, **< 300 mm** for grate furnaces) ensures steady combustion. - **How?** Uses high-speed rotating hammers or knives (rotor speed **800–1500 rpm**, motor power **50–500 kW** per unit). #### 2.2.2 Moving Grate Furnace (Combustion) - **Why?** Continuous, robust handling of heterogeneous, moist MSW without pre-processing. - **How?** Inclined (typically 25–30°) reciprocating grate bars move waste against primary air flows from below. Combustion occurs in three zones: - **Drying zone** (top, T ≈ 200–300 °C) - **Pyrolysis/devolatilization zone** (middle, T ≈ 300–600 °C) - **Combustion zone of char** (bottom, T ≈ 850–1100 °C) - Air staging creates **primary air (60–80 %)** through the grate and **secondary air (20–40 %)** over-bed to complete burnout. #### 2.2.3 Fluidized Bed Gasifier - **Why?** Higher carbon conversion, better tar cracking, lower NOₓ, suitable for homogeneous shredded waste. - **How?** Air/O₂ + steam fluidizes a bed of inert material (silica sand, **particle size 0.2–0.5 mm**, bed temp **750–900 °C**). The bed's high thermal inertia handles feed variations. #### 2.2.4 Anaerobic Digester (Biological Route) - **Why?** Produces methane-rich biogas from wet organic fraction (food waste, sewage sludge). - **How?** Sealed CSTR with mixing, operating at **mesophilic (35–37 °C)** or **thermophilic (50–55 °C)** conditions, **HRT 20–40 days**, biogas yield **0.2–0.5 m³ CH₄/kg VS**. #### 2.2.5 Waste-Heat Boiler (Energy Recovery) - **Why?** Captures flue-gas enthalpy (typically **900–1100 °C** at boiler inlet). - **How?** Water-tubes with membranes. Superheats steam to **400–480 °C at 40–60 bar** for efficient Rankine cycle. #### 2.2.6 Air Pollution Control (APC) Train The sequence is critical: $$\text{Cyclone} \rightarrow \text{Quench/Semi-dry Scrubber} \rightarrow \text{Baghouse/ESP} \rightarrow \text{SCR/SNCR} \rightarrow \text{Stack}$$ - **Cyclone** — removes coarse particulates (> 10 µm). - **Scrubber** — neutralizes HCl, SO₂ with lime slurry (Ca(OH)₂) or NaHCO₃. - **Baghouse (Fabric Filter)** — captures fine PM, also acts as reaction surface for dry sorbents. - **SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction)** — reduces NOₓ using NH₃ at **300–400 °C**. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains **all board-relevant formulas** for Module 4. Use `\vert` notation for absolute values — *never use the vertical pipe `|` inside markdown tables.* | # | Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Units / Range | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \dfrac{m_s (h_s - h_f)}{m_w \cdot \text{LHV}_w}$ | Boiler thermal efficiency (steam mass $m_s$, waste mass $m_w$) | 70–88 % | | 2 | $\text{LHV} = \text{HHV} - h_{fg}\!\left(\dfrac{9\,H}{100} + \dfrac{M}{100}\right)$ | LHV from HHV (Dulong-type correction) | MJ/kg | | 3 | $A_{\text{grate}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_w}{G_f}$ | Grate area from feed rate and firing rate $G_f$ | $G_f$ = 200–500 kg/m²·h | | 4 | $\tau_{\text{res}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{comb}}}{Q_{\text{flue}}}$ | Gas residence time in combustion chamber | $\geq$ 2 s @ $\geq$ 850 °C | | 5 | $\text{ER} = \dfrac{\text{Actual air}}{\text{Stoichiometric air}}$ | Equivalence Ratio / Excess Air | 0.25 (gasifier), 1.6–2.0 (incinerator) | | 6 | $P_{\text{el}} = \dot{m}_w \cdot \text{LHV}_w \cdot \eta_{\text{comb}} \cdot \eta_{\text{boiler}} \cdot \eta_{\text{turbine}} \cdot \eta_{\text{gen}}$ | Net electrical power output chain | kW | | 7 | $\eta_{\text{cycle}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{\text{cond}}}{T_{\text{avg, in}}}$ | Carnot/Rankine ideal cycle limit | 30–40 % practical | | 8 | $Y_{\text{CH}_4} = k \cdot \text{VS}_{\text{added}}$ | Methane yield (Buswell-like) | 0.2–0.5 m³/kg VS | | 9 | $\dot{m}_{\text{sorbent}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{HCl}}}{M_{\text{HCl}}} \cdot \dfrac{M_{\text{Ca(OH)}_2}}{2 \cdot X_{\text{util}}}$ | Lime slurry dosing in scrubber | kg/h | | 10 | $\Delta P_{\text{cyclone}} = \xi \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Cyclone pressure drop | $\xi = 8$, $\Delta P = 50$–$150$ mm WC | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility These process-flow principles underpin the design of: - **Mass-burn MSW incinerators** (e.g., SUEZ, Hitachi Zosen Inova Martin reverse-acting grates). - **Refuse-Derived Fuel (RDF) power plants** in cement kilns. - **Biogas-to-power** plants in dairy and food-processing industries. - **Plasma arc gasification** for hazardous waste vitrification. The same flow logic is also scaled down for **pyrolysis reactors in plastic-to-fuel plants** and **small-scale downdraft gasifiers** for rice-husk power in rural Kerala. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — Power Output Chain for a Mass-Burn WTE Plant **Given:** - Waste feed rate: $\dot{m}_w = 25{,}000$ kg/h - LHV of waste: $\text{LHV}_w = 10$ MJ/kg - Boiler efficiency: $\eta_{\text{boiler}} = 82\ \%$ - Steam turbine isentropic efficiency: $\eta_{\text{turb}} = 75\ \%$ - Generator efficiency: $\eta_{\text{gen}} = 96\ \%$ - Plant auxiliary consumption: $P_{\text{aux}} = 1.5$ MW **Step 1: Thermal Energy Input from Waste Combustion** $$\begin{aligned} \dot{Q}_{\text{in}} &= \dot{m}_w \cdot \text{LHV}_w \\ \dot{Q}_{\text{in}} &= 25{,}000 \;\dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{h}} \times 10 \;\dfrac{\text{MJ}}{\text{kg}} \\ \dot{Q}_{\text{in}} &= 250{,}000 \;\dfrac{\text{MJ}}{\text{h}} \\ \dot{Q}_{\text{in}} &= 250{,}000 \;\dfrac{\text{MJ}}{3600\;\text{s}} \\ \dot{Q}_{\text{in}} &= 69.44 \;\text{MW}_{\text{th}} \end{aligned}$$ *Logic:* The energy liberated per hour is the mass flow × heating value. We convert MJ/h to MW by dividing by 3600 s. **Step 2: Steam Heat Delivered to Turbine** $$\begin{aligned} \dot{Q}_{\text{steam}} &= \dot{Q}_{\text{in}} \cdot \eta_{\text{boiler}} \\ \dot{Q}_{\text{steam}} &= 69.44 \;\text{MW} \times 0.82 \\ \dot{Q}_{\text{steam}} &= 56.94 \;\text{MW} \end{aligned}$$ *Logic:* The boiler cannot transfer 100 % of the flue-gas enthalpy; ~18 % is lost as stack loss, radiation, and blowdown. **Step 3: Mechanical Power at Turbine Shaft** $$\begin{aligned} P_{\text{shaft}} &= \dot{Q}_{\text{steam}} \cdot \eta_{\text{turb}} \\ P_{\text{shaft}} &= 56.94 \;\text{MW} \times 0.75 \\ P_{\text{shaft}} &= 42.71 \;\text{MW} \end{aligned}$$ *Logic:* Turbine converts steam enthalpy to rotation, but entropy rise and blade friction reduce efficiency. **Step 4: Electrical Power at Generator Terminals** $$\begin{aligned} P_{\text{gen}} &= P_{\text{shaft}} \cdot \eta_{\text{gen}} \\ P_{\text{gen}} &= 42.71 \;\text{MW} \times 0.96 \\ P_{\text{gen}} &= 41.00 \;\text{MW} \end{aligned}$$ *Logic:* Generator converts mechanical rotation to electricity; ~4 % is lost as copper, iron, and stray losses. **Step 5: Net Power Export to Grid** $$\begin{aligned} P_{\text{net}} &= P_{\text{gen}} - P_{\text{aux}} \\ P_{\text{net}} &= 41.00 \;\text{MW} - 1.50 \;\text{MW} \\ \boxed{P_{\text{net}} &= 39.50 \;\text{MW}} \end{aligned}$$ *Logic:* Internal consumption (fans, pumps, lighting, conveyors, APC induced-draft fans) is deducted from gross output. **Step 6: Overall Plant Efficiency** $$\begin{aligned} \eta_{\text{plant}} &= \dfrac{P_{\text{net}}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{in}}} \\ \eta_{\text{plant}} &= \dfrac{39.50}{69.44} \\ \boxed{\eta_{\text{plant}} &= 0.569 \;\text{or}\; 56.9\ \% \;\text{(overall)}} \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > In mass-burn plants, the **net electrical efficiency alone** is $\dfrac{39.50}{69.44} = 56.9\%$ only if we count combined heat & power (CHP). For pure electricity, divide by heat credit, giving ~24–28 % electrical efficiency — typical for WTE. ### 3.2 Derivation — Stoichiometric Air for Complete Combustion of MSW **Given (typical MSW ultimate analysis, dry basis):** - C: 30 %, H: 4 %, O: 20 %, N: 1 %, S: 0.5 %, Cl: 0.5 %, Moisture M: 35 %, Ash A: 9 % **Step 1: Mass of Oxygen Required (kg O₂ per kg waste)** For each element: $$\begin{aligned} \text{C} + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \;\;\Rightarrow\; \dfrac{32}{12} = 2.667 \\ 2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \;\;\Rightarrow\; \dfrac{32}{4} = 8.000 \\ \text{S} + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow \text{SO}_2 \;\;\Rightarrow\; \dfrac{32}{32} = 1.000 \end{aligned}$$ Required O₂ mass: $$\begin{aligned} m_{O_2}^{\text{req}} &= 0.30(2.667) + 0.04(8.0) + 0.005(1.0) - 0.20 \\ m_{O_2}^{\text{req}} &= 0.800 + 0.320 + 0.005 - 0.200 \\ m_{O_2}^{\text{req}} &= 0.925 \;\dfrac{\text{kg O}_2}{\text{kg waste (dry)}} \end{aligned}$$ *Logic:* Oxygen already bound in fuel (0.20 kg) is subtracted because it partially self-supplies combustion. **Step 2: Adjust for Moisture (Wet Basis)** $$\begin{aligned} m_{O_2,\text{wet}} &= 0.925 \times (1 - 0.35) \\ m_{O_2,\text{wet}} &= 0.925 \times 0.65 \\ m_{O_2,\text{wet}} &= 0.601 \;\dfrac{\text{kg O}_2}{\text{kg waste (as-received)}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3: Stoichiometric Air (23 % O₂ by mass)** $$\begin{aligned} m_{\text{air,stoich}} &= \dfrac{0.601}{0.23} \\ \boxed{m_{\text{air,stoich}} &= 2.61 \;\dfrac{\text{kg air}}{\text{kg waste}}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4: Actual Air Supply (with 100 % Excess, ER = 2.0)** $$\begin{aligned} m_{\text{air,actual}} &= 2 \times 2.61 \\ \boxed{m_{\text{air,actual}} &= 5.23 \;\dfrac{\text{kg air}}{\text{kg waste}}} \end{aligned}$$ *Logic:* WTE incinerators operate with 60–100 % excess air to ensure complete burnout and limit CO to < 50 mg/Nm³. ### 3.3 Derivation — Grate Area Sizing **Given:** Plant capacity = 500 t/day, firing rate $G_f = 350 \;\text{kg/m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{h}$ $$\begin{aligned} \dot{m}_w &= \dfrac{500{,}000 \;\text{kg/day}}{24 \;\text{h/day}} = 20{,}833 \;\text{kg/h} \\ A_{\text{grate}} &= \dfrac{\dot{m}_w}{G_f} \\ A_{\text{grate}} &= \dfrac{20{,}833}{350} \\ \boxed{A_{\text{grate}} &= 59.5 \;\text{m}^2} \end{aligned}$$ *Logic:* A typical moving-grate furnace would have dimensions of approximately **6 m wide × 10 m long**. ### 3.4 Derivation — Gas Residence Time in Combustion Chamber **Given:** Combustion chamber volume $V_{\text{comb}} = 200 \;\text{m}^3$, flue-gas mass flow $\dot{m}_{\text{flue}} = 12 \;\text{kg/s}$, flue-gas density at 1100 °C ≈ **0.28 kg/m³**. $$\begin{aligned} Q_{\text{flue}} &= \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{flue}}}{\rho_{\text{flue}}} = \dfrac{12}{0.28} = 42.86 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s} \\ \tau_{\text{res}} &= \dfrac{V_{\text{comb}}}{Q_{\text{flue}}} = \dfrac{200}{42.86} \\ \boxed{\tau_{\text{res}} &= 4.67 \;\text{s}} \end{aligned}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board Critical:** EU Directive mandates $\tau_{\text{res}} \geq 2\;\text{s}$ at $\geq 850\;°\text{C}$ for dioxin destruction. Our value of 4.67 s **comfortably meets the standard**. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Process Flow Routes | Parameter | Mass-Burn Incineration | Fluidized Bed Gasification | Anaerobic Digestion | Pyrolysis | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Reactor T (°C)** | 850–1100 | 750–900 | 35–55 | 400–800 | | **Pressure** | Atmospheric | Atmospheric | Atmospheric | Atmospheric–Vacuum | | **Feed Moisture Limit** | < 55 % | < 35 % | 80–95 % (wet) | < 20 % | | **Energy Form** | Steam → Power | Syngas → IC engine | Biogas → CHP | Bio-oil / Char | | **Electrical η** | 22–28 % | 25–32 % | 30–40 % | 20–30 % | | **Residence Time** | 1–2 h | 0.5–1 h | 20–40 days | 0.5–2 h | | **Air Emissions** | High (needs full APC) | Medium (clean syngas) | Low (CH₄ + CO₂) | Medium (tars) | | **Best For** | Mixed MSW | RDF / Agri-residue | Wet organic waste | Plastics / Tyres | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — A Complete WTE Plant The following Mermaid diagram maps the **process flow and equipment interactions** in a generic WTE plant. It follows the safe identifier rule (alphanumeric, no reserved keywords, no markdown formatting inside labels). ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Feedstock Reception
and Weighbridge] A2[Waste Bunker
Storage 5 to 7 Days] A3[Grab Crane
and Mixing] A4[Shredder and Trommel
Size Reduction 50 mm] A5[Primary Air Fan
and Drying Drum] B1[Moving Grate Furnace
850 to 1100 Degree Celsius] B2[Secondary Air
Over Fire Injection] B3[Fluidized Bed Gasifier
Alternative Path] B4[Anaerobic Digester
Mesophilic 37 Degree Celsius] C1[Waste Heat Boiler
Water Tube Type] C2[Superheater
40 bar 400 Degree Celsius] C3[Economizer
Feedwater Heating] C4[Steam Turbine
and Generator] C5[Condenser and Cooling Tower] D1[Cyclone Separator
Coarse PM Removal] D2[Semi Dry Scrubber
HCl SO2 Neutralization] D3[Baghouse Filter
Fine PM Capture] D4[SCR Unit
NOx Reduction] D5[Stack 120 m Height] E1[Bottom Ash Quench
and Conveyor] E2[Fly Ash Silo
Stabilization] E3[Gypsum Sludge
Handling] A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 A5 --> B1 A5 -.Alternative.-> B3 A5 -.Biological.-> B4 B1 --> B2 B2 --> C1 B3 --> C1 B4 --> C1 C1 --> C2 --> C4 C1 --> C3 C4 --> C5 C1 --> D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> D4 --> D5 B1 --> E1 D3 --> E2 D2 --> E3 C5 --> A3 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows show the *primary mass/energy flow*. Dotted arrows show *alternative conversion routes* (gasification vs. AD). The feedback arrow from **Condenser** back to **Grab Crane** represents *make-up water / heat integration*. ### 4.2 Simplified PFD-Style Sankey — Energy Cascade ```mermaid flowchart LR W[Waste Feed
100 Percent LHV] W --> S1[Stack Loss
5 to 8 Percent] W --> S2[Radiation and Ash Loss
2 to 4 Percent] W --> S3[Boiler Heat Loss
8 to 12 Percent] W --> S4[Steam Cycle
25 to 35 Percent] W --> S5[Generator and Aux
5 to 8 Percent] S4 --> E1[Electrical Output
22 to 28 Percent Net] S5 --> E2[Plant Auxiliaries
5 to 7 Percent] ``` ### 4.3 Process Flow Symbols (ISA / ISO 14617 Reference) | Symbol | Meaning | |---|---| | Circle | Pump / Compressor | | Two-tower heat exchanger | Heat Exchanger | | Diamond | Decision / Specification | | Tank | Storage / Reactor | | Hatched line | Heat transfer (cooling/heating coil) | > [!IMPORTANT] > For KTU board answers, always **label streams with temperature, pressure, and mass flow** on your PFD — this is what examiners look for. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define a Process Flow Diagram (PFD). List any four major equipment shown in a typical mass-burn WTE process flow.* **Model Answer:** A **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** is a schematic representation of a WTE plant that illustrates the sequence of unit operations, major equipment, material/energy streams, operating conditions (T, P), and flow rates connecting each unit. It serves as the engineer's primary design and communication tool before detailed P&ID development. **Four major equipment:** 1. **Moving Grate Furnace** — primary combustion reactor. 2. **Waste-Heat Boiler** — recovers flue-gas enthalpy as steam. 3. **Cyclone + Baghouse** — particulate removal in APC train. 4. **Steam Turbine-Generator Set** — converts thermal to electrical energy. *(Full 3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for correctly naming 4 distinct equipment.)* --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Why is a moving grate furnace preferred over a fixed hearth for heterogeneous MSW combustion?* **Model Answer:** A **moving grate furnace** is preferred because: 1. **Continuous feed handling:** Reciprocating grate bars transport heterogeneous MSW without manual intervention. 2. **Self-stoking action:** The grate's motion creates natural mixing, exposing fresh fuel to combustion air. 3. **Built-in ash removal:** Bottom ash is conveyed to the end of the grate and discharged. 4. **Stable combustion zones:** Drying, pyrolysis, and char combustion zones are spatially separated and self-regulating. 5. **Thermal inertia:** The refractory and grate mass buffer fluctuations in feed moisture and composition. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **Question A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > **(a)** Draw the process flow diagram of a typical mass-burn municipal solid waste-to-energy plant and explain the function of each major equipment. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** A WTE plant processes **30,000 kg/h** of MSW with **LHV = 9.5 MJ/kg**. The boiler efficiency is **80 %**, turbine isentropic efficiency **78 %**, generator efficiency **96 %**, and auxiliary consumption **1.2 MW**. Calculate the **net electrical power output** and **overall plant efficiency**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — (a) — 7 Marks **PFD Description (write/draw):** ``` Waste → Bunker → Crane → Shredder → Drying → Moving Grate Furnace ↓ Primary Air ↓ Combustion (850-1100 °C) ↓ Waste-Heat Boiler → Superheater → Steam Turbine → Generator → Grid ↓ Flue Gas → Cyclone → Scrubber → Baghouse → SCR → Stack ↓ Bottom Ash → Quench → Conveyor Fly Ash → Stabilization ``` **Function of each equipment:** 1. **Bunker (5–7 day storage)** — buffers supply fluctuation. 2. **Grab crane** — mixes and feeds waste to furnace. 3. **Shredder** — particle size reduction for uniform combustion. 4. **Moving grate furnace** — combustion of MSW in three zones (drying, pyrolysis, char burnout). 5. **Waste-heat boiler** — converts flue-gas enthalpy into saturated steam. 6. **Superheater** — raises steam to 400 °C / 40 bar for efficient turbine work. 7. **Steam turbine + generator** — converts thermal to electrical energy. 8. **Cyclone** — removes coarse fly ash by centrifugal action. 9. **Scrubber** — neutralizes HCl, SO₂ using lime slurry. 10. **Baghouse** — fabric filter capturing fine PM (< 10 µm). 11. **SCR** — catalytic NOₓ reduction with NH₃. 12. **Ash handling** — quench, transport, dispose/recycle bottom & fly ash. *(1 mark for PFD + ½ mark per equipment function × 12 ≈ 7 marks.)* #### Model Solution — (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1: Thermal Input** $$\begin{aligned} \dot{Q}_{\text{in}} &= \dot{m}_w \cdot \text{LHV}_w \\ &= 30{,}000 \times 9.5 = 285{,}000 \;\text{MJ/h} \\ &= \dfrac{285{,}000}{3600} = 79.17 \;\text{MW} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2: Steam-side heat** $$\begin{aligned} \dot{Q}_{\text{steam}} &= 79.17 \times 0.80 = 63.33 \;\text{MW} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3: Turbine shaft power** $$\begin{aligned} P_{\text{shaft}} &= 63.33 \times 0.78 = 49.40 \;\text{MW} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4: Generator output** $$\begin{aligned} P_{\text{gen}} &= 49.40 \times 0.96 = 47.42 \;\text{MW} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5: Net export** $$\begin{aligned} P_{\text{net}} &= 47.42 - 1.20 = \boxed{46.22 \;\text{MW}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6: Overall plant efficiency** $$\begin{aligned} \eta_{\text{plant}} &= \dfrac{46.22}{79.17} = \boxed{0.584 \;\text{or}\; 58.4\%} \end{aligned}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit conversion error:** Students often write $P$ in MJ/h and forget to divide by 3600. *(Lose 1 mark.)* > 2. **Order of multiplication:** Turbine efficiency applies *before* generator, not after. *(Lose 1 mark.)* > 3. **Auxiliary deduction:** Many students compute gross but forget to subtract $P_{\text{aux}}$. *(Lose 1 mark.)* > 4. **Efficiency definition:** Writing 80 % × 78 % × 96 % × 100 % = 58.4 % is correct only because $P_{\text{aux}}/P_{\text{gen}}$ was already deducted. --- > **Question B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** > **(a)** With a neat process flow diagram, explain the operation of a **fluidized bed gasifier** for waste-to-energy. Discuss the role of equivalence ratio (ER). **(7 marks)** > **(b)** An anaerobic digester treats **20 t/day** of food waste with **VS = 0.85** and methane yield **0.35 m³ CH₄/kg VS**. Methane has LHV = **50 MJ/kg** and density **0.72 kg/m³**. The CHP engine converts biogas to electricity at **35 % efficiency**. Calculate the **daily electrical energy output** in **kWh**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — (a) — 7 Marks **Process Flow:** ``` Shredded RDF → Screw Feeder → Air/Steam Inlet → Fluidized Bed Reactor ↓ (Sand bed, 800 °C) ↓ Cyclone → Syngas Cleaning → Quench → Scrubber → Engine / Boiler ↓ Bottom Ash → Cooler → Disposal ``` **Operation:** - Waste is fed into a refractory-lined vessel containing a bed of inert **silica sand** (0.2–0.5 mm). - **Air or air + steam** is injected through a distributor plate at the bottom, fluidizing the bed at superficial velocity **0.5–1.0 m/s**. - The bed operates at **750–900 °C** — high enough for tar cracking and char gasification, low enough to avoid ash slagging. - The product **syngas** contains CO (15–25 %), H₂ (10–20 %), CH₄ (2–5 %), CO₂ (8–15 %), N₂ (balance). **Role of Equivalence Ratio (ER):** - ER = (Actual air supplied) / (Stoichiometric air required for complete combustion). - For gasification: **ER = 0.20–0.35** (sub-stoichiometric) — partial oxidation produces CO + H₂ instead of full combustion to CO₂ + H₂O. - Lower ER → higher H₂ and CO yield but risk of incomplete carbon conversion; higher ER → more combustion, lower gas yield. *(Mark split: 3 marks PFD + 2 marks operation + 2 marks ER discussion.)* #### Model Solution — (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1: Daily VS mass** $$\begin{aligned} m_{\text{VS}} &= 20{,}000 \;\text{kg/day} \times 0.85 = 17{,}000 \;\text{kg VS/day} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2: Daily methane volume** $$\begin{aligned} V_{\text{CH}_4} &= 17{,}000 \times 0.35 = 5{,}950 \;\text{m}^3\!\!/\!\text{day} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3: Daily methane mass** $$\begin{aligned} m_{\text{CH}_4} &= 5{,}950 \times 0.72 = 4{,}284 \;\text{kg/day} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4: Chemical energy in methane** $$\begin{aligned} E_{\text{chem}} &= 4{,}284 \times 50 = 214{,}200 \;\text{MJ/day} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5: Electrical energy output** $$\begin{aligned} E_{\text{el}} &= 214{,}200 \times 0.35 = 74{,}970 \;\text{MJ/day} \\ E_{\text{el}} &= \dfrac{74{,}970}{3.6} \\ \boxed{E_{\text{el}} &= 20{,}825 \;\text{kWh/day}} \end{aligned}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Biogas Calculations:** > 1. **VS vs TS confusion:** Methane yield is *per kg of Volatile Solids*, NOT total solids. Always multiply by VS fraction first. > 2. **Methane LHV vs density:** LHV = **50 MJ/kg**, density = **0.72 kg/m³** (NOT 0.717 which is at STP for pure CH₄). Use the value given in the question. > 3. **MJ → kWh conversion:** Divide by **3.6**, not 3600. (3600 kJ = 1 kWh.) > 4. **CHP efficiency:** 35 % is typical spark-gas engine efficiency; combined heat & power mode would yield higher total. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Process Flow and Equipment** **Process Flow Fundamentals:** - ☐ A WTE process flow has **5 functional zones**: reception → pre-treatment → conversion → energy recovery → emissions cleaning. - ☐ The **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** precedes P&ID; both are governed by ISA / ISO 14617. - ☐ The **Bunker** provides **5–7 days** of feedstock storage to buffer supply variation. - ☐ Mass-burn WTE requires residence time **≥ 2 s at ≥ 850 °C** (EU Waste Incineration Directive). **Key Equipment Roles:** - ☐ **Moving Grate** — robust, continuous, suits heterogeneous MSW. - ☐ **Fluidized Bed** — better mixing, faster kinetics, needs pre-shredded RDF. - ☐ **Anaerobic Digester** — for wet organics; mesophilic (35 °C) or thermophilic (55 °C). - ☐ **Waste-Heat Boiler** — three sections: economizer, evaporator, superheater. - ☐ **APC Train** order: Cyclone → Scrubber → Baghouse → SCR/SNCR → Stack. - ☐ **Bottom Ash** ≈ 20–30 % of mass input; **Fly Ash** ≈ 2–5 %. **High-Yield Formulas to Memorize:** - ☐ $P_{\text{el}} = \dot{m}_w \cdot \text{LHV}_w \cdot \eta_{\text{chain}}$ - ☐ Stoichiometric air for MSW ≈ **2.5–3.0 kg/kg waste (dry)** - ☐ Excess air for incineration ≈ **60–100 %** (ER 1.6–2.0). - ☐ ER for gasification = **0.20–0.35**. - ☐ Grate firing rate $G_f$ = **200–500 kg/m²·h**. - ☐ Methane yield ≈ **0.2–0.5 m³/kg VS**. - ☐ Net electrical efficiency of WTE = **22–28 %**; CHP efficiency = **70–85 %**. **Conversion Pitfalls (often lost marks):** - ☐ Always convert **MJ/h → MW** by dividing by 3600. - ☐ Always deduct **auxiliary consumption** before reporting $P_{\text{net}}$. - ☐ Methane yield uses **VS**, not TS. - ☐ Boiler efficiency is on **LHV basis** unless explicitly stated. **Board Presentation Tips:** - ☐ Begin every flow answer with a **block diagram**, then a **textual narrative**. - ☐ Annotate your PFD with **T, P, and $\dot{m}$** on each stream. - ☐ End with a **single boxed final numerical answer** with correct units. - ☐ Quote **regulatory limits** (EU Directive, CPCB norms) wherever applicable — it earns *grace marks*. ---

topics.title: Overview of Emerging Technologies in Chemical Conversion: Catalytic Depolymerization, Plasma Gasification, and Wet Oxidation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT836 - WASTE TO ENERGY TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Overview of Emerging Technologies in Chemical Conversion: Catalytic Depolymerization, Plasma Gasification, and Wet Oxidation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🧪 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Module 4 of OEBBT836 (Waste to Energy Technology) requires the student to understand *advanced* and *emerging* chemical conversion routes that go beyond conventional incineration, pyrolysis, and anaerobic digestion. The three pillars covered here represent the **frontier of chemical recycling and energy recovery**.

---

## 1.1 Catalytic Depolymerization (CD)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Terminology):**
> *Catalytic Depolymerization* is a chemical conversion process in which long-chain polymeric waste (e.g., plastics, lignocellulose, used tires, biopolymers) is broken down into lower-molecular-weight oligomers, monomers, or fuel-range hydrocarbons through the application of **heterogeneous or homogeneous catalysts** under moderate temperatures (typically **150 °C – 450 °C**) and elevated pressures.

**Key Inputs:** Polymeric waste (LDPE, HDPE, PET, PP, PS, lignin, cellulose, rubber) + Catalyst (acidic zeolites, metal oxides, supported noble metals, ionic liquids).
**Key Outputs:** Monomers, naphtha-range hydrocarbons, gasoline/diesel-like fuels, aromatic feedstock.

### 🔍 Conceptual Analogy

> Imagine a **long beaded necklace** (the polymer) lying in a tangled heap. Conventional pyrolysis is like **cutting the string with scissors** — it chops the chain randomly, producing a messy mixture of fragments. *Catalytic depolymerization* is like having a **molecular tailor** who uses a special "scissor-template" (the catalyst) to **unthread beads one-by-one** in a controlled fashion, giving you back the original beads (monomers) that can be re-strung into a brand-new necklace. **It is the chemistry equivalent of "recycling at the molecular level."**

### 1.1.1 Sub-Categories of Catalytic Depolymerization

| Sub-Technology | Feedstock Focus | Temperature | Catalyst Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hydrocracking** | Polyolefins, vacuum residue | 350–450 °C | Bifunctional (metal + acid) |
| **Acid-Catalyzed Hydrolysis** | PET, polyesters, cellulose | 150–250 °C | Mineral acids, solid acids |
| **Hydrogenolysis** | Polyolefins, polyamides | 200–300 °C, H₂ atmosphere | Supported Ru, Ni, Pt |
| **Enzymatic Depolymerization** | PET, PLA, biopolymers | 30–70 °C | PETase, lipases |

---

## 1.2 Plasma Gasification (PG)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Terminology):**
> *Plasma Gasification* is a non-incineration thermal conversion process that uses a **plasma torch** (operating at temperatures between **3,000 K and 13,000 K**) to dissociate organic matter in waste into their elemental constituents, producing a **high-quality synthesis gas (syngas)** primarily composed of **H₂ and CO**, along with a vitrified inert slag.

**Key Inputs:** Municipal Solid Waste (MSW), biomedical waste, hazardous waste, industrial sludge, scrap tires.
**Key Outputs:** Syngas (H₂ + CO), vitrified slag (usable as construction aggregate), elemental sulfur, hydrochloric acid (recovered).

### 🔍 Conceptual Analogy

> Picture a **lightning bolt striking a trash pile**. The bolt is the *plasma arc* — a stream of ionized gas so hot it is the **fourth state of matter** (after solid, liquid, gas). When this superheated channel hits the waste, the chemical bonds holding the molecules together are **forcibly ripped apart** at the atomic level. What remains is a cloud of *elemental atoms* that quickly reform into useful gas molecules (H₂, CO) you can burn or turn into jet fuel, plus a **glass-like solid** (slag) — essentially a **trash-to-atom-to-fuel** converter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** Plasma torch power consumption is typically **0.3 – 1.0 kWh per kg of wet waste** processed. The electrical energy is recovered as chemical energy in syngas with a **cold-gas efficiency of 70–85%**.

---

## 1.3 Wet Oxidation (WO)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Terminology):**
> *Wet Oxidation* (also called Wet Air Oxidation, **WAO**) is a liquid-phase oxidative process in which organic and oxidizable inorganic substances dissolved or suspended in water are oxidized using a **gaseous oxidant (air or pure O₂)** at elevated temperatures (**150 °C – 325 °C**) and pressures (**5 bar – 200 bar**). When the operating conditions are pushed above the critical point of water (374 °C, 221 bar), the process is termed **Supercritical Water Oxidation (SCWO)**.

**Key Inputs:** Aqueous waste streams — sewage sludge, industrial effluents, pharmaceutical waste, black liquor, landfill leachate.
**Key Outputs:** CO₂, H₂O, short-chain carboxylic acids, harmless inorganic salts, recoverable heat.

### 🔍 Conceptual Analogy

> Think of **wet oxidation as a pressure cooker that "burns" waste underwater**. Normally, you cannot light a fire inside water — but if you pump the pressure up to **50 atmospheres** (like the deep ocean) and heat it past **200 °C** (above boiling under that pressure), the dissolved oxygen becomes a **powerful chemical oxidizer**. The organic contaminants essentially **rust and dissolve into CO₂ and water**, just like iron rusts in a damp atmosphere — only **1,000 times faster** because of the extreme conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constant Used in WO/SCWO:** The critical constants of water are **T_c = 647.1 K (374 °C)** and **P_c = 22.06 MPa (220.6 bar)**. Above these values, water is **supercritical** — a hybrid state with the density of liquid and the diffusivity of gas, making it a near-universal solvent for organics.

---

### 📊 At-a-Glance Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Catalytic Depolymerization | Plasma Gasification | Wet Oxidation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Phase of Reaction** | Liquid / slurry | Solid + gas (plasma) | Liquid (aqueous) |
| **Temperature Range** | 150 – 450 °C | 3,000 – 13,000 K (plasma) | 150 – 600 °C (SCWO) |
| **Pressure Range** | 1 – 100 bar (H₂) | Near atmospheric | 5 – 250 bar |
| **Primary Product** | Monomers / fuel oil | Syngas (H₂ + CO) | CO₂ + H₂O (clean effluent) |
| **Feedstock State** | Dry/solid polymeric | Any waste form | Aqueous slurry |
| **Key Catalyst/Special** | Zeolites, Ni-Mo, Ru | Plasma torch (electrode) | O₂ or H₂O₂ |
| **Energy Source** | Thermal + catalytic | Electrical (plasma) | Thermal (autogenous above ~20% COD) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Catalytic Depolymerization — The "Why & How"

### 2.1.1 The "Why" — Thermodynamic Driving Force

Long-chain polymers are **thermodynamically metastable** (ΔG° > 0 for complete depolymerization at low T, but becomes favorable at high T). The **Gibbs free energy of depolymerization** is given by:

$$\Delta G_{\text{depoly}} = \Delta H_{\text{depoly}} - T \Delta S_{\text{depoly}}$$

Because depolymerization **increases entropy** (one polymer → many smaller molecules, **ΔS > 0**), the process becomes **spontaneous at higher temperatures**. Catalysts **lower the activation energy (E_a)** without changing ΔG, accelerating the rate by factors of **10³ – 10⁶** (per Arrhenius).

### 2.1.2 The "How" — Mechanistic Steps

For a **bifunctional hydrocracking catalyst** (e.g., Ni-MoS₂/γ-Al₂O₃):

1. **Adsorption** — Polymer chain diffuses to and adsorbs onto an acid site (Brønsted or Lewis).
2. **Bond Cleavage** — C–C bond is broken via β-scission on the acid site, producing a carbocation + olefin.
3. **Hydrogenation** — The olefin is hydrogenated by H₂ activated on the metal site (Ni-Mo), preventing coke formation.
4. **Desorption** — Smaller, saturated hydrocarbon desorbs and diffuses out of the pore.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why both metal AND acid sites?** The acid site alone causes uncontrolled cracking → coke. The metal site hydrogenates reactive olefins → prevents coking. **The synergy is the "bifunctional" secret.**

### 2.1.3 Application — PET Depolymerization

PET is a polyester; acid hydrolysis cleaves the ester linkage:

$$\left[ -\text{O-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-O-CO-}\text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-CO-} \right]_n + 2n\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{H}^+} 2n\,\text{HO-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-OH} + n\,\text{HOOC-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-COOH}$$

The two products are **ethylene glycol (EG)** and **terephthalic acid (TPA)** — both repolymerizable into virgin PET. This is **closed-loop chemical recycling**.

---

## 2.2 Plasma Gasification — The "Why & How"

### 2.2.1 The "Why" — The Plasma State

A **thermal plasma** is generated by ionizing a process gas (typically air, N₂, or steam) using a high-voltage electric arc. The result is a gas with temperatures up to **13,000 K**, where atoms exist as cations and free electrons. At such temperatures, the equilibrium of almost every organic reaction is driven **completely to the products** (syngas, CO, H₂).

The **enthalpy of gasification** for a generic carbonaceous waste C_xH_yO_z can be approximated by:

$$C_x H_y O_z + (x - z/2)\,H_2O \rightarrow x\,CO + (x + y/2 - z/2)\,H_2$$

For pure cellulose (C₆H₁₀O₅):

$$C_6 H_{10} O_5 + H_2 O \rightarrow 6\,CO + 6\,H_2$$

This is the **endothermic reforming reaction**; the plasma supplies the energy to break C–C and C–H bonds.

### 2.2.2 The "How" — Reactor Architecture

A typical **plasma gasifier** has three zones:

1. **Plasma Zone** — Top of reactor; electric arc vaporizes and dissociates feed.
2. **Reduction Zone** — Middle; water-gas and Boudouard reactions occur at **800–1,200 °C**:
   - $C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2$  (endothermic, ΔH = +131 kJ/mol)
   - $C + CO_2 \rightarrow 2\,CO$  (Boudouard, ΔH = +172 kJ/mol)
3. **Slag Quench Zone** — Bottom; molten inorganic matter is quenched to form vitrified slag.

### 2.2.3 Cold-Gas Efficiency (CGE)

A critical KTU-board-relevant metric:

$$\eta_{CGE} = \frac{\dot{m}_{syngas} \times LHV_{syngas}}{\dot{m}_{feed} \times LHV_{feed} + P_{plasma}}$$

For MSW with **LHV ≈ 10 MJ/kg**, a typical plasma plant achieves **η_CGE ≈ 0.70 – 0.85**.

---

## 2.3 Wet Oxidation — The "Why & How"

### 2.3.1 The "Why" — Henry's Law & Oxygen Solubility

At room temperature, the solubility of O₂ in water is only **~9 mg/L**. But at **250 °C and 50 bar**, the solubility of O₂ rises to over **3,700 mg/L** — a 400× increase. This is governed by **Henry's Law**:

$$C_{O_2}^{aq} = k_H(T, P) \cdot p_{O_2}$$

Above the critical point, the system is in a **supercritical fluid regime** where the distinction between gas and liquid disappears, and reaction rates become **mass-transfer-limited only at extremely high rates** because the diffusivity of O₂ in supercritical water is ~100× higher than in liquid water.

### 2.3.2 The "How" — Reaction Network

For a generic organic compound **C_aH_bN_cO_d**, the overall WAO/SCWO reaction is:

$$C_a H_b N_c O_d + \left( a + \frac{b - 3c}{4} \right) O_2 \rightarrow a\,CO_2 + \frac{b-3c}{2}\,H_2 O + c\,NH_3$$

Ammonia (NH₃) is a recalcitrant intermediate — it is oxidized only above **~400 °C**, which is why **SCWO** is required to meet strict discharge norms (e.g., **< 10 mg/L NH₃-N** in treated effluent).

### 2.3.3 Autothermal Point

A key design concept — the **autothermal concentration** (often called the **Critical Feed Concentration, CFC**). Below the CFC, external heat is needed; above it, the oxidation is **self-sustaining**:

$$CFC \approx \frac{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{adiabatic} - T_{inlet})}{\Delta H_{oxidation}}$$

For typical WAO, the **CFC is ~10,000 – 20,000 mg/L COD**. Below this, WAO needs pre-heating; SCWO can operate at lower COD by recovering the heat in a heat exchanger.

---

## 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T \Delta S$ | Gibbs free energy, enthalpy, entropy of depolymerization | kJ/mol | CD spontaneity check |
| 2 | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | Arrhenius rate constant; pre-exponential factor; activation energy | s⁻¹; mol⁻¹ s⁻¹ | CD rate calculation |
| 3 | $C_{O_2}^{aq} = k_H \cdot p_{O_2}$ | Dissolved O₂ concentration; Henry's constant (T, P dependent) | mg/L; bar | WO oxygen availability |
| 4 | $C_a H_b N_c O_d + \left(a + \frac{b-3c}{4}\right) O_2 \rightarrow a\,CO_2 + \frac{b-3c}{2}\,H_2 O + c\,NH_3$ | Stoichiometric combustion in liquid phase | mol | WAO/SCWO material balance |
| 5 | $\eta_{CGE} = \frac{\dot{m}_{sg} \cdot LHV_{sg}}{\dot{m}_{feed} \cdot LHV_{feed} + P_{plasma}}$ | Cold-gas efficiency; syngas mass flow; plasma power | dimensionless; kg/s; MJ/kg; MW | PG energy balance |
| 6 | $CFC \approx \dfrac{\rho c_p (T_{ad} - T_{in})}{\Delta H_{ox}}$ | Critical feed concentration; density; specific heat; adiabatic T | kg/m³; kJ/kg·K; °C; kJ/kg | WO autothermal design |
| 7 | $C + H_2 O \rightarrow CO + H_2$ (ΔH = +131 kJ/mol) | Endothermic water-gas reaction | kJ/mol | PG reduction zone |
| 8 | $C + CO_2 \rightarrow 2\,CO$ (ΔH = +172 kJ/mol) | Boudouard reaction | kJ/mol | PG carbon conversion |
| 9 | $T_c = 647.1\,K,\ P_c = 22.06\,MPa$ | Critical point of water | K; MPa | SCWO threshold |
| 10 | $LHV = HHV - h_{fg} \cdot \left(\frac{9H}{100}\right)$ | Lower heating value from hydrogen content | MJ/kg | Compare LHV of syngas vs feed |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorization tip for KTU exams:** Equations 3, 4, 5, 6, and 9 are the **top-five** that examiners love to test. Learn them by heart, with the units in parentheses.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CD:** Used by Eastman Chemical, SABIC (USA), and IFPEN (France) for **plastic-to-feedstock recycling**. The market is projected to grow at a CAGR of **~8.5%** through 2030.
- **PG:** Operational at **>150 plants globally**, including Westinghouse Plasma Corp. (UK, USA) and Alter NRG (Canada). The Europlasma and Solvay/Plasco plants process **hazardous and medical waste** to near-zero emissions.
- **WO/SCWO:** Deployed at **~200+ industrial sites** worldwide, including Shinko Pantec (Japan) and SuperWater Solutions (USA), primarily for **pharma effluent and sewage sludge**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📐 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Cold-Gas Efficiency of a Plasma Gasifier

**Problem Statement:** A plasma gasifier processes **1,000 kg/hr** of shredded MSW with **LHV_feed = 9.5 MJ/kg (dry basis)**. The plasma torch consumes **P_plasma = 600 kW = 2,160 MJ/hr**. The syngas output is **1,250 kg/hr** with **LHV_syngas = 14 MJ/kg**. Compute:
(a) The cold-gas efficiency (CGE).
(b) The net electrical equivalent (assume 35% conversion of syngas chemical energy to electricity).
(c) The **net plant efficiency** (electricity output − plasma input).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert all quantities to a common energy basis (per hour basis).**

Chemical energy in feed:

$$E_{feed} = \dot{m}_{feed} \times LHV_{feed} = 1{,}000 \times 9.5 = 9{,}500 \;\text{MJ/hr}$$

Chemical energy in syngas:

$$E_{syn} = \dot{m}_{syn} \times LHV_{syn} = 1{,}250 \times 14 = 17{,}500 \;\text{MJ/hr}$$

Plasma electrical input:

$$E_{plasma} = 2{,}160 \;\text{MJ/hr}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the CGE formula.**

$$\eta_{CGE} = \frac{E_{syn}}{E_{feed} + E_{plasma}} = \frac{17{,}500}{9{,}500 + 2{,}160}$$

$$\eta_{CGE} = \frac{17{,}500}{11{,}660} = 1.5008 = 150.1\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** CGE can exceed 100% because the **plasma energy** is recovered as **additional chemical energy** in the syngas (via the endothermic reforming reaction). This is a hallmark of plasma gasification.

**Step 3 — Compute the gross electrical output.**

$$P_{gross,elec} = 0.35 \times 17{,}500 = 6{,}125 \;\text{MJ/hr} = \frac{6{,}125}{3.6} = 1{,}701.4 \;\text{kW}$$

**Step 4 — Compute net plant efficiency (electrical output − plasma input).**

$$P_{net,elec} = P_{gross,elec} - P_{plasma} = 1{,}701.4 - 600 = 1{,}101.4 \;\text{kW}$$

$$\eta_{net,elec} = \frac{P_{net,elec}}{E_{feed}} = \frac{1{,}101.4 \times 3.6}{9{,}500} = \frac{3{,}965}{9{,}500} = 0.4173 = 41.7\%$$

### Conclusion

- **CGE = 150.1%** (energy gain due to plasma energy)
- **Net electrical output = 1,101.4 kW**
- **Net plant electrical efficiency = 41.7%**

This compares favorably to conventional incineration plants (15–22% net electrical efficiency), demonstrating the **energy leverage** of plasma gasification.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air Demand in Wet Air Oxidation

**Problem Statement:** A pharmaceutical wastewater stream contains **phenol (C₆H₅OH)** at a concentration of **20,000 mg/L** (this is the **CFC** scenario, i.e., the autothermal case). Compute the **theoretical oxygen demand** for complete oxidation.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write the balanced oxidation reaction.**

$$C_6 H_5 OH + 7\,O_2 \rightarrow 6\,CO_2 + 3\,H_2 O$$

(Molecular check: C: 6=6 ✓; H: 6=6 ✓; O: 1+14 = 12+3 = 15 ✓)

**Step 2 — Molar mass of phenol.**

$$M_{phenol} = 6(12) + 6(1) + 1(16) = 72 + 6 + 16 = 94 \;\text{g/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric O₂ mass per gram of phenol.**

7 mol O₂ = 7 × 32 = **224 g O₂ per mole of phenol** (94 g).

$$\text{ThOD} = \frac{224}{94} = 2.383 \;\text{g O}_2 \text{ per g phenol}$$

**Step 4 — O₂ demand for 20,000 mg/L phenol solution.**

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ demand} = 20{,}000 \times 2.383 = 47{,}660 \;\text{mg/L} = 47.66 \;\text{g/L}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to air requirement.**

Since air is 21% O₂ by volume (≈23% by mass):

$$\text{Air demand} = \frac{47.66}{0.233} = 204.5 \;\text{g air per litre of wastewater}$$

**Step 6 — For 1 m³ (=1,000 L) of wastewater:**

$$\text{Air} = 204.5 \times 1{,}000 = 204{,}500 \;\text{g} = 204.5 \;\text{kg air per m}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** A 20,000 mg/L phenol stream is **autothermal** — the heat released by complete oxidation is sufficient to maintain reactor temperature without external heating, validating the CFC concept.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Depolymerization Kinetics — First-Order Cracking Model

**Model:** For a polymer chain of length $n$, the scission into smaller fragments often follows **first-order kinetics** with respect to the number of cleavable bonds:

$$\frac{dX}{dt} = k \cdot (1 - X)$$

where $X$ is the **fractional conversion** of the polymer.

**Integration:**

$$\int_0^X \frac{dX}{1-X} = \int_0^t k\,dt$$

$$-\ln(1 - X) = k \cdot t$$

$$X(t) = 1 - e^{-kt}$$

**For a typical hydrocracking catalyst at 380 °C, k ≈ 0.45 hr⁻¹.**

**Step 4 — Compute the conversion after t = 4 hours.**

$$X(4) = 1 - e^{-0.45 \times 4} = 1 - e^{-1.8}$$

$$e^{-1.8} = 0.1653$$

$$X(4) = 1 - 0.1653 = 0.8347 = 83.47\%$$

**Step 5 — Compute the time required for 95% conversion.**

$$0.95 = 1 - e^{-k t} \Rightarrow e^{-kt} = 0.05$$

$$t = -\frac{\ln(0.05)}{k} = \frac{2.996}{0.45} = 6.66 \;\text{hours}$$

### Conclusion

- After 4 hours, ~**83.5%** of the polymer is depolymerized.
- To reach **95% conversion**, ~**6.66 hours** are required at 380 °C.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Reaction Severity Comparison

A "severity factor" is often used to compare different thermal processes:

$$\log R_0 = \log \left[ t \cdot \exp\left(\frac{T - 100}{14.75}\right) \right]$$

where *t* is in minutes and *T* in °C. The reference 100 °C and 14.75 constant come from **Overend & Chornet (1987)**.

| Process | Typical T (°C) | Typical t (min) | log R₀ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Catalytic Depolymerization | 250 – 400 | 30 – 120 | **3.4 – 4.8** |
| Pyrolysis | 500 – 800 | 5 – 30 | **4.0 – 5.6** |
| Plasma Gasification (reactor zone) | 1,200 – 3,000 | < 1 sec | **> 6.0** |
| Wet Air Oxidation | 200 – 300 | 30 – 120 | **3.2 – 4.0** |
| SCWO | 400 – 600 | 1 – 30 | **4.5 – 5.5** |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Strategy:** When a KTU question asks *"Compare the severity of these three processes"*, quote the log R₀ values. A higher log R₀ means more severe thermal processing.
